## AAQ: If we belong to the spiritual world, why did Krishna send us here ? | HH Stoka Krishna Swami
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### desire, independence, spiritual world
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/wB2qyiOywMY?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna has not sent us here. We have come here."
**Summary:** This segment explains that we come to the material world due to our desire to enjoy independently of Krishna and to lord over others, not because Krishna sent us.

Here in classes, we used to learn that if we follow regulative principles, we will go back to Krishna and never come back. But we all come from Krishna only. Why did Krishna send us here? Krishna has not sent us here. We have come here. Why have we come here? Two reasons: One is our desire to enjoy independent of Krishna. Second desire we have is to lord over everyone and everything.

---
### lordship, karma, material world
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/wB2qyiOywMY?t=47
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Here everyone is trying to be the Lord."
**Summary:** This segment contrasts the spiritual world, where Krishna is the only Lord, with the material world, where everyone tries to be the Lord according to their karma.

In the spiritual world, our pleasure, our enjoyment, our happiness is always in association with Krishna. And in the spiritual world, everyone is subordinate to Krishna. Krishna is the only Lord. Ekala, Ishvara, Krishna are Sabha, Vrithya. That's a reality. That's a fact in the spiritual world. But somebody thinks, why should Krishna be the Lord? Why can't I be the Lord? Or why can't I take Krishna's position? Then, for such people, the material world is created by Krishna. Here everyone is trying to be the Lord. And according to our karma, we have different lordship positions. From Brahma up to the most insignificant ant, everyone is Lord in their own field. In Hindi there is a saying, 'Har kutta apna gali mein share.' Of course, that's told in a lighter way. I don't mean that, but what I'm saying is everyone wants to be the Lord. So according to their karma, they can lord over some people.

---
### Brahma, material nature, limitations
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/wB2qyiOywMY?t=117
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Even Brahma doesn't have the power to lord over everyone like Krishna lords over."
**Summary:** This segment discusses Brahma's position as the lord of the universe, highlighting the limitations of his power and his subjection to material nature.

Brahma has got the power, authority over the entire universe. But even this universe is one insignificant universe among millions of universes. So even Brahma doesn't have the power to lord over everyone like Krishna lords over. Krishna is the Lord. But in his own sphere, Brahma thinks I am the Lord. I am the Lord of everyone. His purview is this entire universe which consists of millions of planets. Each planet has millions of living beings. Yes, Brahma is in a very, very, very, very big position. But still Brahma also, even though he is the Lord, he is also subject to the laws of material nature. He is not a free person. If he makes a mistake, he is accountable for the mistake. He is punished. He is punishable.

**Key Quote:** "Brahma has got a fantastic long life span. The longest life span anybody can ever have."
**Summary:** This segment elaborates on Brahma's incredibly long lifespan, comparing it to our perception of time and highlighting the illusion of material existence.

And even Brahma has to die one day. Brahma has got a fantastic long life span. The longest life span anybody can ever have. The duration of the whole universe, universal time. Brahma's life span is this kind in Bhagavad Gita. Sahasra yuga pariyantam har yad brahmano idhu. One thousand yuga cycles. Mahayuga. Satya, Treta, Dwapara, Kali. Four yugas rotating cyclically. One such cycle of yugas passing is called Mahayuga. And thousand Mahayugas. One thousand Mahayugas is twelve hours of Brahma's time scale. So Brahma lives hundred years like that. We calculate Brahma's life span is 311 trillion years according to our calculation. 311 trillion years. But even that long life span, Brahma thinks I have only hundred years to live. And Brahma's already 50 years is over. 50 years of Brahma is over. Half his life span is over. It is like how would a 50 year old person feel in this material world. Half my life span is over approximately. Say we live about hundred years. So half our life span is over, no? When I am 50 years old. Correct? That's why Brahma thinks. Each person thinks about his or her life according to the body they are having.

### spirit soul, material existence, illusion, gunas
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/wB2qyiOywMY?t=283
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "We come here not because Krishna sends us here. We come here because we wanted to lord over everyone and everything."
**Summary:** This segment explains that we are eternal spirit souls trapped in material existence due to our desire for independent enjoyment and lordship, and are subject to the influence of the three gunas.

Actually, we are spirit soul. We are no death. There is nothing like life span for the spirit soul. They are eternal beings. But why do we think in terms of body? That's called material existence. That's called illusion. So we come here not because Krishna sends us here. We come here because we wanted to lord over everyone and everything. So we are given this material world. And we wanted to independently enjoy. So that independent enjoyment is only in the material world, and also under illusion. There is no independence. We are strictly under the stringent laws of the material nature. We are under the three gunas. These three gunas are influencing us. Every action. Sattva guna, raja guna, tamma guna. Combination of that is influencing each one of us.

### illusion, gunas, death, eternity
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/wB2qyiOywMY?t=349
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "We are completely under control, but we think we are free."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes that we are under the control of the three gunas, yet we falsely believe we are independent and will live eternally in our bodies, while the truth is that we are eternal but not in these temporary bodies.

So we are under the stringent control of these three gunas, but we think we are independent because the gunas influence is not perceived by us. Nobody perceives the influence of the gunas. Nobody perceives, except devotees, pure devotees who know what these gunas are, and they can recognize the gunas. Nobody knows what these gunas are. So we are completely under control, but we think we are free. We are independent, and we think we are going to live eternally in this body. But the fact is we are eternal, but not in this body. Any body that anybody inhabits, including Brahma, that body has got a limited duration of life span. So they have to die. They have to die. Dying means they have to quit that body.

## AAQ: Is it offensive to consider Shiva as a demigod? | HH Stoka Krishna Swami
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### demigods, hierarchy, Vaishnavism
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Irt9Qww7vZ4?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So now if we consider Shiva as a demigod, Devata, subordinate to Vishnu or Krishna, will it become an offense? No."
**Summary:** The speaker addresses the question of whether it is offensive to consider Shiva as a demigod subordinate to Vishnu or Krishna, answering in the negative.

Does portraying Lord Krishna the Supreme alter the essence of Hinduism? Because during my childhood, grandparents used to say, Lord Shiva is the Deva of all Devas, therefore his name is Mahadeva. So now if we consider Shiva as a demigod, Devata, subordinate to Vishnu or Krishna, will it become an offense? No.

---
### demigods, hierarchy, time
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Irt9Qww7vZ4?t=38
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Among all the Devas, four personalities have that special position."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the hierarchy among the Devas, highlighting Brahma, Vishnu, and Shiva as Chiraloka Pala, the permanent governors of the universe, whose positions remain unchanged throughout its duration, unlike other Devatas.

When Shiva is described as Mahadeva or the Deva of all Devas, you should understand that there is a hierarchy among Devas. Among all the Devas, four personalities have that special position. First one is Brahma. Brahma, Vishnu and Shiva are called Chiraloka Pala. They are the permanent governors of this universe. That means their position does not change throughout the duration of the whole universe. All other personalities who are holding positions of power, all other Devatas, their duration is of a limited time. Maximum is one Manvantara, 71 Mahayugas, that's it. After that they have to quit their position. Whereas Brahma, Vishnu and Shiva, throughout the duration of the whole universe, they don't change their position.

### Shiva, Brahma, material body
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Irt9Qww7vZ4?t=111
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "But greater than Brahma is Shiva because Shiva is not a Jeeva."
**Summary:** The speaker differentiates Brahma and Shiva, stating that Brahma is a Jeeva (living entity) who has to leave his body, while Shiva is not a Jeeva and does not have a material body, thus he has no death.

Among the three, Brahma is actually a Jeeva, a living entity. But Brahma is immune to all the changes that take place in the universe during the life span of the Brahma himself. So he is unaffected by anything that happens. But greater than Brahma is Shiva because Shiva is not a Jeeva. Shiva is not a living entity. Brahma has to die. Shiva has no death. Shiva doesn't have a material body. That's why he doesn't have to die. Death means what? Quitting the material body is called death. Soul has no death. None of us have death. So even Brahma doesn't have death as soul. But when you say Brahma also has to die means he has to leave his body as Brahma. That Brahma's body. He has to leave that. He has to quit that. But Shiva, he doesn't have a material body. He never accepts a material body. So Shiva's birth is like Krishna's birth. Krishna is born as a son of Vasudeva and Devaki. But Krishna's birth is transcendental. Shiva's birth also is transcendental in this material world. He comes to this material world for the purpose of doing his, playing his part in this whole material arrangement. He is the lord of destruction. He is the husband of Durga Devi. He is the master of the Tamoguna. Director of Tamoguna. Like that. He has got many many positions. Many many powers. Many many responsibilities. But he is one person who doesn't have a material body. Okay.

### hierarchy, Shiva, Vishnu

**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Now Vishnu is above Shiva."
**Summary:** The speaker clarifies the hierarchy, stating that Shiva is superior to Brahma but Vishnu is superior to Shiva because Vishnu never comes in contact with material energy, while Shiva does.

And Shiva in that position as Shiva he is always superior to Brahma and all the other people below Brahma. Among the Devatas, Brahma is the topmost. But Shiva is above Brahma. Because Shiva is not at all a Jeeva. All the other Devatas are Jeevas. But Brahma is also a Jeeva. But Shiva is not a Jeeva. Now Vishnu is above Shiva. Because Shiva, even though he doesn't have a material body, he comes in contact with material energy. Vishnu, like Shiva, he does not accept a material body. So he has also no death. He doesn't want to quit any body. But Vishnu never comes in contact with material life. He never comes in contact with material energy. He never touches material energy. So Vishnu is superior even to Shiva. There is one difference between Vishnu and Shiva. Another difference is Shiva's power is limited. Though it is much much greater than Brahma's power. Brahma's power is more than all other Devata's power. Every Devata has got gradation of power. So everybody in this material world except for Vishnu, everyone's power is limited. Though the different extent of powers they have, limited. But Vishnu has unlimited power. Vishnu has unlimited power. Only Vishnu has unlimited power. Among the Devata's, only Vishnu has unlimited power.

### Durga Devi, Vishnu Maya, agency
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Irt9Qww7vZ4?t=341
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "She is the agency through which, according to Krishna's will, creation, maintenance and destruction takes place."
**Summary:** The speaker explains Durga Devi's position, stating that she is not a Jeeva or a Devata but Vishnu Maya, the personal energy of Krishna, who acts as the agency for creation, maintenance, and destruction according to Krishna's will, executed through Brahma, Vishnu, and Shiva.

What about Durga Devi? Durga Devi is not a Jeeva. She is not a living entity. She is not even in the category of Devata's. She is not in the category of Devata's. She is the personal energy of Krishna or Vishnu. She is called Vishnu Maya. Vishnu Maya. She is the energy through which this material world is created, maintained and destroyed. So in the Devi Purana it is described Durga Devi's glories are described. She is the maintainer. She is the creator. She is the destroyer of the entire material universe. She is greater than even Brahma, Vishnu and Shiva. Brahma only creates. Vishnu only maintains. Shiva only destroys. She is the creator, maintainer and destroyer of entire universe. She is greater than Brahma, Vishnu and Shiva. But what people don't understand is she is definitely the creator, maintainer and destroyer of this material world. But she is the agency through which according to Krishna's will, creation, maintenance and destruction takes place. She is the agency. And that agency works for creating, maintaining and destroying according to the will of Krishna. Krishna's will is executed by three personalities called Brahma, Vishnu and Shiva. Brahma creates according to the will of Krishna through the agency of Durga. Vishnu maintains according to the will of Krishna. Krishna means he himself. According to his own will. Vishnu maintains according to his own will through the agency of Durga. And Shiva destroys according to the will of Krishna through the agency of Durga. So Durga is not independent in creating, maintaining and destroying. She is always subordinate to Krishna. She simply carries out Krishna's will.

But the exact direction for doing it comes through Brahma, Vishnu and Shiva. So Brahma directs the creation. Vishnu directs the maintenance. Shiva directs the destruction. They are directors. They are directors of Durga. They are directors of Durga because Durga as the energy is present in the form of these three gunas. In the Bhagavad Gita, in the 14th chapter, Krishna describes. No. The verse would describe the three gunas. Satvam rajasthama iti guna praktisambhavaha. Material nature consists of three gunas. In the 7th chapter, Krishna describes material nature consists of eight elements. Eight elements. Ashta dhatu. Bhumirapo nalo vayu kham mano buddhi revaca ahankara. Eight elements. These eight elements are inert matter. But where is material energy? Where is energy? Energy is manifest as the three gunas. Satvam rajasthama iti guna praktisambhavaha. ### pancha bhuta, subtle body, gunas
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Irt9Qww7vZ4?t=577
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** The speaker contrasts the common understanding of the five elements (pancha bhuta) with the subtler elements of mind, intelligence, and ego, and then introduces the three gunas as the energy (prakriti) that drives all changes in the material creation.

Generally people who are conversant with little something about the Vedic scriptures, they know all material things are made of pancha bhuta. Five elements. Most people know about this. Our body also. Pancha bhuta. Earth, water, fire, air, sky. Correct? But many people don't know there are three other elements which are called subtle elements. Sukshma. They are subtle elements. Mind, intelligence, ego. These are three subtle elements. They are making up our subtle body. We got a gross body made up of all these organs and senses and all the parts of the body, everything. But there is inside one subtle body. Mind, intelligence and ego. Subtle body. But those are also three elements of matter only. They are also matter. There is no life in mind, intelligence, ego. Real life is coming from the soul. What about then these three gunas? These three gunas is what prakriti is made of. Which is the energy called material energy. Energy means some force is there. That force is actually making all the changes to happen in this material creation.

### Krishna, gunas, Durga Devi
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Irt9Qww7vZ4?t=697
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So Krishna is the topmost personality."
**Summary:** The speaker reiterates Krishna's supreme position, explaining how Brahma executes Krishna's will through Durga Devi's Rajoguna, Vishnu maintains through Satyoguna, and Shiva destroys through Tamoguna, emphasizing that we are all bound by the gunas and the material body.

Just like you sow a seed. It grows up into a big tree. How those changes are taking place? Seasons are changing. Planets are rotating in the orbit. Okay. Whatever changes happening in our body, in the outside world, any changes that are happening, that's the force of the gunas. Those gunas are directed by Brahma, Vishnu and Shiva according to the will of Krishna. So Krishna is the topmost personality. Simply as to will, let there be creation. Brahma will do the necessary execution of that will through Durga Devi. Through the Rajoguna. Rajoguna of Durga Devi. Satyoguna is used by Vishnu for maintenance. Through Satyoguna, maintenance happens. And Tamoguna is used for destruction by Shiva. Through Tamoguna, agency of Tamoguna, Shiva destroys. He is the director of Tamoguna. Director of destruction. Vishnu is the director of Satyoguna. He is the director of maintenance. Like that. So, Durga's position is she is nothing to do with actually anything in this material world except she is simply facilitating creation, maintenance, destruction. She keeps all the living entities bound up in the material world. Through the Gunas. We are bound up by the Gunas. That is through the Gunas this body is actually working. And this body is tying us down to this material world. Because the body we cannot do whatever we want to do. The body is limiting us. Just like a bird has got a body to fly, it can fly. We don't have a body to fly, we cannot fly. In this body I can't fly. I require the help of an aeroplane to fly. But a bird can just fly. Right? So, everybody is bound up by the body. Everybody is bound up by the body. That body is an effect of the interaction of the three Gunas. Anyway, this is a very big subject. So, basically we are all actually under the control of Maya or Durga through this body. Everybody has got a body. Including Brahma. Brahma has got a body also. So, Brahma is also under the control of Durga Devi.

### Durga Devi, Krishna, laws of Karma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Irt9Qww7vZ4?t=827
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Durga on behalf of Krishna is like the jail superintendent."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes by comparing Durga Devi to a jail superintendent who enforces the laws of Karma (Krishna's laws), emphasizing that she is not independent but carries out Krishna's will in controlling the material world.

How? Through his body. Anybody who has got a material body is under control. But Durga Devi is not independent in controlling anybody. She controls exactly according to the laws. Those laws are laws of God. Laws of Krishna. Called laws of Karma. Krishna is the controller. The real controller is Krishna. Not Durga. Durga on behalf of Krishna is like the jail superintendent. Is keeping all the prisoners inside the prison. Controlling the prison. But under higher authority. They say authority, state authority. Who should be imprisoned? Who should be released? When they should be released? What should be the conditions in the prison? Rigorous imprisonment. Somebody's simple imprisonment. All that is regulated by the higher authority. But the jail superintendent even though the most powerful person in the jail, but still is carrying out simply the orders of the higher authority. Enforcing the higher authority's orders. That is Durga Devi's position. She is the jail superintendent of this jail called the material world. She is the jail superintendent of this jail called the material world.

## Can we make Krishna our friend ? | AAQ | His Holiness Stoka Krishna Swami
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### relationship with Krishna, purification, love
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/dn_eKSByIAU?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "So whatever be the relationship that is later, first of all, we have to cleanse our heart."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that everyone has a relationship with Krishna, but to understand it, one must first cleanse their heart through chanting Hare Krishna, leading to understanding Krishna, oneself, and the loving relationship.

Yeah, scriptures explain that all of us have a relationship with Krishna. All of us have a relationship with Krishna. But before we understand our relationship with Krishna, we have to cleanse our heart of so many dirty things that are there which are making us forget Krishna. We have to cleanse our heart. So for cleansing, the best process is chanting Hare Krishna. You chant Hare Krishna, you cleanse your heart. Then after cleansing the heart, you will understand who is Krishna, who you are. And then the relationship also, you will understand. So that relationship is based on love. So similarly, friendship with Krishna, you can have friendly relation with Krishna, you can serve Krishna as a servant. You can serve Krishna as one's own son, like mother Yashoda, Devaki, Vasudeva. They are all serving Krishna as their son. Or Krishna can be served as one's beloved. So whatever be the relationship that is later, first of all, we have to cleanse our heart.

## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | Rath Yatra | 01-07-2022
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### Jagannath, Neelamadhava, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/dOA9Bi8lmlo?t=51
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "On top of Neela hill, you should construct a temple. There I will manifest as Darubrahman, the absolute truth manifested in a wooden form."
**Summary:** The lecture begins with the story of King Indra Dhyumna's search for Lord Vishnu, leading to the manifestation of Lord Jagannath as Darubrahman.

Today is the day of the Rathayatra of Lord Jagannath, Baldev, and Subhadra. The story of Lord Jagannath is given in this Kanda Purana. I will try to narrate that story very briefly. There was a king by the name Indra Dhyumna, who was a great devotee of Vishnu. Indra Dhyumna became very eager to meet Lord Vishnu face to face. Once, a Brahmana came to the palace of King Indra Dhyumna and told him about an incarnation of Lord Vishnu named Neelamadhava. The king then sent different brahmanas out to search for the deity of Lord Neelamadhava. They all returned unsuccessful except for one priest by name Vidyapati. Vidyapati traveled for a long time, after which he came to an area inhabited by non-Aryans called Shabaras. He stayed at the house of a local chief whose name was Vishwavasu. When he arrived, Vishwavasu was not there, but Vishwavasu's daughter Lalitha was at home. Vidyapati stayed there for some time and eventually married Lalitha, the daughter of the Shabara chief, Vishwavasu. Vidyapati noticed some peculiar behavior of his host. Vishwavasu would go out every day around noon and would return to the house scented with fragrance of sandalwood, camphor, and musk. Vidyapati asked his wife about this and informed him that her father went to worship him. Lalitha had been told by her father not to tell anyone about Neelamadhava, but that was in advance of her order. She was to be told by her father. By this, Linda had been pinched from mud Meela made clear her duty to his husband.

---
### Jagannath, divine manifestation, devotee service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/dOA9Bi8lmlo?t=450
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "I am eternally situated here in Nilachala in the form of Lord Jagannath as Daru Brahman. I have no material hands and feet, but with my transcendental senses I accept all the items offered by my devotees."
**Summary:** Continuing the story, King Indra Dhyumna faces challenges in building the temple and carving the deities, ultimately leading to the divine manifestation of Lord Jagannath and the establishment of specific roles for different families in His service.

You will not see me as Neelamadhava. King Indra Dhyumna constructed a temple. He wanted Lord Brahma to consecrate the temple, so he traveled to Brahmaloka and waited there for him. During this time, the temple became covered by sand. While Indra Dhyumna was gone, first Suradeva and then Galamadhava became king of the area. King Galamadhava uncovered the temple from the sand. Shortly afterwards, King Indra Dhyumna returned from Brahma's abode. Indra Dhyumna claimed that he had built the temple, and Galamadhava also claimed the same thing. There was an old crow in the nearby banyan tree who was constantly singing the glories of Lord Rama. The crow had seen the construction of the temple and said that Indra Dhyumna had built the temple and that Gala Madhava had just uncovered it. Because he had not told the truth, Gala Madhava was ordered by Brahma to live outside the temple compound on the western side of Indra Dhyumna Sarovara lake. Indra Dhyumna then asked Lord Brahma to consecrate the temple and the surrounding area which was called Sri Kshetra. He gave the highest type of liberation. Lord Brahma told him that Sri Kshetra is manifested by the Supreme Lord's internal potency and that the Supreme Lord manifests Himself. Therefore, he could not install the Lord here. Lord Jagannath and his abode are eternally situated in the material world. He said he would install the flag on the temple that anyone who sees this flag and offers prostrated obeisances would easily be liberated. After a while, King Indra Dhyumna became a monk. King Indra Dhyumna became frustrated with not seeing Neela Madhava. He decided to lay on a bed of Kusha grass and to fast until death. Lord Jagannath came to him in a dream and told him that I shall come floating from the sea in my wooden form as Daru Brahman. The king went to the seashore and saw a huge piece of wood which had the marks of a conch, club, disk and lotus on it. Many men and elephants tried to move Daru Brahman but they could not move him. That night, Lord Jagannath spoke to Indra Dhyumna in a dream and told him to bring Vishwavasu who used to serve Neela Madhava and bring a golden chariot in front of Daru Brahman. The king did this and Daru Brahman easily was placed on the chariot. Lord Brahma then performed a sacrifice and established a deity of Narasimha Deva on the raised platform of the sacrificial arena. It is said that the deity of Narasimha in the present temple compound that is on the western side of the Mukti Mandapa is the original Narasimha deity. King Indra Dhyumna had the best sculptors come to carve the deity of Lord Jagannath from Daru Brahman. As soon as they started, their chisels broke to pieces. Vishwakarma, the architect of the demigods, assumed a form of an old brahmana and came to Indra Dhyumna. He offered to carve the deities behind closed doors for 21 days. The old sculptor then took Daru Brahman into the temple and the doors were closed. After 14 days passed, the king could not hear any sounds of the artist's tools and he became fully anxious. The king then personally opened the door of the temple by force. The king did not see the sculptor, but instead he saw the three forms of Lord Jagannath, Subhadra and Lord Balarama. Their fingers and toes were unfinished. Thinking himself a great offender, the king decided to give up his life. He then laid on a bed of kushagras and began to fast. Lord Jagannath appeared to him in a dream. Lord Jagannath told the king, I am eternally situated here in Nilachala in the form of Lord Jagannath as Daru Brahman. I have no material hands and feet, but with my transcendental senses I accept all the items offered by my devotees. The fact that you broke your promise is a part of the pastime for me to manifest in the form of Jagannath. Those devotees whose eyes are smeared with the salve of love will always see me as Shamasundara holding a flute. The king then prayed to Lord Jagannath that those in the family of the sculptor who manifested his form will continue to assist in constructing the three karts, the three rathas. He also told the king that the descendants of Vishwavasu who served him as Neelamadhava should generation after generation serve the Lord. They shall be called Daitas. The descendants of Vidyavati born from his brahmana wife should perform the deity worship. The descendants born from his shabara wife Lalitha should cook the Lord's food. King Indrajumna then requested Lord Jagannath that the doors of the temple should be closed only three hours a day. The king also requested that he would not have any descendants so that no one in the future would claim the temple of Lord Jagannath as their own property. This is the story of the appearance of Lord Jagannath.

---
### ecstasy, Vrindavan, Jagannath
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/dOA9Bi8lmlo?t=997
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Thus, just as Krishna appeared as Neela Madhava to satisfy Vishwavasu, he appeared in the Deity form as Jagannath and resides in Jagannath Puri to satisfy the desire of Narada Muni."
**Summary:** The lecture transitions to another story, explaining Jagannath as the ecstatic form of Krishna, Balaram, and Subhadra, manifested when they heard about Krishna's Vrindavan pastimes, and how this form fulfills the desire of Narada Muni.

Now there is also a description of Jagannath being the ecstatic form of Lord Krishna himself. Once during a solar eclipse, Krishna, Balaram, Subhadra and other residents of Dwarka went to bathe in a holy pond at Kurukshetra. Knowing that Krishna would be there, Srimati Radharani, Krishna's parents Nanda Nishoda and other residents of Vrindavan who were burning in the fire of separation from the Lord went to meet him. Inside one of the many tents the pilgrims had set up at Kurukshetra, Rohini, Lord Balaram's mother narrated Krishna's Vrindavan pastimes to the queens of Dwarka and other Dwarka Nivasis. The residents of Dwarka are said to be in the mood of opulence or Aishwarya and they worship Krishna as the Supreme Lord. But the residents of Vrindavan are in the mood of sweetness, Madhurya and they have a confidential relationship with Krishna that surpasses awe and reverence because it is based on friendship and love. Rohini's narration was thus extremely confidential so she posted Subhadra at the door to prevent anyone from entering. Krishna and Balaram came to the door and stood on Subhadra's left and right sides. While listening to Rohini's narration of Krishna's intimate Vrindavan pastimes, Krishna and Balaram became ecstatic and their internal feelings were exhibited externally. Their eyes became dilated. Their limbs became retracted. Seeing these transformations in Krishna and Balaram, Subhadra also became ecstatic and assumed a similar form. Thus, by hearing about Krishna's pastimes in Vrindavan, Krishna and Balaram with Subhadra in between displayed their ecstatic forms of Jagannath, Baladeva and Subhadra. When the sage Narada saw Krishna transformed as Jagannath he prayed to the Lord to appear like this again. Although the Lord is not obliged to anyone, he reciprocates with his devotees to fulfill their desires. Thus, just as Krishna appeared as Neela Madhava to satisfy Vishwavasu, he appeared in the Deity form as Jagannath and resides in Jagannath Puri to satisfy the desire of Narada Muni. This special form of Krishna is also known as Patitapavana, the saviour of the fallen and anyone who takes his audience with proper consciousness is awarded spiritual liberation.

---
### Purushottam Kshetra, liberation, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/dOA9Bi8lmlo?t=1238
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "If men simply see that Deity, they are easily able to go to the Lord's abode."
**Summary:** The speaker quotes the Narada Purana, emphasizing the significance of Purushottam Kshetra and the ease with which one can attain liberation simply by seeing the Deity of Lord Jagannath. He then transitions to another narration from the Krishna book about the origin of Jagannath Rathayatra.

It is said in the Narada Purana in that great abode known as Purushottam Kshetra which is rarely achieved among all the three worlds, the Keshava Deity who was fashioned by the Supreme Lord himself is situated. If men simply see that Deity they are easily able to go to the Lord's abode. There is also a narration in the Krishna book about the origin of Jagannath Rathayatra. Once upon a time while Lord Krishna and Balarama were living peacefully in their great city of Dwaraka, there was the rare occasion of a full solar eclipse such as takes place at the end of every Kalpa or day of Brahma. By astronomical calculation people were informed about this great eclipse prior to its taking place. Therefore everyone both men and women decided to assemble at the holy place in Kurukshetra known as Samantapanjaka. After arriving in Kurukshetra the members of the Yadu dynasty took their baths ceremoniously with self-control as enjoined in the Shastras and they observed fasting for the whole period of the eclipse in order to nullify the reactions of their sinful activities. The ceremonial functions performed by the members of the Yadu dynasty externally resemble the ritualistic ceremonies performed by the Karmis. When a Karmi performs some ritualistic ceremonies ambition is self-gratification, good position, good wife, good house, good children or good wealth. But the ambition of the members of the Yadu dynasty was different. Their ambition was to offer Krishna perpetual devotion with faith. All the members of the Yadu dynasty were great devotees of Krishna.

---
### Krishna, Vrindavan, ecstasy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/dOA9Bi8lmlo?t=1405
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "As soon as Krishna became visible to them, they took him inside their hearts through their eyes and embraced him to their full satisfaction."
**Summary:** The lecture continues with the story of Nanda Maharaj and the residents of Vrindavan going to Kurukshetra to see Krishna and Balarama during the solar eclipse, emphasizing the intense love and attachment of the gopis for Krishna.

When Nanda Maharaj and other residents of Vrindavan had heard that Krishna would be present in Kurukshetra because of the solar eclipse and that all the members of the Yadu dynasty would also be there they had immediately prepared to go there. King Nanda accompanied by his cowherd men had loaded all their necessary paraphernalia and bullock carts and all of their Vrindavan residents had come to Kurukshetra to see their beloved sons Lord Balaram and Lord Krishna. When the cowherd men of Vrindavan arrived in Kurukshetra all the Yadu dynasty members were most pleased. As soon as they saw the residents of Vrindavan they stood up to welcome them and appeared to have regained their life. All the Yadus and Vrindavan residents had been very eager to meet each other and when they actually came forward and met they embraced one another to their heart's satisfaction and remained in embrace for a considerable time. As far as the gopis of Vrindavan were concerned from the very beginning of their lives they did not know anything beyond Krishna. Krishna and Balaram were their life and soul. The gopis were so attached to Krishna that they could not even tolerate not seeing him momentarily when their eyelids blinked and impeded their vision. They condemned Brahma the creator of the body because he foolishly made eyelids which blinked and checked their seeing Krishna. Because they had been separated from Krishna for so many years the gopis having come along with Nanda Maharaj and Mother Yashoda felt intense ecstasy upon seeing Krishna. No one can imagine how eager the gopis were to see Krishna again. As soon as Krishna became visible to them they took him inside their hearts through their eyes and embraced him to their full satisfaction. Even though they were embracing Krishna only mentally they became so ecstatic and overwhelmed with joy that for the time being they completely forgot themselves.

---
### devotional service, gopis, Chaitanya Mahaprabhu
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/dOA9Bi8lmlo?t=1580
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "The Rathayatra festival observed by Lord Chaitanya is the emotional process of taking Krishna back to Vrindavan."
**Summary:** The lecture concludes by describing the gopis' ecstatic trance and Krishna's reciprocation, emphasizing the importance of unalloyed devotional service. It connects the gopis' desire to remain in Vrindavan with Lord Chaitanya's teachings and the Rathayatra festival, symbolizing the return of Krishna to Vrindavan.

The ecstatic trance they achieved simply by mentally embracing Krishna is impossible to achieve even for great yogis constantly engaged in meditation on the Supreme Personality of Godhead. Krishna could understand that the gopis were wrapped in ecstasy by embracing him in their minds and therefore since he is present in everyone's heart he reciprocated the embracing from within. Krishna was sitting with Mother Yashoda and his other mothers Devaki and Rohini but when the mothers engaged in talking he took the opportunity and went to a secluded place to meet the gopis. As soon as he approached the gopis the Lord smiled and after embracing them and inquiring about their welfare he began to encourage them saying my dear friends you know that Lord Balaram and I left Rundhavan just to please our relatives and family members. Thus we were long engaged in fighting with our enemies and were obliged to forget you who were so much attached to me in love and affection. I can understand that I have been ungrateful to you but still I know that you are faithful to me. May I inquire if you have been thinking of us although we had to leave you behind? My dear gopis do you not dislike remembering me considering me to have been ungrateful to you? Do you take my misbehavior with you very seriously? Fortunately you have developed loving affection for me which is the only way to achieve the transcendental position of association with me. Lord Krishna concluded by saying unalloyed devotional service and affection for me are the cause of supreme liberation. The gopis responded to Krishna by saying My dear Krishna we therefore request that you remain within our hearts as the rising sun that will be your greatest benediction. The gopis are always liberated souls because they are always in Krishna consciousness. They only pretended to be entangled in the household affairs in Vrindavan. Because of their separation from Krishna he might have asked them to return with him to Dwarka but the inhabitants of Vrindavan the gopis were not interested in the idea of going with Krishna to Dwarka. They wanted to remain in Vrindavan and thus feel the presence of Krishna in every step of their lives. This transcendental emotional existence of the gopis is the basic principle of Lord Chaitanya's teachings. The Rathayatra festival observed by Lord Chaitanya is the emotional process of taking Krishna back to Vrindavan. Srimati Radharani refused to go with Krishna to Dwarka to enjoy his company in the atmosphere of royal opulence for she wanted to enjoy his company in the original Vrindavan atmosphere. I'll stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

---

## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.10.1 | 22-05-2022
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### ideal governance, divine provision, mismanagement
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ALfrCE66QkE?t=55
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Ultimately it is the Supreme Lord who is the maintainer of everyone in the material world and the spiritual world."
**Summary:** The lecture begins with an explanation of the first verse of the tenth chapter of the first canto, focusing on how Yudhishthira Maharaj ruled his subjects and the concept of Krishna as the ultimate maintainer, providing abundantly, with scarcity arising from mismanagement and sinful activities.

In this session, we will be discussing the first verse of the tenth chapter of the first canto. This is a new chapter entitled 'Departure of Lord Krishna for Dwaraka'. The meaning of this first verse is as follows: Shravana Kamuni asked, 'After killing his enemies who desired to usurp his rightful inheritance, how did the greatest of all religious men, Maharaj Yudhishthira, assisted by his brothers, rule his subjects? Surely he could not freely enjoy his kingdom with unrestricted consciousness.' The previous chapter described that Yudhishthira Maharaj executed the royal power after assuming the throne of Hastinapura, becoming the emperor of the world. He ruled strictly according to the codes and religious principles given in the Dharma Shastra, approved by his uncle Dhritarashtra, and confirmed by Lord Krishna, the Supreme Personality of Godhead. So now the question is being raised by Savanaka Rishi: How did he rule his subjects, assisted by his brothers? In the Mahabharata, there is an elaborate description, and briefly it is described in this chapter how Yudhishthira was the ideal king, primarily because Yudhishthira was a pure devotee of the Supreme Lord. The very conception is that the king is the representative of the Supreme Lord. Ultimately it is the Supreme Lord who is the maintainer of everyone in the material world and the spiritual world. So when Krishna is the maintainer, nobody should have any scarcity because Krishna is unlimitedly opulent, and He provides for everyone. But in human society, there is scarcity sometimes only because of mismanagement, misuse, and abuse. These are the reasons why there is apparently some scarcity. Otherwise, by the arrangement of the Supreme Lord, there is plenty. It is like you consider a small seed of any particular fruit. Now you sow that seed, you get a huge tree, and that tree keeps on giving fruits for several decades. I know of a mango tree near our temple in Mysore, where the gentleman whose house that tree is presently standing says it gives 20,000 mangoes in the season. And that mango tree was planted by his great-grandfather, as he has heard from his parents and grandparents. So this is Krishna's creation. A jackfruit tree, the jackfruit itself is so big. One jackfruit, even one family cannot eat, and a jackfruit tree gives so many fruits in the season. So we have known people who have jackfruit trees, who are not commercially minded, they give away jackfruits to temples, to neighbors, to relatives, and still they are not able to exhaust them. How much can a person eat? We have limited capacity to eat. So therefore, Krishna provides in plenty. Krishna provides in plenty.

---
### divine provision, mismanagement, righteous rule
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ALfrCE66QkE?t=365
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Yudhishthira never wanted to fight with his cousins for the sake of enjoying the kingdom."
**Summary:** The speaker continues to illustrate Krishna's abundant provision with the example of the Food Corporation of India, highlighting the problem of mismanagement. He then contrasts Yudhishthira's righteous rule with Duryodhana's desire to usurp the kingdom, emphasizing Yudhishthira's adherence to religious principles.

Another example I can give, a practical example, the Food Corporation of India is meant to store food grains for preventing any famine or any calamity, that there will be no food grains. The stored food grains will be released by the government. Now the godowns of the Food Corporation of India are over-flooded with grains, so much so that there was a report in the newspaper, the wagons, the railway wagons which bring the food grains after the harvest, after the government procures from the farmers, the wagons bring to the godowns of the Food Corporation of India, and because the godowns are full, the wagons are not emptied, and the wagons are lying in the yard, railway yard, and the grains are getting spoiled because there is no place to store them. So if this is the quantity of food grains that is there in stock, and everywhere the shops are selling grains, there are plenty of grains. There are plenty of grains. So, there is no scarcity because Krishna is providing everybody. But then, due to sinful activities, due to mismanagement, due to abuse, misuse of the natural resources, sometimes there is scarcity. So, Yudhishthira Maharaj, he was perfectly ruling according to, strictly according to religious principles. Now, here, one word is used, dharma-bhritam-varishthah. He is one of those who strictly follow religious principles. He is the greatest among them, Yudhishthira Maharaj. And when he was ruling, then there is no question of any scarcity, any difficulty, any problem for the people. So, Srila Prabhupada says in the purport, 'Maharaj Yudhishthira was the greatest of all men of religion. Thus, he was not at all inclined to fight with his cousins for the sake of enjoying the kingdom.' This is a very important point. Yudhishthira never wanted to fight with his cousins with Duryodhana and party for the sake of enjoying the kingdom. Then why did he fight at all in the battle of Kurukshetra? He fought for the right cause because the kingdom of Hastinapura was his rightful inheritance, and his cousins wanted to usurp it for themselves. See, the clear contrast between Yudhishthira and Duryodhana. Yudhishthira is one who strictly follows all the religious principles. Duryodhana is demoniac minded, demoniac nature.

---
### divine vs. demoniac, justice, Krishna's plan
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ALfrCE66QkE?t=577
**Verse References:** BG 16.6-20
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Everything goes on according to the plan of Krishna."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the contrast between divine and demoniac natures, referencing the Bhagavad Gita. He explains that Yudhishthira fought for justice under Krishna's guidance, while Duryodhana's obstinacy and overconfidence led to his downfall, as everything ultimately unfolds according to Krishna's plan.

So, in the Bhagavad Gita, Krishna clearly says the difference between divine nature and demoniac nature. He says, Krishna says, divine nature people, they follow the injunctions of the scriptures and the spiritual authorities. This is divine nature. And those of demoniac or asuric nature, they do not follow the regulated principles as laid down in the scriptures, but they act according to their own whimsical, physical mind. This is the clear distinction between divine nature and demoniac nature. So, Duryodhana was demoniac nature and Yudhishthira was divine nature. So, because Yudhishthira was divine nature, he strictly followed all the regulations of the scriptures. So, therefore, there was no scarcity at all. It will be described in the later verses how the citizens under the rule of Yudhishthira Maharaj enjoyed everything in plenty. So, the fight on the battlefield of Kurukshetra between the Pandavas and Kauravas was justified on the basis of Yudhishthira because Yudhishthira fought for the sake of justice, and Yudhishthira fought for the right cause under the guidance of Lord Krishna. He fought under the guidance of Lord Krishna. In fact, Yudhishthira Maharaj tried to negotiate things for peace, but Duryodhana would not give even five villages for the five brothers, Pandava brothers, which was a rightful claim. The whole kingdom was a rightful claim. But when the Pandavas reduced their request to five villages to avoid the war, the battle, but Duryodhana would not give. So, obstinacy of Duryodhana. He was, Duryodhana was overconfident. Oh, we are going to win the battle. We are very powerful. He was making all sorts of material calculations. Foolish Duryodhana never understood Krishna is on the side of the Pandavas because Pandavas are devotees. And Duryodhana being demoniac-natured, he will never understand what is Krishna's plan, and everything goes according to Krishna's plan. Nothing goes according to anybody else's wishes or plans. No. Everything goes on according to the plan of Krishna. So, it is the plan of Krishna that the demoniac-natured Duryodhana and his party should be completely vanquished, and the thoroughly righteous religious principled Yudhishthira should be enthroned as the emperor of the world. This was Krishna's plan, and this was to be executed.

---
### Krishna's plan, responsibility, ideal governance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ALfrCE66QkE?t=797
**Verse References:** BG 18.78
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The duty of the king is to care for the citizens for their spiritual upliftment."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes by reiterating that the Pandavas' victory was guaranteed due to Krishna's plan, referencing a verse from the Bhagavad Gita. He emphasizes that Yudhishthira, despite his victory, remained conscious of his responsibility to his citizens and their spiritual upliftment, setting the stage for the next verses which will further elaborate on his ideal rule.

So, it was all arranged by Krishna. Therefore, the Pandavas were guaranteed victory actually, even before the battle started. As I mentioned earlier also, the last verse of the Bhagavad Gita in the 18th chapter says, Yatra Yogeshwara Krishna Yatra Partho Dhanurdharaha Tatra Shri Vijayobhutir Dhruvanitir Matirmama. Even before the battle begins, Sanjaya predicts that victory belongs to the Pandavas because Krishna and Arjuna are on the side of the Pandavas. So, therefore, here it is explained Yudhishthira Maharaj, he won the battle, but he could not enjoy the results of victory, he could not enjoy the results of victory with unrestricted consciousness. That's what is mentioned here. Saunaka Rishi is telling, surely he could not enjoy freely his kingdom with unrestricted consciousness. He was always conscious of his responsibility and duty. As was explained in the previous chapter, the duty of the king is to care for the citizens for their spiritual upliftment. So, how did he rule? Saunaka Rishi wanted to know how did he rule? He wanted to hear the glories of the rule of King Yudhishthira, and that will be explained in the next few verses. I'll stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.10.11-12 | 31-05-2022
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### separation, association, love
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2ULkQGwU3z4?t=34
**Verse References:** SB 1.10.11, SB 1.10.12
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "How then could the Pandavas tolerate his separation? For they had been intimately associated with this person, seeing him face to face, touching him, conversing with him, sleeping, sitting and dining with him."
**Summary:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami introduces verses 11 and 12 from the 10th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam, explaining the Pandavas' intimate association with Krishna and their subsequent feelings of separation when Krishna left Hastinapura for Dwaraka.

Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya Naramchaiva Narottamam Deviim Sarasvatim Vyasam Tato Jayam Udi Rayet. In this session, we will be discussing the 11th and 12th verses of the 10th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. These two verses are given together in the Srimad Bhagavatam; therefore, we are discussing them together. Satsangana Muktadussangohatum Notsahate Budhaha Kirtyamanam Yashoyasya Sakradakarnya Roshayam Satsangana Muktadussangohatum Notsahate Budhaha Tasminyastadhiya Parthaah Saheran Viraham Katham Darshanasparsya Samlapa Shayanasana Bhhojanaihi. The meaning of these two verses is given together: The intelligent who have understood the Supreme Lord in association with pure devotees and have become freed from bad materialism can never avoid hearing the glories of the Lord, even though they have heard them only once. How then could the Pandavas tolerate his separation? For they had been intimately associated with this person, seeing him face to face, touching him, conversing with him, sleeping, sitting, and dining with him.

---
### service, illusion, constitutional position
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2ULkQGwU3z4?t=140
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The living being's constitutional position is one of serving a superior."
**Summary:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami explains that the verses further explain the Pandavas' feelings of separation when Krishna left, elaborating on Srila Prabhupada's explanation of attachment to Krishna and the constitutional position of the living being as a servant.

Satsangana Muktadussangohatum Notsahate Budhaha. This verse further explains the feeling of separation, especially the Pandavas felt when Krishna left Hastinapura and returned to Dwaraka. The Pandavas were very, very, very intimately associated with Krishna. So the general principle is first explained, and then the specific feeling of the Pandavas is explained because they were intimately connected with Krishna. So Srila Prabhupada explains this point, how somebody is attached to Krishna, then what is the position of such a person. The living being's constitutional position is the position of the living being. The living being's constitutional position is the position of the living being. The position of the living being is one of serving a superior. This is the purport Srila Prabhupada is writing. The living being's constitutional position is one of serving a superior. He is obliged to serve by force the dictates of illusory energy in different phases of sense gratification. What is our life in this world? It is one of sense gratification. We are always seeking. We are always seeking satisfaction of our senses. Now, in doing so, we are forced by the illusory energy to serve maya. We are forced by maya to serve maya. That we don't realize. That we don't understand. Again, we don't understand because of the influence of illusion only. So, therefore, the statement by Prabhupada in the beginning of the purport is that it is our constitutional position. The way we are made. We have to serve a superior.

---
### service, illusion, sense gratification
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2ULkQGwU3z4?t=271
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Maya's force is so powerful that there is no respite for us. We keep on serving our senses with the false hope that this particular act of serving the senses will satisfy me, but the reality is I never get satisfied."
**Summary:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami continues to elaborate on the concept of service, explaining that we are always servants, either of Krishna or of illusory energy through sense gratification, and that the conditioned soul is never tired of serving their senses, driven by the false hope of satisfaction.

We cannot be master. We are always servant. Nobody likes to hear this. Nobody wants to be a servant. Everyone wants to be master. But our position is we are servant. And if we don't agree to render service, we are forced to render service by the illusory energy. But the real fact of the matter is that the conditioned soul, the ordinary living beings in this world, are never tired in serving their senses because they are running after sense enjoyment. To become tired of serving the senses, even if somebody may feel tired serving their own senses, the illusory energy perpetually forces him to do so without being satisfied. Now, these are some of the very important truths that Prabhupada is revealing in this purport. Please hear very attentively and think about this, contemplate on this, that Maya's force is so powerful that there is no respite for us. We keep on serving our senses with the false hope that this particular act of serving the senses will satisfy me, but the reality is I never get satisfied.

---
### entanglement, karma, liberation, association
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2ULkQGwU3z4?t=392
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The only way such an entangled soul can get release from this entanglement or bondage is by association with pure devotees."
**Summary:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami explains that the attempt to satisfy the senses leads to entanglement in material bondage through the laws of karma, and the only way to release from this bondage is through association with pure devotees.

On the other hand, my senses demand more and more and more and more, and foolishly a person may go on trying and trying and trying to satisfy the senses. It never happens. Now, as a result of this attempt at satisfying the senses, the conditioned soul becomes entangled. You understand what is entanglement? Material bondage. We have bound up, completely bound up through the stringent laws of karma. We will be bound through the laws of karma, and there is no hope of release from such bondage generally. Now, the only way such an entangled soul can get release from this entanglement or bondage is by association with pure devotees.

---
### association, transcendental consciousness, service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2ULkQGwU3z4?t=466
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "When a person comes to the spiritual consciousness, then such a person can understand that his eternal position is to render service to Krishna, and not render service to the perverted senses."
**Summary:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami describes how association with pure devotees promotes one to transcendental consciousness, enabling them to understand their eternal position is to serve Krishna, not the perverted material senses, which are coverings for the real, spiritual senses.

Only way. What happens when somebody is in the association of pure devotees? As it is said here, by association with pure devotees, one is gradually promoted to transcendental consciousness, spiritual consciousness, Krishna consciousness. Thus, when a person comes to the spiritual consciousness, then such a person can understand that his eternal position is to render service to Krishna, and not render service to the perverted senses. These senses of ours are actually not our real senses. Our real senses are within. These senses are a covering for the real senses, the spiritual senses. So, these material senses which are covering our spiritual senses, they demand satisfaction, and foolishly, we go on trying to satisfy. But, we don't realize, except when we come in touch with pure devotees, and our transcendental consciousness is awakened, and then we are able to understand, I am simply serving lust, anger, greed, enviousness. All these material desires, I am simply serving them. Kama, krodha, loba, moha, madha, vatsarya. We are simply servant of all these different perverted desires. Simply serving them.

---
### lust, bondage, miseries, detachment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2ULkQGwU3z4?t=583
**Verse References:** BG 3
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Material society, friendship, and love are all different phases of lust. Kama, lust, is the greatest enemy of the soul, of every conditioned soul."
**Summary:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami explains that material society, friendship, and love are all forms of lust, which is the greatest enemy of the soul, and that home, country, family, society, and wealth are causes of bondage leading to the three-fold miseries of life, but association with pure devotees and hearing Krishna Katha submissively slackens attachment to material enjoyment.

So, Prabhupada explains further, material society, friendship, and love are all different phases of lust. Kama, lust, is the greatest enemy of the soul, of every conditioned soul. The greatest enemy is lust, kama. Viddhi, enam, iya, vairinam. In the Bhagavad Gita, third chapter, it is described by Krishna. It is the greatest enemy of all of us, who are in this material world. It is lust. And, it is further explained, home, country, family, society, wealth, all are causes of bondage in the material world. And, the concomitant factors of such bondage is the three-fold miseries of life. Tapatraya. Three kinds of miseries: miseries due to our own body and mind, miseries due to other living beings, and miseries due to forces of nature. We have to keep on suffering different miseries at different times, due to the bondage. Now, if somebody is associated with pure devotees, and hears Krishna Katha submissively, then the attachment for material enjoyment becomes slackened. That which is a burning desire in the heart, that burning desire begins to get extinguished.

---
### attraction, Krishna consciousness, separation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2ULkQGwU3z4?t=680
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The Supreme Personality of Godhead, Lord Hari, is so transcendentally attractive that even those who are self-satisfied by self-realization, even those who are liberated completely from all material bondage, they also become devotees of Lord Hari."
**Summary:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami explains that association with pure devotees and hearing Krishna Katha increases attraction for Krishna, leading to progress in Krishna consciousness, and that Lord Hari is so attractive that even liberated souls become devotees, making it understandable how the Pandavas felt separation when Krishna left.

When you are associated with pure devotees, and hear Krishna Katha from them, then, gradually, the attraction for hearing more and more about Krishna, that increases, it increases and becomes prominent. Now, as the attraction for Krishna increases, one keeps on making progress more and more in Krishna consciousness. And, the attraction keeps on increasing more and more and more and more and more. Now, it is said here that the Supreme Personality of Godhead, Lord Hari, is so transcendentally attractive that even those who are self-satisfied by self-realization, even those who are liberated completely from all material bondage, they also become devotees of Lord Hari. This is the power of attraction. Supreme Lord Hari is attractive to everyone, including liberated souls, and those who are self-satisfied due to self-realization. Everyone is attracted to Krishna. That is the power of Krishna. So, under these circumstances, we can easily understand the Pandavas who were constant companions of Krishna, how they would have felt when Krishna left Hastinapura and went to Dwaraka. So, we can't imagine the kind of feeling of separation felt by the Pandavas especially, when Krishna left for Dwaraka.

### separation, attachment, remembrance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2ULkQGwU3z4?t=795

**Summary:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami concludes by stating that the Pandavas felt intense separation due to continuous personal contact with Krishna, and that the nature of attachment for Krishna in pure devotees is so strong that simply remembering Krishna's name, form, qualities, pastimes, or paraphernalia makes the devotee forget everything material, allowing them to experience the same feeling even in Krishna's physical absence.

But, Prabhupada concludes by telling that because of continuous personal contact, the Pandavas were feeling intense separation when Krishna left. We can't imagine how much separation they would have felt. Now, the nature of the attachment for Krishna, which the pure devotees have, is described as so very attractive. Krishna is so very attractive for pure devotees that simply the remembrance of Krishna's name, form, qualities, or pastimes, or paraphernalia, simply remembering that, makes the devotee completely forget about all other material forms, material names, material qualities, material activities. Everything material completely is forgotten by the devotee. So, mukta dusanga, a pure devotee is always free from the contaminating association of the material world, even though the devotee may be in the material world. So, it is always possible by developing that attraction for Krishna, and awakening that intimate attachment, that simply remembering Krishna is enough to again experience that same feeling, especially in the physical absence of Krishna. ## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.10.13 | 01-06-2022
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### separation from Krishna, attachment, spiritual vs. material
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/NM8YmOZcjo4?t=52
**Verse References:** SB 1.10.13
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Attraction or attachment for Krishna is an eternal thing."
**Summary:** This segment discusses the feelings of separation from Krishna experienced by the residents of Hastinapura and contrasts spiritual attachment to Krishna with temporary material attachments.

In this session, we will be discussing the 13th verse of the 10th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. Sarvete Anmishai Rakshaihi Tam Anudruta Chetasaha Vikshanta Sneha Sambaddha Vichelus Tatra Tatraha. The meaning of this verse is, all their hearts were melting for him on the pot of attraction. They looked at him without blinking their eyes, and they moved hither and thither in perplexity. What happened to all those who were attached to Krishna when Krishna left Hastinapura is being described here. They felt so much separation. They were constantly looking at Krishna without blinking their eyes, constantly staring at him, and they don't know what to do, how to even continue their normal activities in the absence of Krishna. So, within the core of their heart, they were feeling that we are going to miss Krishna. It's not a superficial feeling. Unlike material feelings, it's all superficial because we know, our experience says in this material world, however much somebody will be afflicted by some pain or suffering, they can be pacified, or they tend to forget that in course of time. Sometimes they say time is the best healer of some pain of suffering or loss or bereavement somebody suffers. With time, they forget that. But that is not the case with attachment to Krishna, attraction for Krishna. That we have to understand.

---
### eternal relationship, bhakti, maya
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/NM8YmOZcjo4?t=177
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna is naturally attractive for all living beings because he is the chief eternal amongst all eternals."
**Summary:** This segment explains that attraction to Krishna is eternal and spiritual, not material, and that Krishna is the chief among all eternal beings. It also discusses how to revive our eternal relationship with Krishna through bhakti and how this frees us from the illusion of maya.

Attraction or attachment for Krishna is an eternal thing. It is a spiritual feeling, spiritual attraction. It is not material. Krishna is not a material personality. Krishna is not having any material relationship with anyone. It's only spiritual relationship, spiritual attraction. So, therefore it is different. And the purport Srila Prabhupada explains, Krishna is naturally attractive for all living beings because he is the chief eternal amongst all eternals. He is the chief. Krishna is the chief among all the eternal conscious beings, living beings. Krishna is the chief, the topmost and the supreme. He alone is the maintainer of the many eternals. This is stated in the Kathopanishad. This can be lived here and there, so that they can do, he can live. service by chanting Hare Krishna, then our eternal relation with Krishna is revived. Nitya siddha krishna prema sadhya kabunai. It is not something to be externally imposed. This love for Krishna or relationship with Krishna, it is revived within by the process of bhakti. Once this relation is slightly revived, the conditioned soul at once becomes free from the illusion of maya, and living being, such a living being who becomes free from the illusion of maya and is reviving even slightly the eternal relationship, then such a person becomes mad after association of Krishna. One becomes almost like that.

---
### association with Krishna, Srimad Bhagavatam, reviving relationship
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/NM8YmOZcjo4?t=327
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Srimad Bhagavatam is a special literature which trains the conditioned soul to this stage of perfection by submissive hearing from the pure devotees."
**Summary:** This segment explains that association with Krishna is possible through His name, fame, pastimes, qualities, paraphernalia, and devotees. It also highlights the role of Srimad Bhagavatam in training the conditioned soul to revive their eternal relationship with Krishna through submissive hearing from pure devotees.

So, this association with Krishna is made possible not only by personal contact with Krishna, but also by association with Krishna's name, Krishna's fame, Krishna's pastime, Krishna's activities, Krishna's qualities, Krishna's paraphernalia, Krishna's devotees, pure devotees. Any of these also can help us to revive our eternal relationship with Krishna. So, Srimad Bhagavatam is a special literature which trains the conditioned soul to this stage of perfection by submissive hearing from the pure devotees and by listening to the pure devotees. So, what do you have to do to revive your eternal relationship with Krishna? Everyone has an eternal relationship, every living being without exception. So, to revive that, what do you have got to do?
