## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.10.8 | 30-05-2022
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### etiquette, humility, Krishna's nature
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/XWi-tEQus3A?t=58
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna takes pleasure when his pure devotees treat him as an equal or treat him as a subordinate."
**Summary:** This segment explains how Krishna, despite being the Supreme Personality of Godhead, sets an example of humility by showing respect to elders like Maharaj Yudhishtira, and how he enjoys being treated with affection and even as subordinate by his devotees.

In this session, we will be discussing the 8th verse of the 10th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. The meaning of this verse is, afterwards, when the Lord asked permission to depart and the king gave it, the Lord offered his respects to Maharaj Yudhishtira by bowing down at his feet, and the king embraced him. After this, the Lord, being embraced by others and receiving their obeisances, got into his chariot. So this was described as the title of the chapter, Departure of Lord Krishna for Dwaraka from Hastinapura. Krishna, after finishing his work of conducting the battle of Kurukshetra, and installing Maharaj Yudhishtira as the emperor of the world, he was now leaving Hastinapura for Dwaraka. Now the description here is that Krishna, even though he is the supreme personality of Godhead, he conducts himself in an ideal manner, setting an example for everyone else, that how the younger ones should show respect to the elders. Krishna is, that is explained by Prabhupada in the purport, Maharaj Yudhishtira was the elder cousin of Lord Krishna. So Maharaj Yudhishtira is elder to Krishna, and therefore, while departing from him, the Lord bowed down at the king's feet. King refers to Yudhishtira. So Krishna is bowing down to Yudhishtira's feet. This is the etiquette, this is the culture. Krishna is younger to Yudhishtira, so therefore he is bowing down. And Yudhishtira, what is Yudhishtira doing? The king, that is Yudhishtira, embraced him as a younger brother. Yudhishtira embraced him, although the king knew perfectly well that Krishna is the supreme personality of Godhead. Yudhishtira knows. At the time of Krishna's advent in this world, 5,000 years back, very few people knew Krishna to be the supreme personality of Godhead. Most of them could not recognize him. So, this is another reason why people cannot recognize that Krishna behaves as if he is one among all of us. But then Krishna also performs activities which are extraordinary, which establish his position as the supreme lord. When Krishna is playing with the cowherd boys, it looks like he is one among them. But when there is a war, there is a danger, some demon comes to attack, Krishna effortlessly kills the demons and dispatches them. So, that killing the demons is not at all an ordinary activity. It's not possible for anyone else to do that. Or lifting the Govardhan hill, that's not possible for anyone to do. Even though he is just a seven-year-old boy, he lifted the Govardhan hill on his little left finger. So, certain activities of Krishna are extraordinary. But if somebody hears about it, they don't have the proper understanding about who is Krishna. They tend to disbelieve it, or they tend to think it is some kind of mythological story. But actually, Krishna is the supreme personality of Godhead. He has unlimited power. So, it is not at all impossible for Krishna to lift the Govardhan hill, or to kill some big demons. But, Krishna also performs activities as if he is an ordinary person. So, here it is being described how the exchange took place when he was leaving Hastinapura for Dwaraka. When he was leaving Hastinapura for Dwaraka, the Lord took pleasure when some of his devotees accepted him as less important in terms of love. No one is greater than or equal to the Lord, but he takes pleasure in being treated as younger than his devotees. See, this is Krishna's transcendental pastime, that even though those who recognize him, they respect him as the supreme Lord, they offer obeisances always, under all circumstances. Everyone offers obeisances to him as the supreme Lord. But, Krishna takes pleasure when his pure devotees treat him as an equal or treat him as a subordinate. Krishna takes pleasure. So, that is the personal aspect of the Lord. The Lord is the supreme Lord, as a personality. This is the feature of a personality, that they take pleasure or they love something, then it doesn't matter whether etiquette demands something else. So, these are all transcendental pastimes of the Lord, and the impersonalist cannot enter into the supernatural role, played by the devotee of the Lord.

---
### impersonalism, devotion, relationships with Krishna
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/XWi-tEQus3A?t=454
**Verse References:** BG 9.11
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna is the source of the impersonal Brahman, but the impersonalists do not know this."
**Summary:** This segment contrasts the personalist and impersonalist understandings of Krishna, explaining that impersonalists cannot appreciate Krishna's pastimes because they lack understanding of the personality of Godhead, and it further explains the different ways Bhima, Arjuna, Nakula, and Sahadeva related to Krishna based on their ages.

The impersonalist, the understanding is absolute truth, is some impersonal Brahman. They don't have understanding of the personality of Godhead, beyond the impersonal Brahman understanding. Bhagavad Gita explains, brahmano hi pratishtaham. Krishna is the source of the impersonal Brahman, but the impersonalists do not know this. Therefore, they cannot appreciate these pastimes of Krishna, these pastimes of Krishna and his pure devotees. Now, as regards Bhima and Arjuna, because they were equal in age to Lord Krishna, they embraced him. So, mutually they embraced each other, because they are equals. And, Nakula and Sahadeva are younger to Krishna, therefore they bowed down to Krishna.

### separation, attraction, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/XWi-tEQus3A?t=503
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees
**Key Quote:** "Lord Sri Krishna is so attractive for the living beings, especially for the devotees, that it is impossible for them to tolerate separation."
**Summary:** This segment describes the reactions of the residents of Hastinapura upon Krishna's departure, highlighting the unbearable feeling of separation experienced by devotees due to Krishna's supreme attractiveness.

So, this is the description of Krishna leaving for Dwaraka. He mounted his chariot, and after he was properly bowing down, and receiving embraces, etc. The 9th and 10th verse describe further, Subhadra Draupadi Kunti Virata Tanaya Tatha Gandhari Dhritarashtrascha Yuyatsur Gautamo Yamao Vrukodascha Dhaumyascha Striyo Matsya Sudadayaha Nasehire Vimukhyanto Viraham Sarangadhanmanaha. This is two verses, the 9th and the 10th verses. In these verses, it is described, the translation of these two verses. At that time, Subhadra Draupadi Kunti Uttara Gandhari Dhritarashtra Yuyatsur Krupacharya Nakula Sahadeva Bhimasena Dhaumya and Satyavati, all nearby, all nearly fainted, because it was impossible for them to bear separation from Lord Krishna. So this is describing what is the reaction of the residents of the palace of Hastinapura when Krishna was leaving for Dwarka. Krishna has mounted his chariot. Now what was the reaction? They all felt that this separation is impossible to bear for them. All these personalities. So Prabhupada explains in the purport the reason for this. Prabhupada says, Lord Sri Krishna is so attractive for the living beings, especially for the devotees, that it is impossible for them to tolerate separation.

### conditioned soul, illusion, separation from Krishna, Vrindavan
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/XWi-tEQus3A?t=620
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees
**Key Quote:** "If we are not under the influence of illusion, we cannot, we can never forget Krishna."
**Summary:** This segment explains why some people don't feel attracted to Krishna, attributing it to the conditioned state of the soul under the influence of illusion, and it illustrates the intense feeling of separation experienced by the Vrajavasis when Krishna left Vrindavan.

This is the meaning of the word Krishna. He is supremely attractive. So he is attractive for everyone. But then somebody may say, I don't feel attracted to Krishna. That is explained by Prabhupada here as the conditioned soul. All of us are conditioned souls in the present condition, the present situation. We are all conditioned souls. Under the spell of the illusory energy, the illusory energy forgets the Lord. So because of the influence of illusion on all of us, we forget Krishna. That means we do not recognize Krishna. We do not remember Krishna. We do not know about Krishna like that. Otherwise, the living entity can never forget Krishna. If we are not under the influence of illusion, we cannot, we can never forget Krishna. So the feeling of such separation cannot be described but can simply be imagined by devotees only. What is the kind of separation a devotee feels? Another devotee can understand that. Non-devotees cannot understand. What is this? Because this is separation from the most beloved personality, the most attractive personality, the most loving personality, the most intimately related personality. So the separation is unbearable. It's unbearable. Prabhupada also describes, not only an example is given here, Krishna leaving Hastinapura for Dwaraka, but when Krishna left Vrindavan and came to Mathura, after it is described here, after the separation from Vrindavan of the great innocent rural covered boys, girls, ladies and others, they all felt shocked throughout their lives, the Vrajavasis. They felt the shock of separation and they experienced that throughout their lives because Krishna never went back. Though when leaving Vrindavan, Krishna told the Vrajavasis, I'll come back after few days. I got some work in Mathura. But He never returned. Krishna never returned to Vrindavan. So the Brajavasis, rest of their life they simply were crying and crying and crying and crying in separation from Krishna. This is their attachment to Krishna.

---
### Radharani, Kurukshetra meeting, devotee relationships, attachment to Krishna
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/XWi-tEQus3A?t=788
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees
**Key Quote:** "So deep and so intimate is the attachment for Krishna among pure devotees. So this we have to understand."
**Summary:** This segment discusses the unparalleled separation felt by Radharani, the meeting of the Vrajavasis and Krishna at Kurukshetra, and the unique relationships devotees have with the Lord, emphasizing the impossibility of separation for those who have directly contacted Krishna.

So that is described here. The separation of Radharani, the most beloved, covered girl, is beyond expression of all the devotees. The topmost devotee is Radharani. She felt the separation the most. So then it is also described once later on, much later. They met at Kurukshetra during a solar eclipse, the Brajavasis and Krishna coming from Dwaraka to Kurukshetra. Brajavasis coming from Vrindavan to Kurukshetra. And the feeling which was expressed by them is heart-rending. The feeling the Brajavasis expressed on seeing Krishna in Kurukshetra at the time of solar eclipse, that is very, very, very touching. There is of course a difference in the qualities of the transcendental devotees of the Lord. Not all are identical that way. They have varieties of relationships. But none of them who has ever contacted the Lord by direct communion or otherwise can leave him for a moment. That is never possible. Separation is never possible. And if at all they are separated, they feel that within the core of their heart, I cannot tolerate being away from Krishna even for a single moment. A fraction of a second I cannot tolerate. So deep and so intimate is the attachment for Krishna among pure devotees. So this we have to understand. I will stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

### introduction, Krishna's arrival, Dwaraka

**Summary:** This segment introduces the eleventh chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam, focusing on Lord Krishna's entrance into Dwaraka as a continuation of the previous chapter.

Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare. Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya Naram Chai Vanarottamam Devim Saraswatim Vyasam Tato Jayam Udhirayet. In this session, we will be discussing the first verse of the eleventh chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. This is the beginning of the new chapter. This new chapter is entitled Lord Krishna's Entrance into Dwaraka. It is a natural sequence of the previous chapter. The previous chapter described the departure of Lord Krishna for Dwaraka. Now this chapter is going to describe Lord Krishna's arrival in Dwaraka.

---
### Suta Goswami, Dwaraka, Krishna consciousness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Zfojsp8OoLQ?t=95
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Simply because of the presence of Krishna, everything will be in plenty."
**Summary:** This segment explains the verse about Krishna reaching Dwaraka and sounding his conch shell, pacifying the dejection of the inhabitants, highlighting Dwaraka's prosperity due to Krishna's presence and the importance of Krishna consciousness.

Sūtavāca ānartāṁsa upavrajya svṛddhāṁ janapadāṁ svakāṁ dadmau dharavaram teṣāṁ viṣādam śamayan niva. The meaning of this verse is Sūta Gosvāmī said, Sūta Gosvāmī speaking to the sages of Naimishāraṇya. He is describing these events leading to Parīkṣit Mahārāja meeting Śukadeva Gosvāmī. So Sūta Gosvāmī said, upon reaching the border of his most prosperous metropolis known as the country of the ānartas or Dwaraka, the Lord sounded his auspicious conch shell heralding his arrival and apparently pacifying the dejection of the inhabitants. So the description is now being given. Krishna has reached Dwaraka. So he sounded his conch shell to announce, herald means to announce his arrival, and that sound of his conch shell which announced the Lord's arrival was immediately able to pacify the dejection of the inhabitants. Dejection means the low spirits in which they were situated because of the absence of Krishna. Feeling separation from Krishna, they were in low spirits. Suddenly when they heard the conch shell, what happened? That's going to be described very nicely in the next few verses. So they are obviously very, very happy to hear that sound of the Lord's conch shell. Oh, the Lord has come back. And here the description of Dwaraka is the most prosperous metropolis. Simply because of the presence of Krishna, everything will be in plenty, because Krishna is the source of everything. So even in the material world, material nature will supply, as was explained in the previous chapter, material nature will supply everything we require in plenty if we simply become Krishna conscious, if we simply become devotees of Krishna. So that is also being described here, the most prosperous metropolis known as Dwaraka, called as Dwaraka.

---
### separation, eternal associates, melancholia
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Zfojsp8OoLQ?t=269
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "When the lord descends on the earth his eternal associates also come with him."
**Summary:** This segment delves into Srila Prabhupada's explanation of the inhabitants of Dwaraka as eternal associates of Krishna, their melancholia due to separation from Him, and their eagerness for His arrival after the battle of Kurukshetra.

So Srila Prabhupada explains in the purport, the beloved Lord was away from his own prosperous metropolis of Dwaraka for a considerably long period because of the battle of Kurukshetra. Krishna had gone from Dwaraka to Hastinapura, and he wanted to enthrone Yudhishthira as the emperor of the world. Krishna had come for reducing the burden of the earth which was overburdened by demoniac forces, posing as Kshatriyas but actually they were demons. So the last installment of wiping out these demoniac forces was done by Krishna through the battle of Kurukshetra, in which Krishna engaged his devotees, the Pandavas, in killing all these demons. But because of Krishna's presence on the battlefield they were all liberated. And Srila Prabhupada explains, the inhabitants because of Krishna being away from Dwaraka, they were overcome with melancholia due to the separation. Melancholia means sadness. They were overcome with sadness because Krishna was away. So Prabhupada explains what is the relationship between the inhabitants of Dwaraka and Krishna. When the lord descends on the earth his eternal associates also come with him. So these inhabitants of Dwaraka are not ordinary people. They are eternal associates of Krishna, just as the entourage of a king accompanies him. Entourage means those who surround an important personality wherever he goes. He does not go alone, the king. He has his ministers, he has his secretaries, he has his bodyguards, he has his servants. He has all those people surrounding him. So like that the lord also has got his eternal associates, always appearing with him whenever he appears in this world. Such associates of the lord are eternally liberated souls, and they cannot bear the separation of the lord even for a moment because of intense affection for the lord. The associates are very very deeply attached to Krishna. So therefore, when the attachment is very very strong, then the separation also they cannot bear even for one moment. Thus the inhabitants of the city of Dwaraka were in a mood of dejection, in a sad mood, low spirits, and expected the arrival of the lord at any moment. They were alert for receiving news of the lord's arrival. So the heralding sound of the auspicious conch shell was very encouraging.

---

And apparently the sound pacified their dejection. Apparently, because the devotees can never be separated from the lord, because simply by thinking of the lord, they have his association, simply by chanting his name, simply by remembering his pastimes. That's the absolute nature of the lord. But still, there is a certain special feeling in the presence of the lord. All are spiritual feelings, the feeling of separation, the feeling of the presence of the lord bringing in a certain joy, that the lord has come back. All these are varieties of spiritual emotions which are all accommodated in spiritual dealings between the devotees of the lord and the lord himself, in the spiritual world or even in the material world, in the lord incarnates. They were still more aspirant to see the lord amongst themselves, and all of them became alert to receive him in the befitting manner. These are signs of spontaneous love of godhead. So these are all special devotees of Krishna.

**Summary:** This segment begins the discussion of the second verse, describing the conch shell reddened by the touch of Krishna's lips and its spiritual significance, comparing it to a white swan playing in red lotus flowers.

The second verse. The meaning of the second verse is, the white and fat bubble of the conch shell, being gripped by the hand of lord krishna and sounded by him, he blew his conch shell very loudly, apparently, sorry, appeared to be reddened by the touch of his transcendental lips. The conch shell is white. Krishna is blowing by touching it to his lips, and his lips are completely red. So the conch shell appeared to become reddened. It seemed that a white swan was playing in the stems of red lotus flowers. A simile is given. So pravupara explains in the purport, the redness of the white conch shell due to the lip touch of the lord is a symbol of spiritual significance.

---
### matter and spirit, spiritual enlightenment, devotional service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Zfojsp8OoLQ?t=631
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Our life itself, so called material life, can become spiritualized simply by our engaging in devotional service of Krishna."
**Summary:** This segment elaborates on the spiritual significance of the reddened conch shell, explaining the difference between matter and spirit, how matter becomes spiritualized through contact with Krishna and devotional service, and the role of a bonafide spiritual master in this process.

The lord is all spirit, and matter is ignorance of this spiritual existence. See ways to understand what's the difference between matter and spirit. Spirit is the spiritual substance of which the spiritual world is made. Spirit is a living, spirit is full of life, living symptoms, and matter is dead matter. It is dull. But the actual understanding of matter as opposed to spirit is ignorance of spiritual existence. Factually there is nothing like matter in spiritual enlightenment. When somebody is spiritually enlightened, even in the material world, for them there is no matter, or in other words, they are able to engage even matter in Krishna's service, and matter becomes spiritualized when engaged in devotional service. Spiritual enlightenment takes place at once by the contact of the supreme lord Shri Krishna. By contact with Krishna, matter becomes spiritualized. The lord is present in every particle of his existence, and he can manifest his presence in anyone. Lord is everywhere. He is present, fully present everywhere. But for manifesting his presence, we have to make contact with the supreme lord. So how is this contact made? By ardent love and devotional service to the lord, one can actually establish that spiritual contact with Krishna. Everything in spiritual contact with the lord becomes spiritually reddened, like the conch shell in the grip of the lord. And the paramahamsa or the supremely intelligent person plays the part of the ducking swan in the water of spiritual bliss. The pure devotees who are paramahamsas, who are supremely intelligent persons, they always are in touch with Krishna through Krishna consciousness, and they are playing the part of the ducking swan in the water of spiritual bliss. That means they are absorbed in thoughts of Krishna, and in that way they are always in spiritual bliss. They are experiencing eternal bliss in contact with Krishna. So this is the description of the spiritual significance of the conch shell in the lord's hand being reddened, the white conch shell being reddened, spiritualizing matter by contact with Krishna or by engaging matter in Krishna's service. Our life itself, so called material life, can become spiritualized simply by our engaging in devotional service of Krishna always. And this has to be learned from the bonafide spiritual master, Srila Prabhupada, who explains how we can spiritualize our life in these pages of Srimad Bhagavatam. I'll stop here, but this is not here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

### separation, devotion, divine glance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/5jXUlaUC-oM?t=57
**Verse References:** SB 1.11.10
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "How can we exist without your presence?"
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the verse from Srimad Bhagavatam (1.11.10) and provides a translation, focusing on the citizens' feelings of separation from Krishna and his compassionate response.

In this session we will be discussing from the 10th verse of the 11th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. Katham Vayam Natha Chiro Shite Tvai Prasanna Drishtya Khila Tapa Shoshanam Jivemate Sunda Rahasa Shobhitam Apashyamana Vadanam Manoharam Iti Chodiritha Vachachah Prajanam Bhaktavatsam Bhaktavatsalah Shunvaano Nugraham Drishtya Vitanvan Pravishat Puram. The meaning of this verse is O Master, if you live abroad all the time, then we cannot look at your attractive face whose smiles vanquish all our sufferings. How can we exist without your presence? Upon hearing their speeches, the Lord who is very kind to the citizens and the devotees entered the city of Dwaraka and acknowledged all their greetings by casting his transcendental glance over them.

---
### attraction, beauty, transcendence
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/5jXUlaUC-oM?t=132
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "All other material beauty will completely become insignificant."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the citizens' attraction to Krishna's beautiful face and how even a glimpse of it can diminish the allure of material beauty.

Krishna has reached Dwaraka city and the citizens have so nicely expressed their feelings to Krishna that Krishna acknowledged their greetings by casting his transcendental glance over them. So here we can particularly notice that those who are pure devotees of Krishna, they are always attracted by Krishna's most beautiful and attractive face, that they cannot take their eyes off Krishna's face. So even though we have experience of beauty in this material world, this material beauty cannot be compared with Krishna's supreme beauty. So if at all we are able to get a glimpse of Krishna's beautiful face, even for a brief moment, then we get completely attracted to Krishna and consequently all other material beauty will completely become insignificant. We will never, never again go after material beauty. So how do we get attracted to Krishna's beautiful form by just getting a glimpse of Krishna's beautiful face? That is what is being described here.

---
### suffering, devotion, intimacy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/5jXUlaUC-oM?t=248
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The relationship between Krishna and devotees is of varieties, but whatever be the variety of relationship, all relationships with Krishna are very, very intimate."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that the devotees' sufferings disappear with a glimpse of Krishna's face and emphasizes the intimate and varied relationships between Krishna and his devotees.

When we cannot see your attractive face, then we are missing your beautiful smile, which vanquishes all our sufferings. Of course, devotees don't suffer, but this is just to say that even if there were any suffering, it would simply disappear if the devotees just have a glimpse of Krishna's beautiful attractive face. So Krishna is very kind to his devotees and devotees are very much attached to Krishna. So the relationship is very, very, very, very intimate. It doesn't matter what is the variety of the relationship because the relationship between Krishna and devotees is of varieties, but whatever be the variety of relationship, all relationships with Krishna are very, very intimate.

### reciprocity, spiritual world, knowledge
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/5jXUlaUC-oM?t=338

**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Don't consider Krishna to be like another person of this material world; never make that mistake."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses Krishna's reciprocation with devotees in the spiritual world and cautions against misunderstanding Krishna as an ordinary person.

Therefore, any pure devotee of Krishna situated in any variety of relationship is always very, very, very much attached to Krishna, and Krishna equally reciprocates with every kind of devotee. That is a transcendental variegatedness in the spiritual world. There are countless devotees, all of them in the spiritual world, Krishna's personal, are pure devotees. They have very intimate relationship with Krishna. They are completely absorbed in thought of Krishna. They are only engaged in serving Krishna, and Krishna personally reciprocates with each and every devotee. This we have to always remember. Don't consider Krishna to be like another person of this material world; never make that mistake. So we have to carefully read, hear, understand the truth, tattvataha. You have to understand who is Krishna.

### authority, Prabhupada, Krishna's pastimes
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/5jXUlaUC-oM?t=442
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Prabhupad is the pure devotee who perfectly describes Krishna."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the importance of hearing about Krishna from authorized sources, particularly Srila Prabhupada, and begins narrating Krishna's pastimes in Mathura and Vrindavan.

Hear about Krishna from unauthorized sources, from people who do not factually know Krishna. Don't hear from such people who do not factually know Krishna; they will be misleading you. So hear from pure devotees, and for us, Prabhupad is the pure devotee who perfectly describes Krishna, and he describes Krishna from the most authoritative source, the Srimad Bhagavatam. So in the purport to this verse, Srila Prabhupad explains Krishna's attraction is so powerful that once being attracted by him, one cannot tolerate separation from him. The example is there in the 10th canto of the Bhagavatam. Krishna, when he appeared 5000 years back, he was born in Mathura, and because Devaki and Vasudeva were anxious to protect Krishna, so Krishna directed Vasudeva to transfer him and hide him in Vrindavan. So Krishna in his leela was hiding in Vrindavan for about 12 years, but actually Krishna's leela is that his childhood he will spend in Vrindavan in the company of the Brajavasis who are his intimate devotees. Then Krishna left Vrindavan because he wanted to rescue his parents Vasudeva and Devaki who were being harassed by Kamsa.

---
### devotion, separation, Krishna's pastimes
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/5jXUlaUC-oM?t=576
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Once one is attracted by Krishna one cannot tolerate separation from him."
**Summary:** The speaker continues the story of Krishna, describing Jarasandha's repeated attacks on Mathura and the intense feelings of separation experienced by the residents of Vrindavan when Krishna did not return.

Krishna and Balaram went from Vrindavan to Mathura in order to free his parents and Kamsa was killed along with his associates. But then Jarasandha became very envious of Krishna because Jarasandha's daughters were married to Kamsa. So when Kamsa was killed, Jarasandha's daughters became widows. Jarasandha became angry at Krishna that Krishna killed Kamsa. Jarasandha never considered why Kamsa was killed. He never thought about that. He never found fault with Kamsa. This is the way of the demoniac nature, Asuri Swabhava. So Jarasandha decided to attack Mathura and kill Krishna. Of course, demons are foolish, so foolish that they don't realize Krishna can never even be touched or approached by demons or materialistic persons, what to speak of killing Krishna. Anyway, Jarasandha decided to attack Mathura. He went with one division of army, and Krishna in order to not disturb or involve the residents of Mathura in the fight against Jarasandha, Krishna and Balarama with few soldiers, they faced the army, huge army of Jarasandha outside Mathura, and Krishna and Balarama completely destroyed the entire army which had accompanied Jarasandha but spared the life of Jarasandha, and Jarasandha attacked Mathura like this 17 times, and each time Jarasandha was defeated. But because Krishna became busy with all these things, the Brindavan, they were waiting for Krishna to return after finishing some business in Mathura, but Krishna never returned, so they were feeling the separation from Krishna very, very intensely that they could not forget Krishna even for one moment even though Krishna never returned to Brindavan. So that's why Prabhupada says once one is attracted by Krishna one cannot tolerate separation from him.

### eternality, relationship, separation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/5jXUlaUC-oM?t=746
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "We are eternally related with Krishna as the sun rays are eternally related with the sun disc."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the eternal relationship between Krishna and the living entities using the analogy of the sun and its rays, emphasizing that separation is artificial and temporary.

Why is this so? Prabhupada explains because we are eternally related with Krishna as the sun rays are eternally related with the sun disc. The sun rays are molecular parts of the solar radiation. Thus the sun rays and the sun cannot be separated. Can you have sun rays without sun planet? No. Can you have sun planet without sun planet emanating sun rays? That is also not possible. So the sun rays and the sun planet are always together, always connected. Now there is something called a separation by the cloud, but that separation by the cloud of ourselves and the sun is temporary and artificial, and as soon as the cloud is cleared, the sun rays again display the natural effulgence in the presence of the sun. Similarly, the living entities who are molecular parts of the solar ah radiation, sorry, the living entities who are molecular parts of the whole spirit Krishna are separated from the Lord by the artificial energy. So the sun rays are separated from the sun rays. So the sun rays are separated from the sun rays. So the sun rays are separated from the sun rays. So the sun rays are separated from the sun rays. So the sun rays are separated from the sun rays. So the sun rays are separated from the sun rays. So the sun rays are separated from the sun rays. So.

### Dwarka, protection, Vrishni dynasty
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/PSgrIcB1Hdg?t=53
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So was Dwarka protected by the descendants of Vrishni, Bhoja, Madhu, Dasharha, Arha, Kukkura, Andhaka, etc., who were as strong as Lord Krishna."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the verses to be discussed, focusing on the description of Dwarka's protection by the Vrishni dynasty, highlighting their strength and various clans.

In this session we will be discussing from the 11th and the 12th verses of the 11th chapter of the first canto of Shri Madbhagavatam. The 11th verse first, Madhu Bhoja Dasharhaarha Kukkurandhaka Vrishnibhihi Atma Tulya Balair Guptam Nagair Bhogavateem Iva. The meaning of this verse is, as Bhagavati, the capital of Nagaloka, is protected from the Nagas, so was Dwarka protected by the descendants of Vrishni, Bhoja, Madhu, Dasharha, Arha, Kukkura, Andhaka, etc., who were as strong as Lord Krishna. So the description is given that the residents of Dwarka were very nicely protected from the vrishnis. The reason for this is because the brothers of all the 3 great Mountains, Pushkara and Vrishna were also very well protected. They had different clans. They were divided by the Vrishnis. There were several clans of the Vrishni dynasty. They are named as Bhoja Dynasty, Madhu Dynasty, the Dasharha Dynasty, Arha Dynasty, Kukkura Dynasty, Andhaka Dynasty. So the members of these dynasties were known as the Vrishnis. They had three clan of Vrishni. they were protecting Dwarka because they were as strong as Lord Krishna.

---
### Krishna's power, Naga-loka, Dwarka
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/PSgrIcB1Hdg?t=149
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The Lord is unlimitedly powerful but when he descends into this world he manifests a certain strength which is required for the purpose of his pastimes."
**Summary:** The speaker explains Prabhupada's purport on the verse, comparing Dwarka's protection to that of Nagaloka and clarifying that the Vrishnis were as powerful as Krishna manifested his strength on Earth during his Dwarka pastimes.

Now Prabhupad clarifies in the purport what is the meaning of this as strong as Lord Krishna. So Srila Prabhupada first of all says the comparison is made to Nagloka. So in the purport Srila Prabhupada says the Nagloka planet is situated below the earth planet and it is understood that the sun rays are hampered there. The darkness of the planet is however removed by the flashes of the jewels set on the head of the Nagas the celestial serpents and it is said that there are beautiful gardens, rivulets etc. for the enjoyment of the Nagas. It is understood here also that that the place is well protected by the inhabitants called the Nagas so also the city of Dwarka was well protected by the descendants of Vrishni who were as powerful as the Lord insofar as he manifested his strength upon this earth so we'd understand this point the Lord is unlimitedly powerful but when he descends into this world he manifests a certain strength which is required for the purpose of his pastimes so as much strength he manifested in Dwarka for the sake of his Leela as the king of Dwarka to that extent all the Vrishnis were powerful enough to give sufficient protection to the residents of Dwarka.

---
### Dwarka, natural opulence, civilization
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/PSgrIcB1Hdg?t=271
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Perfection of human civilization is made possible by utilizing the gifts of nature in their own way."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses the opulence of Dwarka as described in the twelfth verse, emphasizing the natural beauty and contrasting it with modern civilization's reliance on mills and factories.

In the twelfth verse it is explained the twelfth verse Sarvarthu Sarva Vibhava Punya Vriksha Latashramaihi Udhyana Upavana Udhyana Upavana Udhyana Upavana Udhyana Upavana Udhyana Upavana Udhyana Upavana Aaramair Vrita Padmakara Shriyam the meaning of this verse is the city of Dwarka puri was filled with opulences of all seasons there were hermitages orchards flower gardens parks and reservoirs of water breeding lotus flowers all over. So, Srila Prabhupada explains this beauty of the city of Dwaraka. Perfection of human civilization is made possible by utilizing the gifts of nature in their own way. As we find here with in the description of its opulence, Dwaraka was surrounded by flower gardens and fruit orchards along with reservoirs of water and growing lotuses. There is no mention of mills and factories supported by slaughterhouses which are the necessary paraphernalia of the modern metropolis. The propensity to utilize nature is the same as the propensity to utilize nature. The propensity to utilize nature is the same as the propensity to utilize nature. Nature's own gifts is still there even in the heart of modern civilized man.

### ugra karma, demoniac nature, spiritual instincts
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/PSgrIcB1Hdg?t=487
**Verse References:** BG 16
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Whereas in the case of Dwarka Dham, the arrangement is such that the atmosphere helps to develop the potent spiritual instincts of human beings and that atmosphere gives them a chance to go back to Godhead."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts the detrimental effects of industrial labor (ugra karma) on human instincts with the spiritually conducive atmosphere of Dwarka, which fosters spiritual development and the potential for returning to Godhead, referencing the Bhagavad Gita's description of divine and demoniac natures.

So the labor class, they are engaged in this production and they are not given proper facilities for residing in this city. And as a result of their engaging in ugra karma, pungent activities, their finer instincts as humans deteriorate. Whereas in the case of Dwarka Dham, the arrangement is such that the atmosphere helps to develop the potent spiritual instincts of human beings and that atmosphere gives them a chance to go back to Godhead. So Srila Prabhupada further explains, development of factories and mills is called ugra karma or pungent activities. This ugra karma is mentioned in the Bhagavad Gita also. In the description of divine and demoniac nature in 16th chapter of the Bhagavad Gita, the qualities of divine natured people and the qualities of demoniac natured people are elaborately described in that 16th chapter of the Bhagavad Gita. So one of the qualities described of demoniac natured people is that they engage in ugra karma, pungent activities. And such activities deteriorate the finer sentiments of the human being and society to form a dungeon of demons. The demoniac nature flourishes when there is such ugra karma performed by the laborers.

### pious trees, impious trees, spiritual understanding
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/PSgrIcB1Hdg?t=620
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Human energy, Srila Prabhupada explains, should be properly utilized in developing the finer senses for spiritual understanding in which lies the solution of life."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses the distinction between pious and impious trees, criticizing the modern tendency to plant only impious trees. He emphasizes the importance of utilizing human energy to develop finer senses for spiritual understanding, which leads to the solution of life's problems.

We find herein the mention of pious trees which produce seasonal flowers and fruits. The impious trees are useless jungles only and they can only be used to supply fuels. So among trees also, pious trees and impious trees. Pious trees yield something useful like fruits, flowers or medicinal herbs like that or fragrant flowers, very sweet fruits. Whereas these impious trees, they don't give any such fruits, flowers or useful leaves. They only at best can be used for some burning the wood when the wood is dry after the tree has been cut down. In the modern civilization, such impious trees are planted on the sides of trees. You can see all over in the city. None of the roads where the government arranges to plant trees, they have any pious trees. No flowers, no fruits, only impious trees are planted. Human energy, Srila Prabhupada explains, should be properly utilized in developing the finer senses for spiritual understanding in which lies the solution of life. As human beings, we have developed intelligence. So if the proper atmosphere is created, then we can develop the finer senses. And with such a development, it leads to spiritual understanding. Spiritual understanding gives us the clue for the ultimate solution to all problems.

---
### nature's gifts, spiritual development, ideal civilization
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/PSgrIcB1Hdg?t=757
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Krishna not only teaches by instructing us but also manifesting the ideal atmosphere, ideal development which he demonstrated in Dwarka."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes by emphasizing the importance of nature's gifts in developing finer tissues for spiritual understanding, contrasting this with the demoniac propensities fostered by modern industrial environments. He presents Dwarka as the ideal of human civilization, demonstrated by Krishna himself.

Otherwise, we are simply busy making a temporary patchwork solution to various problems in this material world. So fruits, flowers, beautiful gardens, parks and reservoirs of water with ducks and swans playing in the midst of lotus flowers and cows giving sufficient milk and butter are essential for developing the finer tissues of the human body. There is a whole plan by the Supreme Lord that this nature's gifts can be utilized for developing the finer tissues in the brain to understand spiritual subject matter and thereby prepare ourselves to go back to Godhead and thereby make a complete solution, perfect solution for all problems. As against this, the dungeon of mines, factories and workshops develop demoniac propensities in the working class. The vested interests flourish at the cost of the working class and consequently there are severe clashes between them in so many ways. You see the problem with the modern civilization. The labor class are not properly given facilities and they develop this demoniac tendency and they always keep fighting. The description of Dwarka Dham is the ideal of human civilization. So Krishna not only teaches by instructing us but also manifesting the ideal atmosphere, ideal development which he demonstrated in Dwarka. I will stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

### description, Dwarka, decoration
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/AeQ5CDOgsHM?t=54
**Verse References:** SB 1.11.13
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The whole city is prepared to welcome the Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces verses 13 of the 11th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam, describing the decorations and preparations in Dwarka to welcome the Lord, emphasizing the use of natural items like plantain trees and mango leaves.

In this session we will be discussing verses 13 and 14 of the 11th chapter of the first canto of Shri Madbhagavatam. The 13th verse: Gopura Dvaram Argeshu Krita Kautuka Toranam Chitradhwaja Pataka Grair Antapratihata Tapam. The meaning of this verse is the city gateway, the household doors, and festooned arches along the roads were all nicely decorated with festive signs like plantain trees and mango leaves, all to welcome the Lord. Flags, garlands, and painted signs and slogans all combined to shade the sunshine. The whole city is prepared to welcome the Lord, and how did they prepare? They decorated the entire city, the gateway, the household doors, the arches along the road. Everything was decorated very beautifully with the natural nature's gift to the Lord and to the people of the world. So, Srila Prabhupada explains in the purport, signs of decoration in special festivals were also collected from the gifts of nature such as the plantain trees, the mango trees, fruits and flowers.

### nature, decoration, sustainability
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/AeQ5CDOgsHM?t=149
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Whereas all these natural products, they are, as is said, biodegradable."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on Srila Prabhupada's explanation, highlighting the Vedic culture's preference for natural decorations over artificial ones, emphasizing the environmental benefits of biodegradable materials compared to modern plastics.

Mango trees, coconut palms, and plantain trees are still accepted as auspicious signs. Whenever there is a need in the Vedic culture for decoration, they utilize such natural things. They don't manufacture artificially some decorative items. Such artificial decorative items are later on very difficult to dispose of, just like nowadays so much of hue and cry about plastic. The plastic waste is very difficult to dispose of, whereas all these natural products, they are, as is said, biodegradable. So, there is no difficulty in disposing them off, and they are easily available without much endeavor on the part of the people to purchase them. So, there is no difficulty in disposing them off. So, there is no difficulty in disposing of these natural products.

---
### Hanuman, Garuda, devotee worship
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/AeQ5CDOgsHM?t=263
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna says in Bhagavatam madbhakta puja abhyadika worship of my devotee is greater than worship of me."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses the significance of Hanuman and Garuda as great servitors of the Lord, explaining that paying respects to them pleases the Lord, referencing Krishna's statement in the Bhagavatam that worship of His devotee is greater than worship of Himself.

The entrance of the temple, the picture of Hanuman and Garuda are always painted at the entrance. Even the deity of Hanuman Garuda are there in the beginning of the temple, so they are the great servitors of the Lord. Both render valuable service to the Supreme Lord. For devotees such paintings and decorations are still adored, and the servitor of the master is paid more respects for the satisfaction of the Lord. Krishna says in Bhagavatam, 'madbhakta puja abhyadika,' worship of my devotee is greater than worship of me. Krishna says that in Bhagavatam eleventh canto while instructing Uddhava. So Hanuman and Garuda are great servants of the Supreme Lord, so when they are paid respects by the devotees, the Lord is thoroughly pleased by such respects paid to his dear servants. So the flags were decorated with paintings of Garuda or Hanuman.

### Garuda, Hanuman, devotional service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/AeQ5CDOgsHM?t=352
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Hanuman is extraordinarily powerful but he uses all that power only for service of Lord Rama not for any other purpose."
**Summary:** The speaker narrates pastimes of the Lord with Garuda and Hanuman, emphasizing their selfless service and contrasting it with the self-serving tendencies of people in the material world who misuse their power for personal gratification.

In the Bhagavatam there is a description of different pastimes of the Lord with Garuda participating in those pastimes when Krishna went with Satyabhama to the Indra loka to get one parijata tree. That time he went on the back of Garuda, and when Krishna had to fight against Banasura, then during one month he similar to the time pressure on Lord Shiva to get the tree, then he went to he was carried to that capital of Banasura by Garuda, and Garuda also was assisting the Lord in fighting against Banasura. So in this way even Hanuman, Hanuman serves the Supreme Lord in his form as Ramachandra, and Ramayana the elaborate description is there how Hanuman served Lord Rama in so many ways. Hanuman is extraordinarily powerful but he uses all that power only for service of Lord Rama not for any other purpose as opposed to that people in this world due to some pious credits if they have got some power they want to utilize that for their own good, for lauding over others or for their own sense gratification. But the devotees never utilize anything they may have or they may possess or they may acquire for their own sense gratification. They never use it for lauding over others because the Supreme Lord is the Lord of everyone. He is the Lord and everything should be utilized for His service.

Because everything belongs to him, Isha vastyam idam sarvam yatkincha jagattyaham jagat. So therefore these two devotees servitors of the Supreme Lord are always engaged in the service of the Lord and they are very powerful personalities. This is the devotion of Garuda great personalities they have extraordinary power abilities but everything is used simply for devotional service of the Supreme Lord, that's all, that's the ideal.

### Dwarka, cleanliness, opulence
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/AeQ5CDOgsHM?t=541
**Verse References:** SB 1.11.14
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "See the opulence of Dwarka is to clean all the roads thoroughly with water and then sprinkle scented water nice fragrance to welcome the Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces and explains verse 14, describing the cleanliness and opulence of Dwarka, where roads were cleansed and scented water was sprinkled, and fruits and flowers were strewn everywhere to welcome the Lord.

They teach us the ideal way of utilizing whatever we may possess. The 14th verse: samarjit mahamarga rathya apanaka chatwaram siktaam gandha jalair uptaam phala pushpakshatam kuraihi. The meaning of this verse is the highways, subways, lanes, markets, and public meeting places were all thoroughly cleansed and then moistened with scented water. See the opulence of Dwarka is to clean all the roads thoroughly with water and then sprinkle scented water nice fragrance to welcome the Lord. Fruits, flowers, and unbroken seeds were strewn everywhere and this is also the opulence of the inhabitants of Dwaraka. So many fruits, so much flowers are there because the flower gardens are giving plenty of flowers and fruit orchards are supplying plenty of fruits. So they can afford to strew them everywhere in order to welcome the Lord on the streets, in the junction places, road junctions and in the arches. So Srila Prabhupada explains, scented waters prepared by distilling flowers like rose and khevra were requisitioned to wet the roads, streets and lanes of Dwaraka Dham. Again, scent is not artificially prepared by some chemicals but rather by distilling natural flowers which have got nice fragrance. Such places along with the market place and public meeting places were thoroughly cleansed.

### cleanliness, goodness, Vedic civilization
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/AeQ5CDOgsHM?t=679
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Cleanliness is very prominent in Vedic civilization."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the importance of cleanliness in Vedic civilization, linking it to the mode of goodness and the development of pious activities, contrasting it with the concrete jungles of modern cities.

So clean. Cleanliness is very prominent in Vedic civilization. That means they are situated in the goodness, mode of goodness, if not in the transcendental spiritual platform. Of course Dwaraka Nivasis are on the transcendental platform, but even those who are not on the transcendental platform, they should at least be in the mode of goodness. That helps to develop the pious activities leading to ultimate development of bhakti. From the above description it appears that the city of Dwaraka Dham was considerably big, containing many highways, streets and public meeting places with parks, gardens and reservoirs of water, all very nicely decorated with flowers and fruits. Nowadays we don't find so many parks or gardens in cities especially, because people have become very very greedy for constructing so many buildings. The cities are more like concrete jungles, especially the most developed cities even in our country. Formerly there used to be more gardens and parks and open spaces.

### Krishna consciousness, nature, ideal city
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/AeQ5CDOgsHM?t=773
**Verse References:** BG 3.14
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "When Krishna Consciousness is there, there is plenty of supply of everything by material nature."
**Summary:** The speaker connects the opulence of Dwarka to Krishna consciousness, explaining that material nature provides abundantly when people are God-conscious, and that Dwarka, with its Krishna-devoted residents, serves as an ideal for human civilization.

But now you don't find that. To welcome the Lord such flowers and fruits with unbroken seeds of grain were strewn over the public places. This is the opulence of the residence of Dwaraka. Of course when Krishna Consciousness is there, there is plenty of supply of everything by material nature, because material nature is directed by the Supreme Lord. Maya Dhyakshena Prakriti Hisu Yatesu Characharam. So the Lord will direct nature to supply everything when people are God conscious, Krishna conscious. When people become sinful then material nature withholds the production of grains and fruits and flowers, and grains won't be sufficient like that. Unbroken seeds of grain or fruits in the seedling stage were considered auspicious, and they are still so used by the Hindus in general on festival days. So this is the culture, that decorations are done using natural supplies by nature, natural gifts like fruits, flowers, unbroken seeds, water pots with yogurt and grains like that. So Dwaraka, Dwaraka where Krishna resided with all the Vrishnis, his relatives and friends and his family members was the ideal city, and everyone there was devoted to Krishna, were devotees of Krishna. That is the ideal for human civilization.

### Festivity, Reception of the Lord, Decoration
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/EgisVcHdjKA?t=55
**Verse References:** SB 1.11.15
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "In each and every door of the residential houses, auspicious things like curd, unbroken fruits, sugar cane and full water pots with articles for worship, incense and candles were all displayed."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the verse from Srimad Bhagavatam describing the festive atmosphere in Dwaraka upon Krishna's arrival, highlighting the decorations and auspicious items displayed at every house.

In this session we will be discussing from the 15th verse of the 11th chapter of the 1st canto of Shreemad Bhagavata. Dwarid Dwarigrahanamcha Dadhi Akshata Palekshubhihi Alankratam Purnakumbhair Balibhir Dhupadipakaihi. Further description of the decorations. Translation. Translation of this verse. In each and every door of the residential houses, auspicious things like curd, unbroken fruits, sugar cane and full water pots with articles for worship, incense and candles were all displayed. So, it's a festive atmosphere just because Krishna has arrived in Dwaraka. It's a festival. Receiving the Lord. And everyone is alert to receive the Lord appropriately. Every house, all the doors of every residential house, auspicious things are used to decorate the door, the entrance, unbroken fruits, pots filled with sugar. Every house, all the doors of every residential house, auspicious things are used to decorate the door, the entrance, unbroken fruits, pots filled with sugar cane and incense candles for worship all to receive the lord appropriately.

### Worship, Vedic Culture, Prasadam Distribution
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/EgisVcHdjKA?t=167
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Without distribution of food, no function is complete and that is the way of Vedic tradition."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the importance of worshiping the Lord and distributing prasadam (sanctified food) as an integral part of Vedic culture, contrasting it with modern receptions that lack these essential elements.

So Srila Prabhupada says a very important point here in the purport: the process of reception according to Vedic rites is not at all dry. The reception was made not simply by decorating the roads and streets as above mentioned, but by worshiping the Lord with requisite ingredients like incense, lamps, flowers, sweets, fruits, and other palatable eatables according to one's capacity. You see, so worship of the Lord is the center point in all these decorations, all the arrangements, worship of the Lord. So the Vishnu Purana says the entire varnashrama dharma aims at worship of Vishnu by all the members of this society irrespective of which classification they belong. The worship of the Lord Vishnu is the goal of practicing varnashrama dharma and only by worship of Vishnu, the Supreme Lord, can we perfect our life, can we prepare ourselves to get ultimate liberation and go back home, back to Godhead. So that was clearly demonstrated by the supreme lord Vishnu, these devotees of Dwaraka, how they received the Lord and how they worshipped him. So Srila Prabhupada further says all were offered to the Lord, everything that they were using for decorating and the items they had kept ready to receive the Lord, they were used to not only decorate but also to offer to the Lord like incense, lamps, fruits and edibles, different eatables, and the remnants of foodstuff are distributed amongst the gathering citizens. So it was not like a dry reception of these modern days. Modern days they have reception for some important personalities or leaders, but there is no distribution of foodstuff, there's no worship of the Lord. That is missing, that is lacking. Each and every house was ready to receive the Lord in a similar way and thus each and every house on the roads and streets distributed such remnants of food to the citizens and therefore the festival was successful. Without distribution of food, no function is complete and that is the way of Vedic tradition. Vedic culture, this is the Vedic culture, that Vedic culture means worship of the Lord and the remnants are distributed to the people who participate in this festival. So it's not a dry reception or dry festival, no. So Prabhupada says no function is complete in the Vedic culture without distribution of food.

---
### Karma, Sin, Offering Food
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/EgisVcHdjKA?t=382
**Verse References:** BG 3.13
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "If they eat food which is not offered to Krishna, then they are eating sin."
**Summary:** The speaker quotes Bhagavad Gita to explain that eating food not offered to Krishna results in incurring sin, emphasizing the importance of offering food as a sacrifice to purify oneself from past sinful reactions.

Regarding distribution of food, in the Bhagavad Gita, Krishna explains in the third chapter, thirteenth verse, yajna shishta asinaha santaha mucchante sarva-kilbhishaihi bunjate tetu agham paapa ye pachanti atmakaranat. Very important verse, where Krishna describes in this third chapter of the Bhagavad Gita, that devotees of the Lord are released from all kinds of sins, because they eat food which is offered first for sacrifice. Yajna is sacrifice, yajna shishta is remnants of food offered for yajna. Others who prepare food for personal sense enjoyment, verily eat only sin. Krishna says this in the Bhagavad Gita. Bunjate means they eat. Who? Agham paapa, huge quantities of sin. Who are they? Ye pachanti, those who prepare food, atmakaranat, for their own sense enjoyment. There is no offering made to the Supreme Lord. So, nowadays you can see, most people, they simply either purchase something, or they buy something, or they buy something, some ready-made food, and eat it, or they prepare something at home that is also simply prepared and eaten. They do not know that they have to worship the Lord and offer him foodstuff, and only accept remnants of food. So, what is wrong if simply somebody eats without offering to the Lord? It is explained in this verse of the Bhagavad Gita, if they eat food which is not offered to Krishna, then they are eating sin. Our taking birth itself in this material world is because of our past sinful activities. So we already are carrying a load of sinful reactions from our past life or past lives. Now adding to that more sinful reactions by not offering food and simply consuming food, not offering food is only going to increase our suffering in this world.

---
### Mode of Goodness, Food Offering, Types of Food
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/EgisVcHdjKA?t=530
**Verse References:** BG 17.8
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna accepts only food which is in the mode of goodness."
**Summary:** The speaker describes the types of food that should be offered to Krishna, emphasizing food in the mode of goodness, and contrasting it with food in the modes of passion and ignorance.

Regarding the type of food that should be offered to Krishna, Krishna accepts only food which is in the mode of goodness. So what is food in the mode of goodness? It is described in the 17th chapter, 8th verse. āyuḥ sattva balārogyaḥ sukhaḥ prīti vyavardhanāḥ rasyaḥ snigdhāḥ sthirāḥ hṛdyāḥ āhārāḥ sātvikaḥ priyāḥ. That which is in the mode of goodness, food is in the mode of goodness, increase the duration of life, āyuḥ. Purify one's existence, sattva. Bala, they give strength. ārogyaḥ, they promote good health. Sukhaḥ means they give happiness. And prīti vyavardhanāḥ and they give satisfaction. And further characteristics of such food, such nourishing foods are sweet, juicy, fattening and palatable. So unless food is palatable, unless food is of this characteristic, it is not in the mode of goodness. Typically, food in the mode of passion is very, very pungent, extremely spicy. Excess of spice is used or excess of salt or excess of sourness, chili, like that. And food in the mode of ignorance is completely decomposing and stale. And that which is untouchable, in the Vedic language, untouchable is that which is stool and flesh, animal flesh, animal eggs, animal remains. So these are all untouchables.

---
### Sacrifice, Mode of Goodness, Distribution
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/EgisVcHdjKA?t=676
**Verse References:** BG 17.13
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Merely doing a sacrifice is not enough. The sacrifice has to be performed in the mode of goodness."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that sacrifice should be performed according to scriptural injunctions and in the mode of goodness, emphasizing the importance of distributing spiritual food and chanting hymns.

So when we are to offer food, we should remember, we should offer food only that is in the mode of goodness. And further, regarding sacrifice, Krishna says in the 17th chapter, 13th verse. The meaning of this verse is, that sacrifice performed in defiance of scriptural injunctions, in which no spiritual food is distributed, no hymns are chanted, and no remunerations are made to the priests, and which is faithless, that sacrifice is of the nature of ignorance. So merely doing a sacrifice is not enough. The sacrifice has to be performed in the mode of goodness. So the characteristic of Tamsik sacrifice, or sacrifice in the mode of ignorance is, food is not distributed. As opposed to this, what was described in this 15th verse, in the description of Dwaraka Dham, how they received Krishna, they worshipped Krishna, they offered nice foodstuffs to him, and the remnants were distributed among the people.

---
### Prasadam Distribution, Krishna Consciousness, Devotional Service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/EgisVcHdjKA?t=776
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Prasadam distribution is also important in Kali Yuga because people can purify their heart of sinful reactions simply by taking Prasadam, Krishna Prasadam."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes by highlighting the importance of prasadam distribution in Kali Yuga for purifying hearts, awakening devotion, and facilitating spiritual advancement, emphasizing the qualities of prasadam and its role in developing divine qualities.

Even when Prabhupada established his ISKCON temples, he always said, Prasadam distribution should be there. Prasadam distribution should be there. So it's the Vedic culture, the temple's food is offered to the deity, who is worshipped, Krishna or Vishnu deity, and the remnants are distributed among the temple visitors. This is the Vedic culture. Prasadam distribution. Prasadam distribution is also important in Kali Yuga because people can purify their heart of sinful reactions simply by taking Prasadam, Krishna Prasadam. And not only it purifies their heart of sinful reactions, but it also awakens their dormant devotion. Prasadam is completely satisfying to the tongue and even to the mind. Prasadam nourishes the body very nicely. So such Krishna Prasadam should be offered, nice preparation should be offered, palatable, sweet, juicy, nourishing. Foods in the mode of goodness should be offered to Krishna and the remnants should be distributed. Only then will people easily become Krishna conscious. They will progress on the path of bhakti and they will be able to actually make spiritual advancement, develop all good qualities that are to be found in devatas. Asyasthi bhakti bhagavati akinchana sarvair gunaistatra samasate suraha. All good qualities are to be found in those who engage in pure devotional service and develop all divine qualities. I will stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.11.16-17 Part 1 | 08-07-2022
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### Krishna's family, Devotion, Srimad Bhagavatam
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/dUk_QL3PWDQ?t=37
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "They were extremely happy to hear that Krishna is coming back to Dwaraka Dhamma, returning to Dwaraka Dhamma."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the verses to be discussed, focusing on the joy of Krishna's family upon hearing of his return to Dwaraka and their eagerness to receive him.

Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya Naram Chai Vanarottamam Deviim Sarasvatim Vyasam Tato Jayam Udi Rayet. In this session, we will be discussing the 16th and 17th verses of the 11th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. Nishamya Preshtham Aayantam Vasudevo Mahamanaha Akruras Chogras Enascha Ramascha Adbhuta Vikramaha Pradyumnas Charudeshnascha Sambho Jambavati Sutaha Praharsha Vego Chashita Shayana Asana Bhojanaha. The meaning of these two verses given together here: On hearing that the most dear Krishna was approaching Dwaraka Dhamma, magnanimous Vasudeva, Akrura, Ugrasena, Balaram, the superhumanly powerful, Pradyumna, Charudeshna, and Samba, the son of Jambavati, all extremely happy, abandoned resting, sitting, and dining. So here is a description of the most intimate family members of Krishna, Krishna's father Vasudeva. The most dear Krishna was approaching Dwaraka Dhamma, magnanimous Vasudeva, Akrura, Ugrasena, his grandfather, Balaram, his brother, Pradyumna, his son, Charudeshna, Samba, all sons of Krishna. They were extremely happy to hear that Krishna is coming back to Dwaraka Dhamma, returning to Dwaraka Dhamma. So they completely abandoned whatever they were doing, whether they were resting or sitting or dining. They just gave up and went to receive Krishna.

---
### Vasudeva, Krishna Leela, Family relationships
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/dUk_QL3PWDQ?t=179
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna appeared as, was born as, the eighth son of Vasudeva and Devaki."
**Summary:** The speaker begins to describe the personalities mentioned in the verses, starting with Vasudeva, explaining his lineage, his marriage to Devaki and other daughters of Devaka, and his relationship to Kunti and Subhadra.

So in the purport, Srila Prabhupada gives a brief description of each of these personalities. First of all, he describes the personality of Vasudeva. Now, all these personalities actually are participating in Krishna Leela in certain roles. But actually, in the spiritual world, their actual position may be different in some cases. So here, it's described Vasudeva was the son of King Shurasena. Shurasena was ruling, king of the district of Shurasena, and his son was Vasudeva. Krishna appeared as the son of Vasudeva and husband of Devaki. Vasudeva married Devaki, and Devaki was the mother of Krishna. Krishna appeared from the womb of Devaki. And of course, Vasudeva is described as the father of Lord Shri Krishna. He is also, Vasudeva is also the brother of Kunti. Kunti is the mother of the Pandavas. So, Kunti's brother is Vasudeva, and Vasudeva is also the father of Subhadra. Vasudeva and Devaki had eight sons and one daughter. That one daughter was Subhadra. Krishna appeared as, was born as, the eighth son of Vasudeva and Devaki. Balaram actually was the seventh son of Vasudeva and Devaki, but he was transferred to the womb of Rohini by Krishna's own arrangement for his lila, for his pastimes. Then further, Srila Prabhupada says, Subhadra was married with her cousin, Arjuna, because Subhadra and Arjuna are daughter and son of Vasudeva and Kunti, who are brother and sister. So, therefore, Subhadra and Arjuna are cousins. But Srila Prabhupada says, such marriage is permitted, and this system is still prevalent in some parts of India, even today. Vasudeva was appointed minister of Ugrasena, and later on, he married eight daughters of Ugrasena's brother, Devaka. Ugrasena's brother, Devaka, had eight daughters. Devaki was one of them. So, Vasudeva not only married Devaki, but he also married the other seven sisters of Devaki. So, all the eight daughters of Vasudeva were married to Devaki. So, all the eight daughters of Devaka, Ugrasena's brother, were married by Vasudeva.

---
### Kamsa, Krishna's birth, Divine Plan
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/dUk_QL3PWDQ?t=390
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "It's Krishna's pastime. His appearance, his activities are all divine."
**Summary:** The speaker continues describing Vasudeva's life, including his relationship with Kamsa, his voluntary imprisonment, the prophecy about Devaki's eighth son, and the miraculous circumstances surrounding Krishna's birth and transfer to Gokula.

Kamsa was his brother-in-law. That means Kamsa, as the son of Ugrasena, was the brother-in-law of Vasudeva. Vasudeva accepted voluntary imprisonment by Kamsa on mutual agreement. Then, to deliver the eighth son of Devaki, this is described in the tenth canto of the Bhagavatam, that before Krishna's appearance, there was a prophecy that the eighth son of Devaki would be the cause of death of Kamsa. So, Kamsa immediately arrested. He was about to kill Devaki, but Vasudeva somehow convinced Kamsa not to kill Devaki, on the promise that every child born of Devaki, he would hand over to Kamsa. So, Kamsa, in order to ensure that every child that is born would be handed over to him, he imprisoned Vasudeva and Devaki. Now, this Kamsa, cruel Kamsa, Kamsa killed the first six sons born of Devaki and Vasudeva, Shatgarbha. And, when Devaki became pregnant for the seventh time, then that child was transferred by arrangement of Yogamaya, by Krishna's arrangement through Yogamaya, the seventh son of Devaki and Vasudeva was transferred to Rohini's womb, from Devaki's womb. That was Balaram. And, Kamsa thought the seventh pregnancy was a miscarriage. The child died in the womb. That's what Kamsa thought. And then, Krishna appeared as the eighth son in the womb of Devaki, but when Devaki was pregnant for the eighth time, Kamsa was just waiting. Now, this child is going to take birth. I must kill this child as soon as it's born. But, Kamsa could not actually lay his hands on the child, eighth child which is born of the womb of Devaki. Krishna appeared middle of the night by potency of Yogamaya. Kamsa and all the doorkeepers and prison keepers were all asleep under the influence of Yogamaya. And, that is when Vasudeva and Devaki offered wonderful prayers to Krishna when Krishna appeared. And, because they were anxious to protect Krishna, Krishna instructed Vasudeva to transfer him from the prison of Kamsa to the house of Nanda Maharaj and Yashoda, from Mathura to Gokula. And, that's what Vasudeva did in the middle of the night. And, while all this was happening, Kamsa is fast asleep and all his doorkeepers are also asleep. So, this is called Krishna Leela, actually. It's Krishna's pastime. His appearance, his activities are all divine.

---
### Krishna's divinity, Scriptural authority, Vasudeva's role
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/dUk_QL3PWDQ?t=598
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "We should hear from the scriptures the divine nature of all of Krishna's activities."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the divine nature of Krishna's activities and the importance of understanding them through scripture, not speculation. He then continues describing Vasudeva's role as the maternal uncle of the Pandavas and his involvement in Krishna's pastimes.

And, how to understand these activities as divine is explained in the Srimad Bhagavatam in great detail. We should not speculate or we should not give our own interpretation. No. We should hear from the scriptures the divine nature of all of Krishna's activities. Then, further, it is described Vasudeva as the maternal uncle of the Pandavas. That means, Yudhishthira, Bhima, Arjuna, Nakula, Sahadeva, their mother's brother, maternal uncle is Vasudeva. So, as maternal uncle of Pandavas, he took active part in purificatory processes of the Pandavas, Samskaras. When Krishna appeared within the bars of Kamsa's prison house, Krishna was transferred by Vasudeva to the house of Nandaman. That's what I explained in detail. Krishna disappeared along with Baladeva prior to the disappearance of Vasudeva. Therefore, Arjuna took charge of funeral ceremony after Vasudeva's disappearance. These are all pastimes of Krishna and his devotees, intimate devotees. They are also under the protection of Yoga Maya.
