## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.11.26 | 16-07-2022
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### invocation, description of the Lord
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/81tHpeqqyJ4?t=0
**Verse References:** SB 1.11.26
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So all the four are described very very nicely in this verse."
**Summary:** The lecture begins with prayers and then introduces the 26th verse of the 11th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam, which describes the Lord's chest, face, arms, and lotus feet.

Om Agyana Timiraandhasya Gyananjanishalakaya Chakshurunmilitam Yena Tasvai Shri Gurave Namaha. Nama Om Vishnupadaya Krishna Preshtaya Bhutale Shri Mate Bhakti Vedanta Swamin Nithinamine. Namaste Saraswate Deve Gauravani Pracharine Nirvishesha Shunyavadi Paschatya Deshatarine. Jaya Shri Krishna Chaitanya Prabhu Nithyananda Shri Advaita Gadadhara Shiva Sadhi Gauravakta Vrinda. Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare. Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya Naram Chai Vanarottamam Deveem Saraswateem Vyasam Tato Jaya Mudhirayet. In this session we will be discussing from the 26th verse of the 11th chapter of the 1st canto of Shri Madbhagavatam: Shriyo Nivaso Yasyo Raha Paanapatram Mukham Drisham Bahavo Lokapalanam Saranganaam Padambujam. So here is a description of the Lord's beautiful form. So systematically the description is being given of the chest of the Lord, the moon-like face, His arms, and His lotus feet. Yasya, Uraha, then Mukham, then Bahavaha, and Padaambujam. So all the four are described very very nicely in this verse.

---
### beauty of the Lord, protection, demigods
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/81tHpeqqyJ4?t=133
**Verse References:** SB 1.11.26
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The Lord gives all protection to the demigods who are engaged in administration of this universe."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the verse, explaining that the Lord's chest is the abode of the goddess of fortune, His face is the object of beauty for the eyes, and His arms are the resting places for the administrative demigods, providing them protection by killing demons.

So the chest is the abode of the goddess of fortune. The goddess of fortune, Nivasa, Sriaha, Nivasa. So the moon-like face is the drinking vessel for eyes which hanker after all that is beautiful. Our eyes are always searching, seeking something beautiful to look at, but which is the most beautiful object of vision for our eyes? It is the eyes of the Lord. It is the Lord's form, and particularly the Lord's face. And his arms are the resting places for the administrative demigods. So Prabhupada explains in the purport what this means. The Lord gives all protection to the demigods who are engaged in administration of this universe by actually giving protection through killing the demons, either directly himself or through his agents, through his empowered devotees. He kills the demons and he protects the devotees, the demigods.

---
### devotees, lotus feet, Brahma Samhita, spiritual world
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/81tHpeqqyJ4?t=230
**Verse References:** SB 1.11.26
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The pure devotees always take shelter of the Lord's lotus feet, and they always do discuss about the Supreme Lord."
**Summary:** The lecture continues with the explanation that the lotus feet of the Lord are the refuge of pure devotees, whose only topic of discussion is the Supreme Lord. The speaker then references the Brahma Samhita to describe the transcendental abode of the Lord, where the goddess of fortune serves Him with reverence.

Then his lotus feet are the refuge of pure devotees. So the lotus feet are the refuge of pure devotees. The pure devotees always take shelter of the Lord's lotus feet, and they always do discuss about the Supreme Lord. Their only topic of discussion are the Supreme Lord. So the Purport Shila Prabhupada explains there are different classes of human beings all with different enjoyment from different objects. There are persons who are seeking after the favour of the goddess of fortune. And for them, the Vedic literatures give information that the Lord is always served with all reverence by thousands and thousands of the goddesses of fortune at the Chintamani Dhamma. This is the verse in the Brahma Samhita, Chintamani prakara sadmasu kalpa vriksha lakshavrteshu surabhi rabhi palayantam, lakshmi sahasra shata sambhrama sevya manam, lakshmi refers to the goddess of fortune, sahasra shata, hundreds of thousands of goddesses of fortune are serving him with great reverence, sambhrama sevya manam. This description is there in the Brahma Samhita, the transcendental abode of the Lord where the trees are all desired trees and the buildings are made of touchstone, Chintamani prakara sadmasu. The trees are desired trees. They yield all kinds of desires. They satisfy all kinds of desires. The Lord Govinda is engaged there in herding the Surabhi cows as his natural occupation. Surabhi rabhi palayantam lakshavrteshu. He is surrounded by so many cows, beautiful cows, Surabhi cows. And they yield so much milk, oceans of milk.

---
### beauty of the Lord, impersonalism, artists
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/81tHpeqqyJ4?t=371
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Simply darshan of the Lord's transcendental form means we can simultaneously have darshan of the goddess of fortune because she always resides on the chest of the Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that seeing the Lord's form allows one to see the goddess of fortune. He contrasts this with the impersonalists who, due to their speculative nature, cannot perceive the Lord's form or the goddess of fortune. He also addresses artists, suggesting they focus on the Lord's beautiful face for complete satisfaction, as all beauty in the material world is a reflection of the Lord's original beauty.

These goddesses of fortune can be saved. They are seen automatically if we are attracted by the bodily features of the Lord. There is no need to separately endeavor to have darshan of the beautiful goddess of fortune. Simply darshan of the Lord's transcendental form means we can simultaneously have darshan of the goddess of fortune because she always resides on the chest of the Lord. Now, Srila Prabhupada makes a remark about the goddess of fortune. He says, the impersonalists cannot observe such goddesses of fortune. Why? Because of their dry speculative habit. So, the speculation leads them to conclude that absolute truth is impersonal. No form, no personality. This is their conclusion. So, when they cannot see the form of the Lord, they can neither see the form of the goddess of fortune. Now, there are artists who are overtaken by the beautiful creation, this material creation. Prabhupada said, they should better see to the beautiful face of the Lord for complete satisfaction. Artists want to paint something very beautiful. They admire the creation. But millions and billions of times more beautiful is the beautiful face of the Lord. Of course, the creation has got beauty, no doubt about it. But that's just a reflection of the original beauty of the face of the Lord. So whatever beauty we see in this material world is a reflection of the original beauty of the form of the Lord or the face of the Lord.

---
### beauty, administration, protection, devotees
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/81tHpeqqyJ4?t=499
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "A faithful servant of the Lord engaged in the service of the administration is the ideal executive head and can well protect the interest of the people in general."
**Summary:** The speaker describes the Lord's beauty and voice as reflected in the material world. He then discusses administrative demigods and the fear of competition in the material world, contrasting it with the protection offered by the Lord's arms. He emphasizes that pure devotees are fearless and that a faithful servant of the Lord is the ideal executive head.

The face of the Lord is the embodiment of beauty. All beauty is there in the face of the Lord. What they call beautiful nature is but a smile. What they call the sweet sounds of the birds are but specimens of the whispering voice of the Lord. See, this is a description in the Bhagavatam second canto about the Lord's beauty and Lord's melodious voice that is manifest in this material world as a reflection. There are administrative demigods in charge of departmental service of cosmic management. And there are tiny administrative gods in the state service. They are always afraid of other competitors. Those in position of power in this material world, they are fearful of competitors who may overtake them. But if such administrative gods or administrative demigods, if they take shelter of the arms of the Lord, the Lord can protect them always from the attacks of enemies. This is always a fact. Pure devotees always take shelter of the Supreme Lord's arms. So pure devotees are never afraid. Never afraid. They are called abhaya, nirbhaya. A faithful servant of the Lord engaged in the service of the administration is the ideal executive head and can well protect the interest of the people in general.

---
### administration, protection, devotees, essence
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/81tHpeqqyJ4?t=623
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The Lord's arms are worthy of taking shelter because they can give protection to anyone and everyone in this material world."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the examples of Yudhishthira Maharaj and Parichit Maharaj to illustrate how rulers who take protection of the Supreme Lord can give protection to their citizens. He contrasts this with materialistic administrators who cannot provide real protection. He explains that the Lord's arms are a safe refuge and that pure devotees are called sarangas because they sing and talk about the Lord, who is the essence of everything.

Like Yudhishthira Maharaj, Parichit Maharaj. They were ruling the whole world. But because they had taken protection of the Supreme Lord, they were able to give protection to the citizens. They were able to give proper protection to the citizens. Other so-called administrators are symbols of anachronisms leading to the acute distress of the people who are governed by them. These materialistic administrators, their own life is in danger. What to speak of them giving protection to the citizens they are ruling? It is not possible. They cannot give real protection. They may try, but they are invariably defeated by more powerful persons. That's the nature of this material world. But any administrator can remain safely under the protection of the arms of the Lord. That's what is meant by telling his arms are the resting place for the administrative demigods. The Lord's arms are worthy of taking shelter because they can give protection to anyone and everyone in this material world. So, Prabhupada explains the word saranganam. So Prabhupada says, the essence of everything is the Supreme Lord and therefore he is called saram. Saram is the essence. And those who sing and talk about him are called sarangas. Or the pure devotees. The pure devotees are called sarangas because they are always singing and glorifying and talking about the Supreme Lord who is the essence of everything, Sara. So they are called sarangas. The pure devotees are always hankering after the lotus feet of the Lord. Why?

---
### lotus feet, honey, devotion, Rupa Goswami, prayer
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/81tHpeqqyJ4?t=757
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "O Madhava, I know also that I have no genuine devotion for the service of your lotus feet. But because your lordship is inconceivably powerful, you can do what is impossible to be done."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that devotees are like bees seeking the honey of the Lord's lotus feet. He quotes Srila Rupa Goswami's prayer, where Rupa Goswami compares his mind to a bee seeking the honey of Krishna's lotus feet, expressing his desire to be fixed at the Lord's lotus feet to relish the taste of transcendental service. The lecture concludes with thanks and the Hare Krishna mantra.

Prabhupada explains, the lotus has a kind of honey which is transcendentally relished by the devotees. And the devotees are like bees who are always after the honey of the lotus feet of the Lord. So, Srila Prabhupada is quoting the great devotee Acharya, Srila Rupa Goswami. He has sung a song about the lotus honey comparing himself to the bee. Srila Rupa Goswami says in this song, O my Lord Krishna, I beg to offer my prayers unto you. My mind is like the bee and it is after some honey. Kindly therefore give my bee mind a place at your lotus feet, which are the resources for all transcendental honey. I know that even big demigods like Brahma do not see the rays of the nails of your lotus feet, even though they are engaged in deep meditation for years and years together. Still, O infallible one, my ambition is such, for you are very merciful to your surrendered devotees. O Madhava, I know also that I have no genuine devotion for the service of your lotus feet. But because your lordship is inconceivably powerful, you can do what is impossible to be done. Your lotus feet can deride even the nectar of the heavenly kingdom and therefore I am very much attracted by them. O Supreme Eternal, please therefore let my mind be fixed at your lotus feet so that eternally I may be able to relish the taste of your transcendental service. This is the beautiful prayer offered by Srila Rupa Goswami, the Acharya in the Gaudiya Vaishnava Sampradaya. So, the devotees are satisfied with being placed at the lotus feet of the Lord because there is a certain honey to be relished by the devotees who take shelter at the lotus feet of the Lord. I will stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.11.27 | 17-07-2022
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### Krishna's beauty, Inconceivable potency, Simultaneous events
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/6UFwsN3Ovhk?t=32
**Verse References:** SB 1.11.27
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The adjustment is possible only when we think of the inconceivable potency of the Lord."
**Summary:** This segment focuses on the description of Krishna's entrance into Dwaraka, emphasizing the inconceivable potency of the Lord that allows for the simultaneous appearance of seemingly impossible natural phenomena.

Hare Krishna, Hare Krishna, Krishna Krishna, Hare Hare, Hare Rama, Hare Rama, Rama Rama, Hare Hare, Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya, Narayanam Namaskritya, Naram Jai Vanarottamam, Devim Sarasvatim Vyasam Tato Jaya Mudhirayet. In this session we will be discussing from the 27th verse of the 11th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. This 11th chapter is entitled Lord Krishna's Entrance into Dwaraka. So how the Dwaraka nivasis are receiving Krishna, that's the subject matter of this particular chapter. Sitata Patra Vyajanair Upaskritaha Prasuna Varshair Abhivarshitah. The meaning of this verse is, as the Lord passed along the public road of Dwaraka, his head was protected from the sunshine by a white umbrella. White feathered fans moved in semi-circles and showers of flowers fell upon the holy land. The road, his yellow garments and garlands of flowers made it appear as if a dark cloud was surrounded simultaneously by Sun moon lightning and rainbows. The description here is of a very, very beautiful scene when Krishna is passing on the public road of Dwaraka being welcomed by somebody holding an umbrella, by shower of flowers, by his yellow garments appearing very, very beautiful, all complementing each other to create a very beautiful scene. So here the comparison is made to a natural phenomena that occurs in a beautiful sky. Shri la prabhupada comments in the purport, the Sun, moon, rainbow and lightning do not appear in the sky simultaneously. When there is Sun, the moonlight becomes insignificant, and if there are clouds and a rainbow, there is no manifestation of lightning. These are the natural phenomena that occur in the sky. So how was it possible that all of them are described as simultaneously appearing in the scene? So that is explained here. The Lord's Bodily Hue is just like a new monsoon cloud. He is compared here to the cloud. The white umbrella over his head is compared to the sun, the movement of the bunch hair fan of flukes is compared to the moon, the showers of flowers are compared to the stars, his yellow garments are compared to the rainbow. So all these activities of the firmament, firmament means the sky, being impossible simultaneous factors, cannot be adjusted by comparison. If you try to imagine that the scene was like all these appearing simultaneously, that is never possible as a natural phenomenon. The advantage of the white umbrella over his head is that it can be adjusted by comparison. The adjustment is possible only when we think of the inconceivable potency of the Lord. This is the important point to note here. The Lord's potency is inconceivable. Why? Because the Lord is all-powerful. And in His presence, anything impossible can be made possible by His inconceivable energy. So the Lord, by His inconceivable energy, makes the impossible also possible.

---
### Inconceivable potency, Limitations of science, Vedanta Sutra
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/6UFwsN3Ovhk?t=471
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Things which are beyond your imagination, beyond your reach, beyond your thoughts, beyond your words, beyond your sense perception, how can you understand them by your experimental so-called scientific research?"
**Summary:** This segment discusses the limitations of scientific knowledge in understanding the Lord's creation and emphasizes the importance of accepting knowledge through the descending process, as advised by the Vedanta Sutra.

Then, that scene might appear something like all the four simultaneously coming in the sky. Now, regarding the Lord's inconceivable potency, Srila Prabhupada explains in another place in one lecture delivered while speaking on Bhagavad Gita. Prabhupada says, there are so many things that is not possible even to understand by the modern process of scientific experimental knowledge. The Lord's creation is so very wonderful that if somebody tries to comprehend what is the creation like by their process of scientific experimental knowledge, it is not possible. And, if the creation of the Lord is not possible to be understood by scientific process of knowledge, how can this ever understand God Himself, the source of everything? You cannot even know material objects by your so-called scientific study and research. You can know little more, that's all, by some advancement of your material knowledge. But, you cannot completely understand even this creation, the inferior creation of the Lord. So, Vedanta Sutra advises all of us, achintya khaluye bhava na tam starkena yojoyet. This is what the Vedanta Sutra states. Things which are beyond your imagination, beyond your reach, beyond your thoughts, beyond your words, beyond your sense perception, how can you understand them by your experimental so-called scientific research? It's not possible. Therefore, achintya, achintya means inconceivable. We cannot conceive even material things which are created by the Lord.

---
### Time, Inconceivable potency, Brahmaloka
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/6UFwsN3Ovhk?t=624
**Verse References:** BG 8.17
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So, it's just not possible for us to calculate this. What is the duration of life of the people who live in Brahmaloka, the topmost planet in this material universe?"
**Summary:** This segment delves into the concept of time in Brahmaloka, the highest planet in the universe, illustrating the inconceivable duration of life there and contrasting it with our limited understanding.

In the Bhagavad Gita, it is said, sahasra yuga pariyantam aharyat brahmano viduhu. Prabhupada gives an example for helping us understand what's really inconceivable about this. Brahmanaha means of Lord Brahma and the living creatures in the Brahmaloka, the highest, topmost planet in the entire universe. So in that planet, Brahmaloka planet, the people or living entities who are living there, their duration of life, what is it like? What is their duration of life? The Bhagavad Gita says, sahasra yuga pariyantam. Now yuga, the word yuga used here means according to our calculation on the earth, time calculation on the earth. Yuga here means the four yugas combined together. Now, satya yuga means eighteen lakhs of years. Treta yuga, twelve hundred thousands of years. Dwapara yuga, eight hundred thousands of years. And Kali yuga, four hundred thousands of years. So, totally, all these four put together, forty-three hundred thousands of years makes one Divya yuga. And one thousand such Divya yugas, that is one day of the inhabitants of Brahmaloka. So can you do the calculation? Can you imagine what is the duration of their day? So they live a hundred years, 100 years according to that calculation of day, such day and night, and 365 such days and nights, 100 such years they live. So, it's just not possible for us to calculate this. What is the duration of life of the people who live in Brahmaloka, the topmost planet in this material universe? So, these are all achintya, beyond our conception. It has been calculated elsewhere, and it is said their lifespan, Brahmaloka lifespan, is something like 4 trillion 30 billion of our solar years. 4 trillion 30 billion. That is their lifespan. And even that is limited. That's limited. That is just half a breadth of Maha Vishnu. That is the lifespan of the inhabitants of Brahmaloka or Lord Brahma. Half a breadth of Maha Vishnu. So, what is Maha Vishnu's time scale? So, we cannot imagine all this. We cannot conceive of all this. Then, what to speak of the spiritual world? We cannot calculate even something of the material world perfectly.

---
### Descending process, Parampara, Limitations of human intellect
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/6UFwsN3Ovhk?t=853
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So, the best thing is to follow the process, the Vedic process, to receive knowledge by the descending process."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes the importance of receiving knowledge through the descending process (parampara) due to the limitations of human intellect and the vastness of the universe and its complexities.

So, the best thing is to follow the process, the Vedic process, to receive knowledge by the descending process. Descending process means the perfect knowledge is available from the Supreme Lord, coming down in the parampara system. We simply have to receive it. That's all. And for receiving such knowledge, we have to prepare ourselves. And that preparation is nothing but the practice of spiritual life. And it is perfect knowledge handed down. Because by our experimental method, we can't even conceive what is the nature of things in this one universe. There are millions of such universes. Each universe has millions of planets. Each planet has millions of living entities. Each living entity has millions of complicated structures within their form. There are 3 trillion cells within our body. And there are 200 varieties of cells. All this is inconceivable for our tiny brain to accommodate. So, therefore, the descending process. I'll stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

**Verse References:** SB 1.11.28

**Summary:** The speaker introduces the verses to be discussed, focusing on Krishna's return to Dwaraka and his respectful interaction with his father's wives, emphasizing the importance of respecting all mothers.

In this session we will be discussing from two verses 28 and 29 of the 11th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam, the same topic: Lord Krishna's entrance into Dwaraka, how the Dwaraka Nivasis received Lord Krishna when he returned to Dwaraka from Hastinapura. Pravishtastu Griham Pitro Parishvakta Swamatrabhihi Vavande Shirasa Saptah Devaki Pramukha Mudha. The meaning of this verse is after entering the house of his father he was embraced by the mothers present and the Lord offered his obeisances unto them by placing his head at their feet. The mothers were headed by Devaki, his real mother. So Srila Prabhupada explains in the purport Krishna has entered his father's house. Vasudeva was living in a palace in Dwaraka and Vasudeva, the father of Lord Krishna, had completely separate residential quarters where he lived with his 18 wives. Kings used to have more than one queen, so Vasudeva was a king and he had 18 queens, so he used to live in separate residential quarters with his 18 queens. Out of them Srimati Devaki is the real mother of Lord Krishna. Devaki and Vasudeva gave birth to Krishna, but in spite of all this, all other stepmothers were equally affectionate to Lord Krishna as would be evident from the following verse. So Lord Krishna also did not distinguish his real mother from his stepmothers and he equally offered his obeisances unto all the wives of Vasudeva present on the occasion. So Srila Prabhupada explains...

### Vedic culture, respect, motherhood
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ZZWUvC7WGM0?t=183
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "According to the Vedic conception, there are seven mothers."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the Vedic concept of seven mothers, explaining each category and emphasizing the importance of respecting those in the position of a mother.

In another place that Lord Krishna is teaching us by his example that there are actually seven mothers for everyone, every civilized human being, seven mothers. Atma Mata, Guru Patni, Brahmani, Raja Patnika, Dhenur, Dhatri, Tatha Prithvi. Saptaita Matara Smrita, seven mothers according to the Vedic conception. So the meaning of this particular verse that I have just now recited, according to scriptures there are seven mothers, Saptaita Matara Smrita. First one is Atma Mata, the real mother. Guru Patni, the wife of the spiritual master, Guru Patni. Number three, Brahmani means the wife of a Brahmana. Number four, Raja Patnika, the wife of the king, that means the queen. Then number five, Dhenu, the cow, and number six, Dhatri, means the nurse, and last one, the seventh one, Prithvi, means the earth, mother earth, mother cow, all of them are mothers, all of them are mothers. This is the Vedic culture, very advanced culture. The highest respect is offered to the mother, but mother is not just one. Our limited understanding is generally we have one mother, but no, there are other mothers, there are other mothers, others in the position of mother as regards respect, offering respect, from that perspective it is being said here how one should offer respect to one's mother, the same respect should be offered to the others also who are in the position of respect as one's own mother. Hmm.

### respect, Krishna's example, imitation vs. following
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ZZWUvC7WGM0?t=349
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Lord Krishna, the Lord of the Universe, plays the part of an ideal son just to teach others how to treat their stepmothers."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes that Krishna's example teaches us to respect stepmothers and clarifies that while we should follow Krishna's example of respect, we should not imitate all of his activities.

Even by this injunction of the Shastras, the seven kinds of mothers, the stepmother who is the wife of the father is also as good as one's own mother because the father is also one of the spiritual masters. So if the father is spiritual master and if he has eighteen wives, all the eighteen of them are one's mother. Each one of them is mother. So Lord Krishna, the Lord of the Universe, plays the part of an ideal son just to teach others how to treat their stepmothers. Generally stepmothers are not given as much respect as one's own mother normally, but here Lord Krishna is teaching mother is respectable and seven types of mother we have to respect, that is the perfect culture, Vedic culture, the most advanced developed culture so that we can develop our Krishna consciousness, we can awaken our devotion, dormant devotion to Krishna which is covered right now within the heart. So this culture is important from that perspective. Now Srila Prabhupada says that Lord Krishna teaches by his example certain things which are to be followed by everyone, but that does not mean we can imitate all of Krishna's activities which sometimes people tend to do. Prabhupada explains in another lecture given on the occasion of giving a discourse on Bhagavad Gita...

---
### imitation vs. following, authority, examples
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ZZWUvC7WGM0?t=475
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Anukaran means imitation, Anussaran means following the footprints. If one tries to follow the footprints of great personalities, that is very nice, but we cannot imitate, imitation is dangerous."
**Summary:** The speaker distinguishes between imitation and following, using examples of Lord Shiva and the sun to illustrate the dangers of imitating powerful figures without the capacity to handle the consequences.

Srila Prabhupada says Anukaran and Anussaran, two Sanskrit words are there, one is imitation, the other word refers to following the footprints. Anukaran means imitation, Anussaran means following the footprints. If one tries to follow the footprints of great personalities, that is very nice, but we cannot imitate, imitation is dangerous. Just like Lord Shiva, he drank an ocean of poison and kept it in his throat, he did not allow that poison to go down, so he became known as Neelakanta, blue-throated because he is holding the poison in his neck, in his throat. So if somebody imitates Lord Shiva and indulges in intoxication, ganja, he will go to hell. So this imitation of Shiva is dangerous, is dangerous, very dangerous. Now Shiva is very very very powerful, so somebody may say Shiva is smoking ganja, so why I can't smoke ganja? No you cannot because you cannot imitate Shiva, you can only follow Shiva in his footprints, those activities which Shiva performs among many activities that he does, only those activities which performs for setting an example for the common people, only those activities we have to follow. Now another example Srila Prabhupada gives, just like the sunshine, the sunshine is so very strong that it is absorbing the moisture of the filthy place, contaminated place, contaminated place where one is passing urine, but still the sun is very very very powerful, so the sun doesn't get contaminated by absorbing urine from a filthy place, no. But if we try to live in a filthy place where people pass urine, then we will become immediately contaminated because we don't have the power to counteract that infection, we don't have it. So Bhagavatam says those who are powerful you cannot find some fault with them, nor can you imitate their powerful activities, that's never possible. So for the common people we have to follow the rules and regulations and while following the rules and regulations the examples are set by great personalities, only those examples which are specifically meant for us to follow.

---
### Krishna's pastimes, affection, guidance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ZZWUvC7WGM0?t=692
**Verse References:** SB 1.11.29
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "We should show respect to the seven kinds of mothers, including stepmothers, that is to be followed."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes by discussing the affection of Vasudeva's wives for Krishna, reiterating the importance of respecting all mothers and emphasizing the need for guidance from guru, sadhu, and shastra to discern which activities of great personalities to follow.

The 29th verse: ta putram ankam aaropya snehasnuta payodharaha harsha vikvalitaatmanaha vikvalitaatmanaha shichur si shichur netrajair jalaihi. The meaning of this verse is the mothers after embracing their son, who is the son, Lord Krishna, the Supreme Lord, sat him on their laps, due to pure affection milk sprang from their breasts, they were overwhelmed with delight and the tears from their eyes wetted the Lord. So this is the affection all the mothers, the 18 queens of Vasudeva had for Krishna and this is a natural, eternal, spontaneous affection they have, relationship they have. So Srila Prabhupada says in the purport when Lord Krishna was at Vrindavan even the cows would become moistened by affection towards him and he would draw milk from the nipples of every affectionate living being, so what to speak of the stepmothers who were already as good as his own mother. So we should not imitate Krishna in the matter of Krishna dancing with young gopis in the rasa dance or Krishna lifting Govardhan Hill, don't imitate. But Krishna bowing down, showing respect to all his stepmothers, touching his head to their feet, that is to be followed, that is to be followed. We should show respect to the seven kinds of mothers, including stepmothers, that is to be followed. So one certain activities of Krishna, certain activities of an incarnation of the Supreme Lord, certain activities of powerful personalities like Lord Shiva are meant for us to follow. So how do we know which activity we are supposed to follow and which activity we are not supposed to imitate? That's why we require guidance, for our guidance there are three authorities: guru, sadhu, shastra. Guru means our own spiritual master, the bona fide spiritual master, he guides and the authority also is the scriptures, shastra, and the previous acharyas, their instructions, their injunctions. So three authorities: guru, sadhu, shastra. Guru means one's own spiritual master and sadhu means the previous acharyas and shastra means the scriptures. All the three are meant to be consulted which activities of great personalities we are supposed to follow and which activities we are not supposed to imitate. I'll stop here, thank you very much, Harikrishnan.

## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.11.3 | 25-06-2022
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### darshan, devotion, Dwaraka
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/TLRKys2J_HU?t=35
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Immediately they began to run towards the city entrance, simply to receive the Lord, to have his darshan, long-desired audience."
**Summary:** The citizens of Dwaraka eagerly awaited Krishna's arrival and rushed to greet him upon hearing the sound of his conch shell, demonstrating their deep devotion and desire for his darshan.

Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya Naram Chai Vanarottamam Devi Saraswati Vyasam Tato Jayam Udi Rayet In this session we will be discussing from the third verse of the eleventh chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam, Tam Upashrutya Ninadam Jagadbhaya Bhayavaham Pratyudyayukh Prajasarva Bhartradarsana Lalasaaha, the meaning of this verse is, the citizens of Dwaraka having heard that sound which threatens fear personified in the material world, began to run away. Run towards him fast, just to have a long-desired audience with the Lord, who is the protector of all devotees. So this is the description, how the citizens of Dwaraka responded to that sound of Krishna's conch shell. Krishna was announcing to the citizens, knowing very well that they were eagerly awaiting his arrival in Dwaraka. So he sounded his conch shell. Before he actually entered the city, he sounded his conch shell. And what did the citizens do? Immediately they began to run towards the city entrance, simply to receive the Lord, to have his darshan, long-desired audience.

### liberated souls, devotion, Krishna consciousness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/TLRKys2J_HU?t=177
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The pure devotees, the liberated souls, the eternal associates never forget Krishna even for one moment."
**Summary:** The citizens of Dwarka were liberated souls, eternally devoted to Krishna, constantly thinking of him and experiencing unlimited joy through their relationship with him, unlike those in the material world who forget Krishna and suffer.

As already explained by Srila Prabhupada in the Purport, as already explained the citizens of Dwarka who lived at the time of Lord Krishna's presence there were all liberated souls who descended there along with the Lord as entourage. They accompanied the Lord. They are all his eternal devotees. They are all liberated souls. They have nothing to do with this material world of karma. We take birth here because we have got the force of karma and we accept a material body. These devotees of Krishna, liberated souls, they come in their spiritual form as much as Krishna appears in his original spiritual form. And it is described, all were anxious to have an audience with the Lord although because of spiritual contact, they were never separated from the Lord. We should not think that they are forgetful of Krishna at any time. No. That is our condition that we forget Krishna and we are put into so much material miseries, material difficulties, material problems only because of forgetfulness of Krishna. But the pure devotees, the liberated souls, the eternal associates never forget Krishna even for one moment. They are constantly thinking of Krishna, constantly remembering Krishna, constantly in touch with Krishna through varieties of ways. Just as the gopis of Vrindavan used to think of Krishna while he was away from the village for cowherding engagements. Example is given by Srila Prabhupada, the gopis in Vrindavan. Krishna is to go for cowherding. The gopis would always think of Krishna when he was away from the village. Similarly, the citizens of Dwarka were all immersed in thought of the Lord while he was away from Dwarka to attend the battle of Kurukshetra. They were thinking of Krishna. They were remembering his pastimes. They were thinking of his beautiful form, of his qualities, of his relationship. Everything. They are constantly immersed and that itself is a source of unlimited joy for them. They are always in this. But there are varieties of experiences of this ecstasy in different times, in different ways.

---
### Krishna, forms of God, oneness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/TLRKys2J_HU?t=334
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna is one Supreme Lord, one person, though he presents himself in different forms, in different moods according to the relationship."
**Summary:** Despite Krishna's diverse activities, forms, and qualities in Vrindavan, Mathura, and Dwarka, he remains the same Supreme Lord, one person, and this oneness extends to other incarnations like Rama and Narasimha, as well as forms in the spiritual world.

But there are varieties of experiences of this ecstasy in different times, in different ways. The ecstasy of the devotees is ecstasy. The ecstasy of the devotees is experienced by them. Some distinguished fiction writer in Bengal concluded that the Krishna of Vrindavan, that of Mathura and that of Dwarka were different personalities. Because Krishna's activities are entirely different in Vrindavan, in Mathura and Dwarka. There are varieties of relationships. And varieties of forms. Varieties of activities. Varieties of qualities displayed by Krishna. But Krishna is one Supreme Lord. One person. Though he presents himself in different forms, in different moods according to the relationship. But he is one person. He is not different. There are not many persons there. Only one person. Historically there is no truth in this conclusion. That means Krishna is the same everywhere. In Dwarka, in Mathura, in Vrindavan. The Krishna of Kurukshetra and the Krishna of Dwarka are one and the same personality. Advaitam acchutamanadi manantaroopam Not only Krishna in Dwarka, Vrindavan and Mathura are one person. Even Krishna and Rama and Narasimha and Varaha are the same person, Krishna. In different incarnations. Even in the spiritual world, Narayana, Vasudeva, Sankarsana, Pradyumna, Aniruddha are the same person, Krishna. But he is present in different forms. According to the need of reciprocating with his different forms. Reciprocating with his different devotees.

---
### melancholy, absence of Krishna, protection
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/TLRKys2J_HU?t=468
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The devotees of the Lord know no one else as their protector. Krishna is the only protector of all his devotees."
**Summary:** The citizens of Dwarka felt melancholy in Krishna's absence, similar to the sadness felt at night without the sun, and his return dispelled this darkness, highlighting that Krishna is the sole protector of his devotees.

The citizens of Dwarka were thus in a state of melancholy. Melancholy means feeling of sadness due to the Lord's absence from the transcendental city. When Krishna was absent, the citizens were feeling sad. Krishna is not there. We long for Krishna. As much as we are put into a state of melancholy at night because of the absence of the sun, the example is being given. In darkness, without sunshine at night, we feel sadness. The sound heralded by Lord Krishna was something like the heralding of the sunrise in the morning. When the sun rises in the morning, the darkness is immediately dispelled. And thieves and plunderers and all kinds of rogues, they again go back into hiding. Because they stealthily try to do all their nefarious activities at night in the darkness. Whereas as soon as the sun rises, all these thieves and plunderers, they go away into hiding. Similarly, all darkness is dispelled, all ignorance is dispelled, all sadness is dispelled when Krishna sounded his conch shell and announced his arrival in Dwaraka. So all the citizens of Dwaraka awoke from a state of slumber. Slumber means sleepy, sleepiness. They were like sleeping in the absence of Krishna. And they awoke because of the sunrise of Krishna. And they all hastened towards him just to have an audience, just to have darshan of Krishna's beautiful form. They all rushed towards Krishna, their entrance of the city of Dwaraka. The devotees of the Lord know no one else as their protector. Krishna is the only protector of all his devotees. And Krishna is the only one who can give protection. No one else can protect us. Especially in the material world, we are, that Prabhupada explains in the next paragraph.

---
### Advaya jnana, fear, Maya
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/TLRKys2J_HU?t=626
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The material existence of our present status is full of fear."
**Summary:** The sound of the Lord is identical with the Lord, as Krishna is Advaya jnana, the non-dual substance, and material existence is full of fear due to association with Maya, the illusory energy.

This sound of the Lord is identical with the Lord as we have tried to explain by the non-dual position of the Lord. Advaya jnana. In the Bhagavatam it is explained Krishna is Advaya jnana. The non-dual substance. That means Krishna is absolute. His name, his form, his qualities, his pastimes or his activities, his paraphernalia, his devotees, his abode, all are identical with Krishna. That means Krishna is fully present in his name, in his form, in his qualities, in his abode, in his devotees, pure devotees, in his opulence. Everything is Krishna. There are no two things. In the material world there is always matter and spirit. We have a material body. This body is different from us. We are spirit soul. Whereas Krishna is identical with his form because his form is completely spiritual. Krishna is identical with his name because his name is completely spiritual. So Prabhupada explains further the material existence of our present status is full of fear. Out of the four problems of material existence ahara, nidra, bhaya, maithuna food problem, shelter problem, fear problem, mating problem the fear problem gives us more trouble than the others. We are always fearful due to our ignorance of the next problem. We are faced with many different problems one after another. That is material existence. So the whole material existence is full of problems and thus the fear problem is always prominent. This is due to our association with the illusory energy of the Lord known as Maya. So in the absence of Krishna consciousness when you forget Krishna then we are in touch or we are trapped captured by the illusory energy Maya.

---
### Hare Krishna mantra, Chaitanya Mahaprabhu, liberation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/TLRKys2J_HU?t=772
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "As soon as we say Hare Krishna immediately all fear will vanish all problems will disappear all miseries will go away and we come in direct contact with Krishna if we chant and hear Hare Krishna."
**Summary:** Chanting the Hare Krishna mantra, taught by Chaitanya Mahaprabhu, dispels fear, solves problems, and establishes direct contact with Krishna, leading to liberation through dependence on him.

Yet all fear is vanished as soon as there is a sound of the Lord represented by his holy name as it was sounded by Lord Sri Chaitanya Mahaprabhu in the following sixteen words Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare Chaitanya Mahaprabhu taught us this Hare Krishna mantra. He chanted and he also instructed us to chant Hare Krishna thereby associated with Krishna through his sound representation through his sound form Hare Krishna mantra. Hare Krishna mantra is fully present in the Hare Krishna mantra. And as soon as we say Hare Krishna immediately all fear will vanish all problems will disappear all miseries will go away and we come in direct contact with Krishna if we chant and hear Hare Krishna. Chanting and hearing Hare Krishna is as simple yet very very effective and powerful way of driving away all fear of becoming free from all problems. So that is what Srila Prabhupada explains we can take advantage of these sounds and be free from all threatening problems of material existence. Chaitanya Mahaprabhu is teaching us to put an end to our suffering in this world. Somebody may say how can simply chanting Hare Krishna solve all my problems? The difficulty is that in material existence we forget Krishna and we don't understand that we have to take shelter of Krishna for solving all our problems for getting all our necessities for for getting complete protection from Krishna. But instead in forgetfulness we make so many other arrangements which are incomplete, imperfect, insufficient. All this can be solved simply by chanting Hare Krishna and awakening our Krishna consciousness to come to the state of dependence on Krishna. Full dependence on Krishna. And thereby we become completely liberated from all problems. All our problems are solved. I'll stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

---

In this session, we will be discussing from the 30th verse of the 11th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam.

### opulence, Krishna, desire
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/cGTQWk7XnhQ?t=56
**Verse References:** SB 1.11.30
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The personality of Godhead means one who is full with all power, all energy, all opulences, all beauties, all knowledge, and all renunciation."
**Summary:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami introduces the verse and explains Krishna's unlimited opulence and the significance of the word 'Bhagavan'.

Athavishat Swabhavanam Sarvakamam Anuttamam Prasadayatra Patneenam Sahasranicha Shodasha. The meaning of this verse is, thereafter, the Lord entered his palaces, which were perfect to the fullest extent. His wives lived in them, and they numbered over 16. So, Srila Prabhupada explains in the purport, Lord Krishna had 16,108 wives, and for each and every one of them, there was a fully equipped palace, complete with necessary compounds and gardens. Full description of these palaces is given in the 10th canto. All these palaces were made of the best marble stone. They were illuminated by jewels, and decorated by curtains and carpets of velvet and silk, nicely bedecked and embroidered with gold lace. These things we can't even imagine in our present yuga. The personality of Godhead means one who is full with all power, all energy, all opulences, all beauties, all knowledge, and all renunciation. That's the meaning of the word Bhagavan, full of six opulences. Therefore, in the palaces of the Lord, there was nothing wanting for fulfilling all desires of the Lord. The Lord is unlimited, and therefore his desires are also unlimited. And the supply is also unlimited. Therefore, his desires are also unlimited. Everything being unlimited, it is concisely described here as sarvakamam, sarvakamam anuttamam, sarvakamam, full with all desirable equipment. sarvakamam.

---
### Dwarka, opulence, description
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/cGTQWk7XnhQ?t=176
**Verse References:** SB 10.69
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "In the city of Dwaraka, there were as many as 900,000 great palaces, built of first-class marble, with gates and doors made of silver."
**Summary:** The speaker describes the opulence of Krishna's palaces in Dwarka, based on the description in the 10th canto, 69th chapter, focusing on Narada's visit and the city's beauty.

Now, in the 10th canto, there is a beautiful description of the Lord. There is a beautiful description of the Lord. There is a beautiful description of the Lord. There is a beautiful description of the Lord. There is a beautiful description of the palaces of Krishna. In the 69th chapter of the 10th canto, there is a description. When the great sage Narada heard that Lord Krishna had married 16,000 wives after he had killed the demon Narakasura, sometimes also called Bhamasura, Narada was astonished. He was astonished. The Lord Krishna had expanded himself into 16,000 forms and married these wives simultaneously in different palaces. So, being inquisitive as to how Krishna was managing his household affairs with so many wives, Narada, desiring to see these pastimes, set out to visit Krishna's different homes. So, when Narada arrived in Dwaraka, he saw gardens, and parks full of various flowers of different colors, and orchards overloaded with a variety of fruits. Beautiful birds were chirping, and peacocks crowed delightfully. There were ponds full of blue and red lotus flowers, and some of these tanks were filled with varieties of vegetables, and some of these tanks were filled with varieties of vegetables, and varieties of lilies. The lakes were full of nice swans and cranes, and the voices of these birds resounded everywhere. In the city of Dwaraka, there were as many as 900,000 great palaces. How many? Nine lakh palaces, built of first-class marble, with gates and doors made of silver. The pillars of the houses and palaces were bedecked with jewels, such as touchstone, sapphire, emerald, and the floors gave off a beautiful luster. Floor giving off luster. Have you ever heard of this? The highways, lanes, streets, crossings, and marketplaces were all beautifully decorated. The whole city was full of residential homes, and the city was full of residential homes, and the city was full of residential homes, and the city was full of residential homes, and the city was full of residential homes, and the city was full of residential homes, and the city was full of residential homes, and the city was full of residential homes, and the city was full of residential homes, and the city was full of residential homes, and the city was full of residential homes, and the city was full of residential homes, and the city was full of residential homes, and the city was full of residential homes, and the city was full of residential homes, and the city was full of residential homes, and the city was full of residential homes, and the city was full of residential homes, and the city was full of residential homes, and the city was full of residential homes, and the city was full of residential homes, and the city was full of residential homes, and the city was full of residential homes, and the city was full of residential homes, and the city was full of residential homes, and streets and also the thresholds of every residential house were very clean. On both sides of every path there were bushes and at regular intervals there were large trees that shaded the avenues so that the sunshine would not bother the passer-by. In this greatly beautiful city of Dwarka, Lord Krishna the Supreme Personality of Godhead had many residential quarters.

---
### opulence, Krishna, beauty
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/cGTQWk7XnhQ?t=399
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "All this is a factual description. This is not somebody's imagination. This is not some mythology."
**Summary:** The lecture continues with a detailed description of the interior of Krishna's palaces, emphasizing the use of jewels, precious materials, and the overall beauty and opulence.

The great Kings and Princes of the world used to visit these palaces just to worship Lord Krishna. The architectural plans were made personally by Vishwakarma, the engineer of the demigods, Veshti. And in the construction of the palaces of Lord Krishna, he exhibited all of his talents and ingenuity. These residential quarters numbered more than 16,000. And a different queen of Lord Krishna resided in each of them. As many queens, so many palaces, one for each queen. The great sage Narada entered one of these houses and saw that the pillars were made of coral and the ceilings were bedecked with jewels. The walls as well as the arches between the pillars glowed. The palaces were decorated with decorations made of different kinds of sapphires. Throughout the palace were many canopies made by Vishwakarma that were decorated with strings of pearls. All this is a factual description. This is not somebody's imagination. This is not some mythology. Because the Lord is full of all opulences. So what is surprising if his palaces are so beautifully decorated. The chairs and other furniture were made of ivory and bedecked with gold and diamonds. And jewel lamps dissipated the darkness. The material nature by constitution is dark. But when there is a need of a light source, the Lord's palace was illuminated by jeweled lamps. The jewels which emit light. There was so much incense and fragrant gum burning that the scented fumes were coming out of the windows. The peacock sitting on the steps became illusioned by the fumes mistaking them for clouds and began dancing jubilantly. So beautiful.

---
### servants, spiritual world, attraction
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/cGTQWk7XnhQ?t=589
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "This is just to attract the conditioned soul to prepare themselves to go to the spiritual world."
**Summary:** The speaker describes the servants in Krishna's palaces and contrasts the opulence of the spiritual world with the material world, emphasizing that Krishna's opulence is meant to attract conditioned souls back to the spiritual realm.

The smoke coming out of the windows seemed like clouds for the peacocks sitting on the windows, and they were dancing very jubilantly. There were many maid servants, thousands and thousands of maid servants in each palace, and all of them were decorated with gold necklaces, golden bangles and beautiful saris. There were also many man servants nicely dressed in cloaks and turbans and jeweled earrings. Beautiful as they were, the servants were all engaged in different household duties. So this is just to give us an idea what is the kind of opulence of the Supreme Lord. You cannot compare it with opulence of the richest person in this entire creation, material creation. Of course, in the spiritual world, the Lord enjoys with all his devotees, all the devotees, He shares his opulences. Nobody there is bereft of any need, necessity. To serve the Lord, they have everything that they simply desire, because there are desire trees in the spiritual world. But even when Krishna appeared on this earth planet, He brought down his opulence. His abode descended. Dwaraka Dham, Mathura Dham, Vrindavan Dham descended with Krishna. And naturally, these beautiful places were full of the opulences. This is just to attract the conditioned soul to prepare themselves to go to the spiritual world.

---
### spiritual bliss, invitation, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/cGTQWk7XnhQ?t=738
**Verse References:** BG 18.66
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna invites us. Please come to the spiritual world, and simply by chanting Hare Krishna and perfecting our devotional service, we can immediately transfer ourselves to the spiritual world."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes by inviting listeners to return to Krishna in the spiritual world, emphasizing the miseries of the material world and the possibility of achieving spiritual bliss through devotional service and chanting Hare Krishna.

And live with Krishna as one of his associates in a loving relationship. Each one of us is God with Krishna. Krishna wants to attract us. Why are you suffering in this material world? This material world is filled with miseries. It's the nature of this material world. We don't belong here. Foolishly, ignorant people may think that even if there are miseries, let me make adjustment and learn to live and try to enjoy the pleasures available in this world, the material pleasures available. But Bhagavad Gita says material pleasures are not real pleasures. They are simply due to some sense contact producing some sensation, and it is wrongly interpreted as material pleasure. But actually it is simply different kind of distress. It is filled with distresses, this material existence, and different kinds of distresses are interpreted. Or if there is a relief from some distress that is thought of as happiness, pleasure, material pleasure. So we are entitled to pure spiritual bliss of the kind that Krishna enjoys with his associates. And in the Bhagavad Gita, Krishna is inviting, come, come to me, live with me, enjoy with me, and that too in a loving relationship. Krishna is one. Living entities are countless, but that doesn't mean that Krishna is unavailable for some insignificant living being. No. Krishna is available for each and every living being in the spiritual world. Why spiritual world? Even the material world. He has expanded as Paramatma in the heart of everyone, of each one of us. He is available for each one of us, even in the material world. But material world is not direct. Spiritual world is direct relationship, directly available. So Krishna invites us. Please come to the spiritual world and simply by chanting Hare Krishna and perfecting our devotional service, we can immediately transfer ourselves to the spiritual world.

---

Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya Naram Chai Vanarottamam Deviim Saraswatiim Vyasam Tato Jaya Mudhirayet. In this session, we will be discussing from the 31st verse of the 11th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. ### Krishna, expansion, potency
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2HmuM_oKY3A?t=111
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna expanded himself into 16,108 forms and simultaneously he entered each of the palaces."
**Summary:** The speaker explains Krishna's ability to expand into multiple forms to be with each of his queens simultaneously, emphasizing his supreme and unlimited nature.

He expanded himself into 16,108 forms and simultaneously he entered each of the palaces. So when he was in his home, he is available to each and every queen simultaneously, unlike the living entities of this world or even of the spiritual world. At least in this world, a yogi can expand himself into maximum 10 forms. Krishna expanded himself into as many forms as required. So therefore, nobody can imitate Krishna, nobody can duplicate Krishna. So Krishna is the supreme unlimited personality of Godhead. And the queens, when they saw the supreme lord, their husband, their beloved husband entering the palace, they at once got up from their seats and meditations, but they were also shy, so they were very shy.

### plenary expansion, spiritual identity, potency
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2HmuM_oKY3A?t=201
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The Lord entered his home palaces occupied by 16,108 queens. This means the Lord at once expanded himself in as many forms as possible."
**Summary:** The speaker quotes Srila Prabhupada's explanation of Krishna's plenary expansions, emphasizing that each expansion possesses full power and spiritual identity, highlighting the difference between plenary and separated portions.

So Srila Prabhupada explains elaborately in the purport a very important point. The Lord entered his home palaces occupied by 16,108 queens. This means the Lord at once expanded himself in as many forms as possible. The Lord entered his home palaces occupied by 16,108 queens. This means the Lord at once expanded himself in as much as he could. The Lord at once expanded himself in as much as he could, as many plenary expansions as there were queens and palaces and entered in each and every one of them simultaneously and separately. Here is another manifestation of the feature of his internal potency. He can expand himself in as many forms of spiritual identity as he desires, even though he is one without a second. God is one, but that one Supreme Lord expands himself in plenary expansions. Plenary means with full power in each of the forms that he expands. His form is power is not diminished. It is confirmed in a Shruti mantra that the absolute is one alone, a come a va-advitiya, and yet he becomes many as soon as he so desires. These may be the same as the other shrutis and shrutis do not have the soul. These may be the same as the other shrutis and shrutis do not have the soul manifold expansions of the Supreme Lord are manifested as plenary and separated portions. The separated portions are representations of his energy and the plenary portions are manifestations of his personality. The technical point.

---
### plenary expansion, separated expansion, Yogeshwara
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2HmuM_oKY3A?t=305
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna expands himself, his own personality in plenary portions. Full power each of the plenary expansions."
**Summary:** The speaker differentiates between plenary and separated expansions of Krishna, explaining that plenary expansions (like Rama, Narasimha) have full power, while separated expansions are energies with varying degrees of power. He also introduces the term 'Yogeshwara' to describe Krishna's unlimited potency.

Krishna expands himself, his own personality in plenary portions. Full power each of the plenary expansions, like Krishna, Rama, Narasimha, Varaha, Narayana, Govinda. All of them have same power. All of them are the Supreme Lord. The same Supreme Lord. One and only Supreme Lord. Whereas he has got energies and expansions of the energies are called separated expansions. They are not plenary expansions. That means there is gradation of power in each of the expansions of the energy. As much as is required for the purpose of the expansion of the energy, only that much power will be present in the expanded portion. Separated expansion. Thus the personality of God had manifested himself in 16,108 plenary expansions and simultaneously entered into each and every one of the palaces of the Queens. This is called Vaibhava or the Transcendental Potency of the Lord. This is the technical word Vaibhava. It means this is the spiritual potency or transcendental potency of the Lord. Because he can do so, he is known as Yogeshwara. In the level of the Lord, he is known as Yogeshwara. In the eleventh chapter, it is described Krishna as Yogeshwara. 18th chapter.

---
### Yogeshwara, unlimited potency, marginal potency
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2HmuM_oKY3A?t=414
**Verse References:** BG 18.78
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Never make this mistake. Don't think Krishna is like another ordinary living being. No, he is the supreme lord, infinitely powerful, infinitely expanded."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes Krishna's unlimited potency as Yogeshwara, contrasting it with the limited abilities of ordinary yogis. He cautions against equating Krishna with ordinary beings and introduces the concept of 'tatastha shakti' (marginal potency) from which living beings are expanded.

Yatra Yogeshwara Krishna yatra partho dhanur dharaha tatra sri vijayo bhutir dhruvanitir matir mama. Krishna is Yogeshwara. He has got unlimited siddhis, unlimited siddhis. Ordinarily, a yogi or mystic living being is able to expand himself at the most to tenfold expansions of his body, at most ten expansions, but the lord can do so to the extent of as many thousands or infinitely as he likes. Unbelievers become astonished to learn that Lord Krishna married more than 16,000 queens because they think of Lord Krishna as one of them and measure the potency of the Lord by their own limited potency. Never make this mistake. Don't think Krishna is like another ordinary living being. No, he is the supreme lord, infinitely powerful, infinitely expanded. One should therefore know that the Lord is never on the level of the living beings and these living beings are but expansions of his marginal potency. Krishna has got one potency called tatastha shakti, marginal potency, and living beings are expansions of this tatastha shakti. One should also never equalize the potent and the potency. Krishna is the potent, the possessor of the potency, and the potency is the energy of Krishna, the potency of Krishna, energy of Krishna. So don't equalize the possessor of the potency.

---
### potent and potency, transcendental pastimes, God is great
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2HmuM_oKY3A?t=530
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "God is great is eternal truth."
**Summary:** The speaker continues to explain the relationship between the potent (Krishna) and his potency, emphasizing that they are qualitatively similar but should not be equalized. He mentions the transcendental pastimes (leelas) exchanged between them and reiterates the greatness of God.

All the potencies, Krishna, one supreme lord with the potency itself, don't equalize. As far as the quality is concerned, there is not much difference between the quality of the potent and the potency. Both are spiritual. The queens were also expansions of his internal potency, internal potency, the queens. And thus the potent and the potencies are perpetually exchanging transcendental pleasures known as the pastimes of the lord, Krishna leela, Ram leela, Narsimha leela, Varaha leela. That is the term used only for the lord, his transcendental pastimes exchanged between the potency and the potent. One should not therefore become astonished to learn that the lord married so many wives. On the contrary, one should affirm that even the lord lord marries 16,000 million wives, he is not completely manifesting his unlimited and inexhaustible potency because he is unlimited. That's a fact. He is not completely manifesting his unlimited and inexhaustible potency because he is unlimited. That's a fact. That's a fact. He married only sixteen thousand wives and entered in each and every one of them in different palaces just to impress in the history of the human beings on the surface of the earth that the lord is never equal to or less than any human being, however powerful a human being might be. No one therefore is either equal to or greater than the lord. The lord is always great in all respects. God is great is eternal truth. Therefore, as soon.

### meditation, devotion, Vedic culture
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2HmuM_oKY3A?t=654
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Even in Krishna is away from the palace, the Queen's are engaged in meditating on Krishna constantly."
**Summary:** The speaker describes the queens' constant meditation on Krishna, even during his absence, highlighting their deep devotion. He also touches upon Vedic injunctions for women whose husbands are away and the importance of a wife presenting herself in a happy and joyous mood.

As soon as the Queen saw from a distance their husband was away from home for long periods due to the Battle of Kurukshetra, they all arose from the slumber of meditation and prepared to receive their most beloved. See, even in Krishna is away from the palace, the Queen's are engaged in meditating on Krishna constantly. They cannot forget even for a fraction of a moment. None they're so attached. They're so pleased to always think of him. Krishna says that in the Bhagavad Gita, man mana, always think of me. So the Queen's and all the devotees, not only the Queen's, all the devotees in the spiritual world are always thinking of Krishna every moment, thinking of Krishna and thereby being very much satisfied and full of bliss, transcendental pure bliss. According to Yajnavalkya's religious injunctions, a woman whose husband is away from home should not take part in his social functions, should not decorate her body, should not laugh, and should not go to any relatives house in any circumstance. This is a Vedic culture, scriptural directions also. This is the vow of the ladies whose husbands are away from home. At the same time, it is also enjoined that a wife should never present herself before the husband in unclean state. She must decorate herself with ornaments and good dress and should always be present before the husband in a happy and joyous mood.

### meditation, devotion, gopis
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2HmuM_oKY3A?t=774
**Verse References:** —

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the queens' constant meditation on Krishna, comparing them to the gopis of Vrindavan who were always absorbed in thoughts of Krishna. He emphasizes that true devotees cannot live without meditating on the Lord.

So what about the queens of Dwarka? It is explained the queens of Lord Krishna were all in meditation, thinking of the Lord's absence and were always meditating upon him. The Lord's devotees cannot live even for one moment without meditating on the Lord, and what to speak of the queens who are all goddesses of fortune incarnated as queens in the past times of the Lord and Dwarka, are all Lakshmi. They can never be separated from the Lord either by presence or by trance. When Krishna is personally present, they are with Krishna, and when Krishna is away physically away, then they are always with Lord by meditation, completely absorbed in thinking of him. Only now similarly the gopis at Vrindavan could not forget the Lord when the Lord was away in the forest cowherding. When the Lord boy Krishna was absent from the village, the gopis at home used to worry about his traversing the rough ground with his soft lotus feet. By thinking thus, they were sometimes overwhelmed in trance and mortified in the heart. They feel for Krishna within their heart every moment when he's away from home from the village. Such is the condition of the pure associates of the Lord. They are always in trance and so the queens also were in trance during the absence of the Lord from Dwarka. Presently having seen the Lord from a distance, they at once gave up all their engagements including the vows as women described above.

## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.11.32-33 | 22-07-2022
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### devotion, conjugal love, Krishna
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/kcyhwydg42Q?t=35
**Verse References:** SB 1.11.32
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The embrace of the eyes is more effective in the conjugal relation."
**Summary:** This segment focuses on Suta Goswami's description of the queens of Dwarka's reaction to seeing Lord Krishna after a long absence, emphasizing their restrained yet profound expressions of love through glances, internal embrace, and sending their sons.

Hare Krishna, Hare Krishna, Krishna Krishna, Hare Hare, Hare Rama, Hare Rama, Rama Rama, Hare Hare, Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya, Narayanam Namaskritya, Naram Chai Vanarottamam, Devim Sarasvatim Vyasam Tato Jayam Udhirayet. In this session we will be discussing from the 32nd verse and the 33rd verse of the 11th chapter of the 1st canto Srimad Bhagavatam. The 32nd verse is as follows. The meaning of this verse is, So this is spoken by Suta Goswami. He is addressing the sages of Naimisharanya headed by Shaunaka, who is described here as Bruguvarya, chief amongst the Brugus. That's addressing Shaunaka Rishi, who is the chief of the Brugus. The explanation here is, the ecstasy they experienced as soon as they saw the Lord and their natural reaction was to embrace him. So first they embraced him within the innermost recesses of their hearts, and then they embraced him visually, and then they sent their sons to embrace him, which is equal to personally embracing him. Now after all this, they tried to restrain their feelings by not shedding tears, but they could not avoid that. They inadvertently shed tears. These are tears of joy on seeing their beloved Lord returned to the past. Those tears, impassioned with his love and respect. This is a very important case for the Lord. So, how can this be fair? You know, Shaunaka and his wife were inside a palace after a long period of absence. So, Srila Prabhupada explains this in the Purport. Although due to feminine shyness, there were many hindrances to embracing the dear husband, Lord Sri Krishna, the Queens performed that act by seeing him through the eyes, by putting him in the course of their hearts. As soon as they saw the impression of the Lord within the core of their heart, these were their instincts. Core of their heart and by sending their sons to embrace him still the act remained unfinished and tears rolled down their cheeks despite all endeavors to check them. So Srila Prabhupada further explains here one indirectly embraces the husband by sending the son to embrace him because the son is developed as part of the mother's body. The embrace of the son is not exactly the embrace of husband and wife from the sexual point of view but the embraces satisfaction from the affectionate point of view. The embrace of the eyes is more effective in the conjugal relation and thus according to Srila Jeeva Goswami there is nothing wrong in such an exchange of feeling between husband and wife. So this is the explanation for the meeting of the queens with their husband Lord Krishna, the king of Dwarka.
