### leadership, charity, sacrifice, liberation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/IDHGsMkiGdo?t=85
**Verse References:** BG 9.22
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "That is the responsibility of a ruler: not only he should ensure that people are happy and there is peace and there are no miscreants disturbing saintly persons or weaker sections of society, at the same time he has to also engage the people in acts of sacrifice so that they are ultimately liberated form material bondage."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on Srila Prabhupada's purport, explaining that a king's duty is to ensure the happiness and protection of his citizens, engage them in spiritual activities, and ultimately guide them towards liberation from material bondage.

The two comparisons are made. So Srila Prabhupada explains in the purport: A king becomes famous by his acts of charity, performances of yajnas, protection of the surrendered, etc. So three different aspects are specifically mentioned about Pariksit Maharaj when he grows up to be the king. Pariksit Maharaj is the king of the Ushinara country. Emperor, he will be famous for his acts of charity. A king, if he is qualified as mentioned in the Bhagavad Gita, he should have the qualifications after having been trained and after having assimilated the knowledge, the skill, the proper understanding, and such a king is able to rule the people, the whole kingdom in such a way that people will be happy when the king is ruling and at the end people will be able to get liberation from material bondage. That is the responsibility of a ruler. Not only he should ensure that people are happy and there is peace and there are no miscreants disturbing saintly persons or weaker sections of society, at the same time he has to also engage the people in acts of sacrifice so that they are ultimately liberated form material bondage. Otherwise, if there is no such endeavor for engaging in spiritual activities, then people simply continue the cycle of birth and death.

---
### responsibility, spiritual activities, surrender
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/IDHGsMkiGdo?t=195
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So it is a king's responsibility as the representative of the Supreme Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the king's role as a representative of the Supreme Lord, highlighting the importance of engaging people in spiritual activities to break the cycle of birth and death. He also mentions the three qualifications of an ideal king: charity, performing yajna, and protecting surrendered souls.

Otherwise, if there is no such endeavor for engaging in spiritual activities, then people simply continue the cycle of birth and death and continue to suffer life after life after life after life after life. So it is a king's responsibility as the representative of the Supreme Lord. So here it is said that Srila Prabhupada three specific qualifications of a ideal king. He should be well trained to perform acts of charity, dana. He should perform yajna. He should himself perform yajna for the welfare of the whole kingdom and he should engage others in performing yajna according to their position, according to their duty as enjoined in the scriptures and as taught by the scriptures. The Supreme Lord and his representatives, the bona fide spiritual masters and the king should also give protection to the surrendered souls. This is also a king's duty, responsibility, it's one of his qualifications.

---
### protection, surrender, Ishwara Bhava
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/IDHGsMkiGdo?t=268
**Verse References:** BG 18.55, BG 9.31
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So Ishwara Bhava means factual power to give protection in a righteous cause when the citizens are upright in their behavior."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses the concept of Ishwara Bhava, the attitude of a king to protect surrendered souls, drawing from the Bhagavad Gita. He emphasizes the importance of protecting righteous citizens from miscreants and highlights the Lord's promise to protect his devotees.

Now Srila Prabhupada particularly says here, a Kshatriya king is proud to give protection to the surrendered souls. And this is specifically mentioned in the Bhagavad Gita. This attitude of a king is called Ishwara Bhava. Ishwara Bhava is mentioned in the Bhagavad Gita in the 18th chapter. So Ishwara Bhava means factual power to give protection in a righteous cause when the citizens are upright in their behavior. In their conduct. Then there should be protection by the king to such righteous people for a righteous cause. They should not be overpowered by miscreants, by demons, by materialists, by all kinds of ruffians. No. Then the king is not a capable person, neither he is doing his duty, nor he is going to help the people in ultimately attaining liberation. So in the Bhagavad Gita, Lord instructs living beings to surrender unto him. And the Lord promises all protection. The Lord is all-powerful and true to his word and therefore he never fails to give protection to his different devotees.

---

The Lord never fails to give protection to his different devotees. He says Kaunteya Pratijaanihi Name Bhakta Pranasyati. So that particular instance from the scriptures is narrated by Srila Prabhupada in the purport. Maharaj Shibi was the king of Ushinara, a particular province. And Maharaj Shibi was the intimate friend of Maharaj Jayati. Jayati was a famous king in the former times, same time. And Maharaj Jayati was able to reach the heavenly planets along with Maharaj Shibi.

### protection, sacrifice, Maharaj Shibi, Indra, Agni

**Verse References:** —

**Summary:** The speaker narrates the famous story of Maharaj Shibi, Indra, and Agni, where Shibi protects a pigeon from an eagle, demonstrating his commitment to protecting surrendered souls.

So this Shibi he became a very famous king as a protector of surrendered souls and as a donor of charities. So it is said here that the king of heaven Indra once took the shape of a pigeon hunter bird, an eagle. Indra took the form of an eagle. And Agni, the fire god, took the shape of a pigeon. So, eagle and pigeon, actually they are disguised. Indra is disguised as eagle and Agni is disguised as pigeon. The pigeon while being chased by the eagle took the shelter of the lap of Maharaj Shibi. The pigeon came and landed on Shibi's lap. And the hunter eagle wanted the pigeon back from the king. So, King Shibi, since this pigeon had taken shelter of King Shibi, the king did not want that eagle to take away this pigeon. Kill it and eat the flesh. So, the king wanted to give some other meat to eat for the eagle. And he requested that eagle, please do not kill this pigeon because it has taken shelter of me. I should be giving it protection. So, do not kill this pigeon. You want some flesh, I will give you some other flesh.

**Summary:** The lecture continues the discussion of Srimad Bhagavatam 1.12.20, focusing on the prophecy about Parikshit and comparing him to famous figures like Maharaj Shibi and Bharata, son of Dushyanta.

In this session we will continue to discuss from the twentieth verse of the twelfth chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. The previous session we discussed one part of the purport of this twentieth verse. In this session we will discuss the second part of the purport of this twentieth verse. Just to refresh your memory, the twentieth verse is as follows. So the meaning of this twentieth verse is: This child will be a munificent donor of charity and protector of the surrendered like the famous king Shastra, and he will expand the name and fame of his family like Bharata, the son of Maharaj Dushyanta. In a previous session we discussed about Maharaj Shibi. Now we will discuss about Bharata, the son of Maharaj Dushyanta.

### history, lineage, comparison
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/cDXJGgPfPWY?t=126
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "There are many Bharatas in history of which three are very famous."
**Summary:** Srila Prabhupada briefly mentions the history of Bharata, the son of Maharaj Dushyanta, noting that there are three famous Bharatas: Dasharathi Bharata, Bharata the son of King Rishabha, and Bharata the son of Maharaj Dushyanta.

So in the purport Srila Prabhupada briefly mentions the history of this Bharata, the son of Maharaj Dushyanta. First of all Srila Prabhupada says there are many Bharatas in history of which three are very famous. Three Bharatas are famous. Who are the three Bharatas who are very famous? The first one is the Bharata who is the brother of the king, the brother of Lord Ramachandra, who is actually the son of Dasharatha Maharaj, that Bharata. You can say Dasharathi Bharata. The second Bharata who is famous in history, Vedic history is Bharata, the son of king Rishabha. The Lord appeared as Rishabhadeva, king Rishabhadeva, emperor of the world, and he had one hundred sons. Of these one hundred sons, the eldest one was Bharata Maharaj. That Bharata Maharaj, he renounced his kingdom at a very young age, practiced very nice austerities and became advanced spiritually. But towards the end of his life, when he was performing tapasya, after retiring from his kingdom, he got attached to a deer and he had to take a deer's body in his next life. Even though he was spiritually very advanced and he was given remembrance of his spiritual activities by Krishna within his heart, even in the body of a deer, and he was very, very cautious not to get misled by any material, even in the body of a deer. And then he was again given a chance to take birth in a brahmana family. And in that life, third life, he perfected himself and went back to God. So that Bharata is also famous, the second Bharata. And the third Bharata who is famous is mentioned in this verse, Bharata, the son of Maharaj Dushyanta. He is also very famous. So, all these Bharatas are historically known to the universe.

There are millions of universes in this material creation. And apart from this material creation, there is spiritual world which is not created, which is eternal. So all these are described in the Vedic literature, the Vedic scriptures. So the Vedic scriptures give us knowledge which is comprehensive and vast as compared to the body of knowledge that we have in our modern day. So that is because the Pandavas are born in the dynasty of the famous Maharaj Bharata, who was the son of King Dushyanta. That's why the Pandavas are called Bharata or the members of the Kuru dynasty also. Even Dhritarashtra is addressed as Bharata by Sanjaya in the Bhagavad Gita. So that name Bharata for the descendant of Bharata, the son of King Dushyanta. So this Bharata was famous for performing many many sacrifices. A king should perform sacrifices for the sake of the welfare of the whole kingdom. Because in the Bhagavad Gita it is explained that annad bhavanti bhutani parjanyad anna sambhavah yajnyad bhavati parjanyo yajna karma samutbhavah.

### sacrifice, cycle of nature, dependence
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/cDXJGgPfPWY?t=746
**Verse References:** BG 3.14
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Rainfall is dependent on proper performance of yajña."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the cycle of nature, emphasizing the dependence of living beings on food grains, food grains on rainfall, and rainfall on the proper performance of yajña, as described in the Bhagavad Gita.

There is a cycle that is operating in this earth planet. What is that? That all living beings subsist on food grains directly or indirectly. The vegetarians directly subsist on food grains. But the non-vegetarians they subsist on animals who subsist on food grains. So therefore directly or indirectly all living beings are dependent on food grains. And the food grains are due to rains, proper rainfall. We know that when there is excessive rain or there is scarcity of rain then there is no proper production of grains. So production of grains, sufficient quantity of grains for all creatures, humans and the animals and the other birds etc. depends on proper rainfall. Because we cannot manufacture food grains in factories and industries and all kinds of scientific research. We cannot manufacture food grains. It has to be grown on the land, fertile land. And even if the land is fertile, unless there is proper rainfall, you cannot get sufficient grains production. So now the rains are dependent on yajñād bhavati parjanyo. Parjanyo means rainfall. Rainfall is dependent on proper performance of yajña. So yajña performance by the human beings ensures adequate rainfall, not too much, not too little. And if there is proper rainfall, there is plenty of grain production. And that grain production will nourish all creatures on this earth planet, human beings and the lower creatures also, everyone. And this is the arrangement of the Supreme Lord. Yajña karma samudbhavaha.

---
### duty, sacrifice, leadership
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/cDXJGgPfPWY?t=875
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, General public
**Key Quote:** "The foremost person to perform yajña is the king, the ruler, the leader of all the people in the world."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes that yajña means performing prescribed duties for the welfare of the entire population, and that the king or leader should perform yajña to ensure prosperity for all, as foretold by the brahmanas to King Yudhishthira about Parikshit.

Yajña means to perform prescribed duties, not self-assumed duties. Nowadays people think, oh my duty is something, according to what I think is my duty. No. Duty is something that is prescribed in this jastra for the overall welfare of the entire population of this world. Otherwise, by artificial means, we cannot get our necessities. Not possible. There will only be a failure of the administrators to provide for the necessities of the people of the country by artificial methods is not going to work. So Bhagavad Gita says the natural method is they should perform yajña. And the foremost person to perform yajña is the king, the ruler, the leader of all the people in the world. He should perform yajña. And when he performs yajña, there is prosperity for all the people of the country. And this Dushyanta was famous for having performed many sacrifices. Similarly, Pariksit also will perform sacrifices for the welfare of the entire kingdom. And there will be prosperity because of this child growing up to rule the whole world. That is what is being foretold by the brahmanas to King Yudhishthira. I will stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

**Verse References:** SB 1.12.21
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Amongst great bowmen this child will be as good as Arjuna."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the 21st verse of the 12th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam, focusing on the prophecy that Pariksit will be a great bowman like Arjuna, emphasizing the duties of a king to protect the innocent and subdue miscreants.

In this session we will be discussing from 21st verse of the 12th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. This will be part 1 of this verse which will be discussed. Dhanvinam Agraneer Esha Tulyas Charjunayor Dwayoho Hutasha Iva Durdharsaha Samudra Iva Dustaraha. The meaning of this verse is amongst great bowmen this child will be as good as Arjuna. He will be as irresistible as fire and as fire, unsurpassable as the ocean. So three comparisons are made in this verse. He will be a great bowman like Arjuna. And specifically there is mention of two Arjunas here. Arjunayor Dwayoho, two Arjunas. Prabhupada mentions in the purport in history there are two famous Arjunas. One is Kartyavirya Arjuna, who was the king of Haihaya, and the other Arjuna is the famous grandfather of King Pariksit, who is Arjuna the Pandava. Both the Arjunas are famous for their bowmanship, and the child Pariksit is foretold to be equal to both of them particularly in fighting. King should be powerful fighter to subdue the miscreants, to protect the innocent, to give protection to the brahmanas, the women, the children, the saintly persons. Otherwise if the king is not powerful enough then these miscreants and rogues and ruffians and thieves will plunder and loot from the innocent people. There will be chaos. So Pariksit is foretold to be equal to both these Arjunas who are very very very powerful in fighting.

### lineage, divine birth, samskaras
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Gk1Ub1IeSQ8?t=192
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Arjuna is not an ordinary person."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the lineage and divine birth of Arjuna, highlighting his connection to Indra, his various names, and the ceremonies (samskaras) performed at his birth by Kashyapa Muni sent by Vasudeva.

Now Srila Prabhupada gives a description of Pandava Arjuna, the Arjuna who is the grandfather of this Pariksit. Arjuna's son was Abhimanyu. Abhimanyu's son was Pariksit. In this way Pandava Arjuna is the grandfather of Pariksit. So Prabhupada mentions Pandava Arjuna is the great hero of the Bhagavad Gita. Bhagavad Gita was spoken by Krishna to Arjuna on the battlefield of Kurukshetra, and that's how we know Bhagavad Gita as the most famous scripture. It gives us the essence of the Vedic wisdom. Arjuna is the kshatriya son of Maharaj. Arjuna is the son of Pandu. Queen Kunti Devi could call for any one of the demigods, and thus she called Indra, because Kunti could not beget children through Pandu. So it is allowed in the former ages that a lady, chaste woman, chaste wife, can beget children through the husband's brother if the husband is unable to conceive children in his own wife. So this is called Devarayana Sutodhpakti, and this is prohibited in Kali Yuga. But they were, Kunti and Pandu were living in Dwapar Yuga. So in Dwapar Yuga, Kunti Devi, she had one mantra given by Durvasamuni, and thus she could call any one of the devatas to conceive a child for Pandu. So Kunti called on Indra, and Arjuna was born. So Arjuna is therefore a plenary part of the heavenly king Indra. Arjuna is not an ordinary person. He was. Arjuna has many names. He was born in the month of Phalguna, therefore he is also called Phalguni when he appeared as the son of Kunti. His future greatness is proclaimed by air messages. This is the fame of Arjuna, even at the time of his birth. It is declared through air messages that he is a great personality who has taken birth, and all the important personalities from different parts of the universe, such as the devatas, the demigods, the Gandharvas, the Adityas from the sun globe, the Rudras, the Vasus, the Nagas, the different Rishis of importance, the Apsaras, all attended the ceremony of the birth of Arjuna. So very important personality in the whole universe, Arjuna. The Apsaras pleased everyone by their heavenly dances and songs. Vasudeva, the father of Lord Krishna, and the maternal uncle of Arjuna. Arjuna's mother is Kunti. Kunti's brother is Vasudeva. Therefore Vasudeva, the father of Lord Krishna, is actually the maternal uncle of Arjuna. Vasudeva sent his priest, representative Kashyapa, to purify Arjuna by all the prescribed samskaras or reformatory processes. So Arjuna's samskaras were done by Kashyapa Muni or Kashyapa Rishi sent by Vasudeva.

In this way, Dronacharya forecasted the future battle of Kurukshetra in which Dronacharya was going to fight. Dronacharya was on the opposite side of Arjuna. ### Arjuna, Bowmanship, Comparison
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/fh6Zhz5UgWs?t=32
**Verse References:** SB 1.12.21
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "In history there are two Arjunas."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the second portion of verse 21 from the 12th chapter of the 1st canto, explaining that the verse compares a child to Arjuna, and Srila Prabhupada notes there are two Arjunas: Kartya Viri Arjuna and the grandfather of Pariksit.

Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya Naram Jai Vanarottamam Deviim Sarasvatim Vyasam Tato Jayam Udi Rayet. In this session, we will be discussing the 21st verse of the 12th chapter of the 1st canto. We already discussed the first portion of this verse. In this session, we will be discussing the second portion of this verse. Just to refresh your memory, the verse itself is as follows: Dhanvinam Agraneer Esha Tulyascha Arjuna Yordva Yoho Hutasha Iva Durdharsaha Samudra Iva Dustaraha. The meaning of this 21st verse is amongst great bowmen this child will be as good as Arjuna. He will be as irresistible as fire and as unsurpassable as the ocean. So Srila Prabhupada explains in the purport that in history there are two Arjunas. That's why the mention is made Arjuna Yordva Yoho, the two Arjunas. One is called Kartya Viri Arjuna, the king of Haihaya. He is very famous in history. He has a great king, powerful king and the other is the grandfather of Pariksit, Arjuna, the grandfather of Pariksit, the Pandava, Arjuna and both of them are famous for being expert in fighting in bowmanship. Dhanvinam, the great bowman. Dhanvinam, the great bowman. Dhanvinam, the great bowman. Dhanvinam, the great bowman. Dhanvinam, the great bowman. So, Srila Prabhupada has elaborately described the personality of the Pandava Arjuna.

### Friendship, Pride, Insult
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/fh6Zhz5UgWs?t=261
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Friendship is always between equals."
**Summary:** The speaker narrates the story of Drupada and Dronacharya's friendship during their Gurukul days, Drupada's promise to share his kingdom, and the eventual insult Dronacharya faced when he sought help from the now-rich King Drupada.

The history is that Drupada and Dronacharya, when they were young students, they studied in the same Guruko, and they were good friends in the Guruko. But this friendship was between the rich son of the rich Kshatriya. Drupada was the son of a very powerful and rich Kshatriya, and Dronacharya was the son of a poor Brahmana. So when Drupada grew up, he became the king of the province called Panchaladesha, very rich. And Dronacharya grew up, but he was just a poor Brahmana's son. So he was another poor Brahmana. Now during the Gurukul days, because they were very good friends, once Drupada, on a certain occasion, he promised to Dronacharya, when you grow up, don't worry. I will give you half of my kingdom because I am the only son of my father. So I will give you half of my kingdom. Don't worry. You don't have to suffer in poverty like your father. So Dronacharya took these words of Drupada to heart, and he always remembered, even when they went back to their own country, when they went back to their own home after completing the Gurukul education. And Dronacharya was living in utter poverty. So after he got married and he had a son, Ashwatthama, small boy, the boy one day began to cry to his mother that I have not tasted what is real milk. All my friends say milk is very, very nourishing and very, very tasty and very nice to drink, but I don't know what is milk. So Dronacharya's wife, Ashwatthama's mother, she felt very bad that they were in such poverty stricken condition, that they could not feed their child cow's milk. So she told her husband Dronacharya that we are so poor. We cannot even feed our child some cow's milk. So Dronacharya said don't worry. I will go to my friend, my friend from Gurukul days, Maharaj Drupada, and I will beg from him in charity a cow. He has promised to give me half his kingdom, but I don't want his wealth in that way. But I will just ask for a cow donation. So he set out from his place to visit Maharaj Drupada in Panchala Desha, and because he was a brahmana, there was no restriction for brahmanas going and meeting kings. So he was let in, and when he came in presence of Drupada, Drupada looked at him. Who are you? So then he said you don't remember me. I am your friend from Gurukul days. Then Drupada somehow, he thought it is an insult that a wretched poor person is claiming friendship with a rich and powerful king. So then Drupada, he told Drona that friendship is always between equals. So you are a poor and wretched poverty stricken person. How can you be my friend? What is my status and what is your status? So Dronacharya was feeling very very insulted that this Drupada has become very proud of his power and his position and his wealth. I am going to teach him a lesson. So thinking, he did not utter a single word. He just walked back to his own place.

**Summary:** Dronacharya, insulted by Drupada, becomes the teacher of the Pandavas and Kauravas. After their education, he sends them to challenge and capture Drupada, leading to Drupada's humiliation and the division of his kingdom. Despite this, Dronacharya imparts military secrets to Drupada's son.

Without uttering a single word. Later on, Dronacharya was appointed as the teacher for teaching the Pandavas and the Kauravas by Bhishma. And when he taught the Pandavas and Kauravas, after their education was over, then he remembered Drupada's insult. So he sent his students, the Pandavas and Kauravas, to challenge Drupada, fight against him, and bring him captive without killing him. So the Kauravas tried first, and they were unable to defeat Drupada. Then the Pandavas went to challenge Drupada. They fought against him, and Arjuna brought him captive, alive, in front of Dronacharya, Arjuna's teacher, professor. So Drupada felt very very very very insulted that he was defeated by the students of Dronacharya. Somehow, he had become, Drupada had become inimical against Drona. So Drupada, when he was brought, so Dronacharya said, do you remember me? Do you recognize who I am? So Drupada felt very very insulted. But Dronacharya said that I want to take away your kingdom. After all, you are my friend from Gurukul days. So I just want half your kingdom. So I take half your kingdom, taking away all my property, though you are entitled to it because your students have defeated me. So I am grateful to you for not taking away my entire kingdom of me family. I have the to you take all my family from my country to your capital who be in my land to you take. Dronacharya was powerful enough to kill Drona himself. Dronacharya never hesitated to impart all the military secrets to this boy because he was a Kshatriya and he had come seeking training in the military arts.

### Swayamvara, Incognito, Marriage
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/fh6Zhz5UgWs?t=886
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "When the Pandavas heard about Drupada's deciding to hold a Swayamvara ceremony for marrying his daughter the Pandavas went there for that ceremony."
**Summary:** The speaker describes the Pandavas' escape from the shellac house, their incognito life, and Drupada's decision to hold a Swayamvara for Draupadi. Arjuna, disguised as a brahmana, wins the Swayamvara, and Drupada is overjoyed to learn his true identity.

House shellac house into which the Pandavas were sent and before Duryodhana could set fire the Pandavas escaped through a secret tunnel they dug in the earth and when they were living incognito after escaping that is when Drupada decided to hold a Swayamvara ceremony for marrying Draupadi and he arranged Draupadi's personal selection of a groom who could pierce the eye of a fish hanging on the ceiling. This trick was made especially because only Arjuna could do it and he somehow thought that Arjuna was killed in that fire of the shellac house but actually the Pandavas had escaped. When the Pandavas heard about Drupada's deciding to hold a Swayamvara ceremony for marrying his daughter the Pandavas went there for that ceremony. Of course they were living incognito as brahmanas. They were dressed as some poor brahmanas. So they went there and when all the other Kshatriya kings were there they were dressed as some poor brahmanas. When the Pandavas heard that the Kshatriya kings failed to pass the test set by Drupada then Arjuna in the guise of a poor brahmana he passed the test and Draupadi was garlanded sorry Draupadi garlanded Arjuna though she did not know he was Arjuna. And later on Lord Krishna disclosed Arjuna's identity to Balaram and when Drupada came to know that this poor brahmana is Arjuna he was very very very happy to marry his daughter to Arjuna. So I will stop here. We will continue in the next session.

In this session we will continue discussing the 21st verse of the 12th chapter of the 1st Kanto Srimad Bhagavatam. This is part 3 of that same verse. The third part this is based on the purport by Srila Prabhupada describing the history of Pandava Arjuna. Dhanvinam agraniresha tulyas charjuna yo dvayoha, hutasya iva durdharsaha, samudra iva dustaraha. The meaning of this verse is amongst great bowmen this child will be as good as Arjuna. He will be as irresistible as fire and as unsurpassed. These are words spoken by the brahmanas who are preparing the horoscope of the child newly born child Parikshit and at such a time they read the horoscope and foretell the future of the child because Yudhishthira was anxious that this only surviving descendant of the entire Kuru dynasty. Will he be alive? Will he be as worthy as his forefathers? That was his anxiety. So the brahmanas assured him this child is a highly qualified child. So he was specially protected by Krishna when he was in his mother's womb and he is going to be an excellent because somebody who is a pure devotee is qualified with all wonderful qualifications yasyasthi bhakti bhagavati akinchana sarvair gunaistatra samasate suraha.

### purification, bhakti yoga, chanting
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/oc2KOt71XRg?t=182
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "That you simply chant Hare Krishna, purify your heart of all materials, purify your heart of all material contamination and become first-class devotees eligible to become free from material bondage and go to the spiritual world."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that every soul has good qualities covered by material contamination, and the process of purification, especially through Bhakti Yoga and chanting Hare Krishna, can reveal these qualities and lead to liberation.

This statement is in the Bhagavatam that a pure devotee has all good qualities. Actually every living being a spirit soul has all good qualities but it's only because we are covered by the material contamination that our good qualities get covered and the contamination which brings forth all kinds of bad qualities. So as spirit soul, nobody is a bad person. No living being is a bad person, a spirit soul. But we have to purify our consciousness and become free from all the contaminating bad qualities, which are a result of the material contamination in contact with material nature. So therefore the Bhagavad Gita, Krishna says that by practicing yoga, Yogam Atma Vishuddhi, you purify your heart of all the dirty things, then the good qualities will shine forth from a purified heart. And the process of purification is the process of yoga. But among the yoga processes, the best process is the process of Bhakti Yoga. And that Bhakti Yoga also has been simplified further. Among the different... Yoga system, Bhakti Yoga is the easiest. But even among the different practices of Bhakti Yoga, the simplest practice of chanting the holy name of Krishna, Hare Krishna, Hare Krishna, Krishna Krishna, Hare Hare, Hare Rama, Hare Rama, Rama Rama, Hare Hare, has been especially taught by Sri Chaitanya Mahaprabhu for the fallen souls of this Kali Yuga. That you simply chant Hare Krishna, purify your heart of all materials, purify your heart of all material contamination and become first-class devotees eligible to become free from material bondage and go to the spiritual world where Krishna is engaged in his wonderful, loving pastimes with his devotees. Krishna is inviting, come back to me in my personal abode and we qualify by simply chanting, Hare Krishna and augmenting this chanting with other few other practices which will enrich our Bhakti.

### Arjuna, Subhadra, Abhimanyu, Parikshit
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/oc2KOt71XRg?t=351
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Parikshit is the son of Abhimanyu and Uttara."
**Summary:** The speaker returns to the topic of Parikshit, mentioning his future prowess as an archer, and then elaborates on Arjuna's family, including his four sons from four wives, and the story of how Arjuna married Subhadra.

So the description is being given here about this child, that this child Parikshit will be as expert bowman when he grows up as the grandfather of the child, as the grandfather of the child. The child Parikshit will be as expert bowman when he grows up as the grandfather of the child as the grandfather of the child. First is the physical body, then the spiritual body, then the spiritual body. Then corrective form, Next the supernatural Parikshit. 11, second, The body, 8, 9, 10, 11, 11, earlier it was said Arjuna had four sons through four wives so Shrutakirti, Abhimanyu, Babruvahana and Irawan so Irawan was born through Ulupi and similarly Arjuna met Chitrangada a daughter of the king of Manipur and thus Babruvahana was born so Srila Prabhupada describing how Arjuna had four sons through four wives Lord Shri Krishna made a plan to help Arjuna to kidnap Subhadra sister of Krishna because Baladeva was inclined to hand her over to Duryodhana both Krishna and Balram had one sister Subhadra the daughter of Vasudeva and Devaki and both Krishna and Balram are sons of Vasudeva and Devaki so Balram was inclined to marry his sister Subhadra to Duryodhana Duryodhana was the student of Balram Balram taught Duryodhana the art of fighting with a club Gada he was an expert and Duryodhana had become an expert being taught by Balram how to nicely use that club in a fight in a combat with another Kshatriya but Krishna wanted to hand over his sister Subhadra to Arjuna so Krishna made a plan how Arjuna can actually kidnap Subhadra and marry her Yudhishtira also agreed with Krishna's plan and thus Subhadra was forcibly taken away by Arjuna and married to Arjuna Subhadra's son through Arjuna is Abhimanyu and And Abhimanyu is the father of Parikshit Maharaj, the son of Abhimanyu and Uttara. Parikshit is the son of Abhimanyu and Uttara. But this Abhimanyu, he was killed in the battle of Kurukshetra. And therefore Parikshit was born as the posthumous child of Abhimanyu. Then it is also described, so Prabhupada has clearly explained the four sons who were born of the four wives of Arjuna. Through Draupadi, Arjuna had a son by name Shrutakirti. Through Subhadra, he had a son by name Abhimanyu. Through... Chitrangada, he had a son by name Babruvahana. And through Ulupi, he had a son by name Irawana.

### Arjuna, weapons, Kurukshetra
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/oc2KOt71XRg?t=596
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Arjuna actually was fully equipped to fight that battle of Kurukshetra."
**Summary:** The speaker details Arjuna's acquisition of various weapons and his preparations for the Kurukshetra war, including his interactions with Agnideva, Indra, Shiva, and other demigods.

Further it is described, Arjuna satisfied the fire god, Agnideva, by setting fire to the Khandava forest. And thus the fire god gave him one weapon. Arjuna actually was preparing for the big battle of Kurukshetra. Because the Pandavas were excited. They were exiled by Duryodhana, when they were cheated in a gambling match. They were cheated by Duryodhana. And they were exiled. So after that exile period was over, Duryodhana refused to give back their kingdom to the Pandavas. So then war was inevitable. But in the meantime, during exile, Arjuna actually was... Preparing. So he got one weapon from the fire god. And when the Khandava forest was set on fire, Indra became angry. And Indra, assisted by all other demigods, began fighting with Arjuna. Because Arjuna set fire to the forest. So all the devatas headed by Indra, they were defeated by Arjuna. And Indra returned to his heavenly kingdom after suffering defeat at the hands of Arjuna. Then Arjuna also promised protection to one Mayadanava or Mayasura. And Mayasura... Being protected by Arjuna actually, he presented one valuable conch shell celebrated as the devadatta. Similarly, Arjuna received many other valuable weapons from Indradeva. When Indradeva was satisfied to see the chivalry of Arjuna. When Yudhishthira... When Yudhishthira was disappointed in defeating Jarasandha. Then Arjuna gave assurance to Yudhishthira that... Arjuna, Bhima and Krishna will go to Magadha to challenge Jarasandha. And when they went, Bhima was able to kill Jarasandha. And in that way, Yudhishthira was able to hold the Rajasuya sacrifice. Then Arjuna also underwent severe types of penances when he was rewarded by Indradeva. Shiva also once wanted to try the strength of Arjuna. And Shiva disguised as an aborigin, he met Arjuna. There is a fight between Shiva disguised as the aborigin and Arjuna. And Shiva was satisfied with Arjuna's chivalrous nature. His bravery and his skill in fighting. And Shiva disclosed his identity. So, Arjuna prayed to Lord Shiva in all humbleness. And when Shiva was satisfied with Arjuna's behavior. Shiva presented his Pashupatastra to Arjuna. In this way, Arjuna acquired many other important weapons from different devatas. He got Dandastra from Yamaraj. He got Pashastra from Varuna. He got Antaradhanaastra from Kuvera. And actually, Arjuna was fully equipped to fight that battle of Kurukshetra.

Even when Duryodhana was under the clutches of some Gandharvas. That time, Arjuna actually fought against the Gandharvas and released Duryodhana. And it is also described that Arjuna was the one to whom the philosophical discourses on the Bhagavad Gita were spoken by Lord Krishna on the battlefield of Kurukshetra. And even on the battlefield. Arjuna fought very very wonderfully against the Kauravas. And. Hmm. After the disappearance of Lord Krishna. The message was brought by Arjuna to Maharaja Yudhishtira. And Arjuna actually. When Vasudeva passed away. After the disappearance of Krishna. Then Arjuna performed the funeral ceremony of Vasudeva in the absence of Krishna. So at last. When advised by Vasudeva. All the Pandava brothers headed for Mahaprasthana. On the way. At the request of his brother. Arjuna gave up all important weapons as useless. And he proceeded. On Mahaprasthana. **Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** The speaker introduces the verses to be discussed, focusing on the qualities of the child Parikshit as described by the Brahmanas during the Jatakarma ceremony, highlighting Yudhishthira's satisfaction.

In this session we will be discussing from the 22nd and the 23rd verses of the 12th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. The 22nd verse is as follows: Mrigendra Iva Vikranto Nishavyo Himavaniva Titikshur Vasudeva Asau Sahishnuhu Pitarav Iva. The meaning of this verse is this child will be as strong as a lion and as worthy a shelter as the Himalaya mountains. He will be forbearing like the earth and as tolerant as his parents. So the wonderful qualities of the child Parikshit are being described by the Brahmanas who are performing the Jatakarma samskara for the child. Yudhishthira is hearing all this and he is very satisfied that such a qualified child has actually taken birth in his family.

---
### Strength, Humility, Kshatriya Dharma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/SuRy9TQ5RR0?t=101
**Verse References:** BG 2.47
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "One should be a lamb at home and a lion in the chase."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the comparison of Parikshit to a lion, explaining the importance of strength in chasing an enemy, humility at home, and the qualities of a Kshatriya who never turns back in battle.

So in the purport Srila Prabhupada explains, one is compared to the lion when one is very strong in chasing an enemy among the animals in the jungle. The king of the jungle or the forest is the lion because the lion is very strong in chasing an enemy. One should be a lamb at home and a lion in the chase. So this is the moral instruction that however strong one is or powerful one is that strength or power is not shown at home. At home in front of elders, family members and other well-wishers one should be very mild and humble. But somebody who is a Kshatriya he should be like a lion in the chase means he should be very strong in chasing the enemy. And Srila Prabhupada says the lion is never a failure when it comes to chasing an animal. Similarly the head of the state should never fail in chasing an enemy. Yudhishthira Apalaya, this is the description the Bhagavad Gita the qualities of a Kshatriya one of the qualities is he never turns his back when challenged to fight in a battle the battle is always fought till one of the two kings or Kshatriyas are defeated and they are killed in the battle so a Kshatriya is never afraid of fighting an enemy that is the courage in battle courage in battle so this Parikshit will be very very courageous and very strong in protecting the citizens and and and subduing the enemies.

In the modern age the people have begun to detonate atomic weapons on the surface of the earth. And still the earth is forbearing to the inhabitants like a mother who excuses her little child. Earth is called mother earth. In the Vedic conception there are seven kinds of mothers. Atmamata, Gurupatni, Brahmani, Rajapatnika. Dhenu, Dhatri, Prithvi. These are the seven mothers. Prithvi, mother earth. So because the earth provides all our necessities. We grow grains on the surface of the earth in rich fertile places. Then we cannot manufacture these grains in some factories. Not possible. We can only grow them on the surface of the earth. Then there are so many trees, fruit bearing trees. And then in the earth there are minerals and metals. We can mine the earth for so many minerals and metals. Like that the earth provides everything for our sustenance.

In this session, we will be discussing from the 23rd verse of the 12th chapter, the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. Pita Maha Samas Samye Prasade Girishopamaha Ashraya Sarvabhutaanam Yatha Devo Ramashrayaha. The meaning of this verse is this child will be like his grandfather Yudhishthira or Brahma in equanimity of mind. He will be munificent like the lord of the Kailasha hill Shiva. He will be like the lord of the Kailasha hill Shiva. He will be the resort of everyone like the supreme personality of God at Narayana who is even the shelter of the goddess of fortune. So further description of the wonderful qualities of child Pariksit are being given by the Brahmanas to King Yudhishthira. So here three comparisons are made. In mental equanimity, he will be like his grandfather Yudhishthira or grandfather Brahma. And in being munificent, very generous, he will be like lord Shiva. And he will be the ultimate resort of everyone like lord Narayana is the resort of everyone including goddess of fortune. So Srila Prabhupada,

### equanimity, leadership, welfare
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/WIdlhURKpCg?t=141
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "In both cases, the comparison is equally good because both of them are recognized representatives of the Supreme Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on Srila Prabhupada's explanation of the verse, discussing Brahma and Yudhishthira as representatives of the Supreme Lord and their roles in welfare work, emphasizing the importance of mental equanimity for leaders.

Srila Prabhupada has explained a little elaborately in the purport how these personalities who are referred to are well known for their specific extraordinary qualities. So Srila Prabhupada says in the purport, mental equanimity refers both to Maharaj Yudhishthira and to Brahma, the grandfather of all living beings. According to Sridhar Swami, one of the commentators on the Bhagavatam, one of the previous Acharyas, the grandfather referred to is Brahma. How is Brahma the grandfather of all of us? Brahma's son, Swayambhuva Manu, that Swayambhuva Manu is the father of mankind. So all human beings have Swayambhuva Manu as their father and Swayambhuva Manu's father is Brahma. Therefore Brahma is the grandfather of all human beings. In that way, Brahma is referred to as the grandfather in this particular comparison. Pitamaha Samaha Samye. And then according to Srila Vishwanachakruti Thakur, another great Acharya, previous Acharya in our Sampradaya, he has written a commentary on the Bhagavatam. Srila Vishwanachakruti Thakur says, the grandfather referred to here is Maharaj Yudhishthira himself. Now Srila Prabhupada says, of course Yudhishthira is the grandfather of Pariksit. Pariksit's father is Abhimanyu. Abhimanyu is the son of Arjuna and Arjuna's brother, Yudhishthira. So therefore Yudhishthira is in the position of the grandfather of child Pariksit. Now Srila Prabhupada says something very interesting. In both cases, the comparison is equally good because both of them are recognized representatives of the Supreme Lord. Both Brahma and Yudhishthira are recognized representatives of the Supreme Lord. Thus both of them have to maintain mental equanimity being engaged in welfare work for the living being. Brahma is engaged in the welfare of all the living beings in the whole universe.

---
### criticism, tolerance, leadership
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/WIdlhURKpCg?t=289
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "To be able to do welfare work, any responsible executive agent at the top of administration has to tolerate different types of onslaughts from the very persons for whom he works."
**Summary:** The speaker continues explaining Prabhupada's purport, focusing on the challenges of leadership and welfare work, using the example of Brahma being criticized by the gopis for creating eyelids, and highlighting the need for tolerance and equanimity in the face of criticism.

That is Brahma's position. And Yudhishthira, as the emperor of the world, he was always engaged in welfare work of the citizens of the whole world. So Srila Prabhupada says, to be able to do welfare work, any responsible executive agent at the top of administration has to tolerate different types of onslaughts from the very persons for whom he works. Example is given by Srila Prabhupada. Brahma was criticized even by the gopis, the highest perfectional devotees of the Supreme Lord Krishna. The gopis were divided. The gopis were dissatisfied with the work of Brahma because Brahma as creator of this particular universe created eyelids which obstructed their seeing Lord Krishna. How were the eyelids obstructing our seeing? That Prabhupada explains, they could not tolerate, the gopis could not tolerate a moment's blinking of the eyes for it kept them from seeing their beloved Lord Krishna. When you blink, you have to keep blinking the eyes. So when you blink, for a moment, we are not able to see the object of affection. So the gopis felt that why has Brahma created these eyelids which momentarily stop us from seeing Krishna. So what to speak of others who are naturally very critical of every aspect of life, the action of a responsible man. Even the gopis, the perfectional devotees, they criticise Brahma for making these eyelids which block their vision of Krishna. So what to speak of others? So the conclusion is that anyone in a position of responsibility has to have that equanimity of mind to do the welfare work in spite of criticism from the very people whom they are... from the very people whom they are... from the very people whom they are... they are trying to do welfare activity. Similarly, Maharaj Yudhishthira had to cross over many difficult situations created by his enemies and he proved to be the most perfect maintainer of mental equanimity in all critical circumstances. So, both are fitting comparisons for equanimity of mind.

---
### munificence, demigods, power
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/WIdlhURKpCg?t=459
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Lord Shiva is a celebrated demigod who awards gifts to beggars."
**Summary:** The speaker transitions to discussing Lord Shiva's munificence, explaining his title Ashutosh and Bhutanatha, and recounting the story of Ravana receiving power through Shiva's grace but ultimately failing against Lord Rama.

The next Lord Shiva is described. Lord Shiva is a celebrated demigod who awards gifts to beggars. His name is therefore Ashutosh or one who is very easily pleased. He is also called Bhutanatha or the lord of the common folk who are mainly attached to him because of his munificent gifts even without consideration of the after effects. Shiva is liberal in giving gifts to whoever approaches him and begs for some particular benediction. Shiva liberally gives that. So, it is described here by Srila Prabhupada that Ravana was very attached to Lord Shiva and by easily pleasing him, Ravana became so powerful and he wanted to challenge the authority of Lord Rama. By Ravana's grace, Ravana became very visible and powerful. Lord Rama's grace was so great that Ravana became powerful. And he misused this power in challenging Lord Rama. Ravana was never helped by Lord Shiva when Ravana was fighting against Rama. Because the Supreme Lord is not only Lord of everyone, he is also the Lord of Shiva. So when the battle was going on between Ravana and Rama, Shiva could not help Ravana. Then once Shiva awarded a benediction to one Asura by name Vrikasura. And this benediction was an awkward benediction and it was also very disturbing. Vrikasura became empowered by the grace of Lord Shiva to vanquish Ravana.

### benediction, illusion, protection
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/WIdlhURKpCg?t=575
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Lord Shiva never denies anyone any sort of gift. He is very liberal."
**Summary:** The speaker narrates the story of Vrikasura, who received a dangerous benediction from Shiva and attempted to use it against Shiva himself, ultimately being tricked by Vishnu, illustrating the potential pitfalls of indiscriminate generosity.

Anyone's head simply by touching the head. This is the benediction that Vrikasura asked Shiva. Whosoever head I touch, that person's head should immediately get cut off. Just by its touch. So Shiva knew that this Asura is going to misuse this benediction. So when Shiva was refusing to give such a horrible benediction, then Vrikasura became very very restless. And he started cutting off the portions of flesh, his own flesh, and offering it to Shiva in sacrifice. So Shiva took compassion upon this foolish Asura and stopped him from killing himself in that way. And then Shiva had to give this stubborn Asura, Vrikasura, the benediction he wanted. Then what did he do? What did he do? What did he do? What did he do? What did he do? What did this fellow do? This Vrikasura, after he got the benediction. Although Shiva was the one who gave him the benediction, this cunning Asura, he wanted to make an experiment of the power of this benediction by touching the head of Lord Shiva. Thus Lord Shiva had to take shelter of Vishnu to save himself from this trouble. And Lord Vishnu, what did Vrikasura do? When Vrikasura went chasing, then Shiva took shelter of Vishnu and Vishnu confronted this Vrikasura. And by the illusory energy called Yogamaya, Vishnu was able to ask Vrikasura to make an experiment of this benediction by touching his own head. And, So, Vrikasura under the illusion, he touched his own head to test whether the benediction is actually working or not. And in this way, Vishnu saved the whole world from the trouble being created by this cunning beggar of the demigods. So, why Prabhupada is narrating all this is because Lord Shiva never denies anyone any sort of gift. He is very liberal. He is therefore the most generous among the givers of benediction. Shiva is the most generous. Although, sometimes some kind of mistake also happens in granting benediction.

Then the next description is, about Lord Vishnu. It is described here, Rama means the goddess of fortune. Yatha Devo Rama Shrayah. And her shelter, Rama Devi or Lakshmi Devi, her shelter is Lord Vishnu. Lord Vishnu is the maintainer of all living beings. There are innumerable living beings, not only on the surface of this planet, but also in all other hundreds of thousands of planets in this universe. All of them are provided with all necessities of life for the progressive march towards the end of self-realization. The Upanishads say, Eko bahunam yo vidadhati kaman. That is the position of the Supreme Lord Vishnu or Krishna. That it is only the Supreme Lord who maintains all living beings. But, in spite of this protection by the Lord, Srila Prabhupada says, on the path of sense gratification, these living beings are put into difficulty by the agency of Maya, the illusory energy. When we violate the laws of nature or laws of God, and we encroach on somebody else's property, or we give trouble to others unnecessarily, then the illusory energy actually punishes such wrongdoers. And it's only under illusion that the living beings who are desirous of enjoying this material world, they are traversing the path of a false plan of economic development. Such economic development is never successful because it is illusory. So, instead of seeking directly the protection of the goddess of fortune,

### association, qualities, percentage
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/I3mVUgG-EQE?t=362
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Association with fire makes an object hot."
**Summary:** The speaker explains how association with the Supreme Lord leads to acquiring godly qualities, using the analogy of fire making an object hot. He mentions that one can achieve 78% of the Lord's godly qualities through intimate association, noting that the jiva can only attain a minute quantity of the Lord's infinite qualities.

### association, instructions, absolute
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/I3mVUgG-EQE?t=497
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "To follow the instructions of the Lord is to associate with the Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker explains how to associate with the Lord by following His instructions, emphasizing the absolute nature of the Lord, His name, form, qualities, and instructions. He clarifies that associating with Krishna is not merely a physical presence or contact, as the Lord is not a material object.

Now how to associate with the Lord? If that question is asked, Srila Prabhupada says, to follow the instructions of the Lord is to associate with the Lord. Why? Why is it said like this? Because the Lord is absolute. There is no difference between the Lord, His name, His form, His qualities, His instructions, there is no difference. Both are, all are absolute. Advayagyana, this is called Advayagyana. So there is no difference between the Lord and His instructions. So to follow the instructions of the Lord means to associate with the Lord. Now, Srila Prabhupada also says that we should not mistakenly think to associate with Krishna is something like, a material presence. The Lord is not a material object whose presence one has to feel for such association. Like in our ordinary day to day dealings, material dealings, to associate means we think in terms of physical association, physical contact. But no, the Lord, you cannot have association with Krishna with krishna by physical contact not possible it's not possible because god is not at all having any material attributes he does not have any material qualities or material characteristics so with these material eyes we cannot see the transcendental form of the lord with our material senses we cannot perceive the presence of the lord who is always transcendentally present transcendentally available like that and further Srila Prabhupada says the lord is present everywhere

and uh this is described in the bhagavad-gita mayatatam idam sarvam jagat avyaktam i am all-pervading everywhere i am present krishna says in bhagavad-gita so lord is present everywhere and at all times eternally he is so always always is present he is not limited by time or space unlike us every jiva in this material world at least and even in the spiritual world we are present only in limited space and we are present in limited time even brahma even though he's got a fantastic long life span he also has to die with the divine presence with the dissolution of the entire universe at the end of brahma's lifespan case to die so nobody can remain in their material body forever neither even for the limited duration of time according to our lifespan we cannot be present everywhere simultaneously the lord can be present everywhere simultaneously not can he is he is present everywhere he is present everywhere as brahman and he is present everywhere as paramatma in this material world and therefore sheila prabhapa says it is quite possible to have his association simply by following his instruction

### association, devotion, protection
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/I3mVUgG-EQE?t=783
**Verse References:** BG 9.29
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Even though I am equal to everyone, but I am specially inclined to my pure devotees."
**Summary:** The speaker returns to the topic of Parikshit, explaining how he associated with the Lord from the womb, when Krishna personally protected him from Ashwatthama's Brahmastra. He quotes Krishna from the Bhagavad Gita (9.29), stating His special inclination towards pure devotees. He concludes by mentioning Parikshit's association with Shukadeva Goswami and his acquisition of Krishna's qualities.

because the Lord and his instruction are all identical with him, being absolute knowledge, Advaya Gyan. Now, regarding Parikshit, what is being told here? Parikshit, it was told that this child will be almost as good as Lord Sri Krishna by following in his footsteps. So, it is said here that Maharaj Parikshit associated with the Lord even from the womb of his mother up to the last day of his valuable life. We discussed this earlier, when Parikshit was in his mother's womb, one Ashwatthama, son of Dronacharya, he attempted to kill this last surviving descendant of the Pandavas. And he was killed. By releasing one Brahmastra. Now, Brahmastra, it goes and strikes the target wherever the target is. And only the target, nothing else. But, Parikshit was saved from this attack of Brahmastra by Krishna personally. And Krishna entered the womb of Parikshit's mother, Uttara. Abhimanyu's wife. So, Krishna entered personally and protected the child. Because Krishna has interest in the pure devotee, special interest. Ye bhajan titumam bhaktya maite teshu chaapyaham. Krishna says this in the Bhagavad Gita. Even though I am equal to everyone, but I am specially inclined to my pure devotees. That's what Krishna says in the Bhagavad Gita. So, accordingly, Krishna entered the womb of Mother Uttara, when Parikshit was present in that womb. And Krishna neutralized the Brahmastra and Parikshit was saved. And since that time, his association with Krishna began till the end of his life. The whole Bhagavatam is about Parikshit being cursed when he was ruling. He was cursed to die untimely, to die within seven days. And he accepted to quit the body after seven days. But he wanted to prepare. And he decided to go to holy place. And actually, Krishna prepared himself for quitting the body. And he had association of saintly persons, specially Srila Shukadeva Goswami, from whom he heard the Bhagavatam. So, till the end, he had association of Krishna in that way. And he acquired all good qualities of Krishna in all perfection. So, I will stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

Although a kshatriya by birth, he never ate flesh in his entire life. The scriptures say kshatriyas under exceptional circumstances they may eat a flesh of an animal, but Rantideva was a saintly king who never touched flesh of animal, never whole life he never did that. He is one of those pious kings whose names are remembered in the morning and evening. Shastras prescribe remember great personalities in order to sanctify our existence. So about Rantideva in another part of the Bhagavatam it's explained he never endeavored to earn anything, but he would enjoy whatever he got by the arrangement of providence. ### suffering, hospitality, tolerance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jLPSIu3GPDE?t=336
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "He remained sober. He never became disturbed, agitated. Properly received the guest, fed him, and the Brahmana guest ate his share and went away."
**Summary:** The speaker narrates an instance from the Bhagavatam where Rantideva, after fasting for 48 days, receives a Brahmana guest just as he is about to break his fast, and he selflessly offers the guest food and water.

But still there was an exception to this behavior Ranthideva. What is that? When guests came, he would give them everything he wanted, had thus he underwent considerable suffering along with his members of his family. On one occasion indeed, he and his family members shivered for want of food and water, yet Ranthi Deva always remained sober. So one instance is cited in the Bhagavatam there in a later canto about Ranthi Deva. Once after fasting for 48 days, in the morning Granthi Deva received some water and some foodstuffs made with milk and ghee. But when he and his family were about to eat, a Brahman and his family were about to eat, a Brahman and his family were about to eat a Brahman, a guest arrived. Here the word used in Sanskrit for guest is atithi. Atithi means one who comes without invitation, without prior arrangement, without without any intimation to the host, suddenly arrives. So just Ranthi Deva is about to break his fast after 48 day fasting and the Brahmani guest is arrived because Ranthi Deva is perceived the presence of the supreme Godhead everywhere and in every living entity he received guests with faith and respect and gave him a share of the food. The Brahmana guest ate his share and then went away. So the Brahmana guest was received properly even though Ranthi Deva was about to break the fast his faster 48 days. He remained sober. He never became disturbed, agitated. Properly received the guest, fed him, and the Brahmana guest ate his share and went away.

No, body cannot be equalized, but within the body, the resident of the body, they he not they ha not the body, they he the occupant of the body, the resident of the body, the spirit soul within the body, that spirit soul is equal as part and parcel of Krishna and therefore there is always a relationship between every living being and Krishna. So rantideva was enlightened spiritually so he could see the relationship of every living being with Krishna and therefore he also gave this shudra guest a share of the food.

The dog can be given some food but it has to wait outside your door and maybe it will do some service as guarding your house that's okay but not that you fondle the dog make it sleep next to you in your own bed and allow it to roam everywhere in your and make your whole house unclean dog is the most unclean animal so one should never allow a dog inside one's house to maintain cleanliness but that doesn't mean one has to be cruel to the dog or negligent of the dog no a dog which requires some food you may give it food but it has to wait outside your door so ranthi deva offered respect to the dog recognizing the living being inside a spirit soul part and parcel of Krishna not the dog's body or not the dogs consciousness no not that see this is a difference between ranthi deva treating equally all living beings and foolish people who want to imitate equality of all living beings by being particularly attached to a dog or a cat even though they seem to be very compassionate to some dog or cat but they don't offer the same hospitality or treatment to another fellow human being who may be in need of some help they don't do that.

This will be a discussion on the third part of the final concluding part of this verse. We had discussed in the previous two sessions two different parts of this verse. The verse itself I will read once again to refresh your memory: Sarva Sadgunamahatme E Komukam Ayesha Krishnam Anuvratah Aranti Coordinates Dev Iwodaro Yayatir Iwa dharmikaha. The meaning of this verse is: The child will be almost as good as the child we at that Lord Shri Krishna by following his footsteps. In magnanimity he will become as great as King Rantideva and in religion he will be like Maharaja Yati. So in the previous two sessions we discussed the comparison with Lord Krishna and the comparison with Rantideva. In this third part, the final part, we will discuss the comparison with Maharaja Yati. So Srila Prabhupada in the purport gives a brief history of this great King Yati. The great emperor of the world and the original forefather of all great nations of the world who belong to the Aryan and Indo-European stock. Yati is a great forefather of all races in this world. He is the son of Maharaja Nahusha and he became the emperor of the world due to his elder brothers becoming a great and liberated saintly mystic. He ruled over the world for several thousands of years and performed many sacrifices and pious activities recorded in history. This is the greatness of this Maharaja Yati.

### story, anger, insult
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/fMJMdvbKmFE?t=284
**Verse References:** —

**Summary:** The speaker narrates the story of Devayani and Sharmishta, including the incident where Sharmishta mistakenly wore Devayani's clothes, leading to a rebuke and Sharmishta throwing Devayani into a well.

The Shukracharya's daughter, Devayani, had a girlfriend named Sharmishta before her marriage with Yayati. Now, this Sharmishta, the friend of Devayani was the daughter of one king Vrishapurva. That once it so happened, Sharmishta was sporting in the water with thousands of her girlfriends and Devayani was also there, with Sharmishta sporting in the water. When the young girls saw Lord Shiva seated on this bull carrier with Uma or Parvati, they immediately dressed themselves. Because they were sporting in water, they had left their dress in the outside the bank of the water reservoir. Suddenly they saw Shiva and to respect him, immediately they covered themselves. But Sharmishta mistakenly put on Devayani's clothes because they were in a hurry. Now Devayani became very angry at Sharmishta and rebuked her. Now Sharmishta responded by rebuking Devayani and not only she rebuked her, but Sharmishta also rebuked her. Sharmishta threw her, threw Devayani into a well and left her there and went away with her other friends. She was the daughter of the king. So she was very proud of her special position. So she ill-treated Devayani. By chance, when Devayani was trapped in that well, King Yayati came hunting expedition and he came searching for water. By chance, he found Devayani and rescued her and in this way, Devayani accepted Maharaj Yayati as her husband. But before accepting Yayati as husband, Devayani crying loudly told her father Shukracharya about Sharmishta's behavior.

---
### curse, anger, envy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/fMJMdvbKmFE?t=478
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Shukracharya again became angry against Maharaja Yati and he cursed Yati to become old."
**Summary:** The speaker continues the story, explaining how Shukracharya, angered by the insult to his daughter, cursed Yati to become old when Devayani became envious of Sharmishta becoming pregnant with Yati's child.

Now Shukracharya was very angry at Sharmishta and he went to King Vrishapurva and complained that your daughter has insulted my daughter. And he went to King Vrishaparva and complained that your daughter has insulted my daughter. So because Shukracharya was the Brahmana advisor to the king, so king was very afraid that Shukracharya will leave and go away. So he pacified and said, my daughter Sharmishta will actually become the maidservant of your daughter Devayani. So then Shukracharya was satisfied. He did not chastise Vrishaparva or Vrishaparva's daughter Sharmishta. So then Sharmishta, after the marriage of Devayani with Maharaja Yati, went with Devayani as Devayani's maidservant to Maharaja Yati's palace. Now, when Sharmishta found that her friend Devayani had a son through Maharaja Yati, she also desired to have a son. And she requested Maharaja Yati for sex. And when Sharmishta became pregnant, Devayani became very envious. Devayani became very envious of her maidservant becoming pregnant. And in great anger, she left Maharaja Yati and went away to her father's house. And narrated everything that happened to her father, Shukracharya. Shukracharya again became angry against Maharaja Yati and he cursed Yati to become old. When he was just still young, he was cursed to become old and invalid.

---
### desire, detachment, analogy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/fMJMdvbKmFE?t=580
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "These material desires for enjoyment of lust are like a fire."
**Summary:** The speaker describes Yati's reaction to the curse, his desire to regain his youth, and the arrangement to exchange his old age with his son Puru, followed by years of enjoyment and eventual disgust, leading to the narration of a story about a goat to Devayani, illustrating the insatiability of lusty desires.

And then Yati became very, very morose naturally. That he had lost his youth when he had not actually grown sufficiently by age. So he begged Shukracharya, do something about my youth. How can I regain youth? I have not yet satisfied my desires for enjoyment, material enjoyment. Kindly give me back my youth. So then Shukracharya gave him the benediction that he could transfer his old age to someone else, some young man. And thereby get back the youth. So Yati exchanged his old age for his youngest son, Puru. And thus he was able to enjoy with young girls. Now after many, many, years of enjoyment, in the material world, Yati finally became disgusted with this material enjoyment. When satiated, he devised his story corresponding to his own life and narrated that story before his beloved wife Devayani. The story is described in the Bhagavatam as follows. Once upon a time, this is Yayati telling Devayani this story. Once upon a time, there was a goat who was searching in a forest for different types of vegetables to eat. And by chance, this goat came to a well. And in that well, the goat saw a she-goat. He became attracted to the she-goat and somehow or other delivered her from the well. And they were united. Thereafter, when the she-goat saw the he-goat enjoying sex with another she-goat, she became angry, abandoned the he-goat and returned to her brahmana owner to whom she described. The brahmana became angry and cursed the he-goat to lose his sexual power. Thereupon, the he-goat begged the brahmana's pardon and was given back the power for sex. Then the he-goat enjoyed sex with the she-goat for many years. But still, he was not satisfied. If one is lusty and greedy, even the total stock of gold in the entire world cannot be sold. The he-goat cannot satisfy one's lusty desires.

---
### detachment, devotional service, conclusion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/fMJMdvbKmFE?t=750
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "King Yayati, however, renounced worldly life and divided his property among his sons."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes the story of Yati, emphasizing his eventual renunciation of worldly life, engagement in devotional service, and attainment of perfection, highlighting his religiousness and charity, thus justifying the comparison of Parikshit to Yati.

This is the conclusion of Maharajayati. He says, these material desires for enjoyment of lust are like a fire. One may pour clarified butter on a blazing fire, but one cannot expect the fire to be extinguished because of pouring clarified butter. Such a fire, one must adopt a different process. The shastra therefore advises that by intelligence one renounces the life of enjoyment. So, now those who are not sufficiently intelligent, they cannot give up sense enjoyment because a beautiful woman bewilders even the most learned woman. King Yayati, however, renounced worldly life and divided his property among his sons. He personally adopted the life of a mendicant, giving up all attraction to material enjoyment, and engaged himself fully in devotional service to the Lord. Thus he attained perfection. So, this is the history of Yayati described in the Bhagavatam. This Yayati, even while he was ruling, he performed many many many many sacrifices and ensured the prosperity for everyone in his kingdom. And he was also engaged in very many pious activities by which he gave lot of charity. So, in this respect, Pariksit is compared to King Yayati that Pariksit will be as magnanimous, sorry, as religious and as charitable as Maharaj Yayati, who is famous in history for being very very religious minded. I will stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

### Pariksit, Bali Maharaj, devotional service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/H_NLM8da-zc?t=46
**Verse References:** SB 1.12.25
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Bali Maharaj is a great authority in devotional service because he sacrificed everything to please the Supreme Lord and relinquished the connection with the Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the topic of the session, verse 25 from the 12th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam, focusing on the qualities of Pariksit, particularly his patience, which is compared to that of Bali Maharaj, who is described as one of the 12 authorities in devotional service.

In this session we will be discussing from the 25th verse of the 12th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. This will be discussed in two parts. So in this session we will discuss part 2. The verse 25th verse is as follows. The meaning of this verse is this child will be like Bali Maharaj in patience, a staunch devotee of Lord Krishna like Prahlad Maharaj, a patient devotee of Lord Krishna like Prahlad Maharaj, a performer of many Ashwamedha sacrifices, and a follower of the old and experienced men. Four different qualities are glorified about Pariksit by the brahmanas while foretelling about the greatness of the child when he grows up. So the first comparison is made that this child will be like Bali Maharaj in patience. So the history of Bali Maharaj is described as this, described again in the Bhagavatam in detail, great detail in the 8th canto in connection with the pastime of Lord Vamanthi. So Srila Prabhupada says one of the 12 authorities, Bali Maharaj is one of the 12 authorities in devotional service of the Lord, Mahajana, Dvadash Mahajana. Bali Maharaj is a great authority in devotional service because he sacrificed everything to please the Supreme Lord and relinquished the connection with the Lord. He sacrificed everything to please the Supreme Lord and relinquished the connection with the Lord. He has a close connection with the so called spiritual master who obstructed him on the parts of risking everything for the service of the Supreme Lord.

Now in the brief history of Bali Maharaj, because the comparison is made How great was Bali Maharaj in being very very patient, being very patient, that's how this Pariksit will also be very patient. So the description is, so the description is that the Supreme Lord appeared as Vaman Dev, as the son of Aditi and Kashyapa, and he particularly came on the request of Aditi. So, Vaman Dev wanted to satisfy Aditi's desire that Aditi's sons headed by Indra who had lost their kingdom to Bali Maharaj, they get back their kingdom. So, Vaman Dev came to actually restore Indra's kingdom by taking it away in a way of tricking Bali Maharaj and Vaman Dev took away from Bali Maharaj by trickery and gave it to Indra. So, what was Bali's reaction to all this?
