## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.13.48 | 28-10-2022
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### philosophy, duty, surrender
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/f7jFX9_Y1EY?t=55
**Verse References:** SB 1.13.48
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Therefore, O King, you should look to the Supreme Lord only, who is one without a second, and who manifests himself by different energies, and is both within and without."
**Summary:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami introduces verse 48 of the 13th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam, explaining that Narada Muni advises Yudhishthira Maharaj to look to the Supreme Lord after performing his duties, emphasizing the Lord's supreme control.

In this session, we will be discussing the 48th verse of the 13th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. The meaning of this verse is: "Therefore, O King, you should look to the Supreme Lord only, who is one without a second, and who manifests himself by different energies, and is both within and without." This verse is spoken by Narada Muni, and he is addressing Yudhishthira Maharaj. He is explaining the philosophy first, and then he is going to give the details about where the two uncles of Yudhishthira, Dhritarashtra and Vidura, have gone. That is basically Yudhishthira's question to Narada Muni. So, Narada Muni is going to answer that, but before that, he explains the philosophical basis for the solution to Yudhishthira's problem. Narada Muni has already explained that when our own body is under the clutches of material nature, how can we help somebody else who may be suffering or undergoing difficulty because of their bodies being under the clutches of material nature? So, it is ultimately the Supreme Lord, who is the Supreme Controller, who actually is the ultimate cause of all causes. We have to look towards him only after we do our duties. It is not that we neglect our duties because the Supreme Lord is in control. No, we have to do our duties, but beyond that, we only look towards the Lord. Srila Prabhupada explains this in the purport very elaborately.

---
### God, energy, manifestation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/f7jFX9_Y1EY?t=199
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The Supreme Lord Personality of Godhead is one without a second, that means he is the only one who is the Supreme Lord."
**Summary:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami elaborates on the oneness of the Supreme Lord, refuting challenges to His supremacy with the story of Paundraka, and explaining how the Lord manifests through different energies and forms, including the living beings as marginal energy.

Srila Prabhupada says, "The Supreme Lord, Personality of Godhead, is one without a second." That means he is the only one who is the Supreme Lord. There are no two Supreme Lords. Only one. Supreme means one. And there is nobody else who is a competitor to God. Sometimes we hear that somebody else is challenging God. That is never possible. It never has happened, and never will happen. Even when Krishna was present on this earth, he had incarnated 5000 years ago. He was in Dwaraka. One Asura by the name of Paundraka, he declared himself as the real God, and he told Krishna, he challenged Krishna, that you give up your symbols because I am the real Vasudeva. Krishna actually, when he received that note from the messenger of this Paundraka, Paundraka was a king ruling a particular province, and this Paundraka declared himself to be God. This happens even now, that somebody declares, "I am God." So, these sorts of claims have no real foundation. They have no real basis. So, Krishna just sent his Sudarshan Chakra. The Sudarshan Chakra cut off this Paundraka's head, and that was the end of Paundraka. So, these sorts of claims have no real basis. Therefore, it is very clearly said by Narada, an authoritative statement, "The Supreme Lord, Personality of God, is one without a second, but he manifests himself by different energies because he is by nature blissful." Even though he is only one, he is not a limited person like us, so he has many forms, and all of them are identical to him. They are all only one person: Rama, Narasimha, Vamana, Vasudeva, Sankarsana, Pradyumna, Aniruddha, Narayana, Balarama, Krishna. All are Krishna only, one person. So, he manifests himself by different energies. The living beings are also manifestations of his marginal energy.

---
### spiritual nature, material contamination, illusion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/f7jFX9_Y1EY?t=377
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "We are also energy of the Supreme Lord, but what kind of energy? Called marginal energy."
**Summary:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami explains that living beings are the marginal energy of the Lord, qualitatively one with Him in their spiritual nature, but pervertedly manifested in the material world due to contamination, leading to a false sense of identity and illusory experiences of happiness and distress.

We are also energy of the Supreme Lord, but what kind of energy? Called marginal energy. And this marginal energy characteristic is qualitatively one with the Lord. That means our quality, which is spiritual, is the same as the Lord's quality, which is completely spiritual. So, we are not material beings. We are spiritual beings. And there are innumerable living beings, both within and without the external and internal energies of the Lord. So, apart from this marginal energy, the Supreme Lord has got the external energy, the material energy, and the internal energy, which is the spiritual energy. So, within the material, with spiritual energy, and within the material energy, there are innumerable living beings. Since the spiritual world is a manifestation of the Lord's internal energy, the living beings within that internal potency are qualitatively one with the Lord, without contamination from external potency. They are pure in the spiritual world. There are many, many living beings, but all of them are pure, meaning there is no contamination of the external potency. Although qualitatively one with the Lord, the living being in this material world, due to contamination of the material world, is pervertedly manifested. So, this manifestation here means everyone here has got a material body, and that material body is not only externally covering the soul, but the consciousness also has become polluted by the material atmosphere, with a false sense of identity, as "I am independent, I am the Lord, I am the master, I am the enjoyer, I am the proprietor." All these contaminations are even there in the consciousness. So, that's why Srila Prabhupada says here, "The living being is pervertedly manifested in this material world, and therefore he experiences so-called happiness and distress in the material world."

---
### guidance, spiritual master, cooperation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/f7jFX9_Y1EY?t=573
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The Lord wants to correct our mistaken identity, so he makes arrangement, he makes elaborate arrangements in this material world."
**Summary:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami explains how the Lord rectifies the fallen condition of living beings through internal guidance as Paramatma and external guidance through the spiritual master and scriptures, emphasizing the importance of cooperating with the Lord's outward activities for correcting fallen souls.

What is our original position? Completely blissful, eternally, in the association of Krishna, in the spiritual world. That is our original position. That's our pure state of existence. But this contaminated state in the material world is such that there is illusory happiness, and there is illusory distress. Such experiences are all ephemeral. Ephemeral means they are all temporally manifested and do not affect the spirit soul. The spirit soul is completely aloof from all the material coverings or the material influence. The perception of such ephemeral happiness and distress is due only to the forgetfulness of its qualities. We forget our own spiritual quality in contact with this material nature. We forget our self. We forget our spiritual nature. Now our qualities are completely spiritual, like the Lord's quality, which is completely spiritual. There is, however, a regular current from the Lord himself, from within and without, by which to rectify the fallen condition of the living being. The Lord wants to correct our mistaken identity, so he makes arrangement. He makes elaborate arrangements in this material world. So, within, from within us, he corrects the desiring living beings as localized Paramatma. That means Krishna has entered every living being's heart in this material world as Paramatma, Supersoul, and he corrects the desiring living being. He guides, provided we take his guidance. We can be corrected. We can be saved. We can be delivered from this material existence. We can get out of this entanglement. It's possible. And from without, that means externally, he corrects by his manifestations, the spiritual master and the revealed scriptures, Guru and Shastra. One should look unto the Lord. One should not be disturbed by so-called manifestations of happiness and distress. Look towards the Lord. Internally, look towards the Paramatma, and externally, look towards the spiritual master and the scriptures, and don't be disturbed by so-called happiness and distress. If there is happiness also, there is no cause for jubilation, and if there is distress, there is no cause for lamentation. In either case, we have to go on with our spiritual life. So, therefore, Srila Prabhupada says, "Instead of being disturbed, one should try to cooperate with the Lord in his outward activities for correcting the fallen souls." Outward activities, the Lord is making arrangement for correcting the fallen souls. By his order only, one should become a spiritual master. Very clear.

---
### spiritual master, motivation, detachment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/f7jFX9_Y1EY?t=754
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Nobody can be a spiritual master without the order from the Supreme Lord, explicit order from the Supreme Lord."
**Summary:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami emphasizes that becoming a spiritual master requires the explicit order of the Supreme Lord and should not be motivated by personal gain or material benefit, but by cooperating with the Lord's mission, advising Yudhishthira Maharaj to remain undisturbed by happiness and distress and focus on executing the Lord's mission.

The Lord's order has to be there to become a spiritual master. Nobody can be a spiritual master without the order from the Supreme Lord, explicit order from the Supreme Lord. And one should, in this way, cooperate with the Lord. Nobody can have an independent program of spiritual life. It has to be something which is given by the Lord, which is authorized by the Lord, which is ordered by the Lord. One should not become a spiritual master for one's personal benefit, for some material gain, or as an avenue of business or occupation for earning livelihood. This is very, very bad. Somebody becomes a guru so that they can live off the disciples. That is very wrong. That should never be done. Neither should a spiritual master look for some material benefit from the disciples. No. The spiritual master who looks at the Supreme Lord to cooperate with him is actually qualitatively one with the Lord. See, the bonafide spiritual masters are completely spiritual personalities. They have no tinge of any material contamination. Therefore, they are described as qualitatively one with the Lord. And the forgetful ones are perverted reflections only. The living beings who are forgetful of the Supreme Lord, they are not in their original state of existence. They are perverted reflections. Yudhishthira Maharaj is advised by Narada, therefore, not to be disturbed by the affairs of so-called happiness and distress, but to look only unto the Lord to execute the mission for which the Lord has descended. That was his prime duty. So, this is Narada's instruction to Yudhishthira. I will stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

---

## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.13.49-50 | 30-10-2022
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### Krishna's descent, Demoniac vs. Divine Nature, Devotees and Death
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/suRASGsPB1s?t=51
**Verse References:** BG 16
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "For the devotees, his factual form is disclosed, and for non-devotees, he appears like Kalarupa, which is causal form."
**Summary:** This segment explains Krishna's descent as all-devouring time to eliminate the envious, contrasting the nature of asuras and devatas, and highlighting the difference in how devotees and non-devotees experience death.

In this session, we will be discussing the 49th verse of the 13th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam, सोयम अध्यमहाराज भगवान भूतभावनह कालरूपो अवतीरणो स्याम अभावाय सुरत्विशाम. The meaning of this verse is that the Supreme Personality of Godhead, Lord Sri Krishna, in the guise of all-devouring time, कालरूपा, has now descended on earth to eliminate the envious from the world. So, this verse describes Krishna's descent, also as the all-devouring time, कालरूपो. Prabhupada explains this in the purport. There are two classes of human beings, namely the envious and the obedient, who are called the envious. Since the Supreme Lord is one and the father of all living beings, the envious living beings are also his sons, but they are known as the asuras. But the living beings who are obedient to the supreme father are called devatas or demigods, because they are not contaminated by the material conception of life. Demigod means almost like God, or godly. Demigods also can be called godly persons or godly beings. Not only are the asuras envious of the Lord, even denying the existence of the Lord, but they are also envious of all other living beings. See, this is the nature of the asuras. It's called demoniac nature. This is described in the Bhagavad Gita also, 16th chapter, the demoniac nature and the divine nature. The predominance of asuras in the world is occasionally rectified by the Lord when he eliminates them from the world and establishes a rule of devatas like the Pandavas. The Lord sometimes personally comes and sometimes he deputes his devotee to kill these asuras and establish the proper rule of the devatas, like the Pandavas, godly beings. So his designation, that means the Lord's designation as Kala in disguise, is significant. Kalaropoyam. He is not at all dangerous, but he is the transcendental form of eternity, knowledge, and bliss. He is the transcendental form of eternity, knowledge, and bliss. Kalaropoyam. He is the transcendental form of eternity, knowledge, and bliss. That's the real form of the Supreme Lord, full of bliss, full of knowledge, very, very beautiful form, most attractive, and very pleasing to the eyes. So that is the real form, original form of the Lord. But Prabhupada says, for the devotees, his factual form is disclosed, and for non-devotees, he appears like Kalarupa, which is causal form. So what is the causal form? This causal form of the Lord is not at all pleasing to the asuras. When he appears as death, that is not at all pleasing to anybody, especially for the asuras.

---
### Devotee's Protection, Asuras and Fear of Death, Form of God
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/suRASGsPB1s?t=291
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Devotional service is the only way to conquer death."
**Summary:** This segment discusses how devotees are protected from death by the Lord, contrasting it with the fate of asuras. It also explains why asuras prefer to think of God as formless, as a way to feel secure from divine retribution.

Now, devotees don't have to face death, even devotees who take birth in this material world, because they are personally protected by the Lord. So for devotees, even though once having taken birth, it appears like even devotees die, but they are not dying like the asuras. Asuras are completely finished when they die, because all their power, their wealth, their possessions, their influence, everything is finished. But devotees, because they are on the spiritual platform, their devotional service, their devotional relationship, their devotional assets, everything is perfectly intact. And at the time of so-called death, a devotee changes the body from this body to another spiritual body. So for devotees, there is no death. That's why devotional service is the only way to conquer death. So these asuras, they are always afraid of death. They are always afraid of death and therefore they think of the Lord as formless. Asuras always describe if at all there is some god, he is formless. Why? Because in that way they feel secure that they will not be vanquished by the Lord. They think they can escape death or they can escape the destruction by the Supreme Lord if they declare that God is there but he is formless. This is not the truth. This is not the reality. God is there. He has got his transcendental form. But for the non-devotees he is always present as death.

---
### Pandavas' Role, Lord's Mission, Asuras' Envy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/suRASGsPB1s?t=423
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna is the Supreme Lord. The Supreme Lord has descended with a mission to clean up the earth which was overburdened by Asuras."
**Summary:** This segment explains the meaning of the 50th verse, highlighting the Lord's mission to help the demigods and the Pandavas' role in cooperating with Krishna to cleanse the earth of asuras.

The 50th verse. The meaning is the Lord has already performed his duties to help the demigods and he is awaiting the rest. You Pandavas may wait as long as the Lord is here on earth. So Narada Muni is indicating to the Pandavas that they are to cooperate with Krishna. Krishna is the Supreme Lord. Krishna is the Supreme Lord. The Supreme Lord has descended with a mission to clean up the earth which was overburdened by Asuras. So now the Pandavas are part of the mission of the Lord in this material world. So it is Krishna's desire that the Pandavas rule the world, the devotees, the divine personalities, not the demoniac natured Duryodhana and company. No. So in the Purpose Srila Prabhupada says the Lord descends from his abode, Krishnaloka, the topmost planet in the spiritual sky, in order to help the demigod administrators of this material world when they are greatly vexed by the Asuras. The Asuras give too much trouble to the demigods or devatas or the divine beings in this material world. Then the Supreme Lord comes here. Now these Asuras are described as not only are they envious of the Supreme Lord, they are also envious of the Lord's devotees.

---
### Demoniac Nature, Desire for Lordship, Competition among Asuras
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/suRASGsPB1s?t=528
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "We have come out of our own choice, dictated by a strong desire to lord it over the resource of material world and become imitation lords of all."
**Summary:** This segment delves into the nature of asuras, explaining that they are conditioned living beings who have chosen material association out of a desire to dominate the material world and become imitation gods, leading to competition and conflict among themselves.

This is the nature of these Asuras, demoniac nature, as referred to above. They are conditioned living beings, contact material association by their own choice. So we are not put here accidentally. We are not come here by somebody else's dictation. We have come out of our own choice. And what is that choice we have made? Dictated by a strong desire to lord it over the resource of material world and become imitation lords of all. They survey. This is the whole disease. It is called Bhavaroga, material disease. I want to be the lord of everything I survey, and I want to lord over resources of this material world. So this is the desire which dictates us, contact with material association. And this is only possible with material association. It is not possible in the spiritual existence. Everyone is trying to become an imitation god. Imitation god. We cannot be. We cannot become god. We can try to imitate god. That's what all these Asuras do. They declare there is no god, or I am god, or everyone is god. All these kind of foolish things are done by Asuras only, not by the demigods. There is a keen competition among such imitation gods, and such competitors are generally known as Asuras. Even the Asuras sometimes they fight among themselves. One tries to kill the other, or one tries to overpower the other. When there are too many Asuras in the world, then it becomes a hell for those who are devotees of the Lord. Devotees want to live peacefully doing their own devotional service. They have no desires like the Asuras. But these Asuras become too many in number. The Asuras disturb the devotees also, due to the growth of the Asuras.

---
### Devotees' Prayers, Lord's Intervention, Annihilation of Yadu Dynasty
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/suRASGsPB1s?t=690
**Verse References:** BG 4.7
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Whenever and wherever there is this dharmasya glani, neglect of religious principles, these Asuras, they come in the way of following religious principles, then the Lord personally comes."
**Summary:** This segment explains that when asuras become too numerous and disturb devotees, the devotees and demigods pray to the Lord for relief. The Lord then intervenes, either personally or through devotees, to restore dharma. The segment concludes by discussing the imminent annihilation of the Yadu dynasty and the Lord's departure.

Both of the Asuras, the mass of people who are generally devoted to the Lord by nature, and the pure devotees of the Lord, including the demigods in higher planets, pray to the Lord for relief. And when the devatas, devotees pray to the Lord, then what happens? The Lord either descends personally from his abode, or deputes some of his devotees to remodel the fallen conditions of human society, or even animal society. The Lord can appear in any form, but he remains the transcendental Lord, and his form is always spiritual, though it appears like a material form. It is not a material form. Such disruptions take place not only in human society, but also among animals, birds or other living beings, including the demigods in the higher planets. So in this material world, periodically these disturbances are there, time to time. yadā yadā hi dharmasya glānir bhavati bhārata. Whenever and wherever there is this dharmasya glani, neglect of religious principles, these Asuras, they come in the way of following religious principles, then the Lord personally comes. Lord Shri Krishna descended personally to vanquish Asuras like Kamsa, Jarasandha, Chishupala, and during the reign of Maharaj Yudhishthira, almost all these Asuras were killed by the Lord. Now, it is told in the verse, await the rest. What is that? Now he was awaiting the annihilation of his own dynasty, called the Yadu Vamsha, who appeared by his will in this world. Krishna appeared along with the Yadus, and they were very very powerful. Now, this annihilation of this Yadu Vamsha also, it is not required, except when the Asuras had to be annihilated. After annihilation of the Asuras, the Yadu Vamsha is not required on this earth. So the Lord wanted to take them away, the Yadu Vamsha, before his own departure to his eternal abode. So Narada like Vidura did not disclose the imminent annihilation of the Yadu dynasty, but he directly gave a hint to King Yudhishthira and his brothers to wait till the incident happened, and the Lord departed. So Narada is indicating what is going to happen in the future, immediate future now, that the Yadu Vamsha will be annihilated, and the Lord will go to his own abode, and then the Pandavas can go back to the spiritual world. I will stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

---

## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.13.5-7 | 23-09-2022
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### detachment, spiritual inquiry, Vidura's motivation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/BkVR4_N30WU?t=130
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "This is actually the real business of human form of life to make this inquiry."
**Summary:** The speaker explains Vidura's departure from the palace was not solely due to Duryodhana's insult, but primarily to seek spiritual knowledge and inquire about the self (Atma Vidya).

The limbs and senses become active and existence itself becomes delightful. Vidura was so dear to the members of the Kaurava family that his long absence from the palace was comparable to inactivity. Why was Vidura absent for a long time from the palace? If you remember, it was described previously that Vidura was insulted by Duryodhana when Vidura was trying to advise Dhritarashtra, his elder brother, in some particular issue, particularly Dhritarashtra not treating the Pandavas properly while the Pandavas were actually not treating the Pandavas properly without their father, fatherless children, and Dhritarashtra was a caretaker, but he was not properly treating the Pandavas. He was partial to his own sons, and he did so much injustice to the Pandavas. Therefore, when Vidura pointed that out to Dhritarashtra, Duryodhana became very angry and he insulted Vidura. Of course, Vidura apparently left the palace being insulted by Duryodhana, but in reality, Vidura left the palace because he wanted to inquire about the spiritual subject matter from saintly persons in the places of pilgrimage. Therefore, Vidura left the palace in order to visit holy places of pilgrimage to search out for a saintly person who could explain to him the truth about this self, Atma Vidya or Brahma Vidya. This is actually the real business of human form of life to make this inquiry. In the Vedanta Sutra it is said, Athato Brahma Jignasa. So Vidura actually left the palace not because he was insulted by Duryodhana, but really because he wanted to inquire and acquire the knowledge about this self, Atma Vidya. So Vidura left and went on a pilgrimage.

---
### Vaishnava etiquette, spiritual guidance, guru-disciple relationship
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/BkVR4_N30WU?t=287
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "He didn't want to break the Vaishnava etiquette that when there is a senior devotee or senior personality present, then the younger one should not instruct the seekers of knowledge, spiritual knowledge."
**Summary:** Vidura's pilgrimage led him to Uddhava, who, respecting Vaishnava etiquette, directed Vidura to the elder Maitreya Rishi for deeper spiritual instruction, highlighting the importance of seniority in spiritual guidance.

Vidura left and went on a pilgrimage, and he kept traveling till he met Uddhava in one of the holy places of pilgrimage, and he began to inquire from Uddhava about this self. While Uddhava began to speak, then Uddhava realized that Vidura's questions can best be answered by Maitreya Rishi because even though both Uddhava and Maitreya Rishi were instructed by Krishna before Krishna's departure from this world at the end of his manifest pastimes, even though both were instructed and both were self-realized, both were competent, but Maitreya Rishi was elder in age to Uddhava. Therefore, Uddhava did not want to actually do Maryada Vetikrama. He didn't want to break the Vaishnava etiquette that when there is a senior devotee or senior personality present, then the younger one should not instruct the seekers of knowledge, spiritual knowledge. So Uddhava directed Vidura to go to Maitreya Rishi, who was nearby, and inquire from Maitreya Rishi about this knowledge of the self, Atma Vidya. Accordingly, Vidura went to Maitreya Rishi, and Maitreya Rishi received Vidura very nicely and instructed him.

---
### compassion, duty, self-realization
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/BkVR4_N30WU?t=395
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Vidura came back to the palace simply to deliver Dhritarashtra."
**Summary:** After receiving instruction from Maitreya Rishi, Vidura returned to Hastinapura to deliver his brother Dhritarashtra, who was living comfortably under Yudhishthira's care but neglecting self-realization, highlighting Vidura's compassion and sense of duty.

After Vidura was instructed by Maitreya Rishi, then he came back to Hastinapura. Why did he come back? To actually deliver his brother Dhritarashtra. By this time, already the battle of Kurukshetra was over, and all the Kauravas were killed in that battle. Dhritarashtra and Gandhari were staying under the care of Yudhishthira when Yudhishthira was enthroned as the emperor of the world after the battle of Kurukshetra by Lord Krishna, and Yudhishthira was ruling. Yudhishthira was very, very kind and respectful and compassionate, and very nicely he is taking care of Dhritarashtra and Gandhari, his uncle and aunt. He was taking care of them in the palace. They were living under the care of Yudhishthira very nicely, but Vidura actually thought that Dhritarashtra is actually not pursuing self-realization. He is just living comfortably in the palace under the care of Yudhishthira and wasting his time in simply maintaining the body, simply for the sake of living a comfortable life, while time is running out, death is nearing. He had become an old man. Dhritarashtra had become an old man, so Vidura came back to the palace simply to deliver Dhritarashtra because he himself, Vidura himself, was self-realized. So Vidura came back, and how the members of the royal palace received Vidura is being described here. Vidura was so dear to the members of the Kaurava family that his long absence from the palace is comparable to inactivity.

---
### Vedic culture, hospitality, treating guests
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/BkVR4_N30WU?t=522
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "According to Indian moral codes, that is based on the Vedic culture, Vedic tradition, even an enemy received at home should be so well received that he will not feel any fearful situation."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the Vedic culture of hospitality, emphasizing that even enemies should be treated with utmost respect and care when they are guests in one's home, ensuring they feel safe and welcomed.

All of them were feeling acute separation from Vidura, and therefore his return to the palace was joyful for all. The sixth verse, mumucho prema baashpa, mumucho prema baashpaugham, viraha autkantya kataraha, rajatam arhayam chakre, kratasana parigraham. The meaning of this verse is, due to anxieties and long separation, they all cried out of affection. King Yudhishthira then arranged to offer sitting accommodations and a reception for Vidura. The seventh verse, tam bhukta vantam vishrantam, asinam sukham asane, prashrayavanato raja, prahatesham cha shunvatam. The meaning of this seventh verse is, after Vidura ate sumptuously and took sufficient rest, he was comfortably seated, then King Yudhishthira began to speak to him, and all who were present there listened since he has come back. They are very eager to hear from him about his travel. Now in the purport, Srila Prabhupada explains something very important about the Vedic culture. Srila Prabhupada says, King Yudhishthira was expert in reception also, even in the case of his family members. Vidura was well received by all the family members by exchange of embraces and obeisances. The younger ones to Vidura, they paid obeisances to Vidura, and the equals they embraced, and out of affection for all those family members, Vidura himself, he embraced the affectionate family members. After that, bathing and arrangements for a sumptuous dinner were made by Yudhishthira, and then Vidura was given sufficient rest. After finishing his rest, Vidura was offered a comfortable place to sit, and then the king Yudhishthira began to talk about all happenings, both family and otherwise. That is the proper way to receive a beloved friend or even an enemy. This is the important point you should note. Srila Prabhupada says, not only receiving a friend properly, so nicely, or some dear relative, but even an enemy comes home, he should be received the same way, like an affectionate relative or a dear friend. So Srila Prabhupada says, according to Indian moral codes, that is based on the Vedic culture, Vedic tradition, even an enemy received at home should be so well received that he will not feel any fearful situation. An enemy is always afraid of his enemy, but this should not be so when he is received at home by his enemy. This means that a person when received at home should be treated as a relative, so what to speak of a family member like Vidura was a well-wisher for all the members of the family. Thus Yudhishthira Maharaj began to speak in the presence of all the other members very lovingly. Yudhishthira began to speak to Vidura.

---
### hospitality, Vedic principles, Parikshit Maharaj
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/BkVR4_N30WU?t=765
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "The Vedic culture is to receive any uninvited guest properly, even if the guest happens to be an enemy."
**Summary:** The speaker further explains the Vedic principle of treating even uninvited guests, including enemies, with the same respect and hospitality as relatives, and illustrates this with the story of Parikshit Maharaj and Shamika Rishi.

Now Srila Prabhupada explains elsewhere what is this Vedic culture of receiving a guest, even if the guest, guest means somebody who comes without being invited, not having an invitation. Obviously, an enemy will not have an invitation, so even if an enemy comes or anybody comes without invitation, they are received very nicely by being offered a reception that is supposed to be offered to atithi, a guest who is uninvited, who comes home. How should a householder receive such a guest? So the injunction in the Vedas is that a householder should not be a stranger, not a stranger. So whatever a person is, even if a stranger enters your house, you will receive him in such a friendly way that he will forget that you are his enemy, and he will have no fear, no fear of facing the enemy. That is the way to receive. So Srila Prabhupada explains elsewhere that when Parikshit Maharaj went hunting on some occasion and he became very thirsty and he started looking for water, he could not find any body of water nearby in the forest, and he happened to come across the ashram of one Shamika Rishi. So he entered that ashram of Shamika Rishi, and the Rishi was sitting there, and Yudhishthira Maharaj was not properly welcomed and received by this Rishi. So I'm sorry, Parikshit Maharaj was not received properly by Rishis Shamika, so Parikshit Maharaj became very angry, not being received properly. So Shrila Prabhupada explains why did Parikshit Maharaj become angry at this Shamika Rishi? Actually, Parikshit Maharaj's Kshatriya is in a subordinate position to Shamika Rishi because Shamika Rishi is a Brahmana, so it is not expected that a Brahmana should actually respect a Kshatriya in the normal circumstances, but in this particular case, when Parikshit Maharaj has come to the ashram as a guest, atithi, then even if that atithi happens to be an enemy, what to speak of a good Kshatriya king, the host, the resident of that ashram should receive the guest respectfully, very nicely, very properly. So only because of this particular injunction in the scriptures, it was justified that Parikshit Maharaj became upset with Shamika Rishi.

---
### Krishna's arrangement, Shukadeva Goswami, Parikshit Maharaj

### Srimad Bhagavatam, Narada Muni, Dhritarashtra
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/uKuwRqsgc00?t=51
**Verse References:** SB 1.13.51
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "O King, your uncle Dhritarashtra, his brother Vidura, and his wife Gandhari have gone to the southern side of the Himalayan mountains, where there are shelters of the great sages."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the verses to be discussed, focusing on Narada Muni's description of Dhritarashtra's departure to the Himalayas and Narada's ability to see past, present, and future.

In this session, we will be discussing three verses: 51, 52, and 53, verses of the 13th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. The 51st verse: Dhritarashtraha Saha Bhratra Gandharaya Cha Swabharyaya Dakshinena Himavata Rishinam Ashramam Gatha. So Narada is now disclosing where has Dhritarashtra gone. So he is telling the translation of the 51st verse: O King, your uncle Dhritarashtra, his brother Vidura, and his wife Gandhari have gone to the southern side of the Himalayan mountains, where there are shelters of the great sages. So Prabhupada briefly mentions in the purport: To pacify the mourning Maharaja Yudhishthira, Narada first of all spoke from the philosophical point of view, and then he began to describe the future movements of his uncle, which he had done in the past. He could see by his foreseeing powers and thus began to describe as follows. Narada can see the past, present, and future. Such saintly persons are called Trikalagya. By the mercy of the Lord, they are able to see specific events, either of the past or of the future, apart from seeing the present events, being aware of the present events.

---
### Saptasrota, Ashtanga Yoga, Detachment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/uKuwRqsgc00?t=147
**Verse References:** SB 1.13.52, SB 1.13.53
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "On the banks at Saptasrota, Dhritarashtra is now engaged in beginning Ashtanga Yoga by bathing three times daily, in the morning, noon, and evening, by performing the Agnihotra sacrifice with fire, and by drinking only water."
**Summary:** The speaker continues with verses 52 and 53, explaining Dhritarashtra's location at Saptasrota and his engagement in Ashtanga Yoga, emphasizing the practices of bathing, sacrifice, and controlled diet for mind and sense control.

So the 52nd verse, so Narada Muni is describing to Yudhishthira where have they gone. The translation of the 52nd verse: The place is called Saptasrota divided by 7, because there the waters of the sacred Ganges were divided into 7 branches. This was done for the satisfaction of the 7 great Rishis, Saptarishi. 53rd verse: Snatva Anusavanam Tasmin Hutva Chagnin Yatha Vidhi Abhaksha Upashantatma Saaste Vigatai Shanah. The meaning of this 53rd verse is: On the banks at Saptasrota, Dhritarashtra is now engaged in beginning Ashtanga Yoga by bathing three times daily, in the morning, noon, and evening, by performing the Agnihotra sacrifice with fire, and by drinking only water. This helps one control the mind and the senses and frees one completely from thoughts of familial affection. So specifically, Dhritarashtra is engaged in Ashtanga Yoga practice.

---
### Ashtanga Yoga, Spiritualization, Materialism
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/uKuwRqsgc00?t=249
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "But real purpose of Ashtanga Yoga is to control the senses and mind and divert them from matter to spirit."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the purpose of Ashtanga Yoga, clarifying that it's not merely for physical health but for controlling the mind and senses and diverting them from material to spiritual consciousness.

And that is basically any Yoga practice is to control the mind and senses, Yoga Indriya Samyama, and also become free from all thoughts of family affection. So Srila Prabhupada says in the purport, the Yoga system is a mechanical way to control the senses and the mind and divert them from matter to spirit. This Yoga system, Ashtanga Yoga system, is a mechanical method, and the purpose is very clearly described by Prabhupada, which is missed out by many people who may be following some practice of Ashtanga Yoga, especially nowadays. They think Ashtanga Yoga is meant for maintaining good health or for increasing some mental power, like that. So many other purposes they use this practice of Ashtanga Yoga, but real purpose of Ashtanga Yoga is to control the senses and mind and divert them from matter to spirit. We are completely spiritual beings, but due to our desire for lording over material nature, we have been given this material body, and we have a materialistic conception of life itself. So to correct that is to divert the mind and senses from matter to spirit.

---
### Ashtanga Yoga, Pranayama, Samadhi
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/uKuwRqsgc00?t=361
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Trance is the final stage actually, Samadhi, where one is completely mind is in equilibrium, not disturbed by any external distractions."
**Summary:** The speaker details the eight steps of Ashtanga Yoga, including Asana, meditation, spiritual thoughts, Pranayama, and Samadhi, emphasizing the importance of spiritualizing thoughts and manipulating Prana Vayu to detach from material sense objects.

In Ashtanga Yoga, there are different steps, Anga Ashta Anga, eight steps actually. So Srila Prabhupada says the preliminary processes are the sitting posture, meditation, spiritual thoughts, manipulation of air passing within the body, and gradual situation in trance, facing the absolute person Paramatma. Very nicely Prabhupada is summarizing the eight steps: sitting posture Asana, then meditation, fixing the mind, fixing the mind on the form of the Lord in the heart, spiritual thoughts, completely spiritualizing the thinking. Because the Lord is completely spiritual, we are spiritual beings. There is no possibility of getting out of this material existence as long as we have got material thoughts, material conceptions, material ideas, material identification. We cannot get out. So this spiritual thoughts are very, very important. Manipulation of air passing within the body, inside this body, there is something called Prana Vayu, and that Prana Vayu circulation or flow has to be manipulated. It is called Pranayama. Pranayama exercises which will help manipulate the flow in such a way that we will gradually become detached from all material sense objects, material pleasures, material thoughts. That is also called Vairagya, but it is a mechanical process. Therefore, they work with the Prana Vayu circulation, manipulating that by exercises, just like this inhalation and exhalation. That itself actually can regulate the flow of this Prana Vayu. So specifically exercising that inhalation and exhalation, breathing exercises. So that is called manipulation of air passing within the body and gradual situation in trance. Trance is the final stage actually, Samadhi, where one is completely mind is in equilibrium, not disturbed by any external distractions, nor the mind is absorbed in some internal thinking process or feeling or willing, no internal activity either, but is absorbed in facing the absolute person Paramatma, fixed completely on the form of Paramatma within the heart. Such mechanical ways of rising to the spiritual platform prescribe some regulative principles.

---
### Regulative Principles, Detachment, Illusion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/uKuwRqsgc00?t=575
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Material existence means to be absorbed in the material objective, which is simply illusory."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses the strict rules of Ashtanga Yoga, such as bathing three times daily and fasting, and emphasizes the need to fix the mind on spiritual matters and become free from material objects and illusory objectives.

Some strict rules have to be followed if at all one wants success in this Ashtanga Yoga. So what are the rules? Taking bath daily three times. That's why in the Bhagavad Gita also it is said, and in all the Yoga literature, Ashtanga Yoga literature it is said, go to a secluded place which is suitable for practicing Ashtanga Yoga without any distraction and where there is facility for taking bath three times. So the Himalayan region is ideal because many, many rivers are flowing there. Most all of them are actually branches of the Ganga only, but that river flows in so many places. Anybody can actually take bath very easily three times. Fasting as far as possible. So gradually it is described in the episode on Dhruva Maharaj performing meditation on the Supreme Lord, how he progressively gave up solid food, then liquid, and even he gave up breathing. So this is the strict rules that are prescribed in the Ashtanga Yoga system: fasting as far as possible and sitting and concentrating the mind on spiritual matters. The mind is always absorbed in material things. That's our present condition. So if you want to change this subject matter of thinking for the mind, then one has to fix the mind on spiritual matters and freed from material thoughts and thus gradually becoming free from Vishaya, or material objectives. This material world is Vishaya Sagara. It's an ocean of material objects. Every material object has got some form, some taste, some smell, some sense of touch, some sound. So these material sense objects, we have to become free from them because we have nothing to do with matter or material objects or material things. We have no connection with them. We are spiritual beings and trying to squeeze out some happiness out of matter is frustrating, will never give us the desired happiness or the desired satisfaction because we are spiritual beings. Material existence means to be absorbed in the material objective, which is simply illusory.

---
### Yoga, Paramatma, Detachment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/uKuwRqsgc00?t=821
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Real yoga practice means to forget all these material things and to concentrate on Paramatma, the supreme Lord within the heart, the spiritual all-spiritual being supreme person."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes by reiterating that yoga helps one become free from illusory thoughts and turn towards Paramatma, emphasizing the need to forget material things and concentrate on the Supreme Lord within the heart, as exemplified by Dhritarashtra's attempt to detach from family affairs.

Coverings, material coverings of the spirit, and a yoga system helps one to become free from all these illusory thoughts and gradually turn towards the absolute person Paramatma. This is the whole yoga practice or yoga system. By material associated education, we learn simply to concentrate on flimsy things, but yoga is the process of forgetting them all together. So real yoga practice means to forget all these material things and to concentrate on Paramatma, the supreme Lord within the heart, the spiritual all-spiritual being supreme person. Dhritarashtra, you see the status condition, he was all along trying to improve his family affairs, but now for the sake of turning towards Paramatma, he has to forget all that. Therefore, it is necessary for him to become free from all these illusory thoughts and to concentrate on flimsy things, but now for the sake of turning towards Paramatma, he has to forget all that. Therefore, it is necessary for him to take bath three times, to fast, to concentrate his mind in spiritual thoughts like that. So I will stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.13.54 | 1-11-2022
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### Ashtanga Yoga, Material Nature, Three Gunas
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Odmoah1S3Xs?t=50
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "One has to become free from these influences."
**Summary:** This segment explains the meaning of the verse, focusing on controlling sitting postures and breathing in Ashtanga Yoga to become immune to the contaminations of the three modes of material nature.

In this session, we will be discussing the 54th verse of the 13th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. The meaning of this verse is, one who has controlled the sitting postures, the yogic asanas, and the breathing process, can turn the senses toward the absolute personality of Godhead, and thus become immune to the contaminations of the modes of material nature, namely mundane goodness, passion, and ignorance. So, Narada Muni is describing how that works. In the Ashtanga Yoga system, which is practiced by those who follow this Ashtanga Yoga process, how does it work? Here, *jitāsana* is controlled sitting postures, because unless a yogi is able to sit properly, he cannot become completely absorbed in meditation. He'll be disturbed by the body. So, this practice of yogasana, different sitting postures, or different bodily exercises, is for controlling the sitting posture, the ability to sit for a very, very long time, without any distraction, without any disturbance. Then, the breathing process also has to be controlled. Why does the breathing process have to be controlled? To turn the senses toward the absolute personality of Godhead, and thus become immune to the contaminations of the modes of material nature. We are influenced by the three gunas. That influence is felt through the senses, through the mind, through our material activities. We feel the influence, we experience the influence. Sometimes, we are influenced by goodness, sometimes by passion, sometimes by ignorance. So, a combination of these modes always acts, in most cases. In any case, these influences are material influence. They are all the influence of the material energy, because the material energy consists of these three gunas. *Sattvam rajas tamaiti guna prakati sambhavah. Prakati sambhavah.* They are the ingredients of this material nature, these three gunas. And, they influence the body. They influence the living being in this material world. One has to become free from these influences.

---
### Yoga, Paramatma, Bhakti Yoga
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Odmoah1S3Xs?t=251
**Verse References:** BG 15, BG 18
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Real yoga practice means to focus or concentrate one's attention on the supreme Lord who is seated in everyone's heart."
**Summary:** This segment discusses the preliminary activities of yoga, focusing on concentrating on the Supreme Lord Hari, who is seated in everyone's heart as Paramatma, and contrasts the indirect methods of other yoga systems with the direct approach of Bhakti Yoga.

That we described here. The purpose, Srila Prabhupada says, the preliminary activities of the way of yoga are asana, pranayama, pratyahara, dhyana, dharana, etc. Eight steps are there. Maharaja Dhritarashtra was to attain success in those preliminary actions, because he was seated in a sanctified place and was concentrating upon one objective. What is that one objective? Namely, the supreme personality of Godhead, Hari. So, real yoga practice means to focus or concentrate one's attention on the supreme Lord who is seated in everyone's heart. The Lord is there, and in everybody's heart he has entered. *sarvasya caaham rudhi sannyavishto mattasmrutir jnanam appohanam jnanam*. In the fifteenth chapter, Krishna says, I am seated in everybody's heart. In the eighteenth chapter also, Krishna explains, *ishwaras sarvabhutanam hrudeshe aryoh deshtati*. The supreme Lord is seated in everyone's heart, and he is seated as Paramatma. *Upadrishta anumanta bharta bhokta maheshwaraha Paramatmeti chaapyukto dehesmin purusha paraha*. There is a transcendental enjoyer, the supreme Lord as Paramatma. *Paramatma iti uktaha*. He is called as Paramatma. He is not Atma. He is Paramatma. He is supreme Atma, super soul. Some philosophers say this, super soul and individual soul are one. They don't say two, they say one. But actually, according to the Bhagavad Gita, according to the Vedanta Sutra, according to the Bhagavatam, according to all the Vedic literature, this individual soul and super soul are two distinct identities. *Paramatmeti chaapyukto Upadrishta*. He is a witness. He is a sanctioner. We have no freedom to fulfill our desires unless it is sanctioned by Paramatma who is the actual witnesser and sanctioner of every jiva, every living being in this material world. So therefore, the primary activities are meant of this Ashtanga Yoga. They are meant to concentrate upon the Supreme Lord in the heart. That is the ultimate goal of Ashtanga Yoga practice. Thus, all his senses were being engaged in the service of the Lord. This is the important point. Engaging the senses in the service of the Lord means we become very easily focused on the Supreme Lord. This is actually the way of practice of Bhakti Yoga, to engage all the senses in the service of the Supreme Lord. In Bhakti Yoga, it is done directly. In other yoga systems, it is done indirectly. That is the difference. Of course, indirect methods are slow, very tedious, and they can be very, very difficult at times.

---
### Bhakti Yoga, Purification, Direct Service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Odmoah1S3Xs?t=475
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Directly engage all your senses in the service of the Supreme Lord."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes the effectiveness of Bhakti Yoga, highlighting how directly engaging the senses in the service of the Lord, such as through chanting Hare Krishna, leads to purification and freedom from material contaminations.

The person can get deviated or distracted in between. When the process is too slow, it takes a very long time, then one can very easily get distracted. But in Bhakti Yoga, it is quick, it is highly effective, it is very powerful. Directly engage all your senses in the service of the Supreme Lord. Just like this chanting Hare Krishna. Directly we engage our tongue, our ears, our sense of touch, and our mind attentively here. So directly we engage these four things in service of the Lord. This is service. Chanting Hare Krishna, and hearing, and counting, and taking a vow to chant a fixed number of times. Attentively chant and hear. This is actually direct service to the Lord. Direct service. And engaging our senses in direct service is Bhakti. So Srila Prabhupada says, this process of engaging all the senses in the service of the Lord directly helps the devotee to get freedom from the contaminations of the three material modes of nature.

---
### Material Bondage, Goodness, Transcendental Goodness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Odmoah1S3Xs?t=575
**Verse References:** BG 14.5
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Even the highest mode, the material mode of goodness, is also a cause of material bondage."
**Summary:** This segment explains that even the mode of goodness can cause material bondage, and contrasts it with transcendental goodness, which is the true freedom from all material contamination.

Directly engaging the senses in the service of the Lord, automatically we become purified. We don't have to have a separate process for purifying the contaminations. That is not the way of Bhakti. Even the highest mode, the material mode of goodness, is also a cause of material bondage. This is a secret that is revealed in the scriptures, especially Bhagavad Gita. What does Bhagavad Gita say? *Sattvam rajas tamaiti guna prakriti sambhavah Nibaddhanti mahabaho dehe*. This is the truth about material nature, even the mode of goodness. It is simply a cause of material bondage. Then what to speak of other qualities namely passion and ignorance? Definitely passion and ignorance are very much the cause of material bondage. But goodness also is the cause of material bondage. Passion and ignorance increase the material propensities of hankering for material enjoyment. Of course there is a qualitative difference between goodness and the other two gunas. Goodness is comparatively better than passion and ignorance, but goodness is not the best. Goodness is relatively better. The best is transcendental goodness, shuddha sattva, the spiritual existence, the spiritual platform, the spiritual existence. That is the best, that is the topmost, that is the true freedom from all material contamination.

---
### Lust, Greed, Loss of Discrimination
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Odmoah1S3Xs?t=718
**Verse References:** BG 3.37
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Driven by lusty desires, people are working hard day and night, I want more wealth, more power, more wealth, more power."
**Summary:** This segment discusses how passion and ignorance increase the hankering for material enjoyment, leading to lust and greed, and ultimately causing a loss of discrimination and intelligence.

This passion and ignorance increases the hankering for material enjoyment. And what happens? A strong sense of lust provokes the accumulation of wealth and power. Driven by lusty desires, people are working hard day and night, I want more wealth, more power, more wealth, more power, more wealth, more power. And there is no end to this. You see around you, there are millionaires who want to become billionaires, and there are billionaires who want to become trillionaires. And where will this end? If one goes on trying to satisfy their desires and somehow they are successful, you think it is going to come to an end? What will the person do with, if somebody has billion dollars or billion rupees, what will he do with trillion rupees? He doesn't know what to do with billion rupees itself. So what will he do with trillion rupees? But they lose that sense of discrimination. They lose their intelligence. *Naśta buddhayaḥ kāmaiṣṭaiṣṭe hṛta-jñānaḥ prapadyante anyadevataḥ*. Bhagavad Gita says *naśta buddhayaḥ*. *Hṛta-jñānaḥ* means *naśta buddhayaḥ*. This is the acharya's explanation. Their knowledge becomes completely lost, means their intelligence is lost. They lose the power of discrimination. I have so much money, I can do whatever I want with that money, but whatever I am doing, I am not getting satisfaction. Then why do I think I will get satisfaction by accumulating more money? What will you do with that more money? Why don't you think about that? There is no thinking remaining because it is lost by this greed and lust.

---
### Purification, Goodness, Surrender, Bhakti Yoga
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Odmoah1S3Xs?t=862
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "By purifying this lust and greed, one comes to the platform of goodness."
**Summary:** This segment concludes by explaining that conquering lust and greed leads to goodness, which, while not complete freedom, allows one to control the senses, gain knowledge, and surrender to the Supreme Lord through Bhakti Yoga.

One who has conquered these two base mentalities, this lust and greed, *kāma lobhāda yasya ye, tadārajaṣṭamo bhāva kāma lobhāda yasya ye, cetaye te rana viddham*. When the heart becomes free from this lust and greed, that is a big step towards purification. But by purifying this lust and greed, one comes to the platform of goodness. And even in goodness, there is no freedom from material existence, but it is better. Why? Because in goodness, one may attempt to control the senses and get proper knowledge to be able to surrender to the lotus feet of the Supreme Lord Hari and thereby transcend all the influences. That is the Bhakti Yoga process. I will stop here. Thank you very much.

---

## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.13.55 | 2-11-2022
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### liberation, Ashtanga Yoga, Vedanta philosophy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/uQkM2LNV6Qk?t=51
**Verse References:** SB 1.13.55
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The Ashtanga Yoga system of practice is ultimately meant for liberation from material bondage."
**Summary:** This segment introduces the 55th verse of the 13th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam, explaining how it relates to Vedanta philosophy and the Ashtanga Yoga system for liberation from material bondage.

In this session, we will be discussing the 55th verse of the 13th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. The meaning of this verse is: Dhritarashtra will have to amalgamate his pure identity with intelligence and then merge into the supreme being with knowledge of his qualitative oneness as a living entity with the supreme Brahman, being freed from the blocked sky, he will have to rise to the spiritual sky. This is a typical philosophical verse in the Bhagavatam, which also is explaining the Vedanta philosophy. So what is that philosophy that is being explained here is simplified by Srila Prabhupada in the purport. So basically, in the verse, it is explaining densha. The Ashtanga Yoga system of practice is ultimately meant for liberation from material bondage, and how that liberation happens technically is being explained to some extent in this verse.

---
### desire, service, constitutional position
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/uQkM2LNV6Qk?t=143
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "If we don't have a desire to lord it over material nature, then we act in our constitutional position, in our original state as loving devotional servant of Krishna."
**Summary:** This segment elaborates on Srila Prabhupada's explanation that the living being's desire to lord over the material world and declining to cooperate with the Supreme Lord leads to material bondage, contrasting it with the blissful state of serving Krishna in one's constitutional position.

Srila Prabhupada attached in the purport, the living being, by His desiring to join it over the material world and declining to cooperate with the Supreme Lord, contacts the sum total of the material world. Let me go a little slow and let us try to understand what Srila Prabhupada is telling here. Each one of us is a living being, and the characteristic of every living being is the living being has the capacity or the ability to desire. Desire is integral with the existence as spirit soul, so you cannot be without desire at any stage, in any condition, in any place, in any situation. Any person cannot be desireless. So specifically, a certain type of desire, what is that desire? To lord it over the material world. I want to be the Lord. Now, who is the Lord actually? Krishna. Krishna is eternally the Lord of the material world, the spiritual world, in the whole existence. That is Krishna's position. Our position is not never to be the Lord, but if we develop a desire to be the Lord, and as a result of desiring to be the Lord, what happens next? We decline to cooperate with the Supreme Lord. If we don't have a desire to lord it over material nature, then we act in our constitutional position, in our original state as loving devotional servant of Krishna. So we are happily situated, completely blissfully nicely engaged in Krishna's service as a cooperator.

---
### illusion, Mahatatva, material energy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/uQkM2LNV6Qk?t=273
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Desiring to lord over and declining to cooperate with the supreme lord, the living entity contacts the sum total of the material world called Mahatatva."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that declining to serve Krishna leads to the desire to lord over, which is only possible under illusion in the material world, resulting in contact with the Mahatatva, the sum total of material energy.

Krishna never forces in the spiritual world anyone to serve him because he is not in need of service. It's for our own self-interest that we must serve Krishna, but how is the service rendered? Lovingly with devotion. Then we are happy, we are perfectly situated. But if we decline, I don't want to serve Krishna, then what do you want to do, sir? I want to lord over. So that lording over is only possible under illusion, and that illusion exists only in the material world. So therefore, desiring to lord over and declining to cooperate with the supreme lord, the living entity contacts the sum total of the material world called Mahatatva, the material world, the sum total of this material world, everything material, is the original substance or the energy called as Mahatatva. This is a technical term. It's called Mahatatva, the sum total of all material energy.

---
### false ego, identity, material covering
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/uQkM2LNV6Qk?t=375
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The contact of the living being with this Mahatatva, or the sum total of the material energy, gives rise to a false ego and gives rise to a particular kind of intelligence, and then a particular kind of mind, and particular set of senses."
**Summary:** This segment details how contact with Mahatatva leads to the development of false ego (ahankara), a particular kind of intelligence, mind, and senses, forming a material covering over the pure spiritual identity.

From this Mahatatva, from this sum total of the material energy, his false identity with the material world, false identity with the material world means my false ego, your false ego, each person's false ego, each person's ahankara. Ahankara is samskrit word, the English equivalent is false ego or false identity. So the contact of the living being with this Mahatatva or the sum total of the material energy gives rise to a false ego and gives rise to a particular kind of intelligence and then a particular kind of mind and particular set of senses. All these are developed by the living beings.

---
### spiritual identity, self-realization, yogic process
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/uQkM2LNV6Qk?t=472
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Self-realization means I realize myself as pure spiritual identity, completely apart from this material body, means material senses, apart from the material mind, apart from the material intelligence, apart from the material ego, I am pure spiritual identity."
**Summary:** The speaker describes the original spiritual identity of living beings in relationship with Krishna and how it becomes covered by Mahat Tattva, explaining that self-realization through yogic processes involves realizing one's pure spiritual identity apart from the material body and ego.

Originally we are all spiritual beings, pure spirit. We have our spiritual identity in relationship with Krishna. We have our spiritual senses, our spiritual mind, our spiritual intelligence, our spiritual identity as loving servitor of Krishna. But all that is covered when we come in contact with this mahat tattva, when a covering is generated suitable for the lording over. Now, by this yogic process, particularly the shtanga yoga process or any yogic process, when his pure identity is realized in sastra, self-realization, self-realization means I realize myself as pure spiritual identity, completely apart from this material body, means material senses, apart from the material mind, apart from the material intelligence, apart from the material ego, I am pure spiritual identity. One has to revert to the original position by amalgamating, amalgamating means bringing together, combining the five gross elements and the subtle elements mind and intelligence into the mahathuk va game.

---
### analogy, material problems, false identity
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/uQkM2LNV6Qk?t=580
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Self realization means we don't require this material dress because the cause of all our material problems in this material existence represents general of birth and death false understanding false identity all kinds of material misconceptions."
**Summary:** Using the analogy of a potter making a clay pot, the speaker illustrates how the pure spiritual soul takes on a material covering, emphasizing that self-realization means freeing oneself from this material dress, which is the cause of material problems and false identity.

It is just like if a potter is making a clay pot, he takes some clay and then he mixes it with water and then shapes it into a pot and then bakes that pot in fire. It becomes a pot. Now the pot is used and when the pot is destroyed what happens? The elements which went into making the pot are all separated. Fire is separated, then water is separated and the earth as clay goes back to the earth. Similarly, when we come in contact with this Mahat Tattva, the pure spiritual soul, then we take little false ego, little material intelligence, little material mind, little material senses, and then the material covering called the gross and the subtle body is prepared, and that becomes a dress for the spirit soul for the living being, material dress. But self realization means we don't require this material dress because the cause of all our material problems in this material existence represents general of birth and death false understanding false identity all kinds of material misconceptions, material miseries, material material uh uh material existence itself is completely unnecessary.

---
### spiritual identity, super soul, qualitative oneness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/uQkM2LNV6Qk?t=690
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Realizing the qualitative oneness means I am pure spiritual personality and that pure spiritual personality is eternally the servant of Krishna."
**Summary:** This segment explains that becoming free from material existence means realizing one's spiritual identity and merging into the super soul, which signifies realizing qualitative oneness, not becoming the super soul, but eternally remaining a loving, devotional servant of Krishna.

Becoming free from this material existence means becoming free of then what is left is the spiritual identity of the spiritual soul. Spirit soul in its pure identity is left behind and that has to merge into the super soul. Merge means become one and what is this becoming one actually mean? In other words, Prabhupada says he has to realize that qualitatively he is non-different from the super soul. It doesn't mean that I become the super soul. Merging into the super soul here does not mean I become the super soul. No, always my identity as servant of the supreme lord and supreme lord's identity as the master of everyone eternally remains. That never changes, that never is never is at any time lost. It always exists. So realizing the oneness, qualitative oneness means one in quality. I am spiritual by nature, super soul or quality. I am spiritual by nature, super soul or quality. I am spiritual by nature, super soul or I am servant. So non different means not that I the servant becomes the master. No, servant will never become the master. Master will never become the servant. Master is always master eternally. Krishna is always the master eternally and all the living entities are eternally servants. So, realizing the qualitative oneness means I am pure spiritual personality and that pure spiritual personality is eternally the servant of Krishna. What kind of servant? Loving, devotional servant and when this qualitative oneness is realized.
