## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.13.56 - 57 | 4-11-2022
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### subtle body, detachment, yoga
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/GvneEL2W7G8?t=259
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The yogi he is able to be aloof from the gross body and the subtle body."
**Summary:** The speaker explains how a spiritualist identifies with the subtle body rather than the gross body, and how a yogi can become detached from both.

That means what the senses, even the senses and this body will also deteriorate. As far as the subtle mind and intelligence are concerned, a spiritualist identifies from the material mind and the material intelligence and even the material ego because subtle matter is not exactly like this gross matter which is destructible. In the sense of the term destroying, we talk in terms of something gross, and that destruction also, what does it mean actually? Everything gross is a combination of one or more of the five elements: earth, water, fire, air, sky. So destruction means separating all the elements and returning them to the original reservoir of these five elements in the Mahatatva. As I explained the example of the pot in the previous session, the clay pot is formed or produced by combining earth in the form of clay and water and fire. So clay is first mixed with water and then the pot is given shape and then it is baked in the fire. So earth, water, fire, three are combined and the pot is produced by the potter. Now when it is destroyed means fire goes back to the fire element, water goes back to the water element, the reservoir of water element, and earth, the clay which is earth, nothing but earth, it is returned to the earthly reservoir. So like that there is no destruction of the mind, intelligence, and the false ego because they always exist as subtle elements within the gross body as a subtle bodily covering. So the yogi he is able to be aloof from the gross body and the subtle body.

---
### detachment, false ego, sense gratification
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/GvneEL2W7G8?t=401
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "If I think I am the body, then my satisfaction is satisfaction of the body."
**Summary:** Using the analogy of clothing, the speaker explains that a yogi understands they are not the body, and therefore are not affected by what happens to it, and that the yogic process helps one escape false enjoyment by stopping the pursuit of sense gratification.

And become completely immune to whatever is happening with the gross and subtle body without being affected or without worrying what is happening to me because he understands I am not this body, I am not the gross body, I am not the subtle body. It is like your dress. Supposing the dress has become old and sometimes a worn out dress may tear. So do you think that, oh! I am suffering some injury? Just like if there is something which creates a tear in your skin, some sharp instrument. Something sharp spears in your hand, arm and then there is a cut. So now when you know you are not this body. Supposing there is a cut, now you have no body. And then there is a cut. So now when you know you are not this body. So I have seen, supposing the cloth tears, either it's worn out or it is pierced by some sharp instrument. If the cloth tears, does it mean that something has happened to you or something has happened to your cloth? If you distinctly know you are different from the cloth, which a matured person, an intelligent person will always be aware of that. I am not this cloth. Then nothing has happened to you when the cloth is torn. And if it is old and worn out, you discard this cloth and you take a new cloth. Exactly like that the yogi knows. The body itself is an external covering and it has to be, one has to disidentify, I am not this body, I am spirit soul, spiritual person, personality, apart from the body. So, the yogic process helps a yogi to completely negate all these material coverings. The effect of the yogic process is, the effects of the material modes of nature draw the victim to indefatigable desires of enjoying matter. This desire for enjoying matter is because of the false identity that I am this body. If I think I am the body, then my satisfaction is satisfaction of the body. But if I realize I am not the body, then my happiness is not simply giving satisfaction to the senses, no. That is not going to make me happy. So, this false enjoyment by trying to satisfy the senses, external senses, is stopped by the yogic process. So, then one escapes false enjoyment by following the yogic process.

---
### sense gratification, maya, yoga
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/GvneEL2W7G8?t=581
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The conditioned soul is assaulted on all sides by maya."
**Summary:** The speaker describes how the senses are constantly searching for pleasure in the material world, and how the conditioned soul is assaulted by maya on all sides, making steadiness impossible, but the yogic process stops this pursuit and turns one inward.

Every sense is busy searching for its food. That means eyes are looking for beautiful form, ears are searching for some sweet sound. Nose is looking for some nice fragrance, nice smell, like that. The conditioned soul is assaulted on all sides by maya. Material world is filled with all these different sounds, different forms, different smells, aromas, different tastes, different tasting type of edibles, and different types of objects which are very pleasing to the sense of touch. So, there is no chance to become steady in any pursuit of even searching for some pleasing sense object. There is no question of steadiness. Because always if something beautiful attracts the person due to the beautiful form, immediately there will be some sound or some smell or some sense of touch. So, nobody can pursue this. There is no sense object in a steady way. Whereas the yogic processes stop this complete pursuit of sense enjoyment and go within, go within. That is the actual yogic process.

---
### detachment, yoga, mystic power
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/GvneEL2W7G8?t=696
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Perfect yogis, they don't depend on somebody else to burn this body to dispose of the remnants after death or after quitting the body."
**Summary:** The speaker explains how Narada advised Yudhishthira not to disturb Dhritarashtra because he had become detached through Ashtanga Yoga, and how perfect yogis can quit their bodies and burn them with self-generated fire.

Now, Maharaj Yudhishtira was advised by his uncle, sorry Maharaj Yudhishtira was advised by Narada not to disturb his uncle by attempting to bring him back home. Why? Because Dhritarashtra by practicing the Ashtanga Yoga he had already become detached from everything material. So, there will be no more any interest or attraction for Dhritarashtra to come and stay comfortably with Yudhishtira. He is beyond that. Nirguna means without reaction. The spiritual quality is distinguished from the material qualities by the word nirguna. Nirguna means the spiritual state where one is indifferent to all material attractions, all material objects, all material actions and reactions. One becomes indifferent, disinterested, completely aloof. The next verse, 57th verse. Sava adhyatanadrajan parata panchame ahani kalevaram hasyati svam tachabhasmi bhavishyati. The meaning of this verse is, O king, he will quit his body most probably on the fifth day from today and his body will turn to ashes. So, Narada is telling that Dhritarashtra will perfect his yogic practice that he becomes detached and after detaching he will quit his body by his own mystic power and that mystic power will also enable him to burn the body to ashes, what he is leaving behind. The body will be burned to ashes by the self-generated fire by the yogic process itself. So, perfect yogis, they don't depend on somebody else to burn this body to dispose of the remnants after death or after quitting the body. No, they are able to generate the fire from within to burn the body and the spirit soul cannot be burned by fire. The spirit soul will leave the body by mystic power. So, that is explained here. So, Yudhishthira need not perform any funeral ceremony for Dhritarashtra because Dhritarashtra by the yogic process would perfectly be able to leave the body and also burn the body at the time of quitting, burn the body by self-generated fire. These are all very special facilities for the yogi, Ashtanga yogi. I will stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.13.58 - 60 | 5-11-2022
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### chastity, sati rite, voluntary action
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/mSh0RtBHu_0?t=60
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "It's totally voluntary for a chaste wife to enter the funeral pyre of her husband; it should never be forced by anyone else."
**Summary:** This segment explains Gandhari's sati rite, emphasizing its voluntary nature and contrasting it with the later criminal practice of forcing widows to die.

The meaning of this verse is, while outside observing her husband who will burn in the fire of mystic power along with his thatched cottage, his chaste wife Gandhari will enter the fire with rapt attention. So, now already Narada has told Yudhishthira, Dhritarashtra will follow the yogic process and quit the body and the body will be burnt by the self-generated fire through the mystic power. Now, along with Dhritarashtra's body burning, the thatched cottage in which he was sitting and performing his austerity or the practice itself will burn. And what will Gandhari do? She is not practicing any yoga. She is just faithfully as a chaste wife following her husband. So, Srila Prabhupada explains, Gandhari was an ideal chaste lady, a life companion of her husband. And therefore, when she saw her husband burning in the fire of mystic yoga along with his cottage of leaves, she despaired. Naturally, a wife is very faithfully serving the husband and the husband quits, it's a big loss for the wife because she has always been with him as a chaste wife and she has been serving him. Now, what will she do when he is no longer present in the form as the husband? So, it says here, Srila Prabhupada explains, she left home after losing her husband. So, it says here, Srila Prabhupada explains, she left home after losing her 100 sons and in the forest she saw that her most beloved husband was also burning. She lost her sons in the battle of Kurukshetra. Our husband also, he quit his body which was a self-generated fire, through mystic yoga practice. What will she do? So, Prabhupada says, now she actually felt alone and followed her husband to death. Death she decided to also quit the body now this entering of a chaste lady into the fire of a dead husband is called the sati rite. You would have heard this term, sati, sati, sati ritual, sati rite. What is it? The wife has lost her husband and does not have any surviving sons. Such a wife, if she is a chaste woman, she will enter the fire of her dead husband and quit her body voluntarily and the action, this sati ritual or the sati rite, is considered to be the most perfect for a woman. How is it most perfect? Because Prabhupada says for the woman the fire of separation from her husband is more intolerable than the fire of the burning of the husband's dead body so she decides to quit voluntarily and such quitting the body voluntarily is considered to be the most successful way to get her body into. So Srila Prabhupada says in a later age this sati rite became an obnoxious criminal affair. It became criminal why? Because the ceremony was forced upon even an unwilling woman. See, mind you, it is totally voluntary for a chaste wife to enter the funeral pyre of her husband.

---
### voluntary action, liberation, grahastha dharma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/mSh0RtBHu_0?t=358
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Following her husband, that means she also desires liberation from material bondage, and in following the footsteps of her husband who is also becoming liberated, she will also attain liberation."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes the voluntary nature of the sati rite, contrasting it with forced practices, and explains how a chaste wife following her husband in spiritual activities can attain liberation.

It's totally voluntary. It should never be forced by anyone else. No one can, no one should force, no one has the right to force, but when people started forcing a widow, a woman, that became criminal. It is criminal to force somebody to die in this way. It is criminal. So, Srila Prabhupada says in this present age, fallen age, it is not possible for anyone to follow the sati ritual as Gandhari did and others chaste women did in the past. Why? Because a chaste wife like Gandhari would feel the separation of her husband to be more burning than actual fire. Such a lady can actually observe the sati right voluntarily and there is no criminal being forced by anyone. So, her destination will be a glorious destination because she is voluntarily quitting her body following her husband. That means she also desires liberation from material bondage and in following the footsteps of her husband who is also becoming liberated, she will also attain liberation so she doesn't have to separately practice. That is the grahastha dharma, the wife is chaste, she follows her husband especially in his spiritual duties or spiritual activities, she follows her husband and she simply gets the same benefit as the husband without her having to actually practice the details of all the spiritual practices.

---
### liberation, devotion, mercy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/mSh0RtBHu_0?t=487
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "An offense at the feet of a Vaishnava is more dangerous than an offense at the feet of the Lord."
**Summary:** This segment discusses Vidura's mixed feelings about Dhritarashtra's liberation, explaining that while Vidura was glad Dhritarashtra attained liberation, he was sorry he couldn't make him a pure devotee due to Dhritarashtra's inimical feelings towards the Pandavas.

So this is the wonderful destination of Gandhari. The 59th verse, vidurasthu tad ascharyam nishamya kurunandana harsha shokayutastasmad gantatirtha nisevakah. The meaning of this verse is, Vidura being affected with delight and grief will then leave that place of sacred pilgrimage. Vidura is observing all this. He is gone with Dhritarashtra and Gandhari because he only was the inspiration for Dhritarashtra to quit home. So he went with Dhritarashtra and he saw what did Dhritarashtra do? He followed Vidura's direction and he attained perfection and Gandhari followed as a chaste wife, she followed her husband. So Vidura is delighted, but why is it described he was also having that grief? Prabhupada explains Vidura was astonished to see the marvelous departure of his brother Dhritarashtra as a liberated yogi for in his past life, that is Dhritarashtra's past life, he was very much attached to materialism. Of course, the fact is that it was only due to Vidura that Dhritarashtra attained the desirable goal of life. So Vidura was glad to learn about this Dhritarashtra attaining the ultimate goal, that is liberation from material bondage, but Vidura was sorry that Vidura could not make Dhritarashtra a pure monk because he was feigned sobriety for his pure devotee. Why? Why Vidura could not make Dhritarashtra pure devotee? Because Dhritarashtra was inimical to the Pandavas who were all devotees of the Lord. Therefore Vidura could not make Dhritarashtra pure devotee. An offense at the feet of a Vaishnava is more dangerous than an offense at the feet of the Lord. Vidura was certainly very liberal to bestow mercy upon Dhritarashtra whose past life was very materialistic but ultimately the result of all such mercy depends on the will of the Lord who grants spiritual advancement, who grants spiritual liberation, who grants spiritual benefits.

---
### devotion, Lord's will, spiritual advancement
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/mSh0RtBHu_0?t=653
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Devotees may help, devotees may guide, devotees may direct, devotees may inspire but devotees cannot give anything. It is the Lord only who gives."
**Summary:** This segment emphasizes that while devotees can guide and inspire, only the Lord can grant spiritual advancement and liberation, and that Dhritarashtra can attain devotional service only after many lifetimes of liberation.

It's only the will of the Lord. It's only the Lord. Devotees may help, devotees may guide, devotees may direct, devotees may inspire but devotees cannot give anything. It is the Lord only who gives. Lord is completely under the control, in control of a spiritual potency and by spiritual potency the Lord gives spiritual advancement, spiritual benefits, spiritual liberation, spiritual engagement, everything. So Dhritarashtra attained liberation only but Vidura, sorry Dhritarashtra can, Dhritarashtra attained liberation only but he can attain the stage of devotional service after many many many such liberated states of life. So, bahu nam janma namante dhyana vanamaam prapadyate. One is able to properly surrender to Krishna only after many many lifetimes of living that stage of liberation in proper understanding of one's spiritual identity, in relationship with Krishna. That relationship means, repenting, breathing in words, expressing self forced deeds, confession of great freedom in the spiritual realm, and knowing the divine gift and the real spiritual power, logging into the terms and forms of existence of our forest. No more inimical feeling towards devotees or the Supreme Lord.

---
### liberation, devotion, Narada Muni
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/mSh0RtBHu_0?t=752
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Become a devotee and go back to the spiritual kingdom of Kṛṣṇa. That's where we belong."
**Summary:** This segment explains the last verse, describing Narada's ascent to outer space and Yudhishthira's freedom from lamentation, and encourages listeners to follow Narada's example by becoming devotees and returning to the spiritual kingdom of Krishna.

The last verse of this chapter, sixtieth verse, ityuktvā atha āruhat svargam nārada sahatumburuhu yuriṣṭiro vachastasya hṛdī pritvā jahā chuchah. The meaning of this last verse, sixtieth verse is, having spoken thus, the great sage Nārada, along with his veena, ascended into outer space. Yuriṣṭira kept his instruction in his heart and so was able to get to the fall lamentation. Yuriṣṭira was lamenting that my uncle left home and went away. I don't know where he has gone. Nārada informed him where Dhritarashtra has gone. Nārada informed him what will happen to Dhritarashtra, that Dhritarashtra will be liberated. And therefore, Yuriṣṭira, keeping the instruction of Nārada in his heart, he became free from all lamentation. Now, Nārada is described here by Prabhupāda as an eternal spaceman. He is having a spiritual body, endowed by the Supreme Lord himself. He can travel in outer space of both the material and the spiritual worlds. He is free to travel anywhere, without any restrictions. And he can approach any planet, either in the material world or the spiritual sky. Spiritual sky are the Vaikuntha Lokas. In the material world, there are different kinds of upper planetary systems, Svarga Loka, Maharloka, Janaloka, Tapoloka, Brahmaloka, or the earthly planets, Bhūloka, Bhūvarloka, or even the lower planets, Atala, Vitala, Sutala, anywhere he can go. And why does he travel? He simply travels to preach the message of Kṛṣṇa about bhakti and to deliver people, to liberate people from material bondage. So, one should try to follow in the footsteps of Nārada Muni and not make any attempt to go to some heavenly planets by some material activities. No. You should follow in the footsteps of Nārada Muni. Become a devotee and go back to the spiritual kingdom of Kṛṣṇa. That's where we belong.

## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.13.8 | 24-09-2022
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### invocation, prayer
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/bDU3necB7wM?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya Naram Jai Vanarottamam Devi Saraswati Vyasam Tato Jayam Udi Rayet"
**Summary:** The lecture begins with traditional Vaishnava prayers and invocations.

Om Agyana Timiraandasya Gyananjanishalakaya Chaksharunmeelitam Yena Tasmai Shri Gurave Namaha. Namah Om Vishnupadaya Krishna Preshtaya Bhutale Srimate Bhaktivedanta Swamin Nithinamine. Namaste Saraswate Deve Gauravani Pracharine Nirvishesha Shunyavadi Pashchatyade Shatarine. Jaya Shri Krishna Chaitanya Prabhu Nityananda Shri Advaita Gadadhara Sri Vasadi Gauravakta Vrinda. Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare. Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya Naram Jai Vanarottamam Devi Saraswati Vyasam Tato Jayam Udi Rayet.

In this session we will be discussing from the 8th verse of the 13th chapter of the 1st canto of Sriman Bhagavatam. Yudhishthira Uvacha Api Smaratha No Yushmat Paksha Chhaya Same Dhitam Vipad Gannat Vishag Nyader Mochitayat Samatra Kaha. Your partiality like the wings of a bird saved us from poisoning and arson.' Now Srila Prabhupada explains this. What is this poisoning and arson?

### Mahatma, devotion, spiritual advancement
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/bDU3necB7wM?t=99
**Verse References:** BG 9.13
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "It is reserved for great devotees according to the Bhagavad Gita."
**Summary:** The speaker explains Srila Prabhupada's purport, focusing on Vidura's title of 'Mahatma' and its significance in relation to spiritual advancement and devotion to Krishna, referencing the Bhagavad Gita.

In the purport Srila Prabhupada says, due to Pandu's death at an early age, his minor children and widow were the object of special care by the elderly men, the family members of the family, especially Bhishma Deva and Mahatma Vidura. Vidura is addressed as Mahatma because he was not only very conscious of his responsibility as an elderly member of the family, but he was also spiritually self-realized, spiritually advanced, and self-realized. After him, his travelling to pace the pilgrimage and inquiring from Maitreya Rishi and becoming spiritually enlightened. So therefore he is addressed as Mahatma Vidura. This Mahatma title is not for simply somebody great by some material estimation. No. It is reserved for great devotees according to the Bhagavad Gita.

### devotion, politics, family intrigue
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/bDU3necB7wM?t=177
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Dhritarashtra had an inner desire that somehow his own sons should become the emperors of the world and not the sons of Pandu."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the meaning of 'Mahatma' according to the Bhagavad Gita, then discusses Vidura's partiality towards the Pandavas due to their vulnerable position and Dhritarashtra's desire for his own sons to rule.

Mahatma anastumam partha Daiveem prakritim ashritah Bhajanti ananya manaso Gyaatva bhutadim abhyayam. According to the Bhagavad Gita, who is to be called a Mahatma? One who has taken shelter of Krishna's internal spiritual potency and the symptom of having taken shelter of Krishna's internal potency is that such a person is always engaged in pure devotional service to Krishna without any interruption. So Vidura was such a pure devotee of Krishna. Therefore he is addressed as Mahatma Vidura. Vidura was more or less partial to the Pandavas due to their political position. The Pandavas were fatherless. Kunti was staying with her minor sons in the palace of Dhritarashtra. Dhritarashtra was the Ketakar king, but Dhritarashtra was not treating the Pandavas equally with his own sons because Dhritarashtra had an inner desire that somehow his own sons should become the emperors of the world and not the sons of Pandu who are the rightful claimants to the throne of Hastinapura. So...

### intrigue, protection, family dynamics
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/bDU3necB7wM?t=279
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Mahatma Vidura could follow this intrigue of Dhritarashtra and company and therefore even though he was a faithful servitor of his eldest brother Dhritarashtra, he did not like Dhritarashtra's political ambition for the sake of his own sons."
**Summary:** The speaker details Duryodhana's plot to kill the Pandavas and Dhritarashtra's support of his son, highlighting Vidura's awareness of the intrigue and his efforts to protect the Pandavas despite his loyalty to Dhritarashtra.

Duryodhana plotted to actually kill the Pandavas before they could actually become adults and supported his son Duryodhana in all his nefarious activities. So Srila Prabhupada explains, although Dhritarashtra was equally careful for the minor children of Maharaja Pandu, he was one of the intriguing parties who wanted to wash away the descendants of Pandu and replace them by raising his own sons to become rulers of the kingdom. Mahatma Vidura could follow this intrigue of Dhritarashtra and company and therefore even though he was a faithful servitor of his eldest brother Dhritarashtra, he did not like Dhritarashtra's political ambition for the sake of his own sons. Therefore Vidura was very careful about the protection of the Pandavas and their widow mother Kunti. Thus he was so to speak partial to the Pandavas, preferring them to the sons of Dhritarashtra although both of them were equally affectionate in his ordinary eyes.

### justice, restraint, palace politics
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/bDU3necB7wM?t=371
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "He always chastised Duryodhana for his intriguing policy against his cousins. He always criticized his elder brother Dhritarashtra for his policy of encouragement to his sons. At the same time, he was always alert in giving special protection to the Pandavas."
**Summary:** The speaker describes Vidura's balanced approach, showing affection to both Pandavas and Kauravas while also chastising Duryodhana and criticizing Dhritarashtra's policies, and emphasizing Vidura's role in protecting the Pandavas.

See how Vidura is carefully on the one hand trying to protect the Pandavas. On the other hand, he is also showing equal affection to the Pandavas and Kauravas because both of them are sons of his two brothers. He was equally affectionate to both the camps of Nebus in the sense that he always chastised Duryodhana for his intriguing policy against his cousins. He always criticized his elder brother Dhritarashtra for his policy of encouragement to his sons. At the same time, he was always alert in giving special protection to the Pandavas. All these different activities of Vidura within the palace politics made him well known as partial to the Pandavas. Maharaj Yudhishthira has referred to the past history of Vidura before his going away from home for a prolonged pilgrim's journey. Maharaj Yudhishthira reminded Vidura that he was equally kind and partial to his grown up nephews even after the battle of Kurukshetra, a great family disaster.

### deception, annihilation, intrigue
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/bDU3necB7wM?t=451
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Peaceful annihilation means they should be eliminated in such a way that nobody will suspect any foul play."
**Summary:** The speaker explains Dhritarashtra's policy of 'peaceful annihilation' of the Pandavas before the battle of Kurukshetra, detailing his plan to eliminate them without raising suspicion so his own sons could inherit the throne.

So Srila Prabhupada explains, before the battle of Kurukshetra, Dhritarashtra's policy was peaceful annihilation of his nephews. Peaceful annihilation means they should be eliminated in such a way that nobody will suspect any foul play. It should seem like they died somehow and then because nobody is there to ascend the throne as descendants of Pandu, therefore Dhritarashtra's sons will become the claimants to the throne of Hastinapura. So this was Dhritarashtra's policy and plan. And what did he do? Dhritarashtra do? He ordered Purochana to build a house at Varanavata and when the building was finished, Dhritarashtra desired that his brother's family live there for some time because the Pandavas and Kauravas were sharing that palace in Hastinapura. So Dhritarashtra said to the Pandavas, 'You can go and live there comfortably. This palace is for you. Separately you can live.'

### warning, protection, indirect communication
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/bDU3necB7wM?t=537
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "A weapon not made of steel or any other material element can be more sharp to kill an enemy and he who knows this is never killed."
**Summary:** The speaker recounts how Vidura tactfully warned Yudhishthira about Dhritarashtra's plan when the Pandavas were sent to Varanavata, using indirect language and riddles to convey the danger.

When the Pandavas were going there in the presence of all the members of the royal family, Vidura tactfully gave instructions to the Pandavas about the future plan of Dhritarashtra. The Pandavas did not know what was Dhritarashtra's plan, but Vidura knew and Vidura gave instructions to Yudhishthira very very tactfully about Dhritarashtra coming to know about it. So this is specifically described in the Mahabharata, Adiparva 114. He indirectly hinted. What did he say? What did Vidura say to Yudhishthira? 'A weapon not made of steel or any other material element can be more sharp to kill an enemy and he who knows this is never killed.' See, in the form of a riddle, he is telling something to Yudhishthira. That is to say, Vidura hinted that the party of the Pandavas was being sent to Varanavata to be killed and thus he warned Yudhishthira to be very careful in their new residential palace. He also gave indications of fire and said that fire cannot extinguish the soul but can annihilate the material body, but one who protects the soul can live.

---
### escape, knowledge, protection
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/bDU3necB7wM?t=629
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "By his warning, that is Vidura's warning, Pandavas escaped through a tunnel underneath the earth so that their escape was also unknown to Dhritarashtra."
**Summary:** The speaker continues the story, explaining how Kunti couldn't understand the indirect conversation, but Yudhishthira understood the hint of fire. Vidura later came in disguise to inform them of the housekeeper's plan to set fire to the house, enabling their escape through a tunnel.

Kunti could not follow such indirect conversations between Maharaj Yudhishthira and Vidura and thus when she inquired from her son about the purport of the conversation, Yudhishthira replied to Kunti Devi that from the talks of Vidura it was understood that there was a hint of fire proceeding. Later on Vidura came in disguise to the Pandavas and informed them that the housekeeper was going to set fire to the house on the fourteenth night of the waning moon. It was an intrigue of Dhritarashtra that the Pandavas might die altogether with their mother. By his warning, that is Vidura's warning, Pandavas escaped through a tunnel underneath the earth so that their escape was also unknown to Dhritarashtra. So much so that after setting the fire, the Kauravas were so certain of the death of the Pandavas that Dhritarashtra performed the last rites of their death with great cheerfulness. During the morning period, all the members of the palace became overwhelmed with lamentation, but Vidura did not become so because Vidura had knowledge that the Pandavas were alive somewhere.

### fairness, restraint, gratitude
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/bDU3necB7wM?t=739
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Vidura was always partial to the Pandavas just as a bird protects its eggs by its wing."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes by summarizing Vidura's role in palace politics, his attempts to restrain Dhritarashtra, and his warnings to the Pandavas, emphasizing his partiality towards them as a form of protection, and Yudhishthira's gratitude for Vidura's help.

There are many such instances of calamities and in each of them Vidura gave protection to the Pandavas on one hand and on the other he tried to restrain his brother Dhritarashtra from such intriguing policies. See how Vidura played a fair role in the palace politics. He tried to prevent his brother Dhritarashtra from doing injustice to the Pandavas and he also kept warning the Pandavas so that Pandavas might not be killed by Dhritarashtra's intrigues. Therefore it is described here that Vidura was always partial to the Pandavas just as a bird protects its eggs by its wing. It is natural when a bird lays an egg so that the egg can eventually mature and hatch into a young one. So before the egg hatches, the bird has to protect that egg against attack by enemies or by some other birds which may come and or some reptiles can come and eat away the egg. So the bird has to protect. So like that Vidura in order to protect the Pandavas against being killed by Dhritarashtra and Duryodhana and the Kauravas, he had to protect them very very carefully. So this was the special protection of Vidura and therefore Yudhishthira makes a reference to this after Yudhishthira returned after the battle of Kurukshetra, how because of Vidura's protection Yudhishthira and his brothers and even Kunti were actually protected against being completely killed and eliminated by Dhritarashtra and company. So I will stop here. Thank you very much. Thank you very much.

**Verse References:** SB 1.13.9
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** The speaker introduces the verses to be discussed, Srimad Bhagavatam 1.13.9-10, focusing on Yudhishthira Maharaj's inquiry to Vidura about his travels and means of sustenance.

Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya Naramchaiva Narottamam. Deveem Saraswateem Vyasam Tato Jayam Udi Rayet. Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya Naramchaiva Narottamam. In this session, we will be discussing from the 9th and the 10th verses of the 13th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. The 9th verse is as follows: Kaya Vritya Vartitam Vaha Charadbhi Kshiti Mandalam Tirthani Kshetra Mukhyani Sevitaniha Bhutale. The meaning of this verse is: While travelling on the surface of the earth, how did you maintain your livelihood? At which holy places and pilgrimage sites did you render service? This particular verse is spoken by Yudhishthira Maharaj while enquiring from Vidura about Vidura's travels before he reached Hastinapura, the palace where Yudhishthira was staying and ruling from.

### Vidura, Duryodhana, insult
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/T_xQy6iYHW0?t=116
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Vidura was giving very good advice to Dhritarashtra, and this was not to the liking of Duryodhana."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the background of Vidura's departure from the palace, focusing on the insult he received from Duryodhana, who called him the son of a Shudrani, and how this led to Vidura's decision to leave.

So in the Purport Shrila Prabhupada says about Vidura's travel, Vidura went out from the palace to detach himself from household affairs, especially political intrigues. As referred to here and before, he was practically insulted by Duryodhana's calling him a son of a Shudrani. If you remember the background, Vidura was staying in the palace with Dhritarashtra while Dhritarashtra was the caretaker king before the battle of Kurukshetra. And Vidura as a well-wisher and a younger brother of Dhritarashtra, a step-brother, but still nevertheless an affectionate and younger brother, step-brother of Dhritarashtra, Vidura was giving very good advice to Dhritarashtra. And Vidura was very, very intelligent. The fact is Vidura had appeared as the son of a Shudrani. And Vidura had appeared as the son of a Shudrani. And Duryodhana found Vidura to be not favorable for his nefarious activities in plotting to kill the Pandavas. Even though Duryodhana had the support of his father, still Vidura was advising Dhritarashtra to be favorable. Still Vidura was advising Dhritarashtra to be favorable. Still Vidura was advising Dhritarashtra to be favorable. And this was not to the liking of Duryodhana.

### Vidura, Dhritarashtra, lineage
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/T_xQy6iYHW0?t=241
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Vidura was born out of this maidservant of the queen. Therefore, he was considered the son of a maidservant, a Shudrani."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the circumstances of Vidura's birth, explaining how he was born from the maidservant of Dhritarashtra's mother, Ambika, making him the son of a Shudrani and ineligible for royal inheritance.

So Duryodhana at one stage, he insulted Vidura by calling him the son of a Shudrani. And Srila Prabhupada says, although it is not out of place to talk loosely in the case of one's grandmother, although it is not out of place to talk loosely in the case of one's grandmother, that is Vidura's mother, although a Shudrani, was the grandmother of Duryodhana. How was Vidura's mother a Shudrani, the grandmother of Duryodhana? Duryodhana's father, Dhritarashtra, Dhritarashtra's mothers were the wives of Dhritarashtra's father. Dhritarashtra's father had actually... two wives, Ambika and Ambalika, Pichitravirya, Dhritarashtra's father, was having two wives. And Dhritarashtra's mother, Ambika, her maidservant was a Shudrani. And Vidura was born out of this maidservant of the queen. So therefore, Dhritarashtra was the son of the king and queen, but Vidura was the son of the maidservant of the queen. Therefore, Vidura was considered the son of a maidservant, a Shudrani. Therefore, he was not entitled to an inheritance in the royal kingdom.

### Vidura, renunciation, insult
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/T_xQy6iYHW0?t=377
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Because the remark was an actual fact, it was unpalatable talk to Vidura when Vidura was told that he was son of a Shudrani by Duryodhana."
**Summary:** The speaker continues to explain how, despite being well-treated by Dhritarashtra, Vidura was deeply affected by Duryodhana's insult, leading him to renounce his position and prepare for a life of spiritual pursuit.

So, Dhritarashtra was maintaining Vidura very affectionately, very nicely in the palace. And he was giving much value to the valuable property of Vidura. So, Dhritarashtra was the son of a maidservant, a Shudrani. Therefore, he was not entitled to an inheritance in the royal kingdom. So, Dhritarashtra was maintaining Vidura very affectionately, very nicely in the palace. And he was giving much value to the valuable property of Vidura, valuable advice being given by Vidura, which is not to the liking of Duryodhana. So, Srila Prabhupada says Vidura's mother, although a Shudrani, was a grandmother of Duryodhana and funny talks are sometimes allowed between grandmother and grandchildren. So, even if Duryodhana's remark might have been considered as a humorous remark, but because the remark was an actual fact, it was unpalatable talk to Vidura when Vidura was told that he was son of a Shudrani by Duryodhana. Then, because it was an actual fact, it was unpalatable to Vidura and it was accepted as a direct insult. So, Vidura therefore decided to quit his paternal house and prepare for the renounced order of life.

In the holy places of India, like Vrindavan, Haridwar, Jagannath Puri and Prayag, there are many great devotees and there are still free kitchen houses for purification. There are many persons who desire to advance spiritually. This is one special feature of India or Bharata Varsha that people are spiritually enlightened. Therefore, they travel to holy places and take association of great devotees in those holy places and reside in faith. In the holy places of India, there are many great devotees and there are still free kitchen houses called Kshatras. Shri La Prabhupada says, Maharaj Yudhishthira was inquisitive to learn whether Vidura maintained himself by the mercy of the free kitchen houses.

### Srimad Bhagavatam, chronology, questions and answers
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/gU44V7gUdck?t=46
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The sequence of description in the Bhagavatam follows the questions and answers."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces Chapter 14 of the First Canto of Srimad Bhagavatam, explaining that the Bhagavatam's structure is based on questions and answers rather than strict chronological order, emphasizing the importance of the message over sequential events.

We are going to begin a new chapter, chapter 14 of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. The chapter is entitled 'The Disappearance of Lord Krishna.' This is the speciality of the Bhagavatam. In the Bhagavatam, the topics are not necessarily chronologically described. Chronologically means first this happened, then this happened, then something else happened, then something else happened, then something else happened. No, the sequence of description in the Bhagavatam follows the questions and answers, the questions and the answers. So, according to the question being asked, the answer is given. And the answer is given with support of the historical incidents, describing certain incidents, describing certain important principles illustrated in the life of great devotees or taught by the activities of the Supreme Lord in his various incarnations, etc. So, the disappearance of Lord Krishna is described in the first canto.

---
### verse, translation, Arjuna's visit

**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** The speaker begins to explain the first verse of the 14th chapter, focusing on Arjuna's visit to Dvaraka to see Lord Krishna and inquire about the Lord's future plans.

So the first verse of this 14th chapter of the first canto of Bhagavatam is as follows: sutavvacha samprasthite dvarakayam jisnau bandhu didrikshaya nyatum cha punya shlokasya krishnasya cha vicheshtitam. The meaning of this verse is: Sri Suta Goswami said, Suta Goswami speaking to the sage sunaimisharanya, he said Arjuna went to Dvaraka to see Lord Krishna and other friends and also to learn from the Lord of his next activities.

### Yudhishthira, Arjuna, Lord's mission
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/gU44V7gUdck?t=205
**Verse References:** BG 4.7-8
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The Lord descended on earth for the protection of the faithful and annihilation of the empires."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the context of Arjuna's visit, explaining that Yudhishthira sent Arjuna to Dvaraka to ascertain Lord Krishna's next program after the completion of his mission on Earth, which included the battle of Kurukshetra and the establishment of Yudhishthira as emperor.

So Krishna is ruling, sorry Yudhishthira is ruling in Hastinapura and then he is properly perfectly ruling and Dhritarashtra has quit home, he is liberated. Now after that what did Yudhishthira do? So it is explained in this verse that Yudhishthira sent Arjuna to Dvaraka to find out what is the next program of the Lord. So Srila Prabhupada explains as stated in Bhagavad Gita the Lord descended on earth for the protection of the faithful and annihilation of the empires. So after the battle of Kurukshetra and establishment of Maharaja Yudhishthira as the emperor of the entire world the mission of the Lord was complete.

### foul means, occupational duty, spiritual realization, Krishna consciousness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/gU44V7gUdck?t=858
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "To remain free from all these bad qualities is to be Krishna conscious. And there is no other way."
**Summary:** The speaker defines 'foul means of livelihood' as deviation from one's prescribed occupational duty and explains that greed and other negative qualities arise from forgetting the goal of spiritual realization, emphasizing that Krishna consciousness is the only way to remain free from these qualities.

Now what is the meaning of foul means of livelihood? It means deviation from one's occupational duty. There are prescribed duties meant for the Brahmana, Kshatriya, Vaishya, Shudra. But if a Brahmana without doing his Brahmanical duties, he performs the duties of Kshatriya or Vaishya, Shudra, then that is called foul means of livelihood. Or somebody becomes too greedy for wealth. That is also incorrect, improper. Such a person will become greedy for wealth only if he has no higher objective in life. The goal of life is spiritual realization. But if somebody forgets that goal or misses that goal or does not care for that goal, then such a person has to be affected by greed, anger, corruption, etc. To remain free from all these bad qualities is to be Krishna conscious. And there is no other way.

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** The speaker introduces the 10th verse of the 14th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam, focusing on Yudhishthira's address to Bhima and explaining the concept of 'tapatraya' or the three-fold miseries inherent in material existence.

In this session we will be discussing from the 10th verse of the 14th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. Pashyot Patan Naravyagra Divyaan Bhauman Sadaihi Kaan Darunan Shamsato Durat Bhayamno Buddhi Mohanam. The meaning of this verse is spoken by Yudhishthira to Bhima. So he is addressing Bhima. Jasi O man with the tiger strength. The Sanskrit is Naravyagra. Naravyagra. Three-fold miseries, miseries due to three different causes, which is always there in material existence. Tapatraya, in Kannada they say tapatraya. What is this tapatraya? Tapa means miseries, traya means three kinds. Three kinds of fundamental miseries are always part of this material existence and they have been described here as divyaan, bhavmaan, daihikaan.

### suffering, karma, devotional service

**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes that material science cannot counteract the threefold miseries, which are punishments from Maya. He advocates for performing one's duties while remaining connected to Krishna through devotional service as the means to alleviate suffering.

More suffering due to increased reactions of the threefold miseries by the celestial influence of the stars there are many calamities like excessive heat cold rains or no rains and after effects of famine disease epidemic the aggregate result is the agony of the body and the mind whichever the type of misery ultimately it affects our body or our mind or both man-made material science cannot do anything to counteract these threefold miseries these miseries have always existed it's not in modern times we have eliminated some of the miseries no they've always existed and they continue to harass us in this material world so Srila Prabhupada says they are all punishments from the superior energy of Maya under the direct advice of Upadhyaya hi this is the clear statement of the pray or not we are all punished by the laws of nature laws of nature are the same restriction of supreme law we violate we misbehave and therefore we are punished by the laws of nature laws of nature are fair they're perfectly just but we are the ones who are individually and collectively violating the laws and we are to suffer the consequences of our human duties. Does it mean we stop working? No. We have to do our duties but we have to do it being in constant touch with Krishna through devotional service. And that alone can ensure relief for us from all these miseries without our being disturbed in performing our duties. ### omens, disappearance of Krishna, concern
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/LKYq3LNn7jk?t=68
**Verse References:** SB 1.14.12
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Yudhishthira is observing some bad omens, and he's conjecturing that it may indicate the disappearance of Krishna."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces verse 12, explaining that Yudhishthira is observing bad omens and expressing his concern to Bhima about the possible disappearance of Lord Krishna, as Arjuna has not returned from Dwaraka.

The 12th verse Shivai Shodhyantam Adityam Abhirauti Analanana Mamanga Sarameyoyam Abhirebhati Abhiruvat. The meaning of this verse is, just see, O Bhima, how the she jackal cries at the rising sun and vomits fire, and how the dog barks at me fearlessly. So in the purport Shri Leprava says, these are words spoken by Yudhishthira to Bhima. Yudhishthira is observing some bad omens, and he's conjecturing that it may indicate the disappearance of Krishna. But, he has sent Arjuna to find out Krishna's next program to Dwaraka, and Arjuna has not returned for 7 months. So Yudhishthira is becoming concerned, what could be the reason why Arjuna has not returned? Is everything alright in Dwaraka? Has the Lord left this earth planet? This is the concern of Yudhishthira.

---
### omens, auspiciousness, material vs spiritual
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/LKYq3LNn7jk?t=143
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "In the spiritual world, there is all auspiciousness; there is no inauspiciousness."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the science of 'Nimitta,' explaining that bad omens indicate undesirable events and contrasting the material world, where auspicious and inauspicious signs exist, with the spiritual world, which is purely auspicious.

So he is telling Bhima, oh! telling Bhima about the bad omens that he is observing. So, in the purportional pravapa says just one sentence, these are some bad omens indicating something undesirable in the near future. There is a science in the Vedic literature called science of nimitta. Nimitta means certain indications by which we can say we can expect something auspicious to happen or some other indications which indicate to us that something inauspicious is going to happen. So, in this material world always there is consideration of auspicious symptoms and inauspicious symptoms. In the spiritual world, there is all auspiciousness; there is no inauspiciousness. So, this difference always exists between the material world and the spiritual world.

### omens, scripture, superstition

Well, some people do have some superstitious beliefs which are not based on scriptures that is all right. But if the scriptures are informing us of some inauspicious happenings that we can expect undesirables we can expect in the near future by some bad omens and they are very specific. How they are so sensitive to note all these indications of something inauspicious or undesirable that is going to happen. People were living in harmony with nature. This is the point I mentioned in the earlier session and I am repeating that. That we are meant to live according to a certain design of the Supreme Lord in this material world. People have become indifferent to nature. Of course, they suffer calamities and they do. Take note of that. But then, even before a calamity occurs, the bad omens or the symptoms of such calamitous happenings in the future, that nobody is able to, most people are not able to recognize that. It is common knowledge in the Vedic times. So, Yudhishthira Maharaj is asking Bhima, what is this extraordinary time? What is this extraordinary time? What is going to happen? The 19th verse, na pibanti sthanam vatsa, na duhyanti ca matara, rudanti ashrumukha gaavo, na hrishyanti hrishabhavraje. The meaning of this verse is, the calves do not suck the teats of the cows, nor do the cows give milk. They are standing crying, tears in their eyes. And, the bhima says, what is going to happen? What is going to happen? What is happening? Then, they have had these long cuckoo calls, and the bulls take no pleasure in the pasturing grounds. Especially in the Vedic culture, the cows and the calves and the bulls were treated

by the people who owned them, who reared them, as their own children. This is very important. Among all the domestic animals. The cows were treated as seriously, as nicely, as caringly as their own children. And in the Bhagavad Gita it is particularly said, Gauraksha, special mention is given that it is the duty of householders to give special protection to the cows, though one should never harm any other creature, still specifically the cows had to be given special extra care, extra protection than the other animals, domestic animals. So Yudhishthira is marred. Marking, cows do not suck the teeth of the cows, why? Because the cows are not giving milk. And why are they not giving milk? Because they are standing there crying tears in their eyes, something calamitous is going to happen. The cows are also sensing and Yudhishthira is noticing this. And the bulls, they used to happily go to the pasturing grounds and graze. But now they are not taking any pleasure in grazing. They seem to be disinterested.

### Srimad Bhagavatam, Arjuna's Return, Yudhishthira's Anxiety
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/LuIn2zkS3ww?t=78
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "After Dhritarashtra's departure, Narada had told the Pandavas, you remain here till Krishna remains on the earth, and after that you can depart. So has Krishna departed is Yudhishthira's question."
**Summary:** This segment narrates Arjuna's return from Dwarka in a state of dejection, prompting Yudhishthira to recall Narada's prophecy about Krishna's presence on Earth and to question whether Krishna has departed.

In this series of verses, the 22nd verse, Iti Chintayatas Tasyah, Drishta Arishtena Chetasa, Ragnaha Pratyagamat Brahman, Yadupurya Kapidhvajah, the meaning of this verse is O Brahmana Saunaka. Now remember all this is being narrated by Suta Goswami to the sages and emissaries. Just when he was talking to Bhima, Arjuna returned, came back from the city of Dwarka. Then what happened? 23rd verse, Tampadayor Nipatitam Ayathapurvam Aturam Adho Vadanam Ab Bindoon Srijantam Nayanabhya Yoho. The meaning of this verse is when he bowed at his feet, who was bowing at whose feet? Arjuna was bowing down at the feet of Yudhishthira. The king, that is Yudhishthira, saw that his dejection was unprecedented. His head was down and tears glided from his lotus eyes. This is the state in which Yudhishthira is observing Arjuna has come back, but tears are gliding from his eyes and he was very dejected. His face was showing a lot of unhappiness. The 24th verse, Vilokyo Dvigna Hridayo Vichayam Anujam Nripah Pruchatismasuran Madhye Samsmaran Narade Ritam. The meaning of this verse is seeing Arjuna pale due to heartfelt anxieties, the king, that is Yudhishthira, remembering the indications of the sage Narada foretelling in the past. After Dhritarashtra's departure, Narada had told the Pandavas, you remain here till Krishna remains on the earth, and after that you can depart. So has Krishna departed is Yudhishthira's question.

---
### Yudhishthira's Inquiries, Yadu Dynasty, Krishna's Associates
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/LuIn2zkS3ww?t=257
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna descended to display his pastimes along with his associates from the spiritual world."
**Summary:** Yudhishthira inquires about the well-being of the Yadu dynasty clans, including Madhu, Bhoja, and others, emphasizing their devotion to Krishna and Krishna's role as their chief.

Now Yudhishthira's enquiry to Arjuna, the twenty-fifth verse, the meaning of this verse, Maharaja Yudhishthira said, he is addressing Arjuna, My dear brother, please tell me whether our friend, friends and relatives such as Madhu, Bhoja, Dasharha, Aarha, Satvata, Andhaka, and the members of the Yadu family are all passing their days in happiness. He is enquiring about the well-being of the different provincial kings or clans of the Yadu dynasty who were ruling different parts of the different provinces. So Madhu dynasty, Bhoja dynasty, and so on. So it is said elsewhere in the tenth canto, Krishna was the king of 101 clans of the Yadu dynasty. Krishna was the chief. So there were so many clans and Krishna was the chief of all those, primarily because all of them were heading towards the Yadu dynasty. The members of those clans of the Yadu dynasty, they were all devotees of Krishna, pure devotees of Krishna. So Krishna descended to display his pastimes along with his associates from the spiritual world. Of course, not all are associates. Some of them are associates. Some of them are joining Krishna's pastimes. Some of them are joining Krishna's pastimes after becoming eligible to go back to Godhead. So Yudhishthira is enquiring first of all about the well-being of all those clans of the Yadu dynasty, whether they are all doing well from Arjuna because Arjuna had gone to Dwarka to find out about Krishna's next program and Arjuna has written.

**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So that's how Yudhishthira and Krishna are cousins."
**Summary:** Yudhishthira inquires about his grandfather Shurusena and his maternal uncle Vasudeva, highlighting the family connections between Yudhishthira and Krishna.

Twenty-sixth verse, shuro mata maha kachit, swasthyaas thev vata-kachit. Shuru mata maha kachit. Shuru mata maha kachit. Watha mahrishah. Mada The meaning of this verse is, he is my respectable grand father, Shuru Sena, in a happy mood. And are my maternal uncle Vasudeva and his younger brothers all doing well? Now, Shuru Sena is Vasudeva's father. And Vasudeva is the maternal uncle of Krishna. The meaning of this verse is that he's the father of Vasudeva. So he has maintained his of Yudhishthira because Yudhishthira's mother, Kunti, Kunti's brother is Vasudeva. So therefore, Vasudeva, Krishna's father, is the maternal uncle of Yudhishthira. So that's how Yudhishthira and Krishna are cousins. So he is inquiring, Yudhishthira is inquiring about his grandfather Shurusena, who is the father of Kunti, Yudhishthira's mother. So Yudhishthira is inquiring, maternal grandfather, is he doing well? And maternal uncle, Vasudeva, is he doing well, along with his younger brothers?

Further, Yudhishthira is making inquiry about the wives of Vasudeva. Vasudeva was the king of the Shurusena province. And he had seven wives. These queens were headed by Devaki. And Devaki's sisters were all married to Vasudeva only. So seven sisters. All are the wives of Vasudeva.

The meaning of these two verses. Yudhishthira to Arjuna specifically is asking about the different relatives of Krishna living with him in Dwaraka. Particularly, the mention is made about Balaram, the personality of Godhead, who is also the protector of all devotees. So in the purport, Srila Prabhupada explains, Hastinapura, the capital of the Pandavas, was situated somewhere near present New Delhi. And the kingdom of Ugrasena was situated in Mathura. While returning to Delhi from Dwaraka, Arjuna must have visited the city of Mathura. And therefore, the inquiry about the king of Mathura is valid. Arjuna is returning from Dwaraka, has returned from Dwaraka, to Hastinapura, which is New Delhi. So on the way, he would have passed through Mathura, so he's inquiring about Mathura.

### Balarama, Vishnu Tattva, Expansions of Krishna, Prakasha Vigraha
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/LuIn2zkS3ww?t=661
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Balarama is the first expansion of Krishna."
**Summary:** This segment explains the concept of Vishnu Tattva and Balarama's position as the first expansion of Krishna, emphasizing their qualitative equality.

Amongst various names of the relatives, the name of Rama or Balarama, eldest brother of Lord Krishna, is added with the words, the personality of Godhead, Bhagavan. Because Lord Balarama is the immediate expansion of Vishnu Tattva as Prakasha Vigraha of Lord Krishna. What is this meaning of Prakasha Vigraha? Now, Krishna, The Supreme Personality of Godhead is the one and only one Supreme Lord in the entire creation, the entire existence. But he expands and there are different types of expansions. So the immediate expansion is called the expansion of the Vishnu Tattva. That means the personality of Godhead with his all godly qualities, godly characteristics expands as Vishnu Tattva. They are all the expansions, immediate expansions, the personal expansions, full expansions are having the same power, same qualities. Hmm. They are all the same Supreme Personality of Godhead, Krishna only. So Srila Prabhupada explains this. Balarama is the first expansion of Krishna. So Prabhupada says further, the Supreme Lord, although one without a second, expands himself as many other living beings. The Vishnu Tattva living beings are expansions of the Supreme Lord. Here living beings should not be confused with ordinary living entities. Here living being means their personalities, fully conscious and persons, but they are expansions of the Supreme Lord himself. And all of them are qualitatively and qualitatively equal with the Lord. This is called Vishnu Tattva.

---
### Vishnu Tattva, Jiva Shakti, Balarama as Guru, Prabhupada's Mercy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/LuIn2zkS3ww?t=794
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Balarama is described also as the mercy incarnation of the Supreme Lord, manifested as the spiritual master who is the savior of the pure devotees."
**Summary:** This segment differentiates between Vishnu Tattva and Jiva Shakti, emphasizing the inequality between ordinary living beings and the Supreme Lord, and highlights Balarama as the original guru and Prabhupada as his merciful representative.

Balarama, Sankarsana, Pradyumna, Aniruddha, Keshava, Narayana, all of them are qualitatively and quantitatively equal with the Lord. But expansions of the Jeeva Shakti, the category of ordinary living beings, are not at all equal with the Lord. So even we living beings, ordinary living beings, we are also expansions of the Lord. But we are not equal with the Lord. One who considers the Vishnu Tattva and the Jeeva Shakti to be on equal level is considered a condemned soul of the world. That means you should never equate any ordinary living being, Jeeva, Jeeva Shakti, with Vishnu Tattva, with the personality of Godhead or his expansions. So the Supreme Lord, Krishna, Balarama, Sankarsana, Pradyumna, Aniruddha, all of them are on the level of Supreme God. And we are at a much, much, much, much lower level. Insignificant beings who have got the quality of the Supreme Lord as spirit soul, we are spiritual beings. But quantitatively, very, very, very, very tiny. Lord is infinite. We are infinitesimal. So therefore, Balarama is addressed as the personality of Godhead and the protector of the devotees. So Prabhupada says, therefore, Balarama is described also as the mercy incarnation of the Supreme Lord, manifested as the spiritual master who is the savior of the pure devotees. So Balarama is the first guru, Adiguru, and our spiritual master, Prabhupada, is the representative Balarama as the mercy incarnation. Prabhupada is very, very merciful upon the suffering souls. And therefore, Prabhupada is preaching in this world and wants to rescue the suffering souls and take them back to God.

## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.14.30 - 33 | 15-11-2022
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### Vishnu Tattva, Expansions of Godhead, Chaturvyuha
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/6y1C9X5o-wE?t=33
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Bhagawan can be used for all Vishnu Tattva expansions of the Supreme Lord Krishna."
**Summary:** This segment explains the Vishnu Tattva, focusing on Krishna's expansions as Balarama, the Chaturvyuha (Vasudeva, Sankarsana, Pradyumna, Aniruddha), and their qualitative equality with Krishna.

Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya Naram Jai Vanarottamam Devim Saraswati Vyasam Tato Jayam Udi Rayet. In this session, we will be discussing from verses 30 to 33 of the 14th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. Yudhishthira is making inquiries from Arjuna. Pradyumna Sarva Vrishminam Sukhamaste Maharatha Gambhir Rayo Aniruddho Vardhate Bhagavanuta. The meaning of this verse is, how is Pradyumna, the great general of the Vrishni family? Is he happy? And is Aniruddha, the plenary expansion of the personality of Godhead, faring well? Srila Prabhupada makes a remark: Pradyumna and Aniruddha are also expansions of the personality of Godhead, and thus they are also Vishnu Tattva. Krishna is the originator of the personality of Godhead. His first expansion is Balarama. Balarama expands into the first Chaturvyuha: Vasudeva, Sankarsana, Pradyumna, Aniruddha. So those Pradyumna and Aniruddha are mentioned here also as expansion of the personality of Godhead, and they belong to the Vishnu Tattva category. Vishnu Tattva means they are qualitatively and quantitatively equal with Krishna. At Dwarka, Lord Vaishnodeva, the great general of the Vrishni family, is happy. The quadruple expansions, namely Sankarsana, Pradyumna and Aniruddha, so the Chaturvyuha, the quadruple expansions as Prabhupada says elsewhere, Vasudeva, Sankarsana, Pradyumna, Aniruddha, they are all engaged in transcendental pastimes. And therefore, each and every one of them can be addressed as the personality of Kadaj, as it is mentioned in connection with the name Aniruddha. Here, Gambhirarayo Aniruddho Vardhate Bhagawan Uta, Aniruddha Bhagawan. So Bhagawan can be used for all Vishnu Tattva expansions of the Supreme Lord Krishna.

---
### Krishna's Family, Transcendental Position, Universal Fatherhood
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/6y1C9X5o-wE?t=197
**Verse References:** BG 14.4
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Everyone is ultimately a son of the Supreme Lord, or a daughter of the Supreme Lord."
**Summary:** This segment discusses Yudhishthira's inquiries about Krishna's sons and grandsons, emphasizing the vastness of Krishna's family in Dwarka and the concept of Krishna as the father of all living beings.

The thirty-first verse. The meaning of this verse is, are all the chieftain sons of Lord Krishna, such as Sushena, Charudeshna, Sambha, the son of Jambavati, and Rishabha, along with the Chaturvyuha, they are all their sons, all doing well. So here the enquiry by Yudhishthira about the sons of Krishna. Krishna's sons are called Karshna. So, the reference is made to the sons of Krishna, headed by some of the chieftains. So Srila Prabhupada explains in the purport, as already mentioned, Lord Krishna married 16,108 wives, and each of them had 10 sons. Therefore, Krishna had 161,080 sons, if I were to put it in the terms of lakhs, 161,080 sons. They all grew up, and each of them had 10,000 sons. They all had as many sons as their father. Krishna, through each wife, he had 10 sons, and each of the sons of Krishna had 10 sons each. So, the whole aggregate was something near 1,610,800 family members of the Lord. The Lord is the father of all living beings who are countless. Therefore, only a few of them are called to associate with the Lord in his transcendental pastimes as the Lord of Dwarka on this earth. So, everyone is ultimately a son of the Supreme Lord, or a daughter of the Supreme Lord.

---
### Transcendental Connection, Krishna Consciousness, Spiritual Platform
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/6y1C9X5o-wE?t=346
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "As soon as we understand the Lord's position, we can understand how He is."
**Summary:** This segment explains the transcendental nature of Krishna and His connections, emphasizing that even in the material world, Krishna remains untouched by matter. It also discusses how chanting Krishna's name elevates one to the spiritual platform and the importance of practicing bhakti to maintain that state.

Krishna says in the Bhagavad Gita, Aham bija pradapita. The Lord is the father of all living beings. It is not astonishing that the Lord maintained a visible family consisting of so many members. In Dwarka, Krishna was having 1,610,000 grandsons and 161,000 sons, and 16,108 queens and a huge family. That's Krishna's own family as Krishna was playing the role of the Lord of Dwarka. It is better to refrain from comparing the Lord's position to ours, and it becomes a simple truth. As soon as we understand the Lord's position, we can understand how He is, even as we understand at least a partial calculation of the Lord's transcendental position. Always we should remember this point that the Lord is transcendentally situated eternally. Even when he comes to this material world, he is untouched by matter. He is completely spirit. He remains in his spiritual position eternally always, and all his connections are completely transcendental. So if we become connected with Krishna, we also become transcendentally situated. The simple example, when I vibrate my tongue and say Krishna, I chant the name of Krishna, I chant the name of the Supreme Lord Krishna, immediately I come in touch with the transcendental sound of his name and I am elevated to the spiritual position or platform. But to remain on the spiritual platform, that requires practice of bhakti and purifying the consciousness. I am elevating my consciousness to always remain on the transcendental platform while in the material world. And in the spiritual world, the entire spiritual world is completely on the spiritual plane. So there is no question of any touch of matter in the spiritual world, but in the material world, everything is tainted by the touch of the matter. Even our body, material body, means it's the touch of matter, so we have to elevate ourselves to the spiritual platform and remain there. If we remain on the spiritual platform always, we are also transcendentally situated like Krishna, and that is exactly what is called as Krishna consciousness.

---
### Liberated Souls, Eternal Associates, Mission of the Lord
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/6y1C9X5o-wE?t=557
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The Pāṇḍavas are also liberated souls who descended along with Lord Kṛṣṇa to serve Him in His transcendental pastimes on this earth."
**Summary:** This segment discusses Yudhishthira's inquiries about the well-being of Krishna's associates in Dwarka, including Uddhava and other liberated souls. It explains that these associates, along with the Pandavas, descended to the material world to fulfill the Lord's mission and are eternal companions of Krishna.

King Yudhishthira while inquiring about the Lord's sons and grandsons at Dwaraka mentioned only the chieftains among them for it was impossible for him to remember all the names of the Lord's family members, 1 million 610,000. He did not mention, he just remembered, he is mentioned the chief chieftains. Verses 32 and 33, they are together. I'll read them both: tathai vanu chara shaure, sruta devodhavadayah, sunanda nanda shirsanya, ye chandye sattvatar shabhaah, api swasti asate sarve, rama krishna bhujaashrayah, api smaranti kushalam, asmakam baddha savrindaya. The meaning of these two verses is given together also: shruta Deva with dhaba and others Nanda Sunanda and other leaders of liberated souls who are constant companions of the Lord are protected by Lord Valerian and Krishna, are they all doing well in the respective functions? Do they who are all eternally bound in friendship with us, remember our welfare? These are Yudhishthira's inquiries to Arjuna, because Arjuna has written from Dvārakā. So in the parport Śrīla Prabhupāda explains, the constant companions of Lord Kṛṣṇa, such as Uddhava, are all liberated souls, and they descended along with Lord Kṛṣṇa to this material world to fulfill the mission of the Lord. The Pāṇḍavas are also liberated souls who descended along with Lord Kṛṣṇa to serve Him in His transcendental pastimes on this earth. So both the Yadavas in Dvārakā and the Pāṇḍavas in Hastināpura, all are liberated souls, eternal associates of the Supreme Lord Kṛṣṇa.

---
### Vishnu Tattva vs. Jiva Tattva, Dependence on Krishna, Eternal Servitude
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/6y1C9X5o-wE?t=696
**Verse References:** BG 4.8
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Dependence on Krishna. That is the best dependence. That is eternal protection."
**Summary:** This segment differentiates between Vishnu Tattva and Jiva Tattva, explaining that while liberated souls are free from material contamination, they remain dependent on Krishna for protection and guidance. It highlights the constitutional dependence of the Jiva Tattva on the Supreme Lord.

As stated in the Bhagavad Gita, 4th chapter, 8th verse, the Lord and His eternal associates, who are also liberated souls like the Lord, come down on this earth at certain intervals. The Lord remembers them all but His associates, although liberated souls, do not forget, due to their being tatastha shakti or marginal potency of the Lord. Just like in the 4th chapter of the Bhagavad Gita it is said, Arjuna, he forgot that he had appeared with Krishna several times before. He had forgotten that, whereas Krishna remembers all of them, and that is very clearly explained in the 4th chapter of the Bhagavad Gita. That is the difference between the Viṣṇu Tattva and Jīva Tattva. We belong to Jīva Tattva. Krishna, Vāsudeva, Sankarsana, Pradyumna, Aniruddha, Narayana, Balarāma, Rāma, Narsimha, Varāha, all belong to Viṣṇu Tattva. They never forget anything, whereas we Jīva Tattva, we forget because of our tiny positioning. The Jīva Tattvas are infinitesimal potential particles of the Lord, and therefore they require the protection of the Lord at all times. And to the eternal servitors of the Lord, the Lord is pleased to give all protection at all times. The Lord is very pleased to give protection, and that is our position, dependence. Dependence on whom? Dependence on Krishna. That is the best dependence. That is eternal protection. Dependence on Krishna. The liberated souls never therefore think themselves as free as the Lord.

---
### Constitutional Dependence, Spark and Fire Analogy, Spiritual Ignorance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/6y1C9X5o-wE?t=816
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "As long as the spark is in the fire, it will glow, like the fire. But the spark cannot independently glow when it is separated from the blazing fire."
**Summary:** This segment uses the analogy of a spark and a fire to illustrate the constitutional dependence of the liberated soul on Krishna. It explains that just as a spark glows only when connected to the fire, the Jiva Tattva exhibits its spiritual quality when connected to Krishna. Giving up the protection of the Lord leads to spiritual ignorance and a return to the material world.

The Lord is completely free, completely independent, and the liberated souls, even though they are free from the touch of matter, they don't think themselves independent. They always seek the protection of the Lord in all circumstances, in the material world and in the spiritual world. In the spiritual world there is no question of seeking protection, but their attitude is, I want to be eternally under Krishna's shelter, Krishna's protection. This is the attitude of all devotees, all liberated souls. Now Srila Prabhupada explains a very important point. This dependence of the liberated soul is constitutional, for the liberated souls are like sparks of a fire that are able to exhibit the glow of fire along with the fire and not independently. This is the best example that can help us understand what is our active position. A spark of a blazing fire is very, very insignificant, tiny, but as long as the spark is in the fire, it will glow, like the fire. But the spark cannot independently glow when it is separated from the blazing fire. Independently, the glow of the spark is extinguished, although the quality of fire or the glowing is there, as spirit-soul. Our quality of spirit is actually not exhibited when we are separated from Krishna. Those who give up the protection of the Lord and become so-called Lords themselves, out of spiritual ignorance, come back again to the material world, even after prolonged tapasya of this severe type. That is the verdict of all Vedic literature. So I will stop here. Thank you very much.

---
