### sense gratification, purity, Krishna's protection
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/TvupIFYB8pw?t=298
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Being illusioned by false pleasures of the senses, one becomes a servant of the senses."
**Summary:** This segment discusses the difference between material and transcendental pleasure, emphasizing that conditioned living beings cannot derive true pleasure from impure senses and are driven by sense gratification, necessitating Krishna's protection.

This material pleasure is different from transcendental pleasure. This is Bhagavad-gita's description of transcendental pleasure. So Krishna gives transcendental pleasure to our spiritual senses or purified senses only. The impure conditioned living being cannot derive any pleasure from the senses. Impure condition means under the influence of the three modes of material nature and impure because the senses are contaminated by material contamination, material conceptions, material desires, material loading over material nature, independent material enjoyment. These are all the reasons why we are described as impure condition, so we cannot derive any pleasure from the senses. Being illusioned by false pleasures of the senses, one becomes a servant of the senses, go dasa. This is the description of the shastra. Go means senses, dasa means the servant. We all are servants of the senses. We seek out sense pleasures, being driven by the senses demanding for pleasure. Therefore, we need his protection for our interest. We require Krishna's protection. The Lord is a protector of cows and the brahminical culture.

---
### cow protection, brahminical culture, divine protection
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/TvupIFYB8pw?t=426
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Prosperity in our human society depends on two things: cow protection and brahminical culture."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the importance of cow protection and brahminical culture for societal prosperity, stating that those who neglect these principles are not under Krishna's direct protection, comparing them to prisoners under the jail superintendent.

Go brahmana hitaya. There's a description in the shastra, namo brahmanya devaya go brahmana. And to the Brahminic culture, and therefore such people who are not giving cow protection, who are not giving protection to Brahminic culture are described as not under the direct protection of the Supreme Lord Krishna, just as Prabhupada gives an example to help us understand. The prisoners in the jails are not under the protection of the king but under the protection of a severe agent of the king. It's like even in modern government arrangement, the citizens who are law-abiding are under direct protection of the government, but those who are law breakers and who are punished in jail, they are under the protection of the jail superintendent. That is the arrangement. Without cow protection and cultivation of Brahminical qualities in human society, at least for a section of the members of society, no human civilization can prosper at any length. Prosperity in our human society depends on two things: cow protection. Cow should never be killed.

---
### cow protection, brahminical qualities, spiritual vision
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/TvupIFYB8pw?t=573
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "One can become a qualified brahmana simply by following the brahmanical principles and awaken the good qualities within by practicing brahmanical culture, and then one can see the Lord as He is."
**Summary:** This segment details the usefulness of cows, both living and dead, and outlines the nine qualities of brahminical culture, asserting that anyone can become a brahmana by following these principles and thus gain the ability to see the Lord.

Because they are similar to Krishna and they're very, very useful animals. Every part of the cow is used. It gives the most wonderful form of food, milk, cow's milk for human beings. And after the cow dies, even when it's living, cow urine and cow dung are very, very, very valuable for human society. So many ways cow dung and cow urine can be used by a civilized human being. And after the cow dies natural death, of course, then the cow hide, the cow skin is very, very useful. All of them are useful for human society. So cow is a wonderful animal, wonderful. So such a animal should never be killed by anybody. They should be protected to live their entire lifespan with nice care by human beings. By brahmanical culture, the development of the dormant qualities of goodness, namely truthfulness, equanimity, sense control, forbearance, simplicity, general knowledge, transcendental knowledge, and firm faith in the Vedic wisdom. These are the nine qualities by which brahmanas work. This is the transcendental qualities of the brahmanical culture. One can become a brahmana and thus see the Lord as He is. If we become, anybody can become a qualified brahmana simply by following the brahmanical principles and awaken the good qualities within by practicing brahmanical culture and then one can see the Lord as He is.

---
### brahminical perfection, devotional service, relationships with Krishna
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/TvupIFYB8pw?t=691
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "After surpassing the brahmanical perfection, one has to become a devotee of the Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that after achieving brahminical perfection, one must further elevate themselves to become a devotee, developing loving relationships with Krishna as proprietor, master, friend, son, and lover on the spiritual platform.

And then one can see the Lord as He is. After surpassing the brahmanical perfection, one has to become a devotee of the Lord. So elevate yourself to the brahmanical platform. You become a brahmana with qualifications and then further elevate yourself to become a devotee to the transcendental platform. And then in the transcendental platform what happens? His loving affection in the form of proprietor, master, friend, son, and lover can be transcendentally achieved. Krishna is related to every living being on the spiritual platform. So Krishna is related either as a transcendental proprietor or a transcendental master or a transcendental friend or a transcendental son or a transcendental lover. These are the five kinds of relationships, primary relationships of Krishna which every living entity has on the spiritual platform that is being mentioned. The stage of a devotee which attracts the transcendental affection of the Lord does not develop unless one has developed the qualities of a brahmana as above mentioned.

---
### modes of nature, brahminical qualities, devotional service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/TvupIFYB8pw?t=771
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The Lord is inclined to a brahmana of quality, not of false prestige."
**Summary:** This segment clarifies that while the spiritual platform is beyond the modes of nature, one must first elevate themselves to the mode of goodness (brahminical platform) to reach it. The speaker emphasizes that Krishna is attracted to brahmanas by quality, not merely by birth or false prestige, and that those lacking brahminical qualities cannot establish a relationship with the Lord.

Sometimes devotees think we have nothing to do with this mode of goodness. Of course, on the spiritual platform, there is no question of any touch of any of the modes, neither goodness nor passion or ignorance. It's a transcendental state. But to come to the transcendental state, one has to first of all elevate oneself to the platform of goodness above rajoguna and tamoguna. These two base qualities, rajoguna and tamoguna, or passion and ignorance, are degrading our existence, human existence degrading. So one has to rise to the brahminical platform, the platform of goodness, and then develop all the transcendental qualifications to come to the spiritual platform, vaishnava brahmana vaishnava. The Lord is inclined to a brahmana of quality, not of false prestige. Some people claim I'm a brahmana by birth, though they don't have the qualities of a brahmana. Such brahmanas are not the special attention or subject to special attention of Krishna. Those who are not brahmana by quality, simply claiming to brahmana doesn't attract the attention of the Lord.

---
### relationship with the Lord, brahminical qualities, Krishna's perfection
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/TvupIFYB8pw?t=879
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Those who are less than a brahmana by qualification cannot establish any relation with the Lord, just as fire cannot be kindled from the raw earth unless there is wood."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of fire, wood, and earth to illustrate that one must possess brahminical qualities to establish a relationship with the Lord. He concludes by stating that Maharaj Yudhishthir did not inquire about Krishna's welfare because the Lord is eternally perfect.

Those who are less than a brahmana by qualification cannot establish any relation with the Lord, just as fire cannot be kindled from the raw earth unless there is wood, although there is a relation between wood and the earth. So this is the example given in the first canto of the Bhagavatam in the second chapter. There is earth, there is wood, and there is fire. So fire can be kindled in the wood when earth has been transformed into wood, firewood. Only then fire can be kindled. Raw earth, you cannot kindle a fire. Since the Lord is all perfect in Himself, there could not be any question of His welfare. So Maharaj Yudhishthir did not inquire about the welfare of Krishna because the Lord is all perfect, is eternally the Supreme Lord, so there is no question of His welfare. I'll stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

### Krishna, Balaram, Yadu Dynasty, Protection
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RvGtz7WDtik?t=51
**Verse References:** SB 1.14.35, SB 1.14.36
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna and Balaram are staying in the Yadu dynasty for the welfare, protection and general progress of the entire universe."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the verses to be discussed (Srimad Bhagavatam 1.14.35-36), explaining that Krishna and Balaram reside in the Yadu dynasty for the welfare and protection of the universe, and the Yadus enjoy life like residents of the spiritual sky.

In this session we will be discussing two verses which are given together in the Srimad Bhagavatam, 35 and 36 verses of the 14th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. Mangalaya Cha Lokanam Kshemaya Cha Bhavaya Cha Aaste Yadu Kulam Bodhav Adyo Ananta Sakha Pumaan. Yad Bahudanda Guptayam Swapuryam Yadavor Chitaha Kshemaya Cha Bhavaya Cha Kreedanti Param Aanandam MahaPavuchika Iva. The meaning of these two verses given together: the original personality of godhead, the enjoyer, and Balaram, the primeval lord Ananta, are staying in the ocean of the Yadu dynasty for the welfare, protection, and general progress of the entire universe. See this description. Krishna and Balaram are staying in the Yadu dynasty for the welfare, protection and general progress of the entire universe. That is the position of the Lord. The members of the Yadu dynasty being protected by the arms of the Lord are enjoying life like the residents of the spiritual sky. This completes the meaning of the two verses.

### Vishnu, Garbhodakashayi Vishnu, Kshirodakashayi Vishnu, Maha Vishnu, Universes
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RvGtz7WDtik?t=149
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The personality of Godhead Vishnu resides within each and every Universe in two capacities namely as the garbhodaka shayi Vishnu and the kshirodhaka shayi Vishnu."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on Prabhupada's explanation, describing the three forms of Vishnu in the material existence: Maha Vishnu (outside the universes), Garbhodakashayi Vishnu (entering each universe), and Kshirodakashayi Vishnu (entering the heart of every living being).

Now Prabhupada explains elaborately the comparison made here. It's a beautiful comparison to help us understand how Krishna ultimately is the protector of all of us. In the Purport, Prabhupada says, as we have discussed many times, the personality of Godhead Vishnu resides within each and every Universe in two capacities, namely as the Garbhodakashayi Vishnu and the Kshirodakashayi Vishnu. If you remember, 3 Vishnu forms in the material existence, out of which two are within the universe, and the one is outside all the universes. The outside of all the universes is Maha Vishnu, one supreme form, supreme Lord's form is Vishnu, one form. Whereas Garbhodakashayi Vishnu, the number of forms of Garbhodakashayi Vishnu are as many universes as there are. Garbhodakashayi Vishnu enters into each universe. There are millions of universes. And Kshirodakashayi Vishnu enters into the heart of every living being on each universe. Billions and trillions of living beings on each universe and billions and billions of universes. So we cannot count all this. It's all very, very, very large numbers.

### Kshirodakashayi Vishnu, Shwetadvipa, Anantadeva, Yadu Dynasty, Dwarka, Vaikuntha
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RvGtz7WDtik?t=249

**Summary:** The speaker continues describing Kshirodakashayi Vishnu's abode on Shwetadvipa in the ocean of milk, resting on Anantadeva. He then explains Maharaj Yudhishthira's comparison of the Yadu dynasty to the ocean of milk, Balaram to Ananta, and the citizens of Dwarka to the inhabitants of Vaikuntha.

The Kshirodakashayi Vishnu has his own universe, his own planet on the northern top of the universe. That's called Shwetadvipa. And there is a great ocean of milk. Shwetadvipa means ocean of milk. Dvipa means an island. Island is always in the middle of an ocean. Now, in this great ocean of milk, the Lord resides as Kshirodakashayi Vishnu on the bed of the Ananta incarnation of Baladeva. Vishnu resides on the bed of Anantadeva. Anantashayana. He is also called the Anantashayana Lord. Thus Maharaj Yudhishthira has compared the Yadu dynasty to the ocean of milk, Shri Balaram to Ananta, where Lord Krishna resides. He has compared the citizens of Dwarka to the liberated Yadu dynasty. He has compared the citizens of Dwarka to the liberated Yadu dynasty. He has compared the inhabitants of the Vaikuntha lokas. You see the comparison done by Yudhishthira so beautifully explained by Prabhupada.

### Vaikuntha, Material Universe, Coverings of the Universe, Causal Ocean
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RvGtz7WDtik?t=325
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Beyond these coverings, there is the causal ocean in which all the universes are floating like footballs."
**Summary:** The speaker describes the vastness of the spiritual sky beyond the material universe, including the sevenfold coverings of the universe and the causal ocean where universes float like footballs.

So Prabhupada says about the Vaikuntha lokas, he says, beyond the material sky, further than we can see with our eyes, material sky we can see with our naked eyes, but beyond that and beyond the sevenfold coverings of the universe, each universe. The universe consisting of millions of planets has got several coverings, seven layers of coverings. The coverings are very very very very big. If you consider the entire universe as a huge ball, then if the diameter of our universe is described as four billion miles, diameter of the universe. Now, the thickness of the first layer of covering of the universe is ten times the diameter of the universe. That means it is forty billion miles thick. Forty billion miles thick, the first covering. The second covering over the first covering is ten times thicker than the first covering. So, if the first covering is forty billion miles thick, the second covering is four hundred billion miles thick. So, we cannot even imagine how thick the coverings are and progressively the coverings keep on becoming thicker, thicker, ten times more, ten times more, ten times more than the previous covering. So, the second covering is four hundred billion miles thick. The third covering is four thousand billion miles thick. And then for forty thousand billion miles like that, it goes, it's very very thick covering. So, beyond these coverings, there is the causal ocean in which all the universes are floating like footballs. So, just imagine this situation of this supreme Lord in the spiritual world. Just try to imagine by these descriptions. We are tiny beings on this insignificant earth planet among millions of planets in the universe. And the universe is one insignificant universe among millions of universes in the entire material creation.

### Material Creation, Spiritual Sky, Causal Ocean, Brahmajyoti, Vaikuntha Planets
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RvGtz7WDtik?t=484
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "And the entire material creation itself with all the universes and each universe with all the planets and each planet with all the living beings is one insignificant portion of the spiritual sky."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the insignificance of the material creation compared to the spiritual sky, describing the causal ocean, the effulgence of Brahman (Brahmajyoti), and the innumerable Vaikuntha planets.

And the entire material creation itself with all the universes and each universe with all the planets and each planet with all the living beings is one insignificant portion of the spiritual sky. So beyond all this, there is the causal ocean in the spiritual sky, one corner of the spiritual sky, there is causal ocean. In that ocean these universes are floating like footballs. Beyond the causal ocean, there is an unlimited span of spiritual sky. Beyond the causal ocean. And that is generally known as the effulgence of Brahman, Brahmajyoti. In Sanskrit, it's called Brahmajyoti. In English it is called Effulgence of Brahman. Within this effulgence, effulgence means that glow within this effulgence, there are innumerable spiritual planets and they are known as Vaikuntha planets. Vaikuntha means where there is no anxiety, where there is no misery, where there is no suffering, where there is no material inebriety, no material imperfection, perfect spiritual situation.

So, that reference was made by Yudhishthira, comparing the citizens of Dwarka to Mahapaulukas. So, who are these Mahapaulukas? They are the inhabitants of the Vaikuntha locus. of misery. And they live perpetually in full youthfulness, enjoying life in full bliss and knowledge, without fear of birth, death, old age or disease, and without the influence of Kala or eternal time. So, material world means full of miseries, fundamental miseries – birth, death, disease, old age – full of the influence of Kala, eternal time. So, time destroys everything systematically, whatever was created in this material world. That influence of time is not there in the spiritual world. That influence of the four miseries, fundamental miseries, is not there in the spiritual world. Maharaj Yudhishthira has compared the inhabitants of Dwarka to the Mahapaulukas of Vaikuntha loka, because even in Dwarka, all the citizens are so happy with the Lord and the Lord is happy. ### Vaikuntha Lokas, Bhagavad Gita, Kingdom of God, Supreme Abode
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RvGtz7WDtik?t=741
**Verse References:** BG 8.20-22
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Having gone where one never returns to this material world of repeated birth and death, but lives in the spiritual world with Krishna or with Narayana or with Vishnu, eternal life full of bliss and knowledge."
**Summary:** The speaker references the Bhagavad Gita, mentioning Vaikuntha lokas as the kingdom of God and Krishna's supreme abode, where one never returns to the material world and experiences eternal bliss and knowledge.

In the Bhagavad Gita, there are many references to Vaikuntha lokas and they are mentioned there as Madhama or the kingdom of the Lord. In the Bhagavad Gita, there is a reference to Vaikuntha loka, not directly mentioned as Vaikuntha. But Krishna says, yad gatva na nivartante tad dhama paramam mama – having gone where one never returns to this material world of repeated birth and death, but lives in the spiritual world with Krishna or with Narayana or with Vishnu, eternal life full of bliss and knowledge. That is described by Krishna in the Bhagavad Gita as his supreme abode because every place belongs to Krishna, even the material world belongs to Krishna because it is a part of his material energy – his material energy. All energies are his – Krishna's. So material world also is Krishna's but it is an inferior place. Krishna does not reside here permanently. He sometimes comes to this world. He sabia akic aren't rabindranayatna, to attract all of us, to go back to him in his personal abode, supreme abode, mad-dhama. So that's the description of the Bhagavad Gita.

### Devotees, Fearlessness, Krishna's Protection
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/iNNL1IRLF6w?t=168
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna's devotees have a very special position. This material world is characterized by fear."
**Summary:** This segment explains that Krishna's devotees are protected from fear, which is a characteristic of the material world, and they enjoy the best things.

And that is explained by Prabhupāda in the purport. Śrīla Prabhupāda says those who are directly servitors of the Lord are protected by the Lord from all fearfulness and they also enjoy the best of things even if they are forcibly accumulated. Krishna's devotees have a very special position. This material world is characterized by fear. Every conditioned soul has got four characteristics, eating, sleeping, fearing, and mating. So everyone has got the fear of death. And before death comes, there is always the fear of suffering. There is always some other danger from some unknown enemy or so-called enemy. There is always the fear of losing one's possessions. Like this, there are so many different types of fears in this material existence. But those who are directly serving Krishna, they are always protected by Krishna because of which they are always protected. They are always fearless. And they are able to enjoy the best of things under all circumstances.

---

Krishna is also called devotee, Bhakta vatsala. He is specially affectionate towards his pure devotees. The city of Dvārakā was flourishing, being enriched with the best of things in the material world. The city of Dvārakā was on the earth planet. And earth is in the middle planetary system. The upper planetary system, all the planets have millions of times better facilities for their residents. But the city of Dvārakā, even though on the earth planet, it was special because it was enriched with the best things in the entire material universe. The state assembly house is constructed according to the dignity of the particular state. In heavenly planets, the state assembly house, called Sudharma, was deserving of the place of worship. In heavenly planets, the state assembly house, called Sudharma, was deserving of the place of worship. The state assembly house, called Sudharma, was deserving of the place of worship.

### Demigods, Sudharma, Yadu Dynasty
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/iNNL1IRLF6w?t=347
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "During the time of Lord Krishna, Lord Krishna's presence on the earth, the members of the Yadu dynasty forcibly brought the celestial assembly house to earth and placed it at Dwaraka."
**Summary:** This segment explains the role of demigods, the grandeur of Sudharma, and how the Yadu dynasty brought the celestial assembly house to Dwaraka to please Krishna.

Of the best of the demigods. The demigods are all controllers on behalf of Krishna in this material world. So they are exotic personalities who have got a very special state assembly house called Sudharma. Such an assembly house is never meant for any state on the globe on this earth planet because the human being on the earth is unable to construct it however far a particular state may be materially advanced. We cannot match the opulence or the grandeur of the state assembly hall in the heavenly planets. We can never do that. But during the time of Lord Krishna, Lord Krishna's presence on the earth, the members of the Yadu dynasty forcibly brought the celestial assembly house to earth and placed it at Dwaraka. The members of the Yadu family, they are all related with Krishna. So they want to please Krishna by offering the best of everything. So when they saw that, the assembly house in the heavenly planets is very, very grand. So they decided to forcibly bring it and offer it to Krishna. They are not desiring that we want to enjoy. No. It's for Krishna's pleasure.

### Devotion, Krishna's Protection, Reciprocity
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/iNNL1IRLF6w?t=450
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Pure devotees serve Krishna out of their heart. And they want to offer Krishna only the very best things in the entire creation."
**Summary:** This segment highlights the characteristics of a pure devotee, Krishna's protection, and the reciprocal relationship between Krishna and his devotees.

That's a characteristic of a pure devotee. They were able to use such force because they were certain of the indulgence and protection of the Supreme Lord Krishna. Krishna is affectionate to his devotees. Affectionate to his devotees means when the devotees require any protection, then Krishna is always ready to give them such protection. In other words, the Lord is provided with the best things in the universe by his pure devotees. Pure devotees serve Krishna out of their heart. And they want to offer Krishna only the very best things in the entire creation. So they will go to any length to actually please Krishna by such offerings. Again, we should remember Krishna is not in need of any of these offerings. He is completely independent. He is full of all opulences. He is not in need of anything. But, the Lord is not in need of anything except as a token of their love for them. Love for Krishna. Lord Krishna was provided with all kinds of comforts and facilities available within the universe by the members of the Yadu dynasty. Whatever the best things that are anywhere in the universe, the Yadu dynasty members, they would go there and fetch it for Krishna, for offering to Krishna. And in return, such servitors of the Lord were protected and fearless. Krishna reciprocates with his devotees. And in this case, Krishna assured them protection. Because of it, they became fearless.

---
### Conditioned Soul, Liberation, Fear
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/iNNL1IRLF6w?t=583
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "A forgetful, conditioned soul is fearful. That's our position now. We are forgetful of Krishna. And therefore, we have become conditioned."
**Summary:** This segment contrasts the fearful state of a conditioned soul, forgetful of Krishna, with the fearless state of a liberated soul who knows their spiritual identity.

Now, something important that Srila Prabhupada explains for us, all of us, in this present condition. Srila Prabhupada says, a forgetful, conditioned soul is fearful. That's our position now. We are forgetful of Krishna. And therefore, we have become conditioned. Conditioned means, we have got a material body. And this material body is made up of the material elements. It is subject to the material modes of nature. And we are forced to think in a certain way, work in a certain way, act in a certain way, relate with things in a certain way. All this is called conditioning. And it is a cause for our fear in this material world. But a liberated soul is never fearful. One who is liberated is never fearful. Because a liberated soul knows, I am not this body. I don't belong to this material world. I belong to spiritual world. In this spiritual world, I have a spiritual form. So therefore, the conditioned soul is fearful. And the liberated soul is fearless.

---
### Illusion, Fear, Eternal Relation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/iNNL1IRLF6w?t=676
**Verse References:** BG 2.20
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Fearfulness is a sort of illusion for the living being. Fear comes out of the effect of the illusion."
**Summary:** Using the analogy of a child dependent on their father, this segment explains that fear is an illusion arising from forgetting our eternal relationship with the Lord.

Now Prabhupada gives an example. Just as a small child, completely dependent on the mercy of his father, is never fearful of anyone. So there are instances in the scriptures, where, it is described that, a child, depending on the father, is completely fearless, because of the protection of the father. Similarly, it is explained here, fearfulness is a sort of illusion for the living being. Fear comes out of the effect of the illusion. The effect of the illusory energy. And how does the illusory energy work? The living being, when he is in slumber, slumber means when he is sleeping, and forgetting his eternal relation with the Lord, is actually affected by fear. So fear is a sort of illusion. And fear afflicts every conditioned soul at different times. Since the living being is never to die by his constitution, as stated in Bhagavad Gita, you don't take birth, you don't die. Then, what is the cause of fearfulness?

---
### Bodily Conception, Illusion, Yadu Dynasty
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/iNNL1IRLF6w?t=783
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Fearfulness is due to forgetting the eternal relation with the Lord."
**Summary:** Using the analogy of a dream tiger, this segment explains that fear arises from a bodily conception of life and forgetting our eternal relationship with Krishna, contrasting this with the Yadu dynasty's wakeful service.

So, fearfulness is due to forgetting the eternal relation with the Lord. As soon as we are placed in a material body, we think in terms of the body only. So, a body has taken birth on a certain date, that date is ascribed to the soul, or to the real person. The real person does not take birth, does not die. That's the truth, that's the reality. But, people under illusion, they forget, and they keep on trying and trying and trying to come out of this illusion, but they cannot easily come out. So, in the Bhagavad Gita it is explained, since the living being is never to die by his constitution, as stated in Bhagavad Gita, then what is the cause of fearfulness? If we are never meant to die, why should we be afraid of death? That is because of a bodily conception. Everybody thinks in terms of normal people, they think in terms of body. They do not know anything about the spirit. This is just like, Prabhupada gives an example, in a dream a person may see, while sleeping, in a dream he may see a tiger. Now, this tiger in the dream is actually an effect of illusion, because there is no real tiger. For the person who is awake, the tiger does not exist, what the sleeping man is seeing in a dream. So, the person dreaming and the person awake, both are actually, for both of them there is no tiger. But the person who has forgotten his external position, in the sleep, for him the tiger in the dream is very, very fearsome. And for the person who is awake, it is not fearful. So, when this person who is dreaming wakes up, he also becomes free from the fear, because he sees there is no tiger. Similarly, Prabhupada says, the members of the Yadu dynasty were fully awake in their service to the Lord, and therefore there was no fear for them. And even if, in the wakeful state also, there is some cause for fear, the Lord was always there to protect them completely. That was their position. I will stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

Or threatened you could you not give charity to one who asked or could you not keep your promise to someone these are all different reasons why a respectable personality becomes very depressed or dejected or very unhappy so Yudhishthira is trying to find out what is the cause for Arjuna's dejection so in the parapotra la prapa says a kshatriya or a rich man is sometimes visited by persons who are in need of money when they are asked for a donation it is a duty of the possessor of wealth to given charity in consideration of the person place and time if a kshatriya or a rich man and and fails to comply with this obligation, he must be very sorry for this discrepancy. What is the position of a brahmana? Prabhupada is explaining. Why these five categories are singled out for specifically for the protection of the king? Shri. To be given protection, to be given protection by the king, why? So it is one by one Prabhupada will explain. The brahmanas they are always engaged in society's welfare work and what kind of welfare activity they perform, they are researching knowledge for society's welfare. They are conversant with the Vedas. And therefore they apply the Vedic principles and teach us. So such brahmanas have to be protected by the king. Similarly the children of the state, the cow, the diseased person, the woman and the old man specifically require the protection of the state or a chatriya king. Shri. They have to be given special protection, not the ordinary one. If such living beings do not get protection by the chatriya or the royal order or by the state, it is certainly shameful for the chatriya or the state. So they deserve to get protection and the king should be giving them protection. Then only there will be prosperity and peace in the state, at the state level.

### illusion, cheating, conditioned soul
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/sA3uHqOaueo?t=147
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Particularly, a conditioned living being is endowed with four principles of malpractice: errors, insanity, inability and cheating."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the concept of cheating as a prominent defect of the conditioned soul, rooted in the unnatural desire to lord over material nature, referencing Prabhupada's explanation from Chaitanya Charitamrita.

So this is considered as a very serious matter by Yudhishthira who was perfectly ruling and everybody was living very happily, there was plenty of all supplies by nature, mother nature. But when Yudhishthira started seeing symptoms of some irregularities, then he suspected, maybe it is because the Supreme Lord has disappeared from the face of the earth. So in the purport, Prabhupada explains this particular point about cheating. Particularly, a conditioned living being is endowed with four principles of malpractice. This is explained in the Chaitanya Charitamrita, Prabhupada is quoting from Chaitanya Charitamrita. Four defects of every conditioned soul in this material existence. What are they? Errors, insanity, inability and cheating. Everybody who is conditioned is prone to make mistakes. Everybody who is conditioned is prone to cheat others. Everybody who is conditioned is prone to have defective senses, and that is inability. And everybody who is conditioned is subject to illusion. These are signs of imperfection. And out of the four, the propensity to cheat others is most prominent. Among all the defects and the conditions of all this, this particular defect of cheating propensity is very prominent. This cheating practice is there in the conditioned souls because the conditioned souls are primarily in the material world imbued with an unnatural desire to lord it over the material world. It's unnatural for us. But something unnatural that we all have as conditioned souls is the desire to lord over material nature.

---
### servitude, illusion, control
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/sA3uHqOaueo?t=315
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "A living being in his pure state is not conditioned by the laws because in his pure state he is conscious that a living being is eternally subservient to the supreme being."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that in our pure state, we are conscious of being eternal servants of Krishna, and the desire to control is an unnatural and defective state of existence, leading to dissatisfaction and illusion.

A living being in his pure state is not conditioned by the laws because in his pure state he is conscious that a living being is eternally subservient to the supreme being. Eternally we are servants. And in our unconditioned state, in our spiritual state of existence, in our pure state of existence, we are conscious that I am eternally servant of Krishna. And thus it is always good for him to remain subservient instead of falsely trying to lord it over the property of the supreme lord. The supreme lord is a factual proprietor, the factual master, factual controller, everywhere of everything. But if we artificially try to control, then that is an unnatural desire. That's a defective state of existence. In the conditioned state, the living being is not satisfied, even if he actually becomes the lord of all that he surveys. Let's say under illusion, somebody thinks I am the master of my family, or somebody thinks I am the master of my factory, or I am the master of my business. He thinks like that. He thinks he has become the lord, but actually he never becomes the lord because the supreme lord, Krishna, remains the master. And only under illusion somebody may think I am the master. Therefore he becomes the victim of all kinds of cheating. We are victimized actually under the influence of illusion. To wrongly think or to falsely think I am the master, I have to indulge in cheating others.

---
### disharmony, devotional service, solution
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/sA3uHqOaueo?t=455
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The world of hypocrisy can be checked only by counteraction through devotional service to the Lord and nothing else."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes that devotional service to Krishna is the only solution to mitigate the difficulties of cheating and other defects of the conditioned soul, leading to harmony and genuine relationships.

Even with his nearest and most intimate relations, he engages in all kinds of cheating, even with the nearest and dearest ones, so called near and dear ones. In such an unsatisfactory state of affairs, there is no harmony even between father and sons or between husband and wife. It is expected, at least in a cultured, civilized family life, it is expected that husband and wife, there will be good understanding, good cooperation in the interest of the family, or between father and sons. Because father is very affectionate and he takes good care of his own sons, so there should be good dealings naturally between father and sons. But because of this cheating propensity, the relationship is not always cordial. But one important thing Srila Prabhupada says here, but all these contending difficulties can be mitigated by one process. Is there a solution to these problems? Prabhupada says, yes. There is one solution. What is that? And that is the devotional service of the Supreme Lord Krishna. So, it is devotional service only that can mitigate this difficulty of cheating and all other kinds of defects of the conditioned soul. So, Srila Prabhupada concludes this by saying, the world of hypocrisy can be checked only by counteraction through devotional service to the Lord and nothing else. There is no other solution that works.

### Kali Yuga, Krishna's departure, degradation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/sA3uHqOaueo?t=672
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The beginning of Kali Yuga is when the day Krishna disappeared from this earth after winding up his earthly pastimes."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that Yudhishthira was perturbed by the inhuman symptoms of greed and anger, which were previously unknown, and connects the beginning of Kali Yuga with Krishna's departure, highlighting the reduction of the earth's burden during Krishna's presence.

In the purport Srila Prabhupada explains, such a pious king as Maharaj Yudhishthira at once became perturbed when there were such inhuman symptoms as greed, anger, irreligiosity and hypocrisy rampant in society. He just started seeing such symptoms and he became perturbed. That means they were used to a civilized society where all these things were absent. So when they started appearing, these symptoms of greed and anger and irreligiosity, Yudhishthira Maharaj became perturbed. It appears from this statement that all these symptoms of degraded society were unknown to the people of the time. And when Krishna was present, Yudhishthira Maharaj and all the people, they did not know what is greed, what is anger. It was astonishing for them to have experienced them with the advent of the Kali Yuga or the age of quarrel. You see, it is said, elsewhere it is said, the beginning of Kali Yuga is when the day Krishna disappeared from this earth after winding up his earthly pastimes. When Krishna comes, he comes with his associates, with his abode, with his paraphernalia, with his entourage, and he stays for a certain time, duration of time. One twenty-five years Krishna stayed and performed his wonderful Leela in Vrindavan, Mathura and Dwaraka. And also he traveled to other places like Hastinapura, Mithila, so many other places. And Kurukshetra, he traveled to Kurukshetra. So he performed his pastimes and when his pastimes are over, time is up, then he went back to his personal abode, Krishnae Swadhamopagati, that was explained earlier, that as long as Krishna was there, everything was very nice. And Krishna had come to reduce the burden of the earth, which was overburdened by demoniac persons posing as kings and oppressing saintly persons and innocent people, unnecessarily. So Krishna came, he reduced the burden of the earth and Krishna went back. But when Krishna went back, Kali Yuga began. It was the end of Dwapara Yuga when Krishna came. But Dwapara Yuga is finished when Krishna left. Kali Yuga began. And the symptoms of Kali Yuga are mentioned here. I will stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.14.42 | 22-11-2022
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### social order, Vedic culture, morality
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/PAiiSnmCzoE?t=73
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "This free mixing of men and women is not at all good. It results in so many anomalies."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses Yudhishthira's questioning of Arjuna and Srila Prabhupada's explanation of Vedic culture, emphasizing the restrictions on free contact between men and women for the proper upkeep of society.

Yudhishthira has been seeing some bad omens indicating something undesirable is going to happen, and he is anxiously waiting for Arjuna to return. Even as he is discussing these bad omens with Bhima, Arjuna returns, and then immediately Yudhishthira begins asking Arjuna so many questions. What is the cause for Arjuna's dejection? Arjuna is just not speaking, and Yudhishthira is asking him so many questions. So his questioning continues. Yudhishthira's questioning Arjuna continues till the end of this chapter. We are almost at the end, so this 42nd verse... The meaning of this verse is, have you contacted a woman of impeachable character, or have you not properly treated a deserving woman? Or have you been defeated on the other hand by someone who is inferior or equal to you? Yudhishthira is trying to find out what is the cause for Arjuna's dejection, so different reasons he is offering. Srila Prabhupada explains in the purport a very important aspect of the Vedic culture. It appears from this verse that during the time of the Pandavas, free contact between man and woman was allowed in certain conditions only. We are living in the present time where there is too much free contact between men and women, and that is not very good for the society, for the proper upkeep of the society. This free mixing of men and women is not at all good. It results in so many anomalies.

---
### social hierarchy, respect, Vedic principles
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/PAiiSnmCzoE?t=233
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The wife of a Brahmana is considered one of the seven mothers."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on Srila Prabhupada's explanation of the Vedic social structure, particularly the rules regarding contact between men and women of different castes, emphasizing the respect due to the wife of a Brahmana as one of the seven mothers.

So Srila Prabhupada explains this further. The higher caste men, namely the Brahmanas and Kshatriyas, could accept a woman of the Vaishya or the Shudra community, but a man from the lower caste could not contact a woman of the higher caste. Even a Kshatriya could not contact a woman of the Brahmana caste. Why is this so? That is explained by Prabhupada in the next sentence of this purport. The wife of a Brahmana is considered one of the seven mothers. This is a Vedic conception. There are seven mothers who are all respectable by everyone. Who are the seven mothers? One's own mother, the wife of the spiritual master or teacher, the wife of a Brahmana, the wife of a king, the cow, the nurse, and the earth. We say mother earth, mother cow. So these are the seven mothers in the Vedic conception of mother. And mother means everyone respects, everyone offers respect. So therefore, that is the reason a Kshatriya could not contact a woman of the Brahmana caste. This we are talking about openly mixing. That is not allowed out of respect. It is not a question of curtailing some freedom. No, it is out of respect. Please understand this point. Such contact between man and woman was known as uttama or adhama. Contact of a Brahmana with a Kshatriya woman is uttama, but the contact of a Kshatriya with a Brahmana woman is adhama and therefore condemned.

---

**Summary:** The speaker discusses the consequences of improper mixing of men and women, leading to undesirable progeny and the importance of following codes of conduct to produce a good population with good character, ultimately emphasizing that real happiness and satisfaction are found in association with Krishna.

And in the scriptures it is also described, if there is improper mixing of man and woman, there is what is called as varana-sankara population. This means that the child is not a good person. Undesirable progeny results from improper mixing. The children born will not be having good character. So there is a very great science behind this, behind the rules that are given for mixing between men and women. So to produce a good population, it is necessary that men and women follow the code of conduct for mixing with each other. A woman approaching a man for contact should never be refused, but at the same time, the discretion as above mentioned may also be considered. See how carefully the rules are laid down in the dharma shastras. The idea is the whole material existence is a chance for the living beings to try to enjoy independently and become realized that independent enjoyment will never give the desired satisfaction. That is the point. It will never give the desired satisfaction. It will never give the desired happiness that we are seeking as spirit souls. So such real happiness and real satisfaction is only available in our association with Krishna, because Krishna is the reservoir of pure transcendental bliss to an unlimited extent. Therefore, Krishna can fully satisfy us. Krishna can fully make us happy beyond even our capacity, beyond our expectation. Only possible for Krishna.

---
### Vedic guidance, Krishna, relationships
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/PAiiSnmCzoE?t=573
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "A woman approaching a man for contact should never be refused."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that Vedic literature guides us on how to approach Krishna, emphasizing that everyone is encouraged and given the opportunity, while also discussing the rules regarding men and women approaching each other, stating that a woman should not be refused but discretion must be applied, and men cannot approach women freely.

Therefore, the whole Vedic literature is guiding us. The Vedic rules, regulations, directions, restrictions, everything is guiding us how we can actually approach Krishna. You see in the pages of the Bhagavatam, in the entire Bhagavad Gita, in all the dharma shastras, there is every instance that is explained there in the scriptures is how each one of us can actually approach Krishna, and by proper contact with Krishna, we can develop the loving relationship with him, and in this regard, everyone is encouraged. There is no question of some people being denied the opportunity. Nobody is denied the opportunity. So therefore, it says here a woman approaching a man for contact should never be refused. This is a very, very important point. A woman should not be refused, but at the same time, there is a certain discretion that one has to apply. Who is the woman who is approaching, and who is the man who is being approached? And men cannot approach women freely. Women freely at all. Men cannot approach women freely at all. A woman may approach a man, but man cannot approach a woman. This is civilized society.

---
### examples, social order, progeny, character
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/PAiiSnmCzoE?t=706
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The population which is generated should be of a good quality, should be of good character. That is the central point: good character."
**Summary:** The speaker provides examples from the Bhagavatam and Mahabharata to illustrate the principles of Uttama and Adhama contact, emphasizing that if the rules are followed, the results are excellent, and the goal is to generate a population of good quality and character.

Now examples are given by Prabhupada from the Bhagavatam and the Mahabharata. Bhima was approached by Hidimbi from a community lower than the Shudras. Bhima was a Kshatriya, and Hidimbi was from a community lower than the Shudras. And Yayati, another example from the Bhagavatam, Yayati refused to marry the daughter of Shukracharya because Yayati was a Kshatriya, and Shukracharya's daughter was... Shukracharya was a Brahmana. So a Brahmana's daughter could not be married with a Kshatriya. Vyasadeva, a Brahmana, was called to beget Pandu and Dhritarashtra, obviously in the womb of the wife of Vichitravirya, a Kshatriya, in the womb of a Kshatriya's wife. Pandu and Dhritarashtra were begotten by Vyasadeva. He was a Brahmana. So Satyavati belonged to a family of fishermen, but Parashara, a great Brahmana, begot in her Vyasadeva. See Uttama and Adama contact. This is being explained here. So there are so many examples of contact with women, but in all cases, the contacts were not abominable, nor were the results of such contacts bad. If the rules are followed, the result is excellent. The result of the contact between man and woman is natural, because the way nature has made these mind-body systems, a man is attracted to woman, and a woman is attracted to a man, but that also must be carried out under regulative principles so that social consecration may not be disturbed. What is the result of the contact between a man and woman? The population which is generated should be of a good quality, should be of good character. That is the central point: good character.

---

Now, if the contact in man and woman is regulated, then there will be no unwanted, worthless population. There will be no unwanted progeny. It is called varana-sankara. Varana means a population of generating children of very bad character, very bad. If somebody is born with a bad character, it is not easy to reform, even though there is direction in the shastra how even those born with bad character can be reformed, but it is difficult. Hare Krishna.

## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.14.43 - 44 | 23-11-2022
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### Grihasta Dharma, Vedic Culture, Social Responsibility
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/UmR2yfB8dDI?t=54
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So grihasta dharma is very, very, very important for upkeep of the society, for the spiritual upkeep of the society."
**Summary:** This segment explains the duties of a grihasta (householder) in Vedic society, emphasizing their responsibility to maintain the other three spiritual orders (brahmachari, vanaprastha, and sannyasi) by providing food and spiritual guidance.

In this session, we will be discussing the 43rd and 44th verses of the 14th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. These are the last two verses of this chapter. This chapter is entitled 'The Disappearance of Lord Krishna'. The meaning of the 43rd verse is: 'Have you not taken care of old men and boys who deserve to dine with you? Have you left them and taken your meals alone? Have you committed some unpardonable mistake which is to be considered abominable?' This question itself indicates how very exalted was the Vedic culture, that the consideration always for a person before he takes his food, especially lunch, is that he feeds all those who are desirous of food and he is deserving to be fed by a grihasta. This is called grihasta dharma in the Vedic society. The four spiritual orders are brahmachari, grihasta, vanaprastha, and sannyas. Out of these four orders, the grihasta has certain duties which are absolutely essential to be carried out by the grihasta for the upkeep of the other three spiritual orders. A brahmachari goes and begs from householders. A vanaprastha, he also... of course, a vanaprastha does not really beg from a grihasta, but a vanaprastha may beg from a grihasta if he cannot get anything in the forest in terms of fruits, roots, berries, or something edible like that in nature. And a sannyasi, of course, he travels from door to door to enlighten people, but in the pretext of begging, he tries to approach householders to enlighten them spiritually. So for his subsistence, he may take a little, some bare minimum bhiksha, some food, that's it! So grihasta dharma is very, very, very important for upkeep of the society, for the spiritual upkeep of the society. So Yudhishthira is asking Arjuna, 'Have you not taken care of old men and boys who deserve to dine with you?' So Prabhupada explains in the purport, 'It is the duty of a householder to feed first of all the children, the old members of the family, the brahmanas, and the invalids.' Such a wonderful culture it is, the very culture, the duty of the householder to feed these four categories of persons. Who are they? The children, the old members of the family, the elderly people, the brahmanas, and the invalids. The brahmanas don't work for earning money or for their own maintenance, they don't work. They just give spiritual guidance to the entire society, rest of the society, after themselves becoming well versed in the scriptural knowledge. So they give free consultation to anybody in any subject matter, free of charge. And for the service they give to the society, they are maintained by the other members of the society, especially householders. So householders, they share what they have with brahmanas and the invalids. Due to some reason, somebody is not able to work and earn money or get their livelihood, then they are maintained by the others who are capable of earning and generating wealth. Those able-bodied persons should maintain the invalids. This is the culture.

---
### Grihasta Dharma, Charity, Vedic Injunctions
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/UmR2yfB8dDI?t=379
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Any householder before he takes his meals, he should go out of his house after having fed all these people, children, old members of the family, the brahmanas and the invalids, after having fed them, before he takes his own meals, he goes out and calls three times, 'Is there anybody hungry who needs food? Anyone? They can come.'"
**Summary:** This segment elaborates on the duties of a householder, including feeding the children, elderly, brahmanas, and invalids, and then calling out for any hungry person to come and dine before the householder eats. It also shares a story about a person in Karnataka who experienced this hospitality firsthand.

So Prabhupada says further, 'Besides that, an ideal householder is required to call for any unknown hungry man to come and dine before he himself goes to take his meals.' Any householder before he takes his meals, he should go out of his house after having fed all these people, children, old members of the family, the brahmanas, and the invalids, after having fed them, before he takes his own meals, he goes out and calls three times, 'Is there anybody hungry who needs food? Anyone? They can come.' And actually, they will be fed first before the grahastha himself takes his meals. He will feed them if any unknown hungry man. They won't even ask who are you, no questions asked. You are hungry at lunchtime, please take prasad. This is the culture. This is the chastric injunction. So a grahastha, a householder, is required to call for such a hungry man thrice on the road. In fact, I have heard this, that even now in some provinces, there are people who follow this injunction. I know of a certain gentleman who said he was traveling in Karnataka, in the South Canada district. He was going from one place to another place by his car, and while he was passing through one particular village, it was about lunchtime, say one o'clock in the afternoon, and his car was stopped by a gentleman of the village, and he stopped the car, and he was curious why did that gentleman stop his car. So the gentleman said, 'I am from this village. I stopped you to inquire from you, have you had your lunch?' So this person in the car said, 'No, I have not had my lunch.' 'Please come to my house for lunch,' he invited. So this is grahastha dharma. So then that person in the car said, who is traveling, who is a traveler, he was very pleased, and he went to that gentleman's house of the village and took his meals. So formerly there were no hotels, there were no restaurants. Either people would go to a temple which had the arrangement of feeding any visitors at lunchtime. Even now if you go to Udupi temple, you will get, you will get lunch at lunchtime, or if you go to ISKCON Bangalore temple, you can get lunch at lunchtime. So like this, there are still some temples, even in North India, even in South India, even in Gujarat, in Rajasthan, in Bengal, in Bihar, in Orissa, there are temples which feed people who are visitors in the afternoon at lunchtime. And sometimes the householders will, to a limited extent, they may host a person who is a visitor or a traveler or a person who has no arrangement for cooking his food, they may host such a person and feed him. So this is what is explained by Prabhupada here, 'The neglect of this prescribed duty of a householder, especially in the matter of the old men and children, is unpardonable. It is considered as a violation of the Vedic injunction for a householder who does not feed such deserving people.' So Yudhishthira's question to Arjuna is, 'Did you refuse to feed such deserving people, refuse to feed somebody who was worthy of being fed as a grahastha dharma? You didn't do your duty because of which you are dejected.' The 44th verse, the last verse, kachit preshthat tamenaatha, hridayenaatma bandhuna, shunyosmi rahito nityam, manyasete anyatha naruk. The meaning of this verse is, 'Or is it that you are feeling empty for all time because you might have lost your most intimate friend, Lord Krishna, O my brother Arjuna? I can think of no other reason for your becoming so dejected.'

---
### Separation from Krishna, Dejection, Prophecy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/UmR2yfB8dDI?t=683
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Are you feeling so dejected because you have lost your most intimate friend, Lord Krishna, because Krishna has departed for the spiritual world? Krishna is no longer on this earth planet."
**Summary:** This segment explains Yudhishthira's inquiry into Arjuna's dejection, suspecting it stems from the disappearance of Lord Krishna. It references Narada's earlier indication that the Pandavas would remain on Earth as long as Krishna was present, and how Yudhishthira's anxiety grew during Arjuna's extended absence.

So Srila Prabhupada says in the purport, 'All the inquisitiveness of Maharaj Yudhishthira about the world situation was already conjectured by Maharaj Yudhishthira on the basis of Lord Krishna's disappearance from the vision of the world, and this is now disclosed by him because of the acute dejection of Arjuna, which could not have been possible otherwise.' So even though he was doubtful about it, he was obliged to inquire frankly from Arjuna on the basis of Sri Narada's indication. If you recollect in the previous chapter, 'Dhritarashtra Quits Home', it was indicated by Narada to Yudhishthira that you Pandavas, you remain on the earth as long as Krishna is on the earth, and after Krishna's departure, you also may leave this world. So Krishna's departure was hinted by Narada when Yudhishthira had inquired from Narada about Dhritarashtra's quitting home. So this was in the back of the mind of Yudhishthira. So he sent Arjuna after that to inquire from Krishna what is the future program of Krishna, and Arjuna did not return for seven months. So Yudhishthira became very anxious whether Krishna has departed from this world because of which so many bad omens were being seen by Yudhishthira, and those were described in the beginning of this chapter. So now Yudhishthira is directly inquiring from Arjuna, 'Are you feeling so dejected because you have lost your most intimate friend, Lord Krishna, because Krishna has departed for the spiritual world? Krishna is no longer on this earth planet.' So Yudhishthira is even concluding, 'I can think of no other reason for your becoming so dejected.' And it will turn out that in the next chapter, it will be described how Arjuna replies to all these questions with one answer to begin with, and later on he recollects how he is feeling separation from Krishna because Krishna has departed for the spiritual abode, for the spiritual abode after winding up his pastimes. So that is what Arjuna is going to reply, but Yudhishthira has already figured out by Arjuna not answering in the affirmative for any of the other reasons for Arjuna's dejection. He has denied, 'I am not dejected because of all the other reasons' that Yudhishthira thought could be the reason for Arjuna's dejection. So I will stop here.

---

What is the meaning of plenary expansion? Krishna is the unlimited personality of Godhead. He expands. That means he is one person, but he is simultaneously present in many forms. He himself is present in many forms. And such expansions are called plenary expansions when Krishna's full power is present in each of the expanded forms. Just like Krishna expands, first of all, as Balaram. So Balaram is Krishna only, but Balaram has the full power of Krishna. Balaram is Krishna. ### Incarnations, Bhagavad Gita, Purpose of Avatar

**Summary:** Prabhupada quotes verses from the Bhagavad Gita to illustrate how Krishna incarnates at different times for different purposes, such as delivering the faithful and re-establishing dharma.

count how many plenary expansions are there and each and every one of them each and every plenary expansion although equally powerful executes different functions if it were for the same function there is no need Krishna is all-powerful so for different functions different forms that is Krishna's own personal opulence now Srila Prabhupada quotes the Bhagavad Gita in Bhagavad Gita there are different statements by the Lord and each of these statements is meant for different plenary portions or portions of the plenary portions some of them Srila Prabhupada cites here for example Shri Krishna the Lord says in Bhagavad Gita in the fourth chapter seventh verse what does he say whenever in wherever there is a decline in religious practice or descendant of Bharata and a predominant rise of irreligion at the time I descend myself yada yadahi dharmasya glanir bhavati bhavati that verse is being cited by Prabhupada the English translation of that verse, so at different times Krishna has different incarnations appear so these incarnations are possible plenary portions, or they are portions of plenary portions, amsa and kala. Then again, in the fourth chapter, eighth verse, Krishna says, to deliver the faithful, to annihilate the miscreants, and also to reestablish the principles of occupational duty, I appear in every age. In every yuga, Krishna incarnates for three purposes, and those are mentioned here.

Now these incarnations are different plenary expansions, different plenary portions, different portions of the plenary portions. They come time to time. Each of the incarnations come appropriately. Then again, in the third chapter, twenty-fourth verse, Krishna says, if I should cease to work, then all humanity would be misdirected. I would also be the cause of creating unwanted population, and I would thereby destroy the peace of all sentient beings. So this also, Krishna is speaking. But, in his different expansions, different incarnations, he performs different activities. Not all incarnations are doing the same thing. Just like in his expansion as Mahavishnu, he is responsible for the primary creation, breathing out universes, and breathing in the universes.

### Expansions, Transcendental, Time
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/WJuULo7ycKs?t=640
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna only, only one person, supreme person, unlimited person. He is not ordinary person like us. Transcendental personality full of all so many opulences, so many powers, so many different forms."
**Summary:** Prabhupada explains that Krishna's expansions, such as Sankarsana and Vasudeva, are transcendental and that Krishna appears in each universe once in Brahma's day.

So now Prabhupada continues after citing these four verses from the Bhagavad Gita, Prabhupada says, all the above statements by the Lord apply to different plenary portions of the Lord, namely his expansions such as Sankarsana, Vasudeva, Pradyumna, Aniruddha, Narayana, Sankara. These are all he himself in different transcendental expansions. Krishna only, only one person, supreme person, unlimited person. He is not ordinary person like us. Transcendental personality full of all so many opulences, so many powers, so many different forms. Still the Lord and Shri Krishna functions in a different sphere of transcendental exchange with different grades of devotees. As Krishna himself, he also appears. And that appearance is the appearance of the original personality we have got it. In Mathura, he is born and then he transfers himself to Vrindavan and then again comes back to Mathura, then Dwaraka, so different sphere of transcendental exchange with different grades of devotees, different types of devotees are there. And yet Lord Krishna as he is appears once every 24 hours of Brahma's time, once in a day or after a lapse of 8 billion 640 million solar years. This one day and night of Brahma together is unparalleled. In our calculation on the earth, 8 billion 640 million, so many solar years elapse after Krishna appears again. In each and every universe Krishna appears once in the day of Brahma. And all his transcendental pastimes are displayed in each and every universe in a routine spool. Just like cinema is running.

---
### Transcendental Body, Soul, Identity
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/WJuULo7ycKs?t=773
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna's body and Krishna's soul are identical. In our case what happens? This material body is completely different from I the spirit's soul."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes that Krishna's body and soul are identical, unlike humans where the material body is separate from the spirit soul.

Like that. Every Brahmanda, Krishna will appear, perform his pastimes and disappear. Once in a day of Brahma only. In his form as Krishna. Of course, in his other forms, other expansions, other incarnations he appears in so many different yugas in different times. But in that routine spool the functions of Lord Krishna, Lord Vasudeva, etc. are complex problem for the layman. A layman cannot understand. It is the same personality of God that is appearing. There is no difference between the Lord's self and the Lord's transcendental body. This is important. Krishna's body and Krishna's soul are identical. In our case what happens? This material body is completely different from I the spirit's soul. That's why time and again we have heard many, many times, I am not the body, you are not your body.

Krishna has no material body, he has no material form, then what did we leave behind? Yasmanas Sampado Rajyam Dara Prana Kulam Prajaha Asan Sapatna Vijayo Lokascha Yad Anugrahat. The meaning of this verse is from him only all our kingly opulence, good wives, lives, progeny, control over our subjects, victory over our enemies and future accommodations in higher planets have become possible. All this is due to his causeless mercy upon us. Here is the statement by Yudhishthira which is a very important lesson for all of us.

Srila Prabhupada explains that very nicely in the purport. Srila Prabhupada says material prosperity consists of a good wife, good home, sufficient land, good children, aristocratic family relations, victory over conflict, and that happenings lead to attaining first place position among the competitors and by pious work attainment of accommodations in the higher celestial planets for better facilities of material amenities. Good home, good children, sufficient land and aristocratic family relations and victorious over competitors and also perform pious work to ensure a better next life in terms of elevating oneself to heavenly powers. Now Srila Prabhupada says these facilities are earned not only by one's hard manual labor or by unfair means, but by the mercy of the Supreme Lord.

### divine sanction, personal endeavor, success
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/rg039BDecyM?t=170
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Personal labour must be there in this material world, in addition to the Lord's benediction. So both are required."
**Summary:** The lecture emphasizes that both personal endeavor and the Lord's mercy are essential for success, and that hard work alone is insufficient without divine sanction.

Generally people consider I worked hard and I earned some money or some good result in some work and so on. I worked hard and it is my own hard work that is the cause of my success or my achievement. They forget that in addition to the hard work, most important is the mercy of the Supreme Lord in sanctioning the result. That they forget. So Srila Prabhupada says prosperity earned by one's personal endeavour also depends on the mercy of the Lord. Krishna Kripa, Bhagavad Kripa. You also require the mercy of the Lord for success in any endeavour. Personal labour must be there. It is not that Krishna's mercy is enough, I will not do anything. No. Personal labour must be there in this material world, in addition to the Lord's benediction. So both are required. The personal endeavour and the Lord's mercy or Lord's blessing both are required. But without the Lord's benediction no one is successful simply by personal labour. Professor is reiterating this point.

---
### Kali Yuga, ignorance, five factors of action
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/rg039BDecyM?t=289
**Verse References:** BG 18.14
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "The modernized man of Kali Yuga, believes in personal endeavour and denies the benediction of the Supreme Lord."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts the modern Kali Yuga mentality, which emphasizes personal effort over divine grace, with the Vedic understanding of the five factors of action, including the Lord's blessing.

Your hard work alone is not enough, anybody for that matter, in this material world. The modernized man of Kali Yuga, believes in personal endeavour and denies the benediction of the Supreme Lord. This is the position of most people in the present Kali Yuga. They are ignorant and they are not properly educated and they do not know how subtle laws are working, who is ultimately in control of everything that is going on. Why is it that people do not always succeed in all their endeavors? Actually most people don't have an answer for this. The answer is given here that there are two things: the personal endeavor and the sanction of the Lord, the blessing or benediction or mercy of the Lord. This is explained very nicely in the Bhagavad Gita: five factors of action are dependent on personal endeavor and one is called diva, that is the Supreme Lord's blessing of the mercy.

---
### example, devotee, Krishna's benediction
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/rg039BDecyM?t=364
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Neither personal endeavour is to be ignored, nor should one neglect the Supreme Lord's blessings."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the example of a sanyasi and Maharaja Yudhishthira to illustrate the importance of acknowledging the Lord's role in success, alongside personal effort.

So clearly these five factors are responsible for successful accomplishment of any action in this material world by anybody. Srila Prabhupada cites one instance: even a great sanyasi of India delivered speeches in Chicago protesting the benedictions of the Supreme Lord as if Supreme Lord's benediction don't matter at all. This sanyasi was speaking only your own personal endeavor is important and enough. That is wrong as far as the Vedic shastras are concerned as we find in the pages of Srimad Bhagavatam. The ultimate sanction for all success rests in the hands of the Supreme Lord. Maharaja Yudhishthira admits this truth in his personal success. Yudhishthira is an ideal personality. He is a pure devotee of the Supreme Lord. So he is not ignorant. He is not foolish. He is not a sentimentalist. He is a powerful Kshatriya, but he does his duties properly. He knows that his success is dependent on Krishna's benedictions also, in addition to his personal endeavour. So neither personal endeavour is to be ignored, nor should one neglect the Supreme Lord's blessings.

### divine will, medical science, success
