**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of medical practitioners and their inability to always cure patients, despite their best efforts, to illustrate the role of divine will in all outcomes.

Both are required. Both are essential. Now Maharaj Yudhishthira admits this truth in his personal success and he behooves one to follow in the footsteps of a great king and devotee of the Lord to make life a full success. So Prabhupada is revealing to all of us, how can we ensure full success in our life? We have to personally endeavour and we should also learn to depend on Krishna's blessings. Both are required. Now Srila Prabhupada says one important point and helps us understand the truth, the reality. Prabhupada says, if one could achieve success without the sanction of the Lord, then no medical practitioner would fail to cure a patient. Medical practitioners work very hard, very sincerely to cure somebody who has got a deadly disease. They work very hard. They sincerely work to diagnose, to treat, to administer the medicine, or to do surgery, or to administer specific treatment. And even the most successful people, sometimes they are unable to save the patient. So, one should be very careful. Why is it that every time they give good treatment, they are not successful? That is because the Lord's hand is definitely there. In spite of their hard work and very good care, people die.

---
### death, naturopathy, divine sanction
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/rg039BDecyM?t=575
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "God's sanction is the immediate cause for all happenings, good or bad."
**Summary:** The speaker provides examples of people dying despite medical treatment and others surviving without it, and discusses naturopathy, concluding that God's sanction is the ultimate cause of all events.

Some people die. So Srila Prabhupada says, despite the most advanced treatment of a suffering patient, by the most up-to-date medical practitioner, there is death. And even in the most hopeless case, without medical treatment, a patient is cured astonishingly. I have many instances I can cite. I don't have time now. That there have been cases where people have been rushed to their family doctor, and the family doctor tried his best to treat and cure, and he was not successful. The doctor is the same. The patient is one who has taken treatment earlier, and he has been treated and cured earlier, many, many times. But one particular time, the doctor was not able to cure the patient. The patient died. So, there are instances where people die in spite of the best treatment. And there are instances also where somebody is unable to get treatment, and they are just not having any hope of surviving, because they have got a very deadly disease. And without any treatment, without any medicine, without any kind of intervention, medical intervention, they astonishingly survive. They somehow become cured, without any treatment. There are instances of people following what is called as naturopathy. They purposely don't go to a doctor. They don't take any medicine. They don't take any medical treatment. They just follow a strict diet, and they do fasting. And they become cured. But again, it's not that they're going to live forever. That we have to remember. Whatever be your system of maintaining good health, at a certain time, when the lifespan is over, one has to quit the body in this material world. Death is certain, for one has taken birth after the duration of a certain lifespan. It is certain. Therefore, the conclusion, what is the conclusion? That God's sanction is the immediate cause for all happenings, good or bad.

So never neglect or ignore God's sanction, Krishna's sanction. Krishna, please remember, Krishna is seated on everybody's heart. He is active in everybody's life. He is a constant companion of the conditioned soul, as long as the conditioned soul is in this material world. So he guides. If I quit this body, Krishna will guide me to my next destination. Krishna knows everything. And he will guide me to the next body that I have to accept, if I have to accept the body, or he'll guide me to his personal abode, if I have to go back to God. Exactly everybody is guided by the Supersoul, Paramatma, who is none other than Krishna himself. So Krishna's hand is always there. Any successful man should feel grateful to the Lord for all he has achieved. This is the last statement by Srila Prabhupada in this purport. Have that gratitude. And by having that gratitude for all success in all your endeavors, you become Krishna conscious. Gratitude towards Krishna. You become Krishna conscious. When you become Krishna conscious, that's your success in life. Ultimate success. You get out of this material existence and you go to the spiritual abode of the Supreme Lord to live eternal life full of knowledge and bliss. I will stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

Here is the first verse of this chapter. Suta Vacha Evam Krishna Sakha Krishnao. Suta Vacha Evam Krishna Sakha Krishnao. Suta Vacha Evam Krishna Sakha Krishnao. So he guessed it is only possible because Krishna has returned to his personal abode. He has left this earth planet. Krishna has disappeared. So now this is going to be confirmed by Arjuna and Arjuna has been bewitched. He got lost and he returned again. And when Arjuna is there. He is returning again. And Yudhishthira has returned when Yudhishthira was trying to find the cause or the reason for the bad omens and when Arjuna returned immediately Yudhishthira began to inquire why is Arjuna appearing to be very dejected. And therefore he is guessing the cause for Arjuna's dejection and when Arjuna. And after, when Arjuna could not immediately reply to Yudhishthira's inquiries, so Yudhishthira went on asking, is it because of this, is it because of that, is it because of something else? And finally, when Arjuna would not reply, either yes or no, then Yudhishthira said, the only reason you are so dejected is because you have lost your dear most friend Lord Krishna, you are separated from him. And now Arjuna is going to reply. So, this verse says, in the Purport Shilaprabha says, being too much aggrieved, Arjuna practically became choked up, and therefore it is not possible for him to reply properly to the various speculations and speculative inquiries of Maharaja Yudhishthira. The second verse,

### grief, affection, separation, Krishna
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/c_zX_rBLnXU?t=328
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Arjuna had lost sight of his dear one, his foremost friend, even dearer than his own life and he was very much aggrieved because of that."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the second and third verses, describing Arjuna's physical and emotional state due to grief and separation from Krishna, emphasizing his increasing affection.

The meaning of the second verse, due to grief, Arjuna's mouth and lotus-like heart had dried up, therefore his body lost all life. Now, remembering the Supreme Lord, he could hardly utter a word in reply. The third verse, Krachrena samsthavya shuchaha, paanina mridya netrayo ho, parokshena samunadha pranayautkantya katarah, the meaning of this third verse is, with great difficulty he checked the tears of grief that smeared his eyes. He was very distressed because Lord Krishna was out of his sight, and he increasingly felt affection for him. Arjuna had lost sight of his dear one, his foremost friend, even dearer than his own life and he was very much aggrieved because of that. He was very distressed and he began to feel even more affection for Krishna. Already he was very, very affectionate to Krishna, towards Krishna, but he began to feel even more affection on this separation, experiencing separation from Krishna.

### friendship, soul, material defects, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/c_zX_rBLnXU?t=680
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna's relationship with every living being is soul to the super soul. Perfect relationship."
**Summary:** The speaker explains Krishna's perfect relationship with Arjuna as a fraternal one, contrasting it with material relationships that are based on the body and mind, not the soul. He concludes by summarizing Arjuna's reaction to Yudhishthira's inquiries.

So now, how did Krishna relate with Arjuna? That is being explained here. Sri Arjuna is one of the typical pure devotees of the lord, reciprocating in the fraternal relationship. Fraternal. Fraternal relationship means as a friend. Krishna and Arjuna are related as friend. And the lord's dealings with Arjuna are displays of friendship of the highest perfect order. We have friendly relations with different people in this material world. We have experience. But in this world, all relationships with other persons, is tinged with material defects. The first material defect is that all such relationships are based on body. Anything that happens to the body affects the relationship. Body means there is an external body and there is an inside, internal body, the mind. So the gross body and the sub-body. The subtle mind. These are the basis of our relationships in this material world with other persons. Who similarly also have, like I have a gross body and I have a subtle mind. But I am different from these two. I am spirit soul. Similarly, you, if I have a relationship with you, you are also spirit soul. But you have a covering over your spirit soul. You have a covering of the subtle mind and the gross body. So you are. Your subtle mind, your gross body, is covering you and my gross body and my subtle mind is covering me. And the relationship, the material relationship is between or based on this gross body and subtle mind. It has nothing to do with the soul, factually. So, Krishna's relationship with every living being is soul to the super soul. Or soul to the... Supreme soul. He is a supreme soul. He has no covering. Pure devotees don't have any covering. Pure devotees are their individual pure spirit soul. So the relationship is between soul and supreme soul. Perfect relationship. So, Krishna was not only a well-wisher of Arjuna, but actually a benefactor. And to make it still more perfect, the Lord tied him into a family relationship by arranging Subhadra's marriage with him. And above all, the Lord became a chariot driver of Arjuna in order to protect his friend from warfare risks. And the Lord became happy when he established the Pandavas to rule over the world after the battle of Rukshetra. Arjuna remembered all these one after another and thus he became overwhelmed with such thoughts. So, this is Arjuna's reaction when Yudhishthira Maharaj made inquiries. I'll stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

---

## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.15.10 | 02-12-2022
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### devotee, insult, consequences
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/f5bv6Lnef8w?t=55
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So here is another very very important lesson for all of us: The Lord can tolerate insults upon himself by any miscreant because the father tolerates even insults from the son, but the Lord never tolerates insults upon his devotees."
**Summary:** The speaker narrates the story of Draupadi's insult by Dushasana and the subsequent consequences for the Kauravas, emphasizing the importance of respecting devotees of the Lord.

In this session, we will be discussing the 10th verse of the 15th chapter of the 1st Canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. The meaning of this verse is: 'It was he only who loosened the hair of all the women, the wives of the miscreants, who dared open the cluster of your queen's hair, which had been nicely dressed and sanctified for the great Rajasuya sacrificial ceremony.' At that time, she fell down at the feet of Lord Krishna with tears in her eyes. So this is an incident that happened before the Pandavas went to the forest for exile. In the gambling match, something happened that is described by Prabhupada in the purport. Queen Draupadi had a beautiful bunch of hair, which was sanctified in the ceremonial function of Rajasuya Yajna. The Rajasuya Yajna took place before the Pandavas went to exile. So Draupadi was actually sanctified in that ceremonial function. She had a very beautiful bunch of hair, but when she was lost in a bet during the gambling match between the Pandavas and Kauravas, Dushasana touched her glorified hair to insult her. These are demoniac natured people. They have no consideration that a chaste lady is never to be insulted. So they thought they can do whatever they like and get away with it. So Dushasana actually insulted Draupadi. Draupadi then fell down at the lotus feet of Lord Krishna, and Lord Krishna decided that all the wives of Dushasana and company should have their hair loosened as a result of the banal activity. Thus, after the battle of Kurukshetra, after all the sons and grandsons of Dhritarashtra died in battle, all the wives of the family were obliged to loosen their hair as widows. You see, all the wives of the Kuru family became widows because of Dushasana insulting a great devotee of the Lord. So here is another very very important lesson for all of us: The Lord can tolerate insults upon himself by any miscreant because the father tolerates even insults from the son, but the Lord never tolerates insults upon his devotees.

---
### devotee, insult, consequences
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/f5bv6Lnef8w?t=239
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "If someone insults Krishna, Krishna may tolerate, but if someone insults Krishna's devotees, Krishna will never tolerate."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes that while Krishna may tolerate insults to himself, he will never tolerate insults to his devotees, highlighting the severe consequences of offending a great soul.

The Lord never tolerates insults upon his devotees. If someone insults Krishna, Krishna may tolerate, but if someone insults Krishna's devotees, Krishna will never tolerate. And it is explained here, by insulting a great soul, one has to forego all the results of pious acts and benedictions also. So Dushasana, by insulting Draupadi, the chaste lady and pure devotee of Krishna, invited trouble for himself. So all the Kauravas, all the sons of Dhritarashtra and his grandsons, were all killed in the battle of Kurukshetra. None of them survived, not even one surviving descendant for Dhritarashtra, not even one surviving descendant. And the Pandavas, they were actually protected by Krishna mystically, that they were completely unharmed in that big battle. It was a world war. It was no small battle. It was the world war. And in that, the Pandavas were protected by Krishna. So, the lesson for us is never insult a Vaishnava. Never offend a Vaishnava. It is very dangerous. By offending a Vaishnava, one loses all results of the pious activities and all benedictions also are lost.

---
### devotee, example, preaching
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/f5bv6Lnef8w?t=378
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Haridasa Thakur is a unique devotee who nicely practices and preaches about the chanting of the holy name, so he is the ideal, perfect devotee."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the story of Haridas Thakur, highlighting his exemplary qualities as a devotee who both practices Krishna consciousness and preaches to others, making him an ideal devotee.

There is another incident described in the Chaitanya Charita Amrita, in the life of Srila Haridas Thakur. Haridas Thakur was a devotee of Lord Chaitanya, and he used to chant the Hare Krishna mantra himself very nicely, and he also preached to people on the order of Chaitanya Mahaprabhu. He used to preach others also to take up this chanting of Hare Krishna mantra. He is described as the ideal devotee who not only practices very nicely the Krishna conscious principles, especially chanting, he also preaches on the order of the Lord. So, he is the ideal devotee. Sanatana Goswami glorifies Haridas Thakur as the ideal devotee because while there are some devotees who practice very nicely but don't preach, and there are others who preach very nicely but don't practice adequately, Haridasa Thakur is a unique devotee who nicely practices and preaches about the chanting of the holy name, so he is the ideal, perfect devotee. This is the way Haridasa Thakur is glorified by Sanatana Goswami.

---
### devotee, association, respect
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/f5bv6Lnef8w?t=481
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So when all the devotees, particularly these two Majumdars, they saw Haridasa Thakur, they immediately stood up to receive him with great respect, and they fell at his lotus feet, and they gave him a nice place to sit."
**Summary:** The speaker describes an incident where Haridas Thakur was invited to a meeting of devotees and saintly persons, emphasizing the respect and honor he received from the attendees, particularly the Majumdar brothers.

So Haridasa Thakur was once insulted by a priest, and that is described in the Chaitanya Charita Amrita that in the house of two governmental treasurers called Hiranya Majumdar and Govardhana Majumdar, there was a meeting because they were devotees of Krishna. So there was a meeting of devotees, and there was a meeting of saintly persons. So in that meeting, one Balaram Acharya, who was a priest of this Majumdar household, that Balaram Acharya was invited for the meeting of saintly persons in Majumdar's house. And Balaram Acharya had brought to that meeting Haridasa Thakur also. So Haridasa Thakur was traveling and preaching, and while traveling and preaching, he happened to come to this village where these two brothers, treasurers of the government, were staying, Hiranya Majumdar and Govardhana Majumdar. So when he came to that village, he accepted prasadam in the house of Balaramacharya, and he used to perform chanting in a solitary place where there was a thatched hut. Living in a simple hut, he would chant practically the whole day and take a little prasadam from Balaramacharya's residence. So in that meeting, there were discourses taking place, and the saintly persons participated in that discourse. So when Haridasa Thakur was invited to come to that assembly in which the discourses were to take place, Haridasa Thakur, accompanied by Balaramacharya, came to the house of the Majumdars. So when all the devotees, particularly these two Majumdars, they saw Haridasa Thakur, they immediately stood up to receive him with great respect, and they fell at his lotus feet, and they gave him a nice place to sit. In that assembly were many people, many learned scholars, brahmanas, and respectable gentlemen. In fact, these two brothers, Hiranya and Govardhan Majumdar, were also very learned.

---
### chanting, holy name, liberation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/f5bv6Lnef8w?t=677
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Actually, by chanting the holy name without offences, one awakens his ecstatic love for the lotus feet of Krishna."
**Summary:** The speaker describes a discussion about the glories of chanting the holy name, where Haridas Thakur explains that the true result of chanting is awakening ecstatic love for Krishna, while liberation and freedom from sinful reactions are merely by-products.

So everyone began to speak of Haridasa Thakur's great qualities. So the brothers were very happy to hear everyone glorifying Haridasa Thakur. So it was mentioned that Haridasa Thakur used to chant three lakh names of Krishna every day. Practically, he would chant the whole day. And the scholars began to discuss the glories of chanting the holy name. Some of them said by chanting the holy name one is freed from all reactions of sinful life. Then somebody else said by chanting the holy name one is liberated from material bondage. Haridasa Thakur, however, said that if you chant the holy name, you will be freed from material bondage. These two benedictions are not the true result of chanting the holy name. Actually, by chanting the holy name without offences, one awakens his ecstatic love for the lotus feet of Krishna. Actually, it is described in the scriptures. Chanting of the holy name, offenselessly, awakens the dormant devotion, and one becomes a lover of God, Krishna. Now, as regards liberation and becoming free from sinful reactions are concerned, they are insignificant by-products of chanting the holy name. The holy name of the Supreme Lord. Insignificant by-products.

---
### chanting, holy name, offense
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/f5bv6Lnef8w?t=786
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Even before one becomes a pure chanter of the holy name, even before that, just by a glimpse of offenseless chanting, immediately all sinful reactions are completely nullified."
**Summary:** Haridas Thakur uses the analogy of the rising sun to explain how even a glimpse of offenseless chanting can eradicate sinful reactions, while pure chanting leads to ecstatic love for Krishna, leading to a debate with a brahmana.

Now, Haridasa Thakur gives an example to help us understand. Just like, when the sun rises, it dissipates the world's darkness immediately. So, similarly, when one chants the holy name of the Supreme Lord without offences, automatically all the sinful reactions are completely eradicated. Now, Srila Haridasa Thakur explains that even before the sun rises, there is some first glimpse of sunlight that appears before the sun rises, and even with the first glimpse of this sunlight, the fear of thieves, ghosts, demons, immediately disappears. And when the sun actually rises, everything is manifest and everyone begins performing his religious activities and material duties. So, similarly, at the first hint that somebody is chanting offenselessly the holy name of the Supreme Lord, immediately all reactions to sinful life are completely eradicated. So, when actually one chants the holy name offenselessly, one becomes ecstatic in love of Krishna. So, Haridasa Thakur explains that even before one becomes a pure chanter of the holy name, even before that, just by a glimpse of offenseless chanting, immediately all sinful reactions are completely nullified. So, liberation is actually an insignificant thing for a pure devotee. So, when one particular brahmana, Gopal Chakravarthy, who was the official chief tax collector in that village, he heard this, he became very upset. He said, he had a beautiful body, very nice handsome features, and he began to criticize Haridasa Thakur. He said, look at this emotional devotee saying that liberation is very easily obtained by a glimpse of offenseless chanting of the holy name of the Supreme Lord. Liberation is so difficult. It takes millions of births. When one is perfectly situated in absolute knowledge, only then one may attain liberation. But here is the sentimental person who says very easily you can get liberation.

---
### devotee, insult, consequences
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/f5bv6Lnef8w?t=994
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So, this is the result of offending a pure devotee that this Gopal Chakravarthi, he became afflicted by leprosy and he lost his nose when he insulted Haridasa Thakur."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes the story of Haridas Thakur, recounting how the brahmana who insulted him was afflicted with leprosy and lost his nose, emphasizing the severe consequences of offending a pure devotee.

So, then Haridasa Thakur said, why are you doubting the revealed scriptures? I am just quoting the revealed scriptures. So, then this brahmana said, if it is not true, then I will cut off your nose. Haridasa Thakur humbly accepted. If it is not true, then Haridasa Thakur said, yes, you can cut my nose. But others became very, very upset at this brahmana Gopal Chakravarthi insulting Haridasa Thakur. So, they all protested, and Haridasa Thakur did not take offense and left that place. But very soon, in three days, this brahmana, he got leprosy, and his nose was affected, and it fell off. So, this is the result of offending a pure devotee that this Gopal Chakravarthi, he became afflicted by leprosy, and he lost his nose when he insulted Haridasa Thakur telling, I will cut off your nose if what you are telling is not true. So, we have to be careful not to insult devotees. Otherwise, it is very dangerous for our spiritual life.

---

### Krishna's grace, devotee protection, pastimes
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9hE7nn0Xp4k?t=50
**Verse References:** SB 1.15.11
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "At that time, Lord Krishna, simply by accepting the remnants of food, saved us."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the verse from Srimad Bhagavatam 1.15.11, explaining how Krishna saved the Pandavas from Durvasa Muni's plot by accepting a small offering of food, which satisfied everyone.

In this session we will be discussing from the 11th verse of the 15th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. The meaning of this verse is, during our exile, Durvasa Muni, who eats with his 10,000 disciples, intrigued with our enemies to put us in dangerous trouble. At that time, Lord Krishna, simply by accepting the remnants of food, saved us. By his accepting food thus, the assembly of Munis, while bathing in the river, felt sumptuously fed, and all the three worlds were also satisfied. This is a wonderful pastime of Krishna, described in detail in the Mahabharata, but just mentioned here by Arjuna. Arjuna is recollecting these wonderful incidents, all by Krishna's grace, how the Pandavas were saved, so many different instances are recollected by Arjuna.

---
### Durvasa Muni, Kunti, Duryodhana
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9hE7nn0Xp4k?t=146
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Duryodhana knew that this Durvasa Muni has got lot of mystic powers."
**Summary:** The speaker describes Durvasa Muni's powers and his interactions with Kunti and Duryodhana, setting the stage for Duryodhana's plot to create trouble for Yudhishthira by sending Durvasa Muni as an unexpected guest.

So it is described, Durvasa Muni, he was a very powerful mystic Brahmana, and he had performed a lot of austerities. So he had a lot of power, mystic power. And this Durvasa Muni, he was a partial incarnation of Lord Shiva. And when he was satisfied with somebody's service, he would do lot of good to that person. But if he was dissatisfied, he could bring about the greatest calamity. Once it so happened that he was, this Durvasa Muni was, a guest at the house of Kunti's father. And Kunti was serving him as a young girl, and Durvasa Muni was satisfied with her, and he blessed her with the power to call any devata she desired in order to have a child. Now, Durvasa Muni could foresee that this Kunti, when she grows up, she will not be able to beget children through her husband Pandu. Therefore, Durvasa Muni blessed her in this way. And it turned out that Kunti could beget children only by calling upon some demigods, because she could not beget children through Pandu. So, this Durvasa Muni used to travel in space by his great mystic powers, and he could travel everywhere in the whole universe. Now, once he was a guest at the palace of Duryodhana in Hastinapura. And Duryodhana satisfied this brahmana, Durvasa Muni, and when Durvasa Muni was satisfied, Durvasa Muni wanted to give some benediction to Duryodhana. So, Duryodhana knew that this Durvasa Muni has got lot of mystic powers. So, what did Duryodhana ask this Durvasa Muni? He requested that Durvasa Muni become a guest of Yudhishthira Maharaj, who is in exile, who was in exile at that time, that you become a guest of Yudhishthira, and you should visit him only after Draupadi has finished her meals. At such a time, on such a day, you should visit the King Yudhishthira in exile. So, Durvasa Muni agreed because he was very pleased with Duryodhana.

---
### Duryodhana's plot, Draupadi's devotion, Krishna's mercy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9hE7nn0Xp4k?t=356
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Duryodhana's plan was that when Durvasa Muni visits Yudhishthira with 10,000 disciples, and that too after Draupadi has finished her meal, then how will Yudhishthira feed Durvasa Muni and his 10,000 disciples?"
**Summary:** The speaker details Duryodhana's scheme to inconvenience Yudhishthira by sending Durvasa Muni and his large entourage after Draupadi had finished eating, highlighting the limitations of the magical pot and setting up the crisis that leads to Draupadi's prayer to Krishna.

Now, Duryodhana's plan was that when Durvasa Muni visits Yudhishthira with 10,000 disciples, and that too after Draupadi has finished her meal, then how will Yudhishthira feed Durvasa Muni and his 10,000 disciples? Because the Pandavas during exile, they had been gifted with one special pot. That pot would give enough food to any number of people, till Draupadi would take her meals. After that, the pot would not give any more food, not produce any more food. So, when Durvasa Muni visited the camp of Yudhishthira in the forest as his guest, he visited after Draupadi had finished her meals. So, Yudhishthira received this Durvasa Muni very nicely and requested the Muni to go to the nearby river and take bath and finish his noon duties and then come for lunch. So, the Muni went, Durvasa Muni went with his 10,000 disciples to the river to take bath. All of them were taking bath and in the meantime, Yudhishthira became very anxious. How is he going to serve lunch for Durvasa Muni and his 10,000 disciples? So, in his anxiety, he was praying to Krishna because they are all devotees of Krishna. So, Yudhishthira realized that Durvasa Muni has come after Draupadi has finished her meals. So, how will they feed? In any case, Yudhishthira requested Draupadi to do something. Then Draupadi began to pray to Krishna. And Draupadi, when she prayed to Krishna, Krishna appeared in front of Draupadi. The Lord is very merciful to his devotees, especially to his devotees. So, because Draupadi was in trouble, she was praying to the Lord and Lord Krishna appeared. Then, when Draupadi told Krishna about their dangerous situation, Krishna just requested Draupadi that you give me some food, then your problem is solved. So, Draupadi, when she heard that Krishna is asking for some food, she felt very bad that she could not serve Krishna with any food because she had finished her meals and there was nothing else in stock.

---
### Krishna's acceptance, miracle, devotee protection
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9hE7nn0Xp4k?t=574
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "After Krishna had taken that one small grain remaining in the pot, he told Draupadi, Now, you can invite your guests and you can feed them."
**Summary:** The speaker narrates the miraculous solution where Krishna accepts a single grain of rice from the pot, satisfying everyone, including Durvasa Muni and his disciples, who felt full while bathing in the river.

So, when she was unable to give any food to Krishna, she began to cry, as is natural for a woman who is in difficulty. But, when she began to cry, Krishna called her and said, Why don't you bring that pot which has been gifted by the Sun God to you? Bring that pot now, immediately. So, Draupadi brought that pot. The pot was empty. The pot was not having any food. So then, Krishna examined very carefully inside the pot. There was one small grain of foodstuff sticking to a corner of the pot. So, Krishna picked up that small grain and ate it immediately. After Krishna had taken that one small grain remaining in the pot, he told Draupadi, Now, you can invite your guests and you can feed them. So, on Krishna's instruction, Draupadi went to Yudhishthira and told him, Now, the guests can be invited. So, Yudhishthira sent Bhima to the river to call Durvasamuni and his 10,000 disciples to come for lunch. And meantime, what had happened, that Durvasamuni and his disciples were taking bath in the river and performing their religious duties. They began to feel completely satisfied, completely satisfied. Their hunger was completely mitigated. That, when Bhima came to invite them, Durvasamuni thought, Oh, Yudhishthira should have arranged a big feast and I am feeling very much full. How can I do justice? I better don't go. I don't accept that invitation because I am already feeling completely full in my stomach, in my belly, just by taking this bath. Though actually, Durvasamuni had not taken his meals at all that day, but still he began to feel full. So, since Durvasamuni thought that he didn't want to disappoint Yudhishthira by refusing outright the lunch, so he quietly went away from that place without going to Yudhishthira's camp. He and his disciples all felt completely full and they went away.

---
### Krishna consciousness, satisfaction, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/9hE7nn0Xp4k?t=793
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "When Krishna is satisfied, everyone is satisfied."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the philosophical significance of the story, emphasizing that satisfying Krishna satisfies everyone because He is the center and the complete whole, and concludes by highlighting the importance of offering food to the Lord with devotion.

Now, how is it that without eating anything, actually the 10,000 disciples of Durvasamuni and Durvasamuni himself began to feel full? It is because yasmintushte jagatushtihi. When Krishna is satisfied, everyone is satisfied. When Krishna's hunger is mitigated, everyone's hunger is mitigated. Because Krishna is the center and everyone else is his part, his amsa. He is a complete whole. So, this incident proves that a householder must always offer food to the Lord and in that way a householder will be able to satisfy or please anybody, any number of guests, including guests numbering 10,000 which Durvasamuni used to travel with. So, Yudhishthira Maharaj was able to actually satisfy Durvasamuni with all his 10,000 disciples simply by Krishna accepting a tiny grain of particle. In fact, Krishna is not in need of anything, but he accepts when something is offered with devotion for the sake of benedicting his devotees, for the sake of reciprocating with his devotees. So, I'll stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

---

### Arjuna, Krishna's grace, divine weapons
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/bgJl9llMpcc?t=56
**Verse References:** SB 1.15.12
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "It was by his influence only that in a fight I was able to astonish the personality of God Lord Shiva and his wife the daughter of Mount Himalaya."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces and translates verse 1.15.12 of the Srimad Bhagavatam, focusing on Arjuna's recounting of Krishna's grace and the acquisition of divine weapons.

Yat Tejasatha Bhagavan Yudhisula Panir Vismapitasagirijostram Adhan Nijamme Anyepichaham Amunaiva Kalevarena Prapto Mahendra Bhavane Mahadasanardham Prapto Mahendra Bhavane Mahadasanardham. The meaning of this verse is: It was by his influence only that in a fight I was able to astonish the personality of God Lord Shiva and his wife the daughter of Mount Himalaya. Thus he, Lord Shiva, became pleased with me and awarded me his own weapon. Other demigods also delivered their respective weapons to me, and in addition, I was able to reach the heaven. I was able to reach the heavenly planets in this present body and was allowed a half elevated seat.

---
### devotee, demigods, Krishna's supremacy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/bgJl9llMpcc?t=124
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The devotees of the Supreme Lord know how to respect the demigods, but the devotees of the demigods sometimes foolishly think that the Supreme Personality of Godhead is no greater than the demigods."
**Summary:** The speaker explains Srila Prabhupada's purport, emphasizing that pleasing Krishna leads to the favor of all demigods, while worshiping demigods exclusively is a limited and potentially offensive approach.

These are words spoken by Arjuna. He is speaking to Yudhishthira. He has returned from Dwaraka. He has brought the news that Krishna has departed for his personal abode, and he is recollecting the grace of Lord Krishna by which he was able to do so many wonderful things. So Srila Prabhupada explains this in the purport: By the grace of the Supreme Personality of God Sri Krishna, all the demigods, including Lord Shiva, were pleased with Arjuna. The idea is that one who is favored by Lord Shiva or any other demigod may not necessarily be favored by Arjuna, but he was certainly a great devotee of Lord Shiva. But he could not be saved from the wrath of the Supreme Personality of God Lord Ramachandra. And there are many instances like that in the histories of the Puranas. But here is an instance where we can see that Lord Shiva became pleased even with the grace of Lord Shiva. He was pleased with Arjuna. He was pleased with Arjuna. He was pleased with Arjuna. He was pleased with Arjuna. He was pleased with Arjuna. Even in the fight with Arjuna. The devotees of the Supreme Lord know how to respect the demigods, but the devotees of the demigods sometimes foolishly think that the Supreme Personality of Godhead is no greater than the demigods. By such a conception, one becomes an offender and ultimately meets with the same end as Ravana and others. The instances described by Arjuna during his friendly dealings with Lord Sri Krishna are instructive for all who may be convinced by the lessons that one can achieve all favors simply by pleasing the Supreme Lord Sri Krishna, whereas the devotees or worshippers of the demigods may achieve only partial benefits which are also perishable, just as the demigods themselves are. In the Bhagavad Gita it is said,

The living conditions are different on different planets. The duration of life is different. The atmospheric conditions are different. Just like Srila Prabhupada gives the example, there are so many creations. There are so many creatures, aquatics, living in the water, but we cannot live in the water. Similarly, there are creatures living in the air. Now, we cannot live in the air. So, every creature, created being, has got a suitable body for residing on a particular planet in a particular atmosphere. This is the reason why it is said here simply somebody cannot just go by some mechanical means and enter the heavenly planets. It is not possible. **Summary:** Arjuna recollects his past glories and acknowledges that his power and success, including the killing of the demon Nivartakavacha, were due to Krishna's grace, expressing his current feeling of separation from Krishna.

In this session we will be discussing from three verses 13, 14 and 15 of the 15th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. The 13th verse first: Tatraiva Mev Viharato Bhujadanda Yugvam Gandiva Lakshanam Arati Vadhaya Devaha Sendra Shrita Yad Anubhavitam Aaja Meedha Tenah Madhya Mushita Purushena Bhumah. Meaning of this verse is when I stayed for some days as a guest in the heavenly planets all the heavenly demigods including King Indradeva took shelter of my arms which were marked with the Gandiva bow to kill the demon named Nivartakavacha O King, descendant of Aja Meedha at the present moment I am dead to you. I am dead to you. I am bereft of the supreme personality of Godhead by whose influence I was so powerful. So Arjuna is recollecting when he went to the heavenly planets he stayed there some time under the grace of Krishna even Indra was taking shelter of the protection of Arjuna because Arjuna was expert in using his Gandiva bow to kill the misguided demon to kill the miscreants. So there was one demon by name Nivartakavacha he was troubling the demigods but he was killed by Arjuna using his Gandiva bow. So that is being recollected by Arjuna. O King, descendant of Aja Meedha at the present moment I am bereft of the supreme personality of Godhead. Arjuna is feeling the separation from Krishna.

After Krishna departed for his own personal abode where he is eternally situated. So in the purport, Srila Prabhupada gives this brief purport. He says, The heavenly demigods are certainly more intelligent, powerful and beautiful and yet they had to take help from Arjuna because of his Gandiva bow which was empowered by the grace of Lord Sri Krishna. It is not simply another weapon or another bow or another set of arrows, no. It was the Gandiva bow which Arjuna was actually having and which is described as transcendental in the hands of the pure devotees or the Supreme Lord whatever be the particular item it is all auspicious. The Lord is all powerful and by his grace his pure devotee can be as powerful as he may desire and there is no limit to it. And when the Lord withdraws his power from anyone he is powerless by the will of the Lord. So devotees powerful or powerless according to the will of the Lord is completely surrendered. He does not demand. The fourteenth verse.

**Summary:** Arjuna recalls the formidable strength of the Kauravas in the battle of Kurukshetra and acknowledges that his victory, including regaining the cows, was solely due to Krishna's grace and friendship.

The meaning of this verse is The military strength of the cowrovers was like an ocean in which there dwelled many invincible existences and thus it was insurmountable. But because of his friendship I, seated on the chariot was able to cross over it and only by his grace was I able to regain the cows and also collect by force many helmets of the kings which were bedecked with jewels that were sources of all brilliance. Again Arjuna is recollecting that the battle of Kurukshetra the cowrover's strength was formidable almost unconquerable but by the grace of Krishna Arjuna was able to conquer it. Arjuna was able to defeat the cowrovers and defeat them. And even in the fight against the cowrovers when they attacked the Pandavas while the Pandavas were in exile by the grace of Krishna only Arjuna was able to defeat the cowrovers who had defeated them. Who had stolen the cows of the king of Virat Pradesh.

## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.15.16 Part 1 | 06-12-2022
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### protection, devotion, grace
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/stZW2w7qFk8?t=60
**Verse References:** SB 1.15.16
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Protection by the Lord means complete protection, 100% protection."
**Summary:** The speaker explains verse 1.15.16 of Srimad Bhagavatam, focusing on how Lord Krishna's grace protected Arjuna, drawing a parallel with Prahlad Maharaj's protection by Lord Nrsimadeva.

In this session, we will be discussing the first part of the sixteenth verse of the fifteenth chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. The sixteenth verse is as follows: Yad doshu ma pranihitam guru bhishma karna, naptra trigarta shalya saindhava bahli kaadyai, astrani amogha mahimani nirupitani, nopas prushur nriharidasam ivasurani. The meaning of this verse is, the sixteenth verse. Great generals like Bhishma, Drona, Karna, Bhurishrava, Susharma, Shalya, Jayadratha, and Balika all directed their invincible weapons against me. But by His, that is Lord Krishna's grace, they could not even touch a hair on my head. Similarly, Prahlad Maharaj, the supreme devotee of Lord Nrsimadeva, was unaffected by the weapons the demons used against him. So Arjuna is telling, because of Lord Krishna's grace, Arjuna was completely protected, that even the powerful Kaurava generals, they could not touch even one hair of Arjuna. In a fierce battle like that, how is it that Arjuna escaped completely unhurt, only by the grace of Krishna? And a comparison is made, just like Prahlad Maharaj was a pure devotee of Lord Nrsimadeva, and he was unaffected by so much trouble given by the demons, in so many ways, they tried to kill him, but they could not. Similarly, in the battle of Kurukshetra, the enemy party tried to kill the Pandavas, but they could not do anything to the Pandavas. So Srila Prabhupada explains in the purport, the history of Prahlad Maharaj, the great devotee of Nrsimadeva, is narrated in the 7th chapter. Pralad Maharaj, a small child of only five years, became the object of envy for his great father, Hiranyakashyapu, only because of his becoming a pure devotee of the Supreme Lord. The demon father employed all his weapons to kill the devotee's son, Prahlad, but by the grace of the Lord, Prahlad was saved from all the troubles, and he was not killed. He was saved from all sorts of dangerous actions by his father. Hiranyakashyapu attempted in so many ways, let me kill this boy, but Hiranyakashyapu was unsuccessful, because all the while, Prahlad was perfectly protected by Lord Nrsimadeva directly. And Srila Prabhupada lists here how Prahlad was put into dangerous situations. He was thrown in a fire, in boiling oil, he was thrown from the top of a hill, he was attempted to be crushed under the legs of an elephant, he was administered poison, and at last the father himself took up a chopper to kill his son, and thus Nrsimadeva appeared and killed the heinous father in the presence of the son. So protection by the Lord means complete protection, 100% protection. So Srila Prabhupada concludes this portion by telling, thus no one can kill the devotee of the Lord. Similarly, Arjuna was also saved by the Lord, although all dangerous weapons were employed by his great opponents like Bhishma, Karna, etc. So it's a fact that Arjuna was really saved only because of Krishna.

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Karna was born of Kunti by the sun god prior to her marriage with Maharaj Pandu."
**Summary:** Srila Prabhupada describes the personality of Karna, elaborating on his birth, his charitable nature, and his various names.

Next, Srila Prabhupada describes the personality of Karna in the purport. An elaborate description is given. Karna was born of Kunti by the sun god prior to her marriage with Maharaj Pandu. Karna took his birth with bangles and earrings, extraordinary signs for an undaunted hero. In the beginning, his name was Vasusena, but when he grew up, he presented his natural bangles and earrings to Indradeva, and thenceforward he became known as Vaikartana. After his birth from the maiden Kunti, he was thrown in the Ganges. Later, he was picked up by Adhiratha, and Adhiratha and his wife Radha brought him up as their own offspring.

### Karna, Draupadi, enmity
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/stZW2w7qFk8?t=576
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Karna was very enthusiastic to declare the news, and it is he who ordered Dushasana to take away the garments of both the Pandavas and Draupadi."
**Summary:** The speaker details Karna's role in Duryodhana's court, his animosity towards Draupadi stemming from his exclusion from her Swayamvara, and his involvement in the ill-fated gambling match.

When Duryodhana was in power, he enthroned Karna in the state of Anga. Being baffled in his attempt to win Draupadi, Karna advised Duryodhana to attack King Drupada, and after defeating him, both Arjuna and Draupadi could be arrested. But Dronacharya rebuked them for this conspiracy, and they refrained from the action. Karna was defeated many times, not only by Arjuna, but also by Bhimasena. He was a king of the kingdom of Bengal, Orissa, and Madras combined. Later on, Karna took an active part in the Rajasuya sacrifice of Maharaja Yudhishthira, and when there was gambling between the rival brothers designed by Shakuni, Karna took part in the game, and he was very pleased when Draupadi was offered as a bet in the gambling. That gambling match was ill-fated for the Pandavas. The Pandavas were losing, and Yudhishthira was placing as a bet even Draupadi, and themselves also as a bet. When Draupadi was in the game, Karna was very enthusiastic to declare the news, and it is he who ordered Dushasana to take away the garments of both the Pandavas and Draupadi. Because Karna was very much angry against Draupadi, because Draupadi, Swayamvara, Karna was not able to participate, because he was declared as the son of a carpenter, a shudra. So the Pandavas, when they could not protect Draupadi in that gambling match, at that time, Karna asked Draupadi to select another husband, because she was lost by the Pandavas. And when she was lost by the Pandavas, she in effect became a slave of the Kauravas.

### Karna, enmity, Kurukshetra, Krishna's grace
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/stZW2w7qFk8?t=773
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Arjuna was able to defeat Karna only because of the grace of Krishna."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes the description of Karna, highlighting his continued enmity towards the Pandavas, his understanding of Krishna's role in the Pandavas' victory, his arrogance, and his eventual death at the hands of Arjuna due to Krishna's grace.

Now Karna maintained enmity against the Pandavas, though they had no reason actually. Specifically he had no reason, but he maintained his enmity against the Pandavas. And he tried to curb them by so many ways. Now during the battle of Kurukshetra, he could understand that because Krishna was the chariot driver of Arjuna, he understood that the Pandavas will win the battle. And Karna had some difference of opinion on Bhishma, and sometimes he was very very proud of his prowess, and he said in his pride, I can defeat the Pandavas in five days, if Bhishma would not interfere with my plan of action. This is the arrogant statement of Karna. Now when Bhishma died, Karna was very much mortified. Karna killed the largest number of Pandava soldiers. When there was a severe fight with Arjuna, then Karna was able to knock off Arjuna's helmet. And that's a big achievement because Arjuna was a very very powerful fighter. Now when Karna was fighting his Arjuna, his chariot wheel got stuck in the battlefield mud. And he got down from his chariot to set the wheel right, but Arjuna took the opportunity and killed Karna when Karna was fixing the wheel of the chariot. So this is the history of Karna that's being described by Prabhupada in the Purport. Karna was a very powerful fighter and Arjuna was able to defeat Karna only because of the grace of Krishna. So I'll stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RxGXtrLVK3E?t=40
**Verse References:** SB 1.15.16
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "By Lord Krishna's grace, they could not even touch a hair on my head."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the session's topic: the second part of the purport to the 16th verse of the 15th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam, focusing on Arjuna's protection by Krishna and comparing it to Prahlad Maharaj's protection by Lord Nisimadeva.

Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya Naram Chai Vanarottamam. Devim Saraswati Vyasm Tato Jayam Udhirayet. In this session we will be discussing from the 16th verse of the 15th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. This is the second part. The previous session we discussed the first part. We will be discussing the second part of the purport in this session. The verse, 16th verse is as follows. The meaning of this verse is great generals like Bhishma, Drona, Karna, Bhurishrava, Susharma, Shalya, Jayadratha and Balika all directed their invincible weapons against me. This is Arjuna speaking. By Lord Krishna's grace, they could not even touch a hair on my head. Similarly, Prahlad Maharaj, the supreme devotee of Lord Nisimadeva was unaffected by the weapons the demons used against him.

### Bhurishrava, Kurukshetra, devotee
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RxGXtrLVK3E?t=131
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Bhurishrava advised the sons of Dhritarashtra not to pick any quarrel or fight with the Pandavas."
**Summary:** The speaker summarizes Srila Prabhupada's description of Bhurishrava, his lineage, his presence at Draupadi's Swayamvara and the Rajasuya Yagna, his advice to the sons of Dhritarashtra, his role in the Kurukshetra battle, and his ultimate death at the hands of Satyaki.

So in the previous session we discussed about Karna. Elaborately Prabhupada described Karna's history. And now Srila Prabhupada describes in brief about the other powerful personalities. First Bhurishrava is also called Naptah. So Srila Prabhupada says Bhurishrava was the son of Somadatta, a member of the Kuru family. His other brother was Shalya. Both the brothers and the father attended the Swayamvara ceremony of Draupadi. All of them appreciated the wonderful strength of Arjuna due to his being the devotee friend of the Lord and thus Bhurishrava advised the sons of Dhritarashtra not to pick any quarrel or fight with the Pandavas. All of them also attended the Rajasuya Yagna of Maharaj Yudhishthira. He possessed one Akshauhini regiment of army, cavalry, elephants and chariots and all of these were employed in the battle of Kurukshetra on behalf of Duryodhana's party. He was counted by Bhishma as one of the Yudhapathis. In the battle of Kurukshetra, he was especially engaged in a fight with Satyaki. He killed 19 sons of Satyaki. Later on, Arjuna cut off his hands and he was ultimately killed by Satyaki. After his death he merged into the existence of Vishwadeva.

A brief history of Trigarta or Susharma, same person. So first of all Srila Prabhupada says the Susharma was son of Maharaj Vruddhakshetra and he was the king of Trigarta desha. He was also present in the Swayamvara ceremony of Draupadi. He was one of the allies of Duryodhana and he advised. So this Susharma was ally of on the side of Duryodhana. He advised Duryodhana to attack Matsya desha which is also called Darbhanga now. During the time of cow stealing in Viratnagara. He was able to arrest Maharaj Virata. But later Maharaj Virata was released by Bhima. In the battle of Kurukshetra he also fought very valiantly but at the end he was killed by Arjuna. This Trigarta Susharma fought very bravely but he was defeated by, he was killed by Arjuna.

### Jayadratha, Draupadi, lust
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RxGXtrLVK3E?t=347
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Lust, when it attacks, a person becomes a victim and unable to remain focused on his actual present situation and still carries that desire I want to enjoy Draupadi in an illicit way."
**Summary:** The speaker describes Jayadratha's history, including his lineage, his marriage to Dushala, his presence at Draupadi's Swayamvara, his lustful attempts to approach Draupadi illicitly, and Draupadi's vehement refusal.

Next Srila Prabhupada describes Jayadratha. Jayadratha was another son of Maharaj Vruddhakshetra. He was the king of Sindhu desha, modern day Sindh in Pakistan. His wife's name was Dushala. He was also present in the Swayamvara ceremony of Draupadi. See Draupadi's Swayamvara ceremony was very famous because Draupadi was very very beautiful princess of her time. Mind you Draupadi is described in the Mahabharata as dark complexion. So our conception, modern conception of beauty is totally different than what was the conception of beauty in the Vedic times. In the Vedic times somebody may be dark complexion but they will be considered very beautiful if their bodily features are perfectly in proportion and the facial features are very very nice. Jayadratha was always trying to get in touch with Draupadi after he was not able to win Draupadi's hand in Draupadi's Swayamvara. So how much he tried is described here. When he was going to marry in the Shalya Desha on the way to Kamyavana, he happened to see Draupadi again. And it was too late. He was too much attracted to her. The Pandavas and Draupadi were then in exile and after losing their empire in gambling and Jayadratha thought that he would send news to Draupadi in an illicit manner through one Koti Shasya, one of his associates. See lust when it attacks. A person becomes a victim and unable to remain focused on his actual present situation and still carries that desire I want to enjoy Draupadi in an illicit way. So when Jayadratha tried to approach Draupadi in an illicit manner, Draupadi at once refused vehemently the proposal of Jayadratha. But because Jayadratha was so much attracted by Draupadi's beauty, he tried again and again and again and again to approach Draupadi. So finally he made a plan. To forcibly take away Draupadi on his chariot. And while he attempted that, at first Draupadi gave him a good dashing and he fell down like a cut root tree.

---
### Jayadratha, Draupadi, Pandavas, Kshatriya code
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RxGXtrLVK3E?t=577
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Those days, the Kshatriyas were expected to follow the code of conduct. They cannot touch any other woman or kidnap women or take away women. It's not within the Kshatriyas code of conduct."
**Summary:** The speaker continues the story of Jayadratha's attempted abduction of Draupadi, Dhamya Rishi's protest, the Kshatriya code of conduct, the Pandavas' attack on Jayadratha's soldiers, and Bhima's capture and beating of Jayadratha.

But at that time, when Dhamya Rishi, he saw this incident and he strongly protested the action of Jayadratha. Those days, the Kshatriyas were expected to follow the code of conduct. They cannot touch any other woman or kidnap women or take away women. It's not within the Kshatriyas code of conduct. So Jayadratha tried to somehow forcibly take Draupadi, but he was unsuccessful. So when he tried again and again, brought to notice of Maharaja Yudhishthira then the Pandavas attacked the soldiers of Jayadratha and killed them all and at last Bhima caught hold of Jayadratha and beat him very severely almost dead so this Jayadratha he was taken captive by the Pandavas.

### Jayadratha, Lord Shiva, Kurukshetra, Arjuna, Karma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RxGXtrLVK3E?t=690
**Verse References:** BG 2.47
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Kshatriya once has come to the battlefield, he cannot turn his back, Uddecha api apalayanam in the Bhagavad Gita it is said, Kshatriya quality."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes the story of Jayadratha, including his captivity, his release by Yudhishthira, his penance to Lord Shiva, his role in the Kurukshetra battle, Arjuna's vow to kill him, Krishna's reminder about Shiva's benediction, and the unusual circumstances of his death and his father's death.

And he was introduced as a slave of Maharaja Yudhishthira and in the same condition he was brought before Maharaja Yudhishthira. Maharaja Yudhishthira released him very kindly and he was taken captive. This Jayadratha he went to the Himalayas and undertook a severe type of penance to please Lord Shiva. He asked benediction from Shiva that he will be able to defeat all the Pandavas at least one at a time. So after that there was the battle of Kurukshetra and this Jayadratha was on the side of Duryodhana. He was fighting very valiantly. The first day he was engaged with Maharaja Drupada then he was fighting against Virata then against Abhimanyu and then he was fighting with Duryodhana. Now Abhimanyu's position was very very strong but he was mercilessly killed when he was surrounded by seven great generals. When Jayadratha heard that Arjuna took a vow to kill Jayadratha, then Jayadratha wanted to leave the war field. But he was not allowed to do so. Kshatriya once has come to the battlefield, he cannot turn his back, Uddecha api apalayanam in the Bhagavad Gita it is said, Kshatriya quality. He had to fight with Arjuna. And Krishna reminded Arjuna that Jayadratha had a special benediction of Lord Shiva that whoever would cause Jayadratha's head to fall on the ground, such a person's head would actually get chopped off. So Arjuna through the head of Jayadratha died. Jayadratha fell directly onto the lap of his father, who was engaged in penance at the Shamantha Panchaka pilgrimage site. So Jayadratha's father was surprised to see his head cut off, falling on his lap. He at once threw it to the ground. The father immediately died, Jayadratha's father died because his forehead was being cracked in seven pieces. According to the benediction that Jayadratha had received from Shiva, Jayadratha's own father actually had to die. So that's the end of this purport. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

### Krishna's mercy, Devotee relationship, Arjuna's recollection
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/uymyVX9hBSU?t=38
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So Arjuna is recollecting some past incidents in his intimate relationship with the Supreme Lord Krishna."
**Summary:** Arjuna reflects on Krishna's mercy and his own audacity in engaging the Lord as his chariot driver, highlighting the intimate relationship between Krishna and his devotees.

Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya Naram Jai Vanarottamam Devim Sarasvatim Vyasam Tato Jayam Udi Rayet. In this session, we will be discussing from the 17th verse of the 15th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. Sautye Vratakumatinatmada Ishwarome Yadpada Padmam Abhavaya Bhajantibhavya Maam Shrantavaham Arayo Rathino Bhuvishtham Napraharan Yad Anubhava Nirastam Yadpada Padmam Abhavaya Bhajantibhavya. The meaning of this verse is: It was by his mercy only that my enemies neglected to kill me when I descended from my chariot to get water for my thirsty horses, and it was due to my lack of esteem for my lord that I dared engage him as my chariot driver, for he is worshipped and offered services by the best men to attain salvation. So Arjuna is recollecting some past incidents in his intimate relationship with the Supreme Lord Krishna. And it is clearly established that Krishna is very, very merciful to his devotees, especially his pure devotees. He is very, very kind, very merciful, and he is ready to do anything for his devotees. So this is a good point that is being brought out by Arjuna that Krishna, who is worshipped and offered services by the best of men to attain salvation, is a very, very merciful person. That Supreme Lord, Arjuna is thinking, I dared engage him as my chariot driver. So Srila Prabhupada explains this in the purport, a little elaborately, this point.

### Krishna's universal appeal, Part and parcel, Seed-giving father
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/uymyVX9hBSU?t=372
**Verse References:** BG 14.4, BG 15.7
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Krishna is not only for the devotees or only for the transcendentalists who follow the Vedanta which is the correct philosophy but he is for everyone because everyone is his part and parcel."
**Summary:** Krishna descends to attract everyone, not just devotees or followers of Vedanta, because everyone is His part and parcel, and He is the seed-giving father of all living entities.

Those who are below even the impersonal consider him to be one of the great historical personalities. Apart from the Vedantas who follow the Vedanta philosophy presented by Vyasa Deva, there are others also. There are other philosophers, other transcendentalists. They consider him to be one of the great historical personalities. The Lord, however, descends to attract all by his specific transcendental pastimes. Krishna is not only for the devotees or only for the transcendentalists who follow the Vedanta which is the correct philosophy, but he is for everyone because everyone is his part and parcel. His own mama eva misho jiva loke jiva bhoota sanatana ha sarva bhutani asheshani drakshasi atmani atomai. So in several places in the Bhagavad Gita Krishna explains very carefully sarva yonisha kaunteya murtriya sambhavanti yaha tasam brahma mahad yonir aham bija pradapita I am the seed giving father for all the living entities who take birth in this material world. At the beginning of creation all living beings belong to me. They are mine, they are mine, they are mine, they are mine, they are mine, they are mine, they are mine, they are mine, they are mine, they are mine, they are mine, they are mine. They are within me, they are in me, they are my own part and parcel eternally. They are my parts, they belong to me.

---
### Krishna's presence, Forgetfulness, Transcendental relationships
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/uymyVX9hBSU?t=485
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna never forsakes anybody Krishna never disowns anybody Krishna is never away from anyone but we are forgetful."
**Summary:** Despite our forgetfulness and attempts to be independent, Krishna is always present as Paramatma, facilitating our existence, and He incarnates to attract us with His transcendental pastimes, playing the perfect part of master, friend, son, and lover.

So Krishna does not disown anybody though we may forget Krishna, we may try to be independent of Krishna, we may be neglectful of Krishna though he is prominent in our life even in this material world. Bhagavad Gita elaborately explains this how Krishna is prominent in our ordinary life even in this material world as Paramatma has entered our heart and then he is conducting all the affairs of this body. We are totally helpless in this body because we are spirit and this body is made up of the material energy. So for us material energy is foreign, a foreign substance. So we do not know what is this body, we do not know how to use this body, we cannot live in this body and utilize this body, but Krishna facilitates us Paramatma. So he is active every moment, he is active within every living being, only then the living being can actually live in the body and utilize the body for whatever purposes the living entity wants to. So Krishna never forsakes anybody Krishna never disowns anybody Krishna is never away from anyone but we are forgetful. We means the living entities in the material world who have taken birth by force of karma are the ones who are forgetful of Krishna, are the ones who are neglecting Krishna, are the ones who are wanting to be independent of Krishna. So here Srila Prabhupada says the Supreme Lord Krishna descends, comes down to this material world, that's what we say he incarnates to attract all by his specific transcendental pastimes and thus he plays the part of the most perfect master, friend, son and lover. These are the personal relationships Krishna has with his personal devotees, perfect master. He plays the part of the master when the devotee wants to serve him as a servant, when the devotee wants to serve him as his friend then he acts as a perfect friend, when the devotee wants to act as his father or mother he plays the part of the perfect son and when the devotee wants him as her beloved then he plays the part of the perfect lover. His transcendental relationship Arjuna was in friendship and a lot therefore played the part perfectly as he did with his parents lovers and wives while playing in such a perfect transcendental relation that devotee forgets by the internal potency of the Lord that is friend or son is a supreme personality of God.

---
### Yoga Maya, Devotee's perspective, Krishna's sweetness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/uymyVX9hBSU?t=687
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Intelligent men are attracted by the transcendental acting of the lord with a pure, unalloyed devotee like Arjuna."
**Summary:** Arjuna's repentance stems from Krishna's yoga maya, which facilitates intimate pastimes, and it requires intelligence to understand Krishna's dealings with pure devotees, appreciating His kindness and love without misinterpreting the devotee's actions or Krishna's nature.

Arjuna is repenting that how dare I engaged him as my chariot driver. He is a supreme lord. But the fact is Arjuna is made to forget that Krishna is the supreme lord by Krishna's internal potency, yoga maya. So that it can facilitate the intimate pastimes in Krishna's relationship with his devotees. Now Srila Prabhupada says Krishna wants to play the perfect part as the son or the friend or the lover. And sometimes even though the yoga maya practice is not perfect, the yoga maya covers the fact that Krishna is a supreme lord in his dealings with his devotees. Sometimes the devotee is bewildered by the acts of the lord. So after the departure of the lord, Arjuna was conscious of his great friend. But there is no mistake on the part of Arjuna. We should not think Arjuna made a big mistake by engaging Krishna as his chariot driver. Nor any. Arjuna was not wrongly thinking that Krishna is my friend. No. Intelligent men are attracted by the transcendental acting of the lord with a pure, unalloyed devotee like Arjuna. So it requires intelligence to understand Krishna's dealings with his pure devotees. Others who are conversant with the pure devotional, only they can appreciate Krishna's kindness and magnanimity and love for his devotees. Only on a pure devotional platform, we can understand these dealings of Krishna. Otherwise, we may mistake either that Krishna is an ordinary person or we may wrongly think that devotees are too puffed up and proud to engage Krishna as their friend or as their son or as their lover. No. There is no mistake on the part of the devotees. Neither Krishna is an ordinary person. He retains. He is eternally the transcendental supreme personality of Godhead. But he, out of his own sweet will, he wants his devotees to serve him in an intimate way, as his son or, sorry, as his mother or father or lover or friend. Krishna wants that. And therefore, the devotees are never at fault. I'll stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

---

### Krishna's pastimes, Friendship, Remembrance

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "His jokings and frank talks were pleasing and beautifully decorated with smiles."
**Summary:** Arjuna recollects his pleasing and heartwarming interactions with Krishna as a friend, highlighting Krishna's endearing qualities.

Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya Naram Jai Vanarottamam Deviim Saraswatim Vyasam Tatho Jayam Udi Rayet. In this session, we will be discussing from three verses: verse numbers 18, 19, and 20 of the 15th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. So, one verse at a time, the 18th verse to begin with. Narmani Udara Ruchira Smita Shobhitani He Partha He Arjuna Sakhe Kuru Nanda Neti Sanjal Pitani Naradeva Hrudis Prushani Smartur Luthanti Hrudayam Mama Madhavasya. Beautiful verse. Now the translation of this 18th verse. This is spoken by Arjuna to Yudhishthira. He is recollecting the nice pastimes of Krishna in Arjuna's relationship with Krishna as his friend. So, Arjuna is telling, "O King! His jokings and frank talks were pleasing and beautifully decorated with smiles. He addresses unto me, his addresses unto me as O son of Pritha, O friend, O son of the Kuru dynasty, and all such heartiness are now remembered by me, and thus I am overwhelmed."
