**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "People are not conscious, they are not aware many times that it is sinful and sinful activities are the cause of our suffering."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes Parikṣit's role as an ideal king who wanted to curb sinful activities. He contrasts the past, where such activities were rare and restricted, with the present, where they are openly advertised and indulged in, leading to ignorance and suffering.

Now, Pariksit Maharaj being the ideal king, Now, Pariksit Maharaj being the ideal king, at the beginning of Kali Yuga, he wanted to take action or steps against such causes of unrest. So, Prabhupada says, this means that at least up to the regime of Maharaj Pariksit, such symptoms of public life were practically unknown. And as soon as they were slightly detected, And as soon as they were slightly detected, he wanted to root them out. That means people could not freely indulge in meat-eating, intoxication, gambling, publicly. Some remote place, some rare instance, some real low-class fallen person may do it occasionally. But not that there are advertisements everywhere. But not that there are advertisements everywhere. But not that there are advertisements everywhere. But not that there are advertisements everywhere. Come, enjoy meat-eating, intoxication, gambling, illicit sex. Of course, we don't see advertisements in India for illicit sex openly. But neither for gambling, but for intoxication, you see boats, bar, bar and restaurant, pub, and non-vegetarian food, so much advertisement, so much advertisement. So, this means that people openly indulge in it and they are not conscious, they are not aware many times that it is sinful and sinful activities are the cause of our suffering. People are ignorant. People are, people are victimized by bad influence. It's not that people are born with some habit like meat-eating or intoxication. Prabhupada says that. Nobody is born

---
### bad association, duty of the king, culture
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/O3d4aVn7Uyc?t=807
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "It is only by bad association it is only by bad influence it is only by enticement by other sinful people that somebody really becomes addicted or habituated to sinful activities"
**Summary:** The lecture concludes by stating that sinful habits are acquired through bad association, not innate. It reiterates the king's duty to protect citizens from Kali's influence and highlights the importance of a culture that avoids sinful activities, using Gujarat and Rajasthan as examples.

with some habit like meat-eating or intoxication. Prabhupada says that. Nobody is born with a cigarette in his mouth no and as soon as a child is born what is he fed he's fed the mother's milk correct so it's not true that right from birth somebody is a non-veteran or a person who indulges in the sinful activities no it is only by bad association it is only by bad influence it is only by enticement by other sinful people that somebody really becomes addicted or habituated to sinful activities so Pariksit Maharaj he actually did not indulge in the sinful activities he did not indulge in the sinful activities he did not indulge in the sinful activities he did not indulge decided to root out these symptoms of Kali so even though Kali Yuga is destined to be an age of sinful activities it doesn't mean that the king should sit back and say after all it is destined so I don't have to bother about it no Maharaj Pariksit as his duty to save the citizens from the association of agents of agents of Kali he wanted to punish the miscreants indulging in the symptoms of Kali and thus save the innocent citizens were pure in habit by culture of religion you see our culture is very culture is to avoid sinful activities almost completely that is the culture just like even today in the state of Gujarat and Rajasthan it is recorded in the census data most of the people are vegetarians in these two states particularly Gujarat and Rajasthan maximum people are vegetarians so that is the kind of culture that is completely of culture that is there that was the culture that is prevalent in the whole country but in course of time people became more and more degraded and the influence was more so Srila Prabhupada says it is a duty of the king to give such protection and Maharaj Pariksit was perfectly right when he prepared himself to fight against miscreants who indulge in sinful activities I'll stop here thank you very much Hare Krishna

## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.16.11-12 | 16-01-2023
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### royal procession, Vedic aesthetics, leadership
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/zPPH4x2SRRU?t=49
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "There is a good sense of aesthetics in everything people did in the Vedic culture."
**Summary:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami discusses verses 11 and 12 of Srimad Bhagavatam 1.16, comparing Maharaj Pariksit's chariot to Arjuna's and highlighting the aesthetic and protective aspects of a Vedic royal procession.

In this session, we will be discussing two verses, the eleventh and the twelfth verses of the sixteenth chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. The eleventh verse. The meaning of this verse is, Maharaj Pariksit sat on a chariot drawn by black horses, Shyamaturanga. His flag was marked with the sign of a lion, Mrigendra Dhwajam, being so decorated and surrounded by charioteers, cavalry, elephants, and infantry. Cavalry is soldiers on horseback, infantry is soldiers on foot. And there were warriors or soldiers on chariots and soldiers on elephant back. He left the capital to conquer in all directions. So, Pariksit Maharaj is going to root out the symptoms of Kali from his kingdom, when Kali is just trying to conquer and to infiltrate his kingdom. So, that's the description given here. In the purport, Srila Prabhupada says, Maharaj Pariksit is distinguished from his grandfather Arjuna, for black horses pulled his chariot instead of white horses. You remember the description of Arjuna's chariot being pulled by white horses. This is the description of the first chapter of the Bhagavad Gita. Both Krishna and Arjuna were seated on a chariot, on a great chariot, Mahatishyandane, Shwetahayai, drawn by white horses. And in contrast to that, Maharaj Pariksit was the grandson of Arjuna. His chariot was being pulled by black horses, Shyamaturangayojitam. Then again, Srila Prabhupada says, the distinction between Pariksit's chariot and Arjuna's chariot. Again, one more difference is, Pariksit marked his flag with the mark of a lion, Mrugendra Dhwajam, and his grandfather Arjuna marked his with the mark of Hanumanji, Kapit Dhwajam. Kapi refers to Hanuman. A royal procession like that of Maharaj Pariksit, surrounded by well-decorated chariots, cavalry, elephants, infantry, and band, is not only pleasing to the eyes, but also is a sign of a civilization that is aesthetic even on the fighting front. There is a good sense of aesthetics in everything people did in the Vedic culture, even a royal procession. The king is proceeding to conquer all directions. The emperor. He is not just a king. He is an emperor. So he is surrounded by beautifully decorated chariots, elephants, infantry. So this is very, very pleasing to the eyes to see, and also this indicates how well prepared he was to actually give protection to the citizens, innocent citizens, or the well-behaved citizens. The twelfth verse.

---
### geography, Vedic cosmology, tribute
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/zPPH4x2SRRU?t=324
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Maharaj Pariksit then conquered all parts of the earthly planet."
**Summary:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami explains verse 12, detailing Maharaj Pariksit's conquest of various regions and the system of tribute, then delves into Vedic geography, describing the division of the earth into Dvipas and Varshas.

Bhadrashvam ketum alamcha bharatam chottaran kuram kim purushadini varshani vijitya jagrihe balim. So these are names of different parts of the earthly planet that Pariksit conquered. So in the translation, the translation is as follows: Maharaj Pariksit then conquered all parts of the earthly planet, Bhadrashva, Ketumala, Bharata, the Northern Kuru, Kim Purusha, etc., and exacted tributes from their respective rulers. The system was the subordinate kings had to pay a portion of the tax they collected from their citizens to the emperor. So Maharaj Pariksit went to different parts of his kingdom, that means the whole world, earthly planet, and exacted the taxes from the respective rulers. So Srila Prabhupada gives a brief description of these different parts that were the parts of the earth as divided during the time of Maharaj Pariksit. So first of all in the purport he describes Bhadrashva Varsha. It is a tract of land near Meru Parvata, and it extends from Gandhamadana Parvata to the salt water ocean. There is a description of this Varsha in the Mahabharata, in the Bhishma Parva 7th chapter verses 14 to 18. Mahabharata is the history of Greater India. So it is called Itihasa. It is compiled by Vyasadeva. So Srila Prabhupada is quoting from this, that this Bhadrashva Varsha is described in this particular portion of the Mahabharata. The description was narrated by Sanjaya to Dhritarashtra about this Bhadrashva Varsha. Maharaj Yudhishthira also conquered this Varsha, and thus the province was included within the jurisdiction of his empire. Maharaj Pariksit was formally declared to be the emperor of all lands ruled by his grandfather, but still he had to establish his supremacy while he was out of his capital to exact tribute from such states. The king is not simply a namesake king. He has got his power to rule, and he challenges the subordinate kings. If they accept his supremacy then there is no fighting. Otherwise there is a battle fought between the two kings, and whoever is victorious becomes the ruler. Ketumala Varsha, another part of his kingdom. This earth planet is divided into seven Dvipas by seven oceans.

---
### Jambudvipa, Varshas, Mahabharata
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/zPPH4x2SRRU?t=567
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The earth planet is divided into seven islands."
**Summary:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami continues describing the Vedic geography, focusing on Jambudvipa and its nine Varshas, referencing descriptions from the Mahabharata and highlighting the beauty and health of the inhabitants of certain Varshas.

And the central Dvipa called Jambudvipa is divided into nine Varshas, or parts, by eight huge mountains. See. The whole earth planet, even as we know it today, there are so many oceans, Pacific Ocean, Indian Ocean, and Atlantic Ocean, like that. So there are seven oceans dividing the earth planet into seven islands. The land mass between two oceans, surrounded by water is called an island, Dvipa. So Saptva Dvipa Vasundhara. This is a description in the Mahabharata. The earth planet is divided into seven islands, and the central island is called Jambudvipa. Dvipa means island. Jambudvipa. And this Jambudvipa, the central part, central island, is divided into nine Varshas, or nine parts, called Varshas. Dvipa and Varsha. So the names here, Ketumala Varsha, Bhadrasho Varsha, and, sorry, Bharata Varsha, Ilaavrata Varsha, Kimpurusha Varsha, like that. There are nine Varshas, nine divisions. And how are these nine divisions made? In the central island, in the Jambudvipa, by eight huge mountains separating some tracts of land. ## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.16.13-15 | 17-01-2023
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### Invocation, Introduction
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/d30rNAe9YZg?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "In this session we will be discussing from three verses, the two verses of the Shri Advaitha Gadadhara 13, 14 and 15 of the 16th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam."
**Summary:** The speaker begins with traditional Vaishnava prayers and announces the verses from Srimad Bhagavatam that will be discussed.

Om Agyana Timiran Dhasya Gyanan Janishalakaya Chakshurun Meelitam Yena Tasmai Shri Gurave Namaha. Nama Om Vishnu Padaya Krishna Preshtaya Bhutale Shri Mate Bhakti Vedanta Swamin Nithinamine. Namaste Saraswate Deve Gauravani Pracharine Nirvishesha Shunyavadi Pashchatya Deshatarine. Jaya Shri Krishna Chaitanya Prabhu Nithyananda Shri Advaita Gadadhara Shiva Sadhi Gauravakta Vrinda. Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare. Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya Naram Chai Vanarottamam Deveem Saraswateem Vyasam Tato Jayam Udeerayet. In this session we will be discussing from three verses, the two verses of the Shri Advaitha Gadadhara 13, 14 and 15 of the 16th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. These three verses are given together by Srila Prabhupada. So I will read the three verses and then we will hear the translation and then we will discuss something about these three verses from the purport.

---
### Recitation, Sanskrit
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/d30rNAe9YZg?t=76
**Verse References:** SB 1.16.13, SB 1.16.14, SB 1.16.15
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So all the three verses one after another I shall now recite."
**Summary:** The speaker recites the three Sanskrit verses (13, 14, and 15) from the 16th chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam.

So all the three verses one after another I shall now recite. 13th verse: Tatra Tatropa Srinvanaha Swapurvesham Mahatmanam Pragiyamanam Chayashaha Krishnamahatmya Suchakam. In the 14th verse: Atmanam Ch Paritratam Ashwatthamno Astratejasaha Sneham Ch Vrishniparthanam Tesham Bhaktim Ch Kesave. The last verse. Last verse in this series of three verses, the 15th verse. So the translation of these three verses given by Srila Prabhupada is as follows.

---
### Translation, Devotion, Krishna's Protection
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/d30rNAe9YZg?t=143
**Verse References:** SB 1.16.13, SB 1.16.14, SB 1.16.15
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So Parikshit also heard how he himself had been protected by the Lord from the powerful heat of the weapon of Ashwatthama."
**Summary:** The speaker presents Srila Prabhupada's translation of the three verses, focusing on Parikshit's hearing the glories of his forefathers, Krishna's protection of him in the womb, and the affection between the Vrishnis and Pandavas due to Kunti's devotion.

Wherever the king visited, he continuously heard the glories of his great forefathers who were all devotees of the Lord. That means basically the Pandavas. All the Pandavas were great devotees of Krishna, Keshava. And also of the glorious acts of Lord Krishna. People used to describe the forefathers of Parikshit and even the glorious activities of Krishna. Because Krishna was also ruling Dwaraka when Yudhishthira was ruling in Hastinapur. So Parikshit also heard how he himself had been protected by the Lord from the powerful heat of the weapon of Ashwatthama. Parikshit's birth was extraordinary. He's protected by Krishna even when he was in his mother's womb. He was attempted to be killed by Ashwatthama at the end of the battle of Kurukshetra, but Krishna saved him in his mother's womb. So that also people were glorified. People also mentioned the great affection between the descendants of Vrishni and Prutha due to the latter's great devotion to Lord Keshava. The descendants of Kunti, that is the Pandavas, and descendants of Vrishni, that means the family members of Krishna, there was a very nice affection between these two families because there was a very nice affection between these two families because Kunti was devoted to Krishna. Kunti was a pure devotee of Krishna. So therefore there was nice affectionate relationship between the Vrishnis and the Pandavas.

---
### Glorification, Krishna Consciousness, Bhagavatam
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/d30rNAe9YZg?t=284
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "That's why the Bhagavatam, which contains nothing but the glorification of Krishna and his pure devotees, is also called Krishna Samhita."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that King Parikshit was pleased to hear the glories of Krishna and His devotees, and rewarded the singers. He emphasizes that Krishna is the central subject of glorification, and that the Bhagavatam is called Krishna Samhita because it exclusively glorifies Krishna.

So King Parikshit being very pleased with the singers of such glories, opened his eyes in great satisfaction. Out of magnanimity, he was pleased to award them very valuable necklaces and clothing. He was very pleased to hear. What was he pleased to hear? The glories of Lord Krishna and the glories of Krishna's pure devotees, the Pandavas. And when he was pleased by such an act of glorification of Krishna and his pure devotees, he rewarded the singers with very valuable necklaces and very valuable clothing. So Srila Prabhupada explains a significant point to be noted in this, in these verses, in the Purpose Srila Prabhupada says, Kings and great personalities of the state are presented with welcome addresses. This is the Vedic culture. This is a system from time immemorial and Maharaj Parikshit, since he was one of the well-known emperors of the world, was also presented with addresses of welcome in all parts of the world as he visited those places. He was a very great emperor. So naturally people would welcome him. A great personality is welcomed when he visits a certain place. People who are conversant with his greatness, they will glorify him for the benefit of everyone else. The subject matter of those welcome addresses was Krishna. This is the most important point. How is it that Krishna is the subject matter of all those welcome addresses when Parikshit was travelling in different parts of his kingdom? Because Parikshit was a great devotee of Krishna. Parikshit's forefathers who were ruling the earth planet, the Pandavas, they were also great devotees of Krishna. So, whether somebody glorifies Krishna or Krishna's pure devotees, always the subject matter of such glorification is Krishna. That's why the Bhagavatam, which contains nothing but the glorification of Krishna and his pure devotees, is also called Krishna Samhita.

---
### Krishna and Devotees, Devotion, Spiritual World
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/d30rNAe9YZg?t=459
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna and his unalloyed devotees cannot be separated. And therefore, glorifying the devotee means, glorifying the Lord and vice versa."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the concept of Krishna Samhita, explaining that Krishna is never alone and is always with his devotees. He clarifies that glorifying a devotee is equivalent to glorifying Krishna, as devotees are always connected to Him.

That means, it is that special Vedic literature, Samhita means Vedic literature, which is exclusively devoted to descriptions of the glorifications of Krishna. That is the speciality of the Bhagavatam. So, therefore, it is called Krishna Samhita. It is a very special literature. Similarly, here it is said, wherever Parikshit went, the welcome addresses that were given to Parikshit, the subject matter was Krishna. So, Aushala Prabhupada clarifies, Krishna means, Krishna and his eternal devotees as the king means, the king and his confidential associates. A king is never alone. When a king travels, he has got his confidential associates. Similarly, Krishna is never alone. Krishna is always with his confidential associates, who are all his devotees. All are his devotees. Krishna is the one and only one Supreme Lord. And everyone else, especially in the spiritual world, everyone else is a devotee. Actually, everyone in the material world also is a devotee of Krishna. But, in the material world, people are forgetful of their devotional relationship with Krishna. That is the difference between the spiritual world and the material world. So, Aushala Prabhupada says, Krishna and his unalloyed devotees cannot be separated. And therefore, glorifying the devotee means, glorifying the Lord and vice versa.

---
### Bhakti, Krishna Consciousness, Devotee's Mind
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/d30rNAe9YZg?t=595
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Bhakti refers to devotion towards Krishna only and not devotion towards some other material personality."
**Summary:** The speaker defines Bhakti as devotion to Krishna alone, distinguishing it from other forms of devotion or patriotism. He emphasizes that devotees are constantly thinking of Krishna, making any glorification of them inherently a glorification of Krishna.

So, when Shastras say Bhakti, Bhakti refers to devotion towards Krishna only and not devotion towards some other material personality. Some other object of glorification. No. Just like they say, Desh Bhakti. Desh Bhakti. Devoted to the country. That word Bhakti cannot be used strictly speaking for somebody who is very devoted to who is patriotic. The word Bhakti cannot be used. Bhakti refers to devotion to Krishna only. So, even if somebody glorifies the devotee, that glorification will necessarily be glorification of Krishna only. Because, devotees are all the time thinking of Krishna only. So, devotees are all the time thinking of Krishna only. Because, devotees are all the time thinking of Krishna only. Because, devotees are all the time thinking of Krishna, speaking about Krishna, remembering Krishna, do every activity for the sake of pleasing Krishna.

---
### Devotee's Attachment, Krishna's Presence, Remembrance
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/d30rNAe9YZg?t=681
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "A devotee always wants the presence of the Lord. And how does a devotee always be in the presence of the Lord? By constantly thinking of Him, by constantly remembering Him, by constantly chanting His name, by constantly hearing about His glories, by constantly engaging in different acts of devotion and service to Lord Krishna."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that Parikshit was pleased by the glorification of Krishna and His devotees because it reminded him of Krishna. He emphasizes that a devotee's attachment is to Krishna and His devotees, and that devotees constantly seek the presence of the Lord through various devotional practices.

That is Bhakti. So, Srila Prabhupada further says, Maharaj Pariksit would not have been glad to hear about the glories of his forefathers like Maharaj Yudhishthir and Arjuna had they not been connected with the activities of Lord Krishna. This is a characteristic of a devotee. He is not simply attached to his family members like ordinary people are. No. His attachment is for devotees. His attachment is for Krishna and Krishna's devotees. The devotees, devotees are glorified by the presence of the Lord because they cannot live for a moment without the presence of the Lord and His different energies. This is the symptom of a devotee. A devotee always wants the presence of the Lord. And how does a devotee always be in the presence of the Lord? By constantly thinking of Him, by constantly remembering Him, by constantly chanting His name, by constantly hearing about His glories, by constantly engaging in different acts of devotion and service to Lord Krishna. So, therefore, Maharaj Pariksit felt the presence of the Lord when Krishna was glorified by His activities, especially when Pariksit was saved by Lord Krishna in the womb of His mother. So, that reminded him of Krishna. Whatever glorification people did, simply reminded Pariksit of Krishna, Krishna, Krishna, Krishna, Krishna, Krishna.

---
### Krishna's Protection, Leela, Bhagavad Gita
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/d30rNAe9YZg?t=796
**Verse References:** BG 11.55
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna would not have been glorified as speaker of Bhagavad Gita had his devotees like the Pandavas not been entangled in the battle of Kurukshetra."
**Summary:** The speaker reiterates that Krishna was the sole object of glorification. He explains that devotees are sometimes placed in dangerous situations, and when Krishna saves them, He becomes glorified. He uses the example of the Pandavas in the battle of Kurukshetra to illustrate how Krishna's devotees' involvement in difficult situations leads to His glorification.

So, therefore, So, therefore, Krishna was the only object of glorification in all such welcome addresses. That is the important point. So, Shlaprapada explains, The material world is full of joy. There are dangers at every step. That's the description of the Bhagavatam, padam, padam, yat vipadam, every step there is danger and the devotees sometimes are apparently placed into dangerous positions and when they are saved by the Supreme Lord Krishna, Krishna becomes glorified. See, this is the description that is there in the scriptures. Sometimes devotees are also put into dangers, but devotees have no other shelter other than Krishna. So, when they are in danger, especially devotees pray to Krishna, they think of Krishna, they seek shelter in Krishna's lotus feet and Krishna is always very, very, very attentive to his devotees. First of all, devotees cannot be harmed by anybody. In case, apparently, they are not harmed by anybody. In case, apparently, they are not harmed by anybody. In case, apparently, they are not harmed by anybody. Immediately, Krishna is there to save the devotees. Immediately, Krishna is there to save the devotees. As this current Bhagavad Gita. So, Shlaprapada says, So, Shlaprapada says, Krishna would not have been glorified as speaker of Bhagavad Gita had his devotees like the Pandavas not been entangled in the battle of Kurukshetra. See, the Pandavas' involvement in the battle of Kurukshetra is part of Krishna's Leela. See, the Pandavas' involvement in the battle of Kurukshetra is part of Krishna's Leela. Leela for what purpose many multiple purposes that Krishna came to reduce the burden of this earth planet which is overburdened by demons so Pandavas assisted Krishna by fighting in the battlefield of Kurukshetra and killing those demons or demoniac Nietzsche persons so they are simultaneously participating in Krishna Leela and also reducing the burden of this world which was Krishna's purpose of descent.

---
### Glorification, Falsehood, Vedic Culture
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/d30rNAe9YZg?t=955
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The singers of the glories of the great kings would not simply flatter them by some false statements, no never."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts the current practice of flattery in welcome addresses with the factual glorification in Vedic times. He notes that in the past, kings would reward singers generously, but nowadays, leaders are often glorified falsely and without genuine reward.

So Srila Prabhupada also says nowadays also there are presentations of welcome address but people glorify or flatter that's the correct word the the Leela even though it's allowed to say the word of God of abundance these people junks or leaders or the great personalities but always such glorification is not factual simply for pleasing the person they may say so many things he or she may or may not be actually worthy of all such glorification the singers of the glories of the great kings would not simply flatter them by some false statements, no never and also the Kings would actually reward the singers with nice presentations but nowadays where is such a reward being given because they themselves are not sufficiently rich and if they are rich they are not willing to part with their wealth so there is a difference between the way formerly great personality used to be received and welcomed and rewarded and what goes on today I will stop here thank you very much Hare Krishna

---

## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.16.16 | 18-01-2023
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### invocation, prayer
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/hd4IJZQfiIQ?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya Naram Jai Vanarottamam Deveem Saraswateem Vyasam Tato Jaya Mudhirayet"
**Summary:** The lecture begins with traditional Vaishnava prayers and mantras, invoking blessings and paying respects to spiritual teachers and deities.

Om Agyana Timiran Dhasya Gyanan Janishalakaya Chakshuran Militam Yena Tasmait Shri Gurave Namaha. Nama Om Vishnu Padaya Krishna Preshtaya Bhutale Shri Mate Bhakti Vedanta Swamin Nithinamine. Namaste Saraswate Deve Gauravani Pracharine Nirvishesha Shunyavadi Pachatya Deshatarine. Jaya Shri Krishna Chaitanya Prabhu Nityananda Shri Advaita Gadadhara Shri Vasadi Gaurabhakta Vrinda. Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare. Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya Naram Jai Vanarottamam Deveem Saraswateem Vyasam Tato Jaya Mudhirayet.

---
### devotion, Krishna's mercy, service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/hd4IJZQfiIQ?t=49
**Verse References:** SB 1.16.16
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Maharaj Pariksit heard that out of his causeless mercy Lord Krishna, or Vishnu, who is universally obeyed, rendered all kinds of service to the malleable sons of Pandu."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces Srimad Bhagavatam 1.16.16, explaining how Lord Krishna, despite being supreme, served the Pandavas, which overwhelmed Maharaj Pariksit with devotion.

In this session we will be discussing from the 16th verse of the 16th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. This is a very important verse. It is a very nice verse. Sarathya Parashada Sevana Sakya Dautya Veerasana Anugamana Stavana Pranaman Snigdheshu Pandushu Jagat Pranathimcha Vishno Bhaktim Karo Tindrapathish Charanara Vinde. Beautiful verse. What is the meaning of this verse? Maharaj Pariksit heard that out of his causeless mercy Lord Krishna, or Vishnu, who is universally obeyed, rendered all kinds of service to the malleable sons of Pandu by accepting posts ranging from chariot driver to president, to messenger, friend, night watchman, etc., according to the will of the Pandavas, obeying them like a servant and offering obeisances like one younger in years. When Pariksit heard this, he became overwhelmed with devotion to the lotus feet of the Lord.

---
### pure devotion, unalloyed bhakti, material contamination
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/hd4IJZQfiIQ?t=128
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Lord Krishna is everything to the unalloyed devotees like the Pandavas. Unalloyed devotees means pure devotees. Their bhakti is not mixed with any material contamination."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the concept of pure devotion (unalloyed bhakti) as described by Srila Prabhupada, contrasting it with devotion contaminated by material desires.

What a beautiful description here. So the people were describing glorifying Krishna that Krishna, even though he is a supreme Lord, he is obeyed universally by everybody, everyone else is a servant. He is the only master. But, because of his affection for his devotees, Krishna agrees to become a servant of his devotees. He is willing to pay obeisances to his devotee even though he is the superior in all circumstances. He is the supreme in all circumstances. So, Shrila Prabhupada says in the purport, Lord Krishna is everything to the unalloyed devotees like the Pandavas. Unalloyed devotees means pure devotees. Their bhakti is not mixed with any material contamination. What are the material contaminations in our rendering devotional service? When somebody performs bhakti for some material benefit, then such bhakti is called materially contaminated bhakti. Because bhakti should be rendered only for the sake of awakening our love for Krishna, awakening our devotion to Krishna, for the sake of becoming purified of all material contamination. Not increasing the material contamination, not keeping the material contamination very, very securely. No. That's not real bhakti. Though sometimes that also is called bhakti. When somebody prays to Krishna, I want some wealth or I want some relief from some disease, this is called bhakti. Bhakti is called bhakti. Bhakti which is contaminated by material desires.

---
### pure devotion, analogy, gold
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/hd4IJZQfiIQ?t=270
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "When you say gold, we mean pure gold. Similarly, when we say bhakti in the scriptures, when it is said bhakti, it means pure devotion. 100% devotion to Krishna."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of pure gold versus gold alloys (like 18 or 16 carat gold) to illustrate the difference between pure devotion and devotion mixed with material desires.

So the Pandavas and many such great devotees described and glorified in the Bhagavatam are all pure devotees, unalloyed devotees. Just like pure gold. Pure gold. So unalloyed. That is no mixture of any other metal. Pure gold. Similarly, pure bhakti. Bhakti means pure bhakti. Of course, nowadays they have a concept of 18 carat gold or 16 carat gold. That means it's not pure gold. 16 parts out of 24 are gold. So that means 8 parts, 1, 1/3 of that ornament is actually some other cheaper metal like copper or some other metal. So that's not pure gold. So 16 carat gold strictly cannot be called gold. When you say gold, we mean pure gold. Similarly, when we say bhakti in the scriptures, when it is said bhakti, it means pure devotion. 100% devotion to Krishna. And what is that 100% devotion? No material desires. Free from all material desires. So whenever somebody renders devotional service to Krishna, it should be free from any material desires. That's called unalloyed devotion. Pure devotion.

---
### pleasing Krishna, selfless service, pure devotees
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/hd4IJZQfiIQ?t=373
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Simply to please Krishna by one's service. That's all. So all pure devotees render service to Krishna only to please him and for no other purpose."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes that the sole purpose of pure devotional service is to please Krishna, exemplified by the Pandavas, for whom Krishna was everything.

Real devotion. If there is no material desire or material ambition to be fulfilled while rendering service to Krishna, then why render service at all? So there is a certain particular purpose in rendering devotional service to Krishna and that is only for one purpose. Simply to please Krishna by one's service. That's all. So all pure devotees render service to Krishna only to please him and for no other purpose. For no other purpose. So the Pandavas, they are pure devotees. They render service to Krishna simply for the sake of pleasing Krishna. So for the Pandavas, Krishna was the supreme lord, the spiritual master, the worshipable deity, the guide, the chariot driver, the friend, the servant, the messenger, and everything they could conceive of. You see.

---
### intimate relationship, reciprocity, Krishna's supremacy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/hd4IJZQfiIQ?t=452
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "In their intimate relationship, devotees forget Krishna is the supreme personality of Godhead. And Krishna also likes the devotees reciprocating with him or serving him intimately while not being conscious of Krishna's supreme position."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the intimate relationship between Krishna and his devotees, where devotees sometimes forget Krishna's supreme position, allowing for deeper loving service and reciprocation, using the example of Mother Yashoda.

Why would the Pandavas make Krishna their chariot driver, Arjuna? Why did he make Krishna his chariot driver? If he has got devotion to Krishna, how can he even think of asking Krishna, can you please be my chariot driver when I fight in the battle? This is because of the intimate relationship between Arjuna and Krishna. That's described in the scriptures. Intimate relationship. In their intimate relationship, devotees forget Krishna is the supreme personality of Godhead. And Krishna also likes the devotees reciprocating with him or serving him intimately while not being conscious of Krishna's supreme position. Because that supremacy of Krishna comes in the way of their rendering intimate loving service. Just like Mother Yashoda. If she is conscious that Krishna is the supreme lord, then she cannot show her motherly affection to Krishna. If Krishna becomes naughty, Mother Yashoda does not chastise Krishna, then where is motherly affection? Nor can Mother Yashoda actually do any service in terms of her motherly affection? If she knows Krishna is the supreme lord, where is the question of feeding Krishna? If Krishna is the supreme lord, he never becomes hungry, he is never in need of any food. He is the one who is supplying everybody. He is completely self-satisfied. He is full in himself. He is not in need of any food. He never gets hungry. So, the service is simply for the sake of loving reciprocation between the lord and his pure devotees. This should be understood.

---
### reciprocity, servant, master
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/hd4IJZQfiIQ?t=592
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Just like devotees want to please Krishna by serving Krishna, Krishna wants to please his devotees by serving them."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses the reciprocal relationship between Krishna and his devotees, where Krishna also serves his devotees to please them, clarifying that the concept of 'servant of Krishna' is different from material servitude.

Now, when the devotee renders service to Krishna, to please Krishna, Krishna reciprocates by himself becoming the servant of his devotees, or himself rendering service to the devotees, again to please the devotees. Just like devotees want to please Krishna by serving Krishna, Krishna wants to please his devotees by serving them. Please understand, when scriptures say we are servants of Krishna, the concept of servant of Krishna is not like the concept of servant in the material world. Never mistakenly think that servant means servant like in the material world. No. In the spiritual world, all positions are absolute positions. Prabhupada explains this in another place. The servant is the master. The master is the servant. There is no difference. What is the meaning of that? Like Krishna demonstrated, before the battle of Kurukshetra, Krishna is the lord, or playing the role of the lord, and Arjuna is playing the role of the subordinate to the lord. But on the battlefield of Kurukshetra, Krishna agreed to become the chariot driver of Arjuna, and Arjuna ordered Krishna, please take my chariot between the two armies, so that I can see with whom I have to fight. He is ordering Krishna. Krishna says, okay, according to your order, I will take the chariot between the two armies. So, this is the kind of reciprocation between the lord and his devotees.

---
### transcendental reciprocation, Krishna's kindness, devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/hd4IJZQfiIQ?t=729
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna is so kind to his devotees, that he is willing to do anything for his devotee."
**Summary:** The speaker explains how Maharaj Pariksit appreciated Krishna's transcendental reciprocation with his devotees, leading to an arousal of ecstatic devotion, using the example of Krishna acting as a night watchman for the Pandavas.

So, Maharaj Pariksit, as a pure devotee of the lord, he could appreciate the lord's transcendental reciprocation of the feelings of his devotees. And thus, he himself was also overwhelmed with the dealings of the lord. He began to feel Krishna is so kind to his devotees, that he is willing to do anything for his devotee. Just like the Pandavas, Krishna acted as a night watchman for the Pandavas, because they were exiled. And in the forest, you see, they are not with all their army and the security. No. They are without that. So, if there is danger, what happens? Krishna's devotees. So, naturally, when they are in danger as devotees, they think of Krishna. And Krishna immediately responds to his devotees. So, because Pandavas were in forest, and there was danger at night, Krishna used to be guarding the Pandavas as the night watchman. Then they have to take some rest. You see, there is a word used here. Prabhupad says that in this description of what all Krishna was to the Pandavas, Veera Asana. Veera Asana, Krishna accepted the post of Veera Asana means acceptance of the post of a watchman with a drawn sword at night. Like he is holding a sword and waiting upon the Pandavas that nobody should harm the Pandavas. If somebody comes or attempts to even attack the Pandavas, they are going to be immediately killed with the sword. So, that kind of service Krishna accepted out of his affection for the Pandavas. Simply because of affection. So, in this way, Pariksit heard that the dealings of the Lord and his pure devotees is so very sweet, so very relatable, that Pariksit began to feel himself, his devotion towards Krishna being aroused into ecstasy. And out of ecstasy, he began to shed tears of love for Krishna. And that is being described here.

---
### transcendental dealings, spiritual reciprocation, devotional service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/hd4IJZQfiIQ?t=901
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Hearing about Krishna's activities itself is devotional service. It is completely a transcendental activity. And it purifies us and makes us eligible to go back, come back to God."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes by emphasizing the transcendental nature of Krishna's dealings with his devotees, highlighting that hearing about these activities is itself a form of devotional service that purifies and leads one back to God.

So, Srila Prabhupada says, even though the Lord's feelings towards, dealings with his devotees appear to be like ordinary human dealings, but the truth is that they are all completely transcendental. They are spiritual reciprocation between Krishna and his pure devotees. They have no tinge of any material contamination in them. There is no material sentiment. That Krishna did this to me, gave me this favour, so now I have to actually do some other favour to Krishna. No. None of those calculations are there. Neither they think, oh, Krishna is powerful, so he is capable of protecting me. No. No such calculation. No such calculation. So, there is a distinct difference between Krishna's activities, his dealings with his devotees, his incarnations, and the conditioned souls performing some activities or relating with other conditioned souls. There is a lot of difference between the two. So, therefore, hearing about Krishna's activities itself is devotional service. It is completely a transcendental activity. And it purifies us and makes us eligible to go back, come back to God. So, I will stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

---

## Daily Evening Bhagavatam Discourse | HH Stoka Krishna Swami | SB 1.16.17-18 | 20-01-2023
**Speaker:** HH Stoka Krishna Swami

### devotion, service, Krishna consciousness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/-BM_v38XcEA?t=35
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna is willing to do anything for his devotees, and devotees are completely surrendered to Krishna."
**Summary:** This segment introduces the verses to be discussed, focusing on Maharaj Pariksit's rule and the relationship between Krishna and his devotees, emphasizing Krishna's willingness to serve them and their complete surrender to Him.

Hare Krishna Hare Krishna Krishna Krishna Hare Hare Hare Rama Hare Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya Narayanam Namaskritya Naram Chai Vanarottamam Devim Saraswati Vyasam Tato Jayam Udi Rayet. In this session, we will be discussing the seventeenth and eighteenth verses of the sixteenth chapter of the first canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. The seventeenth verse is as follows: Tasyaivam Vartamanasya Purvesham Vrittim Anvaham Natidhure Kilashcharyam Yadasi Tannibodhame. The meaning of this verse is as follows: Now you may hear from me of what happened while Maharaj Pariksit was passing his days hearing of the good occupations of his forefathers and being absorbed in thought of them. So this has reference to the previous few verses in which Suta Goswami is describing the regime of Maharaj Pariksit. If you remember, the Pandavas retired after they heard that Krishna had gone to his personal abode after winding up his pastimes. Before retiring, Yudhishthira Maharaj handed over charge of ruling the world to Pariksit Maharaj. So Pariksit Maharaj was trained, he took charge, and he performed, he got married, he had good sons, headed by Janamejaya, and he performed three hard sacrifices under the able guidance of qualified Acharyas, Brahmanas, and he was ruling very nicely. So when he went out for conquest, he heard everywhere he went how the Pandavas who were ruling before him were great devotees of the Supreme Lord Krishna and how the Pandavas were able to get from Krishna anything they desired because of their service attitude towards the Supreme Lord. So it was described that Krishna, who is the Supreme Lord, rendered service to the Pandavas by accepting posts ranging from chariot driver to president to messenger to friend to night watchman. Like that, Krishna is willing to do anything for his devotees, and devotees are completely surrendered to Krishna. They simply want to please Krishna in whichever way Krishna wants them to serve him. This is the wonderful relationship that exists between Krishna and his pure devotees in the spiritual world. And even in the material world when Krishna incarnates, he comes down with his confidential associates and he demonstrates to us what is life like in the spiritual world. So that's what Krishna is demonstrating.

---
### human life, Vedic knowledge, suffering
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/-BM_v38XcEA?t=247
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "We can become free from all suffering if we study the Vedas, hear from the Vedas, or learn the Vedas, and adopt a lifestyle that is in accordance with the Vedic principles."
**Summary:** This segment discusses the purpose of human life, the importance of reviving our relationship with Krishna, and how Vedic literature can answer fundamental questions about life, death, and suffering, offering a path to liberation through Vedic principles.

In his relationship with the Pandavas, in his relationship with Kunti, in his relationship with Draupadi, in his relationship with Yashoda, Nanda Maharaj, the whole Brajavasis, the Mathura Nivasis, the Dwarka Nivasis, the members of the Yadu dynasty, they were all so attached to Krishna. So actually each one of us has got a relationship with Krishna, and we are meant to revive that relationship, especially when we have this human form of body. Because we realize in the human form of body, I don't want suffering, but it is forced on me. I want to know who am I, I want to know what is my source. Where was I before birth? What will happen to me after death? So these questions are best answered in our Vedic literature, the Vedas. They are very special because they answer these fundamental questions. And not only do they answer the questions, they show us the way how to be living, really. They continue to stay out of this predicament that we are in right now. We don't want suffering, but suffering is forced on us. So we can become free from all suffering if we study the Vedas, hear from the Vedas, or learn the Vedas, and adopt a lifestyle that is in accordance with the Vedic principles. The Vedic principles are meant for those human beings who want to make a permanent solution to all their problems and get out of all this entanglement. So now Suta Goswami is going to describe when Parikshit Maharaj is ruling and he was passing his days hearing of the good occupation of his forefathers, what happened? I mean, already it has been told that Parikshit was ruling when Kali Yuga was just beginning. So what are the symptoms of Kali and what are the remedies for the specific conditions in Kali Yuga which are unfavorable for peaceful living and for prosperity of the people. So he is, Suta Goswami is going to describe this now.

---
### Dharma, Kali Yuga, Mother Earth
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/-BM_v38XcEA?t=462
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "When the bull and the cow are in a joyful mood, it is to be understood that the people of the world are also in a joyful mood."
**Summary:** This segment introduces the eighteenth verse, explaining how Dharma, in the form of a bull, met Mother Earth, in the form of a cow, grieving. It then explains Srila Prabhupada's commentary on the symbolism of the bull representing moral principles and the cow representing the earth, and how their condition reflects the state of human society.

In the 18th verse he says, The meaning of this verse is, now Suta Goswami is addressing the sages of Naimishananya. He is telling, The personality of religious principles, Dharma, was wandering about in the form of a bull, and he met, that is, Dharma, met the personality of earth in the form of a cow, who appeared to grieve like a mother who had lost her child. She had tears in her eyes and the beauty of her body was lost. Thus, Dharma questioned the earth as follows. So what will follow after this 18th verse are the questions, various questions asked by Dharma to Mother Earth. So in the Purport, Srila Prabhupāda comments on this condition of mother earth in the form of a cow in which Dharma found her wandering when he was wandering. So Srila Prabhupada says the bull is the emblem of the moral principle. What's the meaning of emblem? Emblem is a symbolic representation of something, just like the moral principles that human beings follow in human society are reflected in the condition of the bull, are reflected in the condition of the cow, and the cow is the representative of the earth. The cow is the representative of the earth. So mother earth in the form of a cow is indicating what is the status of the earth in Kali Yuga, and the bull is indicating the status of the population in terms of moral principles that they follow or don't follow. So Srila Prabhupada says when the bull and the cow are in a joyful mood, it is to be understood that the people of the world are also in a joyful mood. People are happy and are very joyful if the bull and cow are in a joyful mood.

---
### domestic animals, bull, cow
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/-BM_v38XcEA?t=661
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Among all the domestic animals, the most important are the bull and the cow."
**Summary:** This segment differentiates between domestic and wild animals, highlighting the importance of domestic animals, especially the bull and the cow, in human society. It emphasizes their role in agriculture, providing milk, and representing moral principles and the earth.

These bull and cow are very important domestic animals. Perhaps you are aware that animals are divided into two categories: the domestic and the wild animals. Wild animals live in the forest, and there the law of the jungle prevails. There is no... There are so many other words. They are trying to bring restrictions. Different governments are telling that animals, wild animals, should not be exploited for entertaining the people in general. Taming a wild animal is no longer encouraged or permitted by so many governments. Anyway, so the wild animals live in the jungle, and there the very nature of those animals is such that nature regulates them in nature's own way. Whereas these domestic animals, they are dependent for protection and engagement in a specific way on the human society. Just like cows, bulls, goats, sheep, dogs, and cats. All these are domestic animals. They very easily are befriended by any human being, very easily. And they give some service, and they are also taken care of. They are given some food, or they are given some shelter, or they are given some kind of protection. There is reciprocation also with these domestic animals. So, among all the domestic animals, the most important are the bull and the cow. Why? So, Prabhupada says because the bull is the emblem of moral principles in human society and the cow is the representative of the earth. And scriptures, the Vedic scriptures especially recommend that human society should particularly protect the bull and the cow. Particularly they should protect the bull and the cow. Why?

---
### Brahmanical culture, cow protection, spiritual values
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/-BM_v38XcEA?t=861
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "If the cow and bull are protected for the good of human society, then there is advancement of Brahmanical culture."
**Summary:** This segment explains the importance of protecting cows and bulls for the advancement of Brahmanical culture, emphasizing their contributions to agriculture and nourishment. It contrasts this with the mistreatment of these animals by those lacking spiritual values, leading to suffering in society.

Because the bull helps production of grains in the agricultural field. Not only production of grains. The bulls are used in so many ways, at least in the Vedic culture, for drawing water from the well, for removing the husk from the grains after they are grown, for extracting oil from oilseeds, for transportation. The bull can pull. It has got an immense amount of strength to do all these activities. And the cow delivers milk, the miracle of aggregate food values. So, cow is specially meant for giving us milk, which is meant for our well-being, that it contains all the nourishment and nutrition that we require as human beings, particularly the cow's milk is highlighted. So, if the cow and bull are protected for the good of human society, then there is advancement of Brahmanical culture. Brahmanical culture is a culture meant for self-realization, spiritual realization, spiritual advancement. And if the bull and cow are neglected, or worse still, they are killed for being eaten as food, that is done by people who do not know the value of Brahmanical culture. They are uncultured, uncivilized, non-Brahmanical people. They have no spiritual values in life. They have no higher goal in life. Simply they live like for eating, sleeping, mating, defending, like animals they live. So, then if the cow and bull are mistreated, then the whole human society has to suffer in so many ways. And that is what is going to be described in the next few verses. I will stop here. Thank you very much. Hare Krishna.

### Srimad Bhagavatam, Dharma, Mother Earth
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/37GcMWFJ8oo?t=59
**Verse References:** SB 1.16.19
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Dharma in the form of a bull asked madam addressing the cow. Are you not hale and hearty? Why are you covered with the shadow of grief?"
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the 19th verse of the 16th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam, focusing on Dharma's inquiry to Mother Earth, represented as a cow, about her state of distress.

In this session we will be discussing from the 19th verse of the 16th chapter of the 1st canto of Srimad Bhagavatam. Dharmavacha Kachit Bhadre Anamayam Atmanaste Vichayasi Mlayateshan Mukhena Alakshaye Bhavateem Antaradhim Dure Bandhum Shochasi Kanchanam Ba. The translation of this 19th verse: Dharma in the form of a bull asked madam addressing the cow. Are you not hale and hearty? Why are you covered with the shadow of grief? It appears by your face that you have become black. Are you suffering from some internal disease? Or are you thinking of some relative who is away in a distant place?

---
### Kali Yuga, Suffering, Anxiety
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/37GcMWFJ8oo?t=131
**Verse References:** SB 1.16.19
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "This exactly reflects the condition of people in this present age Kali Yuga."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the verse, explaining how it symbolically represents the suffering and anxieties prevalent in Kali Yuga, drawing a parallel between the cow's distress and the condition of people in the current age, referencing Srila Prabhupada's explanation.

So this symbolic representation of the condition of human society reflected in the condition of the cow, the representative of mother earth, the cow. And even the condition of the bull, that will be described in the next verse, the condition of the bull. So dharma is inquiring from mother earth. Madam addressing Bhadre. This is a respectful way of addressing a lady in the samsara. In Sanskrit language, Bhadre. Madam. Are you not hale and hearty? Are you not keeping good health? Are you not happy? Why are you covered with the shadow of grief? That means not only is mother earth unhappy, but she is unhappy about something which seems like it is a cause for distress or unhappiness, continues to linger for a long time. It's not something which comes and immediately goes away. It's not like a pinprick, that momentarily there is some pain and after the cause for the pinprick is removed then the pain is gone. It's not like that. You are covered with the shadow of grief, and it appears by your face that you have become black. That means the pain has been there. The problem of this cause for the pain has been there. Are you suffering from some internal disease? And what is the cause of your pain? Why are you suffering? Some internal disease? Is there some disease that has afflicted you? Or are you thinking of some relative who is away in a distant place? You are separated from your relative, and you are not able to live together happily. You are separated, and you are not able to actually be together due to some reason. So Aushriya Prabhupada very nicely explains that this exactly reflects the condition of people in this present age Kali Yuga. We can relate with it because as Prabhupada explains, this is our position today in Kali Yuga.

---
### Sadhu, Compassion, God Consciousness
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/37GcMWFJ8oo?t=330
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "But they can't tolerate to see the suffering of the other people who are not in God consciousness."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses the nature of saintly persons (sadhus) and their compassion for those suffering in Kali Yuga due to a lack of God consciousness, emphasizing that a true sadhu is one who is unhappy due to the unhappiness of others.

Shriya Prabhupada says in the purport, the people of the world in this age of Kali are always full of anxieties. In the beginning of the first chapter, when the sages Naimisharanya could foresee the conditions of people in Kali Yuga, fourth coming age Kali Yuga, at the end of Dwapar Yuga, then they were anxiously inquiring from Suta Goswami what is the remedy for people's problems in Kali Yuga. Because these saintly persons they are called sadhus, and sadhus means they live for welfare of others. They themselves are situated in Krishna consciousness, God consciousness so they are happy, and they have solved their problems by God consciousness, by being in God consciousness. But they can't tolerate to see the suffering of the other people who are not in God consciousness, who suffers in this material world especially in Kali Yuga, those who are not God conscious. So the saintly persons who are happy because of being God conscious, they can't tolerate to see the suffering of others. Therefore they want a remedy to remove the suffering of the people in general. How can their suffering be removed? How can they become happy? This is the position of the sadhu. Sadhu is not a person with a big beard, with some big tilak, with some saffron dress. That is not a real sadhu. Those may be external symbols to indicate to somebody else that I am a sadhu, but real symptom of a sadhu is Paradukhe Dukhi. Actually sadhu means a devotee, a genuine devotee of Krishna, because a genuine devotee is Paradukhe Dukhi. He is unhappy because of others unhappiness. Himself he is not unhappy on his own account, because a devotee by practicing Krishna consciousness, by being in Krishna consciousness.

---
### Krishna Consciousness, Anxiety, Health
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/37GcMWFJ8oo?t=473
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The people of the world in this age of Kali are always full of anxieties."
**Summary:** The speaker continues to describe the characteristics of a devotee and the suffering prevalent in Kali Yuga, highlighting anxieties, diseases, and the general unhappiness reflected in people's faces, referencing Srila Prabhupada's statements.

He is always happy in Krishna consciousness. Krishna is the protector, Krishna is the maintainer, Krishna is the provider, Krishna is the servitor of his devotees. So a devotee of Krishna has no reason to be unhappy on his own account, but he is unhappy looking at the condition of the suffering souls who are bereft of God consciousness, who are non-devotees. So in this age of Kali, the sages of Naimisharan were pointing out to Suta Goswami, Upadrutaha. People are always disturbed in the mind. That means the mind is full of anxieties. That is what Prabhupada is telling here. The people of the world in this age of Kali are always full of anxieties. Some reason will be there for them to be filled with anxiety. So it is said here by Srila Prabhupada, everyone is diseased with some kind of ailment. Needless to say, hospitals are prospering in business, in doing business. The private hospitals of course, not the government hospitals. The private hospitals are prospering, and hospital beds are not available, not easily available, specially during the pandemic and after the pandemic. So many cases of sickness, ailments. People are suffering in so many ways, even before the pandemic, because of Kali Yuga. People have some heart problem, people have some cancer, some people have some kidney problem, some people are diabetic, some people have got high blood pressure, and there are accidents, and there is nutritional deficiency. Constitutionally some people are weak, so they are susceptible to fall sick because of not having the necessary nourishment. The food is, I mean I can go on like this. It can be very lengthy tale of woe, but it is a fact. People suffer in so many ways in this present age, but some kind of ailment. Very few people are very healthy and very cheerful, free from anxieties. Very few people. Then further Prabhupada says from the very faces of the people of this age, one can find out the index of the mind. This is another saying in the scriptures. The face is the index of the mind. If you look at somebody's face, you can understand whether they are internally happy or not happy. You can make out. You look at the face of people in general in Kali Yuga, most people, you can make out that they are not happy.

---
### Family, Separation, Kali Yuga
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/37GcMWFJ8oo?t=687
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The particular symptom of the age of Kali is that no family is now blessed to live together."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses the breakdown of the family structure in Kali Yuga, highlighting separation and internal suffering, and concludes by summarizing the three main problems of people in this age as mentioned in the verse.

Everyone feels the absence of his relative who is away from home. First thing is people are, people's mind is always disturbed because of some anxiety that they go through. Second symptom is being described here that they have some kind of ailment, some nutritional deficiency, or some weakness, or some particular condition, heart, condition of the heart, or condition of the kidney, or condition of the digestive system, or some kind of skin rashes, or some kind of allergy. So many, we can go on listing so many problems in terms of the poor health of the people in general in this Kali Yuga, majority of the people. And the third thing is that everyone practically feels the absence of his or her relative who is away from home. And what does this mean? Srila Prabhupada says the particular symptom of the age of Kali is that no family is now blessed to live together. It's very rare. As you know, the joint family system is already broken. Very few joint families are there. So there are very very small family units, husband, wife, and children at the most. And even that husband, wife, and children, it is described by Prabhupada, this small unit, even if they have just one son or one child, the one child, the one child, the one child, to earn a livelihood, the father lives at a place far away from the son because the job demands. The father has to travel, or the father is transferred from one city to another city. You see. Or the wife lives far away from the husband. Sometimes the wife is working, she has to work in a different location, and the husband is working in a different location. And both of them have to work for making ends meet, although they can't maintain the family. So in this way, the family members are not together. They are not able to stay together. There are sufferings from internal suffering, but from internal diseases separation from those near and dear anxieties for maintaining the status quo proper summarizes three specific problems of people in Kali Yuga mentioned in this verse. These are but some important factors which make the people of this age always unhappy that's reflected in the condition of the cow mother earth appeared in the form of a cow so I'll stop here thank you very much Hare Krishna.

Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya. Narayanam Namaskritya Naram Chai Vanarottamam Deveem Saraswateem Vyasam Tato Jayam Udhirayet. In this session, we will be discussing from the second verse of the 16th chapter of the first canto of Shri Madbhagavatam. Sa Uttarasya Tanayam Upayema Iravatim Janame Jaya Adim Chaturaha Tasyam Utpadyat Sutan. The meaning of this verse is King Parikshit married the daughter of King Uttara and begot four sons headed by Maharaj Janamejaya.

### Family Relationships, Vedic Marriage, Gotra
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Sw-oGkaIUIY?t=87
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Even though they are cousin brother and sister, they can get married if they don't belong to the same gotra."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the family relationships between Parikshit, Uttara, Arjuna, and Abhimanyu, and discusses the Vedic rules regarding marriage between cousins and the importance of different gotras.

So this chapter describes how Parikshit received the age of Kali. Parikshit ascended the throne as the emperor of the world after Yudhishthir Maharaj. Then he married and begot four sons. He married the daughter of King Uttara. So Srila Prabhupada explains the purport. Maharaj Uttara was the son of Virudhara, and this Maharaj Uttara was the maternal uncle of Maharaj Parikshit. Why? Or how is this relationship? Because when the Pandavas were living incognito in the kingdom of Virudhara, Arjuna was instructing Virudhara's daughter. So, of course, Arjuna was in disguise as a dance teacher. Then when later on Virudhara came to know that this Arjuna as a dance teacher was not an ordinary dance teacher but he was Arjuna, then Virudhara wanted to marry his daughter to Arjuna. But Arjuna refused because Arjuna said as a teacher, I am like the father. I am in the position of a father, so I can't marry her, but my son can marry her. So Arjuna's son Abhimanyu, he married Virudhara's daughter. Now Parikshit is the son of Abhimanyu. So Uttara, sorry, Iravati, the daughter of Maharaj Uttara, who is the brother-in-law of Abhimanyu, is actually a cousin to Parikshit. So Iravati was a cousin sister of Maharaj Parikshit. Maharaj Uttara's daughter and Abhimanyu's son Parikshit were cousins. So Srila Prabhupada says cousin brothers and sisters were allowed to get married if they did not belong to the same gotra or family. So this is the important point. Even though they are cousin brother and sister, they can get married if they don't belong to the same gotra.

---
### Gotra, Vedic Marriage, Lineage
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Sw-oGkaIUIY?t=258
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "In the Vedic system of marriage, the importance of the gotra or family was stressed."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the importance of gotra in Vedic marriages, explaining why sagotra marriages are discouraged and providing the example of Arjuna marrying Subhadra, who was also a cousin but from a different gotra. He then transitions to discussing the history of Janamejaya.

Gotra means family. In the Vedic system of marriage, the importance of the gotra or family was stressed. Sagotra marriage is not allowed because the progeny will be weak. Sagotra marriage. The children they conceive will be weak. So therefore, different gotra, belonging to different gotra, is important for consideration of marriage. And Prabhupada gives another example. Arjuna also married Subhadra, although she was his maternal cousin sister. Arjuna married Krishna's sister. Krishna was the cousin brother of Arjuna because Arjuna's mother Kunti and Krishna's father Vasudeva were sister and brother. So therefore, Arjuna and Krishna were cousins. But therefore, Arjuna and Krishna, not only Arjuna and Krishna, Arjuna and Subhadra were also cousins. How could they marry each other if they were cousins, brother and sister? That is because they belong to a different gotra. Now Srila Prabhupada in the purport explains the brief history of Janamejaya. Janamejaya was the eldest son of Parikshit. Parikshit begot four sons headed by Janamejaya. So Janamejaya's brief history is given by Srila Prabhupada. He says one of the Rajarshi kings and the famous son of Maharaj Parikshit, Janamejaya, is a Rajarshi, a saintly king, Raja, Rishi. Both Raja and Rishi. That means he is a saintly king. His mother's name was Iravati. Janamejaya's mother's name was Iravati, or according to some, Madravati.

---
### Janamejaya, Sacrifice, Kingship
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Sw-oGkaIUIY?t=377
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "These emperors or kings were very responsible in leaving behind a well-trained, qualified successor."
**Summary:** The speaker continues with the history of Janamejaya, mentioning his sons, his sacrifices in Kurukshetra, and his eventual succession to the throne after Parikshit's retirement. He emphasizes the responsibility of kings to leave behind qualified successors for the welfare of the world.

Maharaj Janamejaya begot two sons of the names Gnatanika and Shankukarna. He celebrated several sacrifices in the Kurukshetra pilgrimage site. This Janamejaya, later on it will be described that Maharaj Parikshit handed over charge of ruling the world to his eldest son Janamejaya when Parikshit retired from his royal duties, when he was cursed to die within seven days by a snake bite. Those incidents will be described in the later chapters. So therefore it is described when Janamejaya took charge of ruling the world from his father Parikshit, he also got married and he begot two sons of the names Gnatanika and Shankukarna. These emperors or kings were very responsible in leaving behind a well-trained, qualified successor for the welfare of the world, for ruling the world properly, there is a need for a good Kshatriya, a qualified Kshatriya, a proper Kshatriya to rule the world for the welfare of the citizens.

### Revenge, Sacrifice, Janamejaya
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Sw-oGkaIUIY?t=536
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "He performed a great sacrifice called Sarpayagna to kill the race of serpents."
**Summary:** The speaker details Janamejaya's reaction to his father's death by snakebite, including his performance of the Sarpayagna to kill all snakes and his eventual decision to stop the sacrifice due to the advice of demigods and sages.

And it is also described here, Janamejaya had three younger brothers named Shrutasena, Ugrasena, and Bhimasena II. So he had three younger brothers also. He invaded Takshashila Rajanta and he decided to avenge the unlawful curse upon his great father, Maharaj Pariksit. Later on it will be described that is, Pariksit was cursed to die by snake bite. So this Janamejaya, when he learnt about it, he wanted to take revenge against the snake which bit his father, according to the curse. So what did he do to take revenge? This is Janamejaya. He performed a great sacrifice called Sarpayagna to kill the race of serpents. He wanted to kill all the snakes because one serpent bit his father. Of course, his real anger was against Takshaka, one of the serpents, one of the serpents who bit his father, because his father was cursed to die by snake bite, particularly Takshaka's bite. So this Janamejaya arranged a Sarpayagna to be performed by qualified Brahmanas, and his aim was to kill the race of serpents, including Takshaka. Now on request from many influential demigods and sages, he had to change his decision to kill the race of serpents. That means, when he undertook this sacrifice, he was told by, advised by many influential devatas and sages and saintly persons, don't do this. Don't simply kill all the serpents. This is unfair. This is not proper. And then he actually agreed to stop the sacrifice when he was instructed or advised by many saintly persons and even devatas not to do this kind of sacrifice to kill all the serpents.

### Sacrifice, Dakshina, Vyasadeva, Kurukshetra
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Sw-oGkaIUIY?t=684
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Vyasadeva, he is an incarnation of the Supreme Lord for the sake of preparing the scriptures, especially the scriptures for the people of Kali Yuga and the common people."
**Summary:** The speaker describes Janamejaya's generosity in rewarding those involved in the Sarpayagna, even after stopping the sacrifice. He then discusses the presence of Vyasadeva at the sacrifice and his narration of the history of the battle of Kurukshetra to Janamejaya.

But in spite of stopping the sacrifice, what did Janamejaya do? He satisfied everyone concerned in the sacrifice by rewarding them properly. He gave the proper Dakshina, even though the sacrifice was stopped. It was not conducted, but he did give the Dakshina to the sages, to the brahmanas who were appointed for performing the sacrifice. In this ceremony, Mahamuni Vyasadeva was also present in the sarpayagna, preparation for sarpayagna, and he personally narrated the history of the battle of Kurukshetra before King Janamejaya. So Vyasadeva, he is an incarnation of the Supreme Lord for the sake of preparing the scriptures, especially the scriptures for the people of Kali Yuga and the common people. So Mahamuni Vyasadeva was present in that sacrifice and he personally narrated the history of the battle of Kurukshetra to Janamejaya. How? Janamejaya's great grandfather, Pariksit's grandfather is Arjuna. So Janamejaya's great grandfather is Arjuna. How Arjuna fought in the battle of Kurukshetra, how Arjuna was instructed by Krishna, how Arjuna conducted himself very bravely, etc., etc.

Later on, by the order of Vyasadeva, Vyasadeva's disciple, Narayana, narrated before King Janamejaya the subject matter of Mahabharata. Now the Bhagavad Gita is a small portion of Mahabharata. The Mahabharata is the history of greater India. It is a very, very big scripture. While Bhagavad Gita has 700 verses, Mahabharata has 100,000 verses or one lakh verses. Mahabharata has lakh shlokas. Bhagavatam has only 18,000 shlokas. Mahabharata has one lakh shlokas. So that Mahabharata was narrated to Janamejaya by Vaishampayana, Rishi, who was a disciple of Vyasadeva. On the instruction of Vyasadeva, he did so. So this Janamejaya was affected very much by the father's untimely death, and he expressed his desire to Vedavyasa, I want to see my father again. And Vyasadeva fulfilled his desire. So when his father was presented by Vyasadeva, that is Pariksit, then both Pariksit and Vyasadeva were worshipped.
